timeline.event: Born as the second son of 8 children in Ajaccio, Corsica.
Went to France with his older brother Joseph and entered the religious school attached to the Autun Abbey.
Entered the military school of Brienne.
Entered the Ecole Militaire in Paris.
His father Carlo died of stomach cancer.
Passed the artillery officer appointment exam and was commissioned as a second lieutenant at the age of 16.
Assigned as an artillery officer to the La Fere regiment stationed in Valence.
Worked there when the La Fere regiment moved its garrison to Auxonne.
The French Revolution broke out.
Took a leave and returned to his hometown of Corsica.
Louis XVI was executed.
Detained by Paoli's side, his house and farm were looted, and he escaped Corsica with his family.
Won by recapturing the L'Eguillette and Balaguier forts at the Siege of Toulon and was promoted to brigadier general.
The Thermidorian Reaction occurred, and Robespierre's group was executed.
Imprisoned due to his friendship with Robespierre's younger brother Augustin but soon released.
Suppressed the royalist riot in Paris.
Married Josephine de Beauharnais.
Appointed commander of the Army of Italy.
Occupied the Austrian capital of Vienna and signed the Treaty of Campo Formio.
Landed at the port of Alexandria in Egypt with about 50,000 troops, beginning the Egyptian campaign.
The French navy was defeated by Horatio Nelson's British fleet at the Battle of Aboukir Bay.
Escaped from Egypt and returned to France.
Staged the Coup of 18 Brumaire with the revolutionary government's power figure Sieyes.
A new constitution establishing three consuls was submitted to a national referendum and passed.
The French expeditionary force crossed the Great St Bernard Pass.
Achieved victory at the Battle of Marengo against the Austrian army.
Established the Bank of France to promote economic stability.
Amicably settled relations with the United States through the Treaty of Mortefontaine.
Signed the Concordat of 1801 with Pope Pius VII.
Sent an expeditionary force of about 30,000 to Haiti (Saint-Domingue) in an attempt to recapture the colony and revive slavery.
Signed the Treaty of Amiens with Britain, achieving stability after 10 years.
Created the Legion of Honour.
Became First Consul for life, strengthening his dictatorial power.
Sold Louisiana to the United States for $15 million.
Discovered the assassination plot by Georges Cadoudal and royalists who opposed Napoleon and hoped for a Bourbon restoration.
Gave up the occupation of Haiti and withdrew troops.
Haiti formally became independent, becoming the first black republic.
The acceptance of the empire was decided through a national referendum.
Held his coronation at Notre-Dame Cathedral, becoming Napoleon I, the first Emperor of the French Empire.
The French navy was devastatingly defeated by the British navy led by Nelson at the Battle of Trafalgar.
The French army won a decisive victory at the Battle of Austerlitz, and Austria surrendered by signing the Treaty of Pressburg.
Placed his younger brother Louis on the throne of Holland.
Defeated the Prussian army at the Battle of Jena-Auerstedt and entered Berlin.
Issued the Continental System through the Berlin Decree.
Signed the Treaties of Tilsit with Alexander I of Russia.
Invaded Portugal when it violated the Continental System.
Occupied Rome.
The residents of Madrid started a riot against the French army.
Annexed the Papal States.
Arrested the Pope and imprisoned him in Savona after he declared excommunication.
Divorced Empress Josephine.
Remarried Marie Louise, daughter of Austrian Emperor Francis I.
Sweden offered the Swedish succession to French Marshal Bernadotte.
Marie Louise gave birth to Napoleon II, King of Rome.
Conducted the Russian campaign leading an army of 600,000.
Forcibly transferred the Pope to Fontainebleau.
The French army won the large battle near Borodino.
Occupied Moscow.
The French army retreated from Russia.
Signed the Concordat of Fontainebleau with Pope Pius VII.
Suffered a crushing defeat by the allied forces at the Battle of Leipzig.
The French capital of Paris fell.
Signed the Treaty of Fontainebleau and was exiled as the sovereign of Elba.
Escaped from Elba.
Entered Paris and was restored as Emperor of the French.
Defeated by the combined attack of Britain and Prussia at the Battle of Waterloo, ending the Hundred Days.
Exiled to Saint Helena in the South Atlantic.
Passed away at 5:49 PM.
His remains were returned to France and entombed at Les Invalides in Paris.

keyAchievements.title: Enactment of the Napoleonic Code
keyAchievements.description: Enacted the French Civil Code, the so-called Napoleonic Code, in 1804, which greatly influenced civil law worldwide.

keyAchievements.title: Development of military tactics and systems
keyAchievements.description: Influenced modern warfare militarily and developed tactics, strategy, training, organization, logistics, clothing, and reward systems.

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Siege of Toulon
keyAchievements.description: After leading the victory at the Siege of Toulon in 1793, he was promoted to brigadier general at the age of 24.

keyAchievements.title: Success of the Italian campaign
keyAchievements.description: Achieved great success in the Italian campaign in 1796, winning consecutive victories in the War of the First Coalition, and rode a wave of success as a soldier.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Bank of France
keyAchievements.description: Established the Bank of France in 1800 to promote economic stability.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Legion of Honour
keyAchievements.description: Created the famous Legion of Honour in 1802.

keyAchievements.title: Concordat of 1801
keyAchievements.description: Signed a political and religious concordat with Pope Pius VII in 1801, seeking reconciliation with the Roman Catholic Church and recognizing Protestants.

keyAchievements.title: Paris redevelopment project
keyAchievements.description: Promoted modernization policies for the city to make Paris, the capital of France, the world's foremost capital after becoming emperor.

faq.question: Was Napoleon actually short?
faq.answer: No, his actual height was 167-168cm, which was taller than the average height of 164cm for French soldiers in their 20s at the time. The misconception that he was short is due to differences in units of measurement and confusion with Napoleon III.

faq.question: How did Napoleon die?
faq.answer: His official cause of death is known as stomach cancer, but a large amount of arsenic was detected in his hair, raising theories of poisoning.

faq.question: Was Beethoven's Symphony 'Eroica' dedicated to Napoleon?
faq.answer: Beethoven considered Napoleon a hero of republicanism and named his Symphony No. 3 'Bonaparte', but was disappointed when Napoleon became emperor, abandoned the dedication, and changed the title to 'Eroica'.

faq.question: What started Napoleon's downfall?
faq.answer: His downfall began due to the lack of goods and daily necessities caused by the Continental System in 1806, the failure to occupy the Iberian Peninsula, and the failure of the Russian campaign in 1812.

timeline.event: Born in Junsu-bang, Hanseong, as the third son of King Taejong and Queen Wongyeong.
Invested as the new Crown Prince after his older brother, Grand Prince Yangnyeong, was deposed.
Ascended to the throne as the 4th King of Joseon at Geunjeongjeon in Gyeongbokgung Palace following Taejong's abdication.
Appointed Yi Jong-mu as the Commander-in-Chief of the Three Provinces to lead 227 warships and 17,000 soldiers to conquer Tsushima Island.
Established the Hall of Worthies (Jiphyeonjeon) inside the palace as a policy research institute to operate the centralized system.
Exempted from the tribute of virgins and gold/silver to the Ming Dynasty.
Troops led by Kim Jong-seo swept the Jurchens in the Hamgil province and established the Six Garrisons to pioneer the northern territory.
Personally created 28 letters out of pity for the common people who could not easily learn Chinese characters, the writing system at the time.
Promulgated Hunminjeongeum, which he personally created.
His illness worsened, he moved to the house of Grand Prince Yeongeung, and passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Hunminjeongeum
keyAchievements.description: Created Hunminjeongeum, an efficient and scientific writing system that common people could easily learn.

keyAchievements.title: Support for scientific and technological development
keyAchievements.description: Fully supported the invention of the armillary sphere, Angbuilgu (sundial), Jagyeokru (water clock), and rain gauge, and sponsored scholars such as Jang Yeong-sil and Choe Hae-san.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening national defense
keyAchievements.description: Dispatched Yi Jong-mu to conquer Tsushima Island, and expanded the borders by sending Choe Yun-deok and Kim Jong-seo to the north to pioneer the Four Counties and Six Garrisons.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the political system
keyAchievements.description: Appointed Hwang Hui, Maeng Sa-seong, Yun Hoe, and Kim Jong-seo to preside over state affairs, which became the beginning of the State Council system.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the taxation system
keyAchievements.description: Established the land tax system of Joseon by enacting public laws such as the 6-grade land system and 9-grade harvest system.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of books
keyAchievements.description: Compiled books in various fields, including 'Songs of the Moon Shining on a Thousand Rivers', 'Songs of the Dragons Flying to Heaven', 'Straight Talk on Farming', and 'History of Goryeo'.

faq.question: When was King Sejong born?
faq.answer: Sejong was born on May 15, 1397 (April 10 in the lunar calendar) in Junsu-bang, Hanseong, as the third son of King Taejong and Queen Wongyeong.

faq.question: How did King Sejong ascend to the throne?
faq.answer: He was invested as Crown Prince when his older brother, Grand Prince Yangnyeong, was deposed, and ascended to the throne in 1418 following Taejong's abdication.

faq.question: What is King Sejong's greatest achievement?
faq.answer: One of his greatest achievements is the creation of Hunminjeongeum, an efficient and scientific writing system that the common people could easily learn.

faq.question: What achievements did King Sejong leave in the field of national defense?
faq.answer: He dispatched Yi Jong-mu to conquer Tsushima Island, and expanded the borders by sending Choe Yun-deok and Kim Jong-seo to the north to pioneer the Four Counties and Six Garrisons.

faq.question: When did King Sejong pass away?
faq.answer: Sejong passed away on March 30, 1450 (February 17 in the lunar calendar).

timeline.event: Temujin was born to his father Yesugei and mother Hoelun at Deligun Buldag near the Onon River.
Known as the birth year of Genghis Khan, but it varies among historical records (the current Mongolian holiday is November 14).
Visited Dei Sechen of the Onggirat tribe and formally married his daughter Borte.
Elected as the tribal chief of the old Khamag Mongol (Kiyat).
Various lords of the Kiyat Borjigin clan enthroned Temujin as the Khan of Khamag Mongol and bestowed the title Genghis Khan.
The power of the Kiyat tribe grew, and fought the Battle of Dalan Balzhut (Battle of the Thirteen Wings) against Jamukha.
Toghrul Khan, who had been ousted due to internal strife in the Keraites, was restored with Temujin's support.
With the help of the Keraites, Temujin attacked and destroyed the Jurkin clan, unifying the Borjigin clan.
Conducted an expedition and conquered the Merkit tribe remaining in the north of the plateau.
Held a Kurultai at Temeen Keer and declared the conquest of the Naimans.
Genghis Khan allied with the Keraites and attacked the Naimans in the Altai Mountains area.
The remnants of the Tatars, Merkits, and Tayichiud tribes allied and enthroned Jamukha as Khan. Annihilated Jamukha's alliance at the Battle of Koyiten against the northeastern alliance.
Temujin engaged the Tatars, killed their leader Jalin Bukha, and executed over a hundred remaining Tatars.
Ong Khan, deceived by Senggum's plot, attacked Temujin's pasture area, and Temujin successfully ambushed the main Keraite force, winning a great victory.
The Khamag Mongol army drove out and conquered the Naimans.
Temujin defeated the Western Naimans and Northern Merkits, the last major forces left on the Mongolian plateau, and executed Jamukha.
Temujin was enthroned as Genghis Khan at a meeting of tribal chiefs held by the Onon River, founding the Mongol Empire.
Sent his son Jochi to conquer and subjugate the forest tribes. Emperor Xiangzong of Western Xia promised subjugation to Mongolia and sent his daughter to marry Genghis Khan.
Signed a peace treaty with Western Xia and gained recognition of Mongol suzerainty. Subjugated the Kingdom of Qocho (Uyghurs of the Tian Shan).
Sent Kublai to conquer the Turkic nomadic Karluks, and their leader Arslan Khan surrendered. Genghis Khan notified the Jin Dynasty that he would cease tributary relations.
Declared and started war with the Jin Dynasty, defeating a 400,000-strong Jin army at the Battle of Yehuling.
Led his army to attack the Jin Dynasty, conquering and annexing Jin territory south of the Great Wall.
When the Jin Dynasty sued for peace, he took Princess Qiguo as a wife, received compensation, and retreated.
The Mongol army besieged and captured Zhongdu (Beijing), the former capital of the Jin Dynasty.
Genghis Khan dispatched a trade envoy to the Khwarazmian Empire, but Inalchuq, the governor of Otrar, killed the envoy.
Genghis Khan launched an invasion of Khwarazmia with a force of 200,000.
Genghis Khan finished his war of revenge against the Khwarazm-Shah and withdrew.
Hearing news of a rebellion in Western Xia, Genghis Khan left Peshawar and returned to the Mongolian plateau.
Genghis Khan arrived at the Mongolian plateau.
The Mongol army invaded Western Xia, attacking its borders.
Genghis Khan stayed at his main camp in the Liupan Mountains and accepted Western Xia's surrender, but rejected the Jin Dynasty's peace offer.
Genghis Khan died.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of the Mongol Empire and First Khagan
keyAchievements.description: At the 1206 Kurultai, he was enthroned as Genghis Khan, founding the Mongol Empire and becoming its first Khagan.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Mongol tribes
keyAchievements.description: As a military and political leader who unified various Mongol tribes, he conquered the tribes of the Mongolian steppes.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of vast territories
keyAchievements.description: Conquered vast territories ranging from the Korean Peninsula to Central Asia and Eastern Europe, including Mongolia and China.

keyAchievements.title: Merit-based military organization
keyAchievements.description: Led a strong army based on meritocracy and is evaluated as one of history's greatest conquerors and most successful military leaders.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Mongolian script
keyAchievements.description: With the help of a minister from the Naiman tribe, he helped establish the Mongolian language into a written script.

faq.question: What is Genghis Khan's real name?
faq.answer: Genghis Khan's real name is Borjigin Temujin.

faq.question: When did Genghis Khan ascend to the throne of the Mongol Empire?
faq.answer: At the meeting of tribal chiefs held by the Onon River in February 1206, Temujin was enthroned as Genghis Khan, founding the Mongol Empire and ascending as the first Khagan.

faq.question: How is Genghis Khan evaluated?
faq.answer: In Mongolia, he is praised as a god or national founding hero, but in Eastern Europe and Western Asia, which were invaded, there are contrasting evaluations of him as a mass murderer, looter, and destroyer of civilization.

faq.question: How did Genghis Khan's father die?
faq.answer: When Genghis Khan was 9 years old, his father Yesugei was poisoned by the Tatars, who had a long-standing feud with the Mongolian royal Borjigin clan.

faq.question: Why did Genghis Khan attack the Khwarazmian Empire?
faq.answer: Because Inalchuq, the governor of the city of Otrar, massacred an Islamic caravan of 450 people under Genghis Khan's protection, and the Shah of the Khwarazmian Empire refused to compensate for this.

timeline.event: Born in Pella, Macedonia, to King Philip II and Queen Olympias.
Tutored by the philosopher Aristotle.
Ascended to the throne of Macedonia following the assassination of his father.
Began his campaign against the Persian Empire, winning the Battle of the Granicus.
Defeated the Persian King Darius III at the Battle of Issus.
Founded the city of Alexandria in Egypt.
Decisively defeated Darius III at the Battle of Gaugamela, effectively ending the Persian Empire.
Invaded India and won the Battle of the Hydaspes, but his exhausted army mutinied and forced him to turn back.
Died in Babylon at the age of 32 from a mysterious illness.
Died at the age of 33 in Babylon.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of the Persian Empire
keyAchievements.description: He completely dismantled the vast Achaemenid Persian Empire, the superpower of the day, in a rapid series of brilliant military campaigns.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a Vast Empire
keyAchievements.description: By the age of 30, he had created one of the largest empires in ancient history, stretching from Greece to northwestern India.

keyAchievements.title: Spread of Hellenism
keyAchievements.description: His conquests facilitated the spread of Greek culture, language, and ideas across the Middle East and Asia, initiating the Hellenistic Age.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to the formation of Hellenistic culture
keyAchievements.description: Founded cities named 'Alexandria' in various conquered regions, contributing to the fusion of Eastern and Western cultures and playing a major role in the formation of Hellenistic culture.

keyAchievements.title: East-West fusion policy
keyAchievements.description: Pursued East-West fusion policies by adopting Persian culture, marrying Greeks with residents of conquered regions, and employing Persian officials.

faq.question: Did Alexander really never lose a battle?
faq.answer: Yes, according to historical records, he never lost a single battle, despite often being heavily outnumbered, earning him a reputation as one of the greatest military commanders in history.

faq.question: How did he die?
faq.answer: The exact cause of his death at age 32 is unknown. Theories range from diseases like malaria or typhoid fever to poisoning or complications from his numerous battle wounds and heavy drinking.

faq.question: What happened to his empire after he died?
faq.answer: He died without naming a clear heir. His generals (the Diadochi) immediately fought over his empire, eventually fracturing it into several Hellenistic kingdoms, such as the Ptolemaic Kingdom in Egypt and the Seleucid Empire in Asia.

faq.question: How did Alexander the Great destroy the Persian Empire?
faq.answer: He crushed the Persian army in decisive battles such as the Battle of Issus and the Battle of Gaugamela, drove Darius III to his death, and destroyed the First Persian Empire.

faq.question: What cities did Alexander the Great build?
faq.answer: He built cities named 'Alexandria' in various conquered regions, the most famous being Alexandria in Egypt.

alexander-the-great.missingDataNote: 모든 정보는 제공된 출처 원문에서 직접 확인된 사실만을 기반으로 작성되었습니다.

timeline.event: Walter Elias "Walt" Disney was born.
Decided to become a cartoonist and went to Kansas.
Founded the Disney Brothers Studio with his older brother Roy Disney.
Produced and released the world's first feature-length animated film <Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs>.
Established a huge children's amusement park called Disneyland in the suburbs of Los Angeles.
Diagnosed with terminal lung cancer and died.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of the Walt Disney Studio
keyAchievements.description: Founded the Walt Disney Studio with his older brother Roy Disney in 1923.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Oswald the Lucky Rabbit character
keyAchievements.description: Developed a rabbit character, 'Oswald the Lucky Rabbit', amidst the prevailing cat characters of the time, introducing it as the first work of personality animation.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Mickey Mouse
keyAchievements.description: Created the 'Mickey Mouse' character, which gained immense popularity, and named it 'Mickey' based on his wife Lillian Disney's suggestion.

keyAchievements.title: Production of the world's first sound animation
keyAchievements.description: Produced the third Mickey Mouse work, 'Steamboat Willie', which became the world's first sound animation and the official debut of Mickey Mouse.

keyAchievements.title: First Academy Award for a color animation
keyAchievements.description: Produced 'Flowers and Trees', the first color animation utilizing the three-color printing process, and enjoyed the honor of winning the first Academy Award for Best Short Subject, Cartoons.

keyAchievements.title: Production of the world's first feature-length animation
keyAchievements.description: Produced and released the world's first feature-length animated film, <Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs>, in 1937, creating massive buzz at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Disneyland
keyAchievements.description: Established a huge children's amusement park called Disneyland in the suburbs of Los Angeles in 1955, solidifying his position as a businessman beyond a film producer.

faq.question: When was Walt Disney born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Walt Disney was born on December 5, 1901, and died of terminal lung cancer on December 15, 1966.

faq.question: When was the Walt Disney Studio founded?
faq.answer: The Walt Disney Studio was founded in 1923 with his older brother Roy Disney.

faq.question: What is the representative character created by Walt Disney?
faq.answer: Walt Disney created 'Mickey Mouse', boosting its popularity, and this character represents The Walt Disney Company to this day.

faq.question: What was the world's first feature-length animation produced by Walt Disney?
faq.answer: He produced and released the world's first feature-length animated film, <Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs>, in 1937, achieving great success.

faq.question: Is the rumor that Walt Disney was cryogenically frozen true?
faq.answer: The rumor that Walt Disney was cryogenically frozen circulated for a time, but it is a false rumor; his remains were cremated and interred at Forest Lawn Memorial Park in Glendale.

timeline.event: Born in Milan, Ohio, and grew up in Port Huron, Michigan.
Developed an automatic telegraph.
Established the Menlo Park Laboratory, known as the world's first private research facility.
Developed the phonograph.
Developed the telephone transmitter.
Developed a new type of generator and lighting accessories.
Experimented with electric streetcars.
Built power plants and improved the industry.
Developed engine models and storage batteries.
Delivered a speech at the 50th-anniversary celebration of the invention of the incandescent light bulb at the age of 82.
Passed away at the age of 84 on October 18.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the phonograph
keyAchievements.description: One of the most famous inventions in the world, it is one of the numerous patents registered under Edison's name.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the light bulb
keyAchievements.description: An invention that illuminated city nights, brought development in the power generation and transmission/distribution sectors, and served as the catalyst for electricity to be supplied to every household.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Menlo Park Laboratory
keyAchievements.description: Established the Menlo Park Laboratory, known as the world's first private research facility, in 1876 and continued inventing.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of General Electric
keyAchievements.description: Later founded General Electric, based on the electric lighting company Edison had established.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Edison effect
keyAchievements.description: An important scientific discovery that formed the basis for the invention of the triode vacuum tube, opening the way for the invention of the radio.

faq.question: How many patents did Thomas Edison register?
faq.answer: There are 1,093 US patents registered under Edison's name, and it is unofficially said that he invented 2,332 items.

faq.question: How much formal education did Thomas Edison receive?
faq.answer: Edison received only 3 months of formal education, but thanks to the dedicated education of his mother, who was a teacher, he exhibited his talents.

faq.question: What kind of person was Thomas Edison to his family?
faq.answer: Edison devoted himself to inventions and business but neglected his family; he did not attend his first wife's funeral, and his children's lives were not good.

faq.question: How did Thomas Edison's inventions affect American science and technology?
faq.answer: Without academic theories, Edison's inventions through experimentation and curiosity made the United States a country that could overwhelm Europe in applied technology.

timeline.event: Born as Gaius Julius Caesar.
Became the head of the family at the age of 16 after his father's death.
Nominated as Flamen Dialis, the high priest of Jupiter, and married Cornelia.
Returned to Rome after Sulla's death and began working as a lawyer.
Studied abroad in Rhodes to perfect his rhetoric.
Elected as quaestor, and his aunt Julia and wife Cornelia died in the same year.
Elected as aedile and married Sulla's granddaughter Pompeia.
Ran for and was elected to the position of Pontifex Maximus, the head of the Roman state religion.
Divorced Pompeia due to the Bona Dea festival scandal while serving as praetor and was appointed governor of the province of Hispania Ulterior.
Elected as consul along with Marcus Calpurnius Bibulus and formed the First Triumvirate.
Appointed governor of the province of Gallia Cisalpina and Illyricum, beginning the Gallic Wars.
The figures of the Triumvirate met at Lucca in Gallia Cisalpina and extended Caesar's term as governor for 5 years.
Conducted the first Roman invasion of Britannia after resolving the Germanic tribe issues.
His daughter Julia died in childbirth, and Crassus was killed in the Battle of Carrhae, breaking the Triumvirate.
Essentially conquered Gaul by suppressing the massive rebellion led by Vercingetorix in central Gaul at the Battle of Alesia.
Crossed the Rubicon with just one legion, igniting the fuse of the civil war.
Pacified Hispania and had the praetor Lepidus appoint him as dictator.
Became consul for the second time and resigned from the dictatorship 11 days later.
Headed to Greece to confront Pompey.
Started the battle against Pompey's army at the plains of Pharsalus.
Assassinated by a group of senators led by Marcus Junius Brutus.
The Senate officially consecrated Caesar as a Roman god.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Gaul
keyAchievements.description: Conquered Gaul from 58 to 52 BC, expanding the territory of the Roman Empire to the North Sea.

keyAchievements.title: Leading the First Triumvirate
keyAchievements.description: Formed the First Triumvirate with Marcus Licinius Crassus and Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus, dominating Roman politics for years.

keyAchievements.title: Reform of the Roman Republic
keyAchievements.description: After seizing power, implemented extensive reforms to Roman society and politics and highly centralized the Republic's aristocratic politics.

keyAchievements.title: Declaration of Dictator for Life
keyAchievements.description: Eventually declared himself Dictator for Life, playing a crucial role in the transformation of the Roman Republic into the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Invasion of Britannia
keyAchievements.description: In 55 BC, became the first Roman to carry out an invasion of Britannia.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Acta Diurna
keyAchievements.description: Posted the Acta Diurna, equivalent to a modern newspaper, in the forum to disclose the minutes of the Senate and popular assemblies to the public.

keyAchievements.title: Proposal of the Agrarian Law
keyAchievements.description: Attempted social reform by proposing the Agrarian Law (lex Iulia de agraria), which redistributed public lands to the poor.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of war records
keyAchievements.description: Personally authored war records such as the Gallic Wars and the Civil War to make his life known to future generations.

faq.question: What is the origin of Julius Caesar's name 'Caesar'?
faq.answer: Pliny the Elder claimed that an ancestor of Caesar was born via Caesarean section, hence the name meaning 'cut from the womb'. Other theories suggest it was due to thick hair, bright grey eyes, or an ancestor killing an elephant.

faq.question: Is it true that Julius Caesar was born via Caesarean section?
faq.answer: No. People had the name 'Caesar' before Julius Caesar, and at the time, Caesarean sections were only performed on dead women, whereas Caesar's mother Aurelia lived a long life after giving birth to him.

faq.question: What political line did Caesar follow?
faq.answer: Caesar aligned with the Populares and was a politician who preferred appealing directly to the voters instead of traditional processes.

faq.question: When did Caesar say his famous quote 'The die is cast'?
faq.answer: On January 12, 49 BC, when Caesar crossed the Rubicon with just one legion to start the civil war, Plutarch quoted him saying in Greek 'Let the die be cast!', while Suetonius wrote it in Latin as 'The die is cast'.

faq.question: How was Julius Caesar assassinated?
faq.answer: On March 15, 44 BC, he was assassinated by a group of senators led by Marcus Junius Brutus, who conspired to restore the Republic.

timeline.event: Born on July 30 in a rural area west of Detroit, Michigan, USA.
His mother passed away when he was 12 years old.
Dropped out of school at 15 to become a machinist.
Invited as an engineer to Edison Illuminating Company and began work.
Completed the internal combustion engine and built an automobile.
After leaving Edison Company, he founded the Ford Motor Company at age 40 and began production of the Ford Model T, introducing the belt conveyor system.
Published his book 《My Life and Work》.
Published his book 《My Philosophy of Industry》.
Passed away on April 7 at the age of 84.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Ford Motor Company
keyAchievements.description: In 1903, at the age of 40, he founded the automobile company 'Ford'.

keyAchievements.title: Production of the First Automobile
keyAchievements.description: In 1892, he completed the internal combustion engine and built an automobile.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Ford Model T and Mass Production System
keyAchievements.description: In 1903, he pioneered the belt conveyor system, beginning mass production of the Ford Model T automobile.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Fordism
keyAchievements.description: He put forth four management philosophies: abandoning fear of the future and reverence for the past, not working primarily for competition, prioritizing service over profit, and manufacturing cheaply to sell cheaply.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Ford System
keyAchievements.description: He promoted the '3S' movement—Standardization of products, Simplification of parts, Specialization of tasks—and adopted the conveyor system, contributing to increased labor productivity.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of Worker Welfare
keyAchievements.description: After founding the company, he offered groundbreaking conditions to workers, such as '8 hours of work a day and a minimum wage of $5'.

faq.question: When was Henry Ford born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Henry Ford was born on July 30, 1863, and passed away on April 7, 1947, at the age of 84.

faq.question: What company did Henry Ford establish?
faq.answer: Henry Ford established 'Ford Motor Company,' an automobile manufacturing company, in 1903.

faq.question: What are Henry Ford's major achievements?
faq.answer: He built an automobile in 1892, and in 1903, he pioneered the belt conveyor system, beginning mass production of the Ford Model T.

faq.question: What was Henry Ford's relationship with Edison?
faq.answer: Henry Ford worked as a chief engineer at the Edison Company founded by Edison, and in 1890, he was invited to work as an engineer at the Edison Illuminating Company.

timeline.event: Born as Magdalena Carmen Frida Kahlo y Calderon.
Contracted polio at age 6, leaving her right leg withered.
Severely injured her spine, right leg, and uterus in a traffic accident at age 18.
Married Diego Rivera.
Divorced Diego Rivera in November.
Remarried Diego Rivera.
Painted "The Love Embrace of the Universe".
Died from recurrent pneumonia on July 13.
Exhibition of Frida Kahlo's works at the Sejong Center for the Performing Arts Museum and SOMA Museum of Art.
Exhibition of works by Frida Kahlo and Diego Rivera at the Seoul Arts Center.

keyAchievements.title: Founding a Mexican painting style
keyAchievements.description: Founded a unique painting style that combined Mexican realism, surrealism, symbolism, and Mexican indigenous culture.

keyAchievements.title: Masterpiece "Frida and the Miscarriage"
keyAchievements.description: One of her representative works depicting her life suffering from congenital pelvic deformity.

keyAchievements.title: Masterpiece "Henry Ford Hospital"
keyAchievements.description: One of her representative works depicting her life suffering from congenital pelvic deformity.

keyAchievements.title: Masterpiece "The Love Embrace of the Universe"
keyAchievements.description: A work depicting Frida Kahlo herself holding her lover Diego Rivera, who has the appearance of a child, in her arms.

faq.question: What kind of painter was Frida Kahlo?
faq.answer: She was a Mexican surrealist painter and communist, who created a painting style combining Mexican realism, surrealism, symbolism, and indigenous culture.

faq.question: What was Frida Kahlo's life like?
faq.answer: It was a continuation of suffering, such as contracting polio at age 6 and being severely injured in a traffic accident at age 18.

faq.question: What are Frida Kahlo's representative works?
faq.answer: They include "Frida and the Miscarriage", "Henry Ford Hospital", and "The Love Embrace of the Universe".

faq.question: Who did Frida Kahlo marry?
faq.answer: She married Mexican painter and revolutionary Diego Rivera, and after one divorce, they remarried.

faq.question: What does Frida Kahlo's name 'Frida' mean?
faq.answer: It is a name given by her German father, Guillermo Kahlo, and it means peace in German.

timeline.event: Born as the eldest son of a wealthy equestrian family in Arpinum, Italy.
Served in the military.
Civil war broke out between Gaius Marius and Sulla, during which he studied Plato and Aristotle under Philo.
Began his career as a lawyer for the first time.
Gained fame by successfully defending Roscius, who was accused of patricide.
Left Rome to study rhetoric and philosophy in Athens and Rhodes, learning from Posidonius.
Entered public office starting as quaestor.
Elected as aedile, handled the Verres case, secured Verres' conviction, gained fame as Rome's best lawyer, and wrote the "Against Verres" speeches.
Elected as praetor, wrote a speech supporting Pompey, and published the "Pro Murena" speech.
Became consul, the highest executive office, thwarted Catiline's state treason conspiracy, and delivered the "Against Catiline" speeches.
Published the "Pro Archia" speech.
The First Triumvirate of Caesar, Pompey, and Crassus began, plunging him into a political crisis.
Exiled to Macedonia and had his mansion and villas destroyed due to the passage of a law by Tribune Clodius Pulcher.
Returned to Rome with the help of his supporters and received compensation for damages.
Published "De Oratore".
Published "De Partitione Oratoria".
Elected as augur and advanced to public office again.
Published "De Legibus".
Elected governor of Cilicia, left Rome, and published "De Re Publica".
Elected governor of Capua, left Rome, and joined Pompey's side when the civil war between Caesar and Pompey broke out.
Caesar defeated Pompey at Pharsalus, and Cicero obtained Caesar's permission to return to Italy.
Published "Paradoxa Stoicorum", "Brutus", "Orator", etc.
His beloved daughter Tullia died, and he published "Consolatio", "Hortensius", "De Finibus Bonorum et Malorum", "Tusculanae Disputationes", "De Natura Deorum", etc.
Caesar was assassinated, and Cicero returned to the political stage.
Delivered a series of 'Philippics' speeches criticizing Antony.
Published "De Divinatione", "Cato Maior de Senectute", "De Fato", "Laelius de Amicitia", "De Virtutibus", "De Gloria", "De Officiis", "Topica", etc.
Octavian began the Second Triumvirate with Antony and Lepidus.
Murdered by Antony's soldiers in Formiae (Caieta) and passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Classical Latin
keyAchievements.description: The framework of Classical Latin was established by Cicero, and his Latin style is considered the standard of Classical Latin.

keyAchievements.title: Gained fame as Rome's best lawyer
keyAchievements.description: In the Verres case in 70 BC, he won against the top lawyer of the time and earned the reputation as Rome's best lawyer.

keyAchievements.title: Thwarting Catiline's conspiracy as consul
keyAchievements.description: During his term as consul in 63 BC, he thwarted Catiline's state treason conspiracy, saving the Roman Republic from crisis.

keyAchievements.title: Acceptance of Greek philosophy into Latin
keyAchievements.description: Devoted himself to translating Greek philosophical theories into Latin during his hiatus from political activities.

faq.question: When and where was Cicero born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Cicero was born on January 3, 106 BC in Arpinum, Italy, and died after being murdered in Formiae on December 7, 43 BC.

faq.question: What was the political evaluation of Cicero?
faq.answer: The evaluation of Cicero's political life is generally harsh; he is evaluated as a wandering intellectual in the center of the fray and a versatile opportunist.

faq.question: What was Cicero's contribution to Classical Latin?
faq.answer: The framework of Classical Latin was established by Cicero, and his Latin style is considered the standard of Classical Latin and is used as a standard for Latin learning to this day.

faq.question: How did Cicero meet his death?
faq.answer: On December 7, 43 BC, he became a target of the purge by Antony, who started the Second Triumvirate, and was murdered in Caieta; his head and hands were displayed in the Roman Forum.

timeline.event: Helen Adams Keller was born in a house at Ivy Green in Tuscumbia, Alabama, USA.
Helen's mother, Kate Keller, was inspired by reading Charles Dickens' 'American Notes' and went to Baltimore to seek advice from J. Julian Chisolm.
Anne Sullivan arrived at Helen's house and began to teach her.
Helen enrolled in the Perkins School for the Blind.
Wrote 'The Frost King' at the age of 11.
Helen Keller and Anne Sullivan moved to New York to attend the Wright-Humason School for the Deaf and the Horace Mann School for the Deaf.
Returned to Massachusetts and attended The Cambridge School for Young Ladies.
Admitted to Radcliffe College, the women's college affiliated with Harvard University.
Published her autobiography 'The Story of My Life' with Anne Sullivan and Sullivan's husband, John Macy.
Graduated from Radcliffe College at the age of 24, becoming the first deafblind person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree.
Wrote 'The World I Live In'.
Joined the Socialist Party of America at the age of 29.
Joined the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW).
Published 'Out of the Dark', a series of essays on socialism.
Founded an organization called 'Helen Keller International' with George Kessler.
Wrote articles for the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW).
Appeared in the silent film 'Deliverance'.
Helped found the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU).
Published her spiritual autobiography 'My Religion'.
Anne Sullivan died.
Asked about the loyal dog Hachiko during a visit to Akita Prefecture, Japan, and received an Akita dog named 'Kamikaze-go'.
When 'Kamikaze-go' died, she received his older brother 'Kenzan-go' as an official gift from the Japanese government.
William Gibson's script for Playhouse 90 was realized as the first production of 'The Miracle Worker'.
Polly Thomson died of a stroke.
Suffered a series of strokes and spent her final years at home.
The film 'The Miracle Worker' starring Anne Bancroft and Patty Duke won Academy Awards.
Received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from US President Lyndon B. Johnson.
Inducted into the National Women's Hall of Fame at the New York World's Fair.
Died at the age of 88 at her home, Arcan Ridge, in Easton, Connecticut.
Helen Keller was selected as one of the 18 'most widely admired people of the 20th century' by Gallup.
The state of Alabama featured Helen Keller on its state quarter (25-cent coin).
A bronze statue of Helen Keller was added to the National Statuary Hall Collection.

keyAchievements.title: First deafblind person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree
keyAchievements.description: Graduated from Radcliffe College in 1904, becoming the first blind and deaf person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific author and educator
keyAchievements.description: Published a total of 12 books and numerous articles, and was active as an American author and educator.

keyAchievements.title: Socialist and human rights activist
keyAchievements.description: Actively participated in various social movements including women's suffrage, pacifism, abolition of the death penalty, and opposition to child labor and racism.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Helen Keller International
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded 'Helen Keller International' with George Kessler in 1915, dedicated to research in vision, health, and nutrition.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to the founding of the ACLU
keyAchievements.description: Helped found the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) in 1920.

keyAchievements.title: National honors
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1964, inducted into the National Women's Hall of Fame in 1965, and honored in various ways posthumously.

faq.question: When was Helen Keller born and when did she die?
faq.answer: Helen Keller was born on June 27, 1880, and died on June 1, 1968, at the age of 88.

faq.question: How did Helen Keller lose her sight and hearing?
faq.answer: At 19 months of age, she was diagnosed with scarlet fever and meningitis, which left her with lifelong visual and hearing impairments.

faq.question: Who had the greatest influence on Helen Keller's education?
faq.answer: Teacher Anne Sullivan arrived at Helen's house in March 1887 and had a decisive influence on her life by teaching her language by spelling words into her hand.

faq.question: What social movements did Helen Keller participate in?
faq.answer: Helen Keller was a socialist intellectual who practiced various human rights movements, including women's suffrage, pacifism, abolition of the death penalty, and opposition to child labor and racism.

faq.question: What is the famous work about Helen Keller's life?
faq.answer: The film 'The Miracle Worker', which depicts Helen Keller's childhood and her relationship with Anne Sullivan, is the most well-known, and the 1962 film won Academy Awards.

helen-keller.missingDataNote: 생후 19개월 후 발생한 질병으로 인한 시청각 장애 발생 시점의 정확한 연도가 원문에 명시되지 않아 타임라인에서 생략되었습니다.

timeline.event: Baptized at the Catholic Parish of St. Remigius in Bonn, Germany.
Promoted as a six-year-old 'child prodigy' on the poster for his first public concert.
Began taking lessons from Christian Gottlob Neefe in Bonn.
Began working as Neefe's assistant organist.
His first published work, the piano variations 'Nine Variations on a March by Ernst Christoph Dressler, WoO 63', was published.
Three Piano Sonatas, WoO 47, were published.
The first article about Beethoven appeared in the 'Magazin der Musik'.
Began working as a paid employee at the court chapel and was permanently appointed as the second court organist.
His father Johann was forced to retire from the court, and an order was issued that half of his pension be paid directly to Beethoven to support the family.
Believed to have been commissioned to compose the 'Cantata on the Death of Emperor Joseph II, WoO 87' and the 'Cantata on the Accession of Emperor Leopold II, WoO 88'.
Believed to have been first introduced to Joseph Haydn when Haydn stopped in Bonn during his trip to London.
Commissioned to compose the ballet music 'Musik zu einem Ritterballett, WoO 1'.
Met Haydn again in Bonn on Haydn's return trip from London to Vienna, and Haydn agreed to take Beethoven as a student.
Left Bonn for Vienna with the help of the Elector.
Learned of his father's death shortly after arriving in Vienna.
Established a reputation as a piano virtuoso in Vienna.
Bonn fell to the French.
Performed one of his piano concertos at his first public concert in Vienna.
Arranged for the publication of his first work assigned an opus number (Three Piano Trios Nos. 1-3, Op. 1).
Toured cultural centers in Central Europe, including Prague, Dresden, Leipzig, and Berlin.
Composed Cello Sonatas Nos. 1-2, Op. 5 in Berlin and dedicated them to King Frederick William II.
Returned to Vienna.
Traveled to Pressburg and Pest.
Suffered from severe illness every summer and autumn.
First noticed problems with his hearing.
As his hearing gradually weakened during a trip to Prague, he eventually gave up concert tours altogether.
Composed his first six String Quartets, Op. 18 at the request of Prince Joseph Franz Lobkowitz.
Won a piano 'duel' against the virtuoso Joseph Woelfl.
Piano Sonata No. 8 'Pathétique', Op. 13 was published.
Completed the Septet, Op. 20.
Began teaching piano to the daughters of the Hungarian noblewoman Anna Brunsvik.
Won a piano 'duel' against Daniel Steibelt.
Hired the Burgtheater for the premiere of his first symphony and staged an extensive program.
With the premiere of Symphonies Nos. 1 and 2, he came to be regarded as the most important of a young generation of composers.
The first six String Quartets, Op. 18 were published and dedicated to Prince Lobkowitz.
Completed the ballet music 'The Creatures of Prometheus, Op. 43' and premiered it at the Burgtheater in Vienna.
Met the young countess Giulietta Guicciardi.
Taught Ferdinand Ries.
Young Carl Czerny studied under Beethoven.
Published Piano Sonata No. 14, Op. 27 No. 2 ('Moonlight Sonata') and dedicated it to Giulietta Guicciardi.
Completed Symphony No. 2, but the concert was canceled.
Moved to Heiligenstadt just outside Vienna to try and come to terms with his condition, and wrote the Heiligenstadt Testament.
Began studying piano and composition with Archduke Rudolf of Austria.
Composed Symphony No. 3 'Eroica'.
Count Joseph Deym died.
His position at the Theater an der Wien ended due to a change in management.
Disillusioned by Napoleon declaring himself 'Emperor', he scratched Napoleon's name off the title page of the manuscript of Symphony No. 3.
Symphony No. 3 'Eroica' was premiered.
The opera Fidelio was premiered to nearly empty houses due to the French occupation of the city.
Symphony No. 3 'Eroica' was published.
Musician and publisher Muzio Clementi secured the rights to publish his works in Britain.
Prince Esterhazy commissioned a mass (Mass in C major, Op. 86) for his wife's name day.
Organized and premiered a massive benefit concert that included Symphonies Nos. 5 and 6, Piano Concerto No. 4, the Mass in C major, and the Choral Fantasy.
Was offered the position of Kapellmeister at the court of Kassel by Jerome Bonaparte, leading Archduke Rudolf, Count Kinsky, and Prince Lobkowitz to pledge him a pension to stay in Vienna.
Commissioned to compose incidental music for Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's play Egmont.
The incidental music for Egmont (Op. 84) appeared.
Proposed marriage to Therese Malfatti but was rejected.
Prince Lobkowitz suspended his pension payments.
Fell seriously ill with headaches and high fever.
Met Goethe in Teplitz.
While in Teplitz, he wrote a 10-page love letter to his 'Immortal Beloved'.
Visited his younger brother Johann, hoping to end his cohabitation with Therese Obermayer.
His brother Johann and Therese Obermayer were married.
Prince Kinsky died after falling from a horse.
Went through a difficult emotional period and his compositional output dropped.
Lent 1,500 florins to his brother Kaspar.
Motivated to start composing by the news of Napoleon's defeat at the Battle of Vitoria by the coalition led by the Duke of Wellington.
Wellington's Victory (Op. 91) and his Seventh Symphony, Op. 92 were first performed at a charity concert for war victims.
Wellington's Victory was received with repeated performances.
Beethoven made his final public appearance as a soloist playing in the Archduke Trio.
Began using an ear trumpet designed by Maelzel.
Produced music with a patriotic tone to entertain the many heads of state and diplomats attending the Congress of Vienna.
Received compensation from Prince Lobkowitz by resorting to the law.
Composed 'Calm Sea and Prosperous Voyage', Op. 112, for choir and orchestra based on poems by Goethe.
After his brother Kaspar died, he became embroiled in a long legal battle with Kaspar's wife Johanna over the custody of his nephew Karl.
Beethoven's output dropped to a level unequaled in his mature life.
Succeeded in taking his nephew Karl away from his mother's custody.
Completed 'To the Distant Beloved', Op. 98.
Suffered from a prolonged illness ('inflammatory fever').
Sought and obtained Beethoven's endorsement for Maelzel's newly developed metronome.
Beethoven's health improved, and his eleven-year-old nephew Karl moved in with him in January.
Completed Piano Sonata No. 29 'Hammerklavier', Op. 106.
Began musical sketches that would become part of his final Symphony No. 9.
Lost sole guardianship when the case was moved to the Vienna magistracy.
It was announced that Archduke Rudolf was promoted to Cardinal-Archbishop.
Publisher Anton Diabelli invited 50 Viennese composers, including Beethoven, to compose a variation.
Completed twenty of what would become the thirty-three Diabelli Variations, Op. 120.
Publisher Moritz Schlesinger visited Beethoven for the first time.
Regained custody of his nephew Karl after a fierce legal battle.
Rudolf's installation in Olomouc (triggered the Missa solemnis, Op. 123).
Piano Sonata No. 30, Op. 109 was dispatched to the publisher.
Suffered from rheumatism and jaundice, showing poor health once again.
In a letter to Rudolf, Beethoven revealed his belief in a personal God.
Piano Sonata No. 31, Op. 110 was published.
Wished to reconcile with his married brother Johann.
'Calm Sea and Prosperous Voyage', Op. 112 was published with a dedication to the poet Goethe.
The Philharmonic Society of London proposed a commission for a symphony.
Prince Nikolai Galitzin requested three string quartets from Beethoven.
Anton Schindler began working as the composer's unpaid secretary.
Missa solemnis, Symphony No. 9, and Diabelli Variations were completed.
Presented a copy of the completed Mass to Rudolf.
Symphony No. 9 and sections of the Missa solemnis were performed for the first time at the Kärntnertortheater to great acclaim.
The second performance, for which the producer guaranteed a minimum fee, had poor attendance.
String Quartet No. 12 in E-flat major, Op. 127 was premiered by the Schuppanzigh Quartet.
Suffered a sudden illness while writing Quartet No. 15 in A minor, Op. 132.
The final contrapuntal movement of Quartet No. 13 in B-flat major, Op. 130 proved very difficult for both performers and audience at its premiere.
Despite reconciling with Schindler, his secretary was replaced by Karl Holz.
Nephew Karl, who had been seeing his mother again, attempted suicide.
Moved to his brother Johann's house in Gneixendorf with his nephew and stayed for three months.
Completed the substitute finale for Quartet No. 13 in B-flat major, Op. 130, his final work.
Caught pneumonia on the return journey to Vienna in an open carriage in cold and damp weather.
His nephew Karl left to join the army in Jihlava.
Said to Schindler and others, 'Applaud, friends, the comedy is over.'
Died at the age of 56.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a composer and pianist
keyAchievements.description: A German composer of Western classical music and pianist, he is a major musician who was active during the transition between the Classical and Romantic eras.

keyAchievements.title: Moniker of 'Saint of Music'
keyAchievements.description: Often depicted as a heroic figure, he is called by the moniker 'Saint of Music' (Akseong).

keyAchievements.title: Major representative works
keyAchievements.description: His most widely known works include Symphony No. 3, Symphony No. 5, Symphony No. 6, Symphony No. 9, Pathétique Sonata, and Moonlight Sonata.

keyAchievements.title: First published work
keyAchievements.description: In March 1783, he published his first work, the piano variations 'Nine Variations on a March by Ernst Christoph Dressler, WoO 63'.

keyAchievements.title: Early piano sonatas
keyAchievements.description: The Three Piano Sonatas, WoO 47, published in 1783, were praised for giving an early glimpse of his talent and boldness as a composer.

keyAchievements.title: Ballet music commission
keyAchievements.description: In 1791, he was commissioned to compose the ballet music 'Musik zu einem Ritterballett, WoO 1', which was Beethoven's first work for the stage.

keyAchievements.title: First Opus work
keyAchievements.description: In 1795, he achieved financial success by publishing his first work assigned an opus number (Three Piano Trios Nos. 1-3, Op. 1).

keyAchievements.title: Cello Sonatas Nos. 1-2, Op. 5
keyAchievements.description: Composed in Berlin in 1796, they are famous for carefully considering the different characters of the cello and piano and combining the virtuoso parts well.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 8 'Pathétique', Op. 13
keyAchievements.description: Published in 1799, this work was described as surpassing any of his previous works 'in strength of character, depth of emotion, level of originality, and ingenuity of motif and tone'.

keyAchievements.title: Septet, Op. 20
keyAchievements.description: Completed in 1799, it was one of his most popular works during his lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: First six String Quartets, Op. 18
keyAchievements.description: Composed between 1798 and 1800, they were published in 1801 and dedicated to Prince Lobkowitz.

keyAchievements.title: Symphonies Nos. 1 and 2
keyAchievements.description: Premiered in 1800 and 1803, they led him to be regarded as the most important of the young generation of composers after Haydn and Mozart.

keyAchievements.title: Ballet music 'The Creatures of Prometheus, Op. 43'
keyAchievements.description: Completed in the spring of 1801, it premiered at the Burgtheater in Vienna and achieved success.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 14 'Moonlight Sonata', Op. 27 No. 2
keyAchievements.description: Published in 1802 and dedicated to Giulietta Guicciardi.

keyAchievements.title: Symphony No. 3 'Eroica'
keyAchievements.description: Composed in 1803-04, it is a major work that marks the beginning of his middle or 'heroic' period.

keyAchievements.title: Opera Fidelio
keyAchievements.description: Premiered in November 1805, it was later completed through revisions.

keyAchievements.title: Mass in C major, Op. 86
keyAchievements.description: Composed in 1807 commissioned by Prince Esterhazy.

keyAchievements.title: Massive benefit concert
keyAchievements.description: In December 1808, he organized and premiered a massive benefit concert that included Symphonies Nos. 5 and 6, Piano Concerto No. 4, the Mass in C major, and the Choral Fantasy.

keyAchievements.title: Incidental music for Goethe's play Egmont, Op. 84
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned in late 1809 and appeared in 1810, it fit well into Beethoven's 'heroic' style.

keyAchievements.title: String Quartet No. 11 in F minor, Op. 95 ('Quartetto Serioso')
keyAchievements.description: Known as another work from this period.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Trio No. 7 in B-flat major, Op. 97 ('Archduke Trio')
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to his patron Rudolf.

keyAchievements.title: Wellington's Victory, Op. 91 and Symphony No. 7, Op. 92
keyAchievements.description: First performed at a charity concert on December 8, 1813, achieving great success.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 27 in E minor, Op. 90
keyAchievements.description: His first piano sonata in five years, composed in the summer of 1814.

keyAchievements.title: To the Distant Beloved, Op. 98
keyAchievements.description: Completed in 1816, it introduced the song cycle into the classical repertoire.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 29 'Hammerklavier', Op. 106
keyAchievements.description: One of the major works completed in 1818.

keyAchievements.title: Missa solemnis, Op. 123
keyAchievements.description: Triggered by the announcement of Archduke Rudolf's promotion in 1819, it was completed in 1823.

keyAchievements.title: Thirty-three Diabelli Variations, Op. 120
keyAchievements.description: Started at the request of Antonio Diabelli in 1819 and completed in 1823.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonatas No. 30, Op. 109 and No. 31, Op. 110
keyAchievements.description: Some of his final piano sonatas, published in 1820 and 1821 respectively.

keyAchievements.title: Symphony No. 9
keyAchievements.description: Starting sketches in 1818, it was completed in 1823 and premiered on May 7, 1824, receiving praise that 'the inexhaustible genius has shown us a new world'.

keyAchievements.title: String Quartet No. 12 in E-flat major, Op. 127
keyAchievements.description: Premiered by the Schuppanzigh Quartet in March 1825.

keyAchievements.title: Quartet No. 14 in C-sharp minor, Op. 131
keyAchievements.description: The final part of the series, evaluated by Beethoven as his most perfect single work.

keyAchievements.title: Quartet No. 16 in F major, Op. 135
keyAchievements.description: An additional quartet completed while staying in Gneixendorf.

faq.question: When and where was Beethoven born?
faq.answer: He was born in Bonn, Germany, and there is a record of his baptism on December 17, 1770. Since it was customary at the time to baptize within 24 hours of birth, Beethoven himself considered December 16 as his birthday.

faq.question: What are Beethoven's major works?
faq.answer: His most widely known works include Symphony No. 3, Symphony No. 5, Symphony No. 6, Symphony No. 9, Pathétique Sonata, and Moonlight Sonata.

faq.question: When did Beethoven die, and what is suspected to be the cause of his death?
faq.answer: He died on March 26, 1827, at the age of 56. Liver cirrhosis, complications from pneumonia, and lead poisoning are suspected as causes of death.

faq.question: Did Beethoven completely lose his hearing?
faq.answer: Beethoven gradually lost his hearing, but even in his later years he could distinguish low tones and sudden loud noises, and did not become completely deaf.

faq.question: What is Beethoven's moniker?
faq.answer: He is often depicted as a heroic figure and is also called by the moniker 'Saint of Music' (Akseong).

faq.question: During which musical period was Beethoven active?
faq.answer: He is a major musician who was active during the transition between the Classical and Romantic eras.

beethoven.missingDataNote: 베토벤의 정확한 출생일은 원문에 명시적으로 나와 있지 않고, 세례 기록과 당시 관습을 통해 추정됩니다. 사망 원인 또한 여러 가지가 의심된다고만 언급되어 있으며, 한 가지로 확실하게 밝혀져 있지 않습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Hyde Park, New York.
His father, James Roosevelt I, died.
Graduated from Harvard University in 3 years.
Enrolled in Columbia Law School.
Married Eleanor Roosevelt.
Passed the New York bar exam and dropped out of Columbia Law School.
Elected to the New York State Senate.
Inaugurated as New York State Senator.
Re-elected to the New York State Senate.
Appointed Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Woodrow Wilson.
Eleanor Roosevelt discovered his affair with Lucy Mercer.
Ran as James M. Cox's vice presidential running mate but was defeated.
His lower body became paralyzed due to illness and was diagnosed with polio.
Established a rehabilitation center in Warm Springs, Georgia.
Elected Governor of New York.
The Wall Street Crash occurred and the Great Depression began.
Re-elected as Governor of New York.
Won the US presidential election against Herbert Hoover.
Survived an assassination attempt by Giuseppe Zangara.
Inaugurated as the 32nd President of the United States.
Declared a four-day 'bank holiday'.
The Emergency Banking Act was passed.
Signed the Cullen-Harrison Act, ending Prohibition.
Implemented the National Industrial Recovery Act (NIRA).
Reformed financial regulations through the Glass-Steagall Act.
Established the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).
Established the Federal Communications Commission (FCC).
The US Supreme Court declared the National Industrial Recovery Act unconstitutional.
The Social Security Act was passed.
Established the Works Progress Administration (WPA).
Re-elected in a landslide victory in the US presidential election.
Failed to pass the Judicial Procedures Reform Bill.
The Housing Act was passed.
Founded the National Foundation for Infantile Paralysis.
The Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) was passed.
Passed the Reorganization Act and established the Executive Office of the President.
World War II broke out with Germany's invasion of Poland.
Won the US presidential election, becoming the only president to serve more than three terms.
Delivered the 'Four Freedoms' speech.
Obtained congressional approval for the Lend-Lease Act.
Received a declaration of war from Japan following the attack on Pearl Harbor.
Germany and Italy declared war on the United States.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Longest-serving US President
keyAchievements.description: He is the only president in US history to serve the longest and complete more than three terms.

keyAchievements.title: Implementation of the New Deal
keyAchievements.description: Implemented the New Deal policies to overcome the Great Depression, providing relief to the unemployed and farmers, and seeking economic recovery.

keyAchievements.title: Political realignment and pioneering modern liberalism
keyAchievements.description: Built the 'New Deal Coalition', reorganizing US politics into the Fifth Party System and pioneering modern liberalism.

keyAchievements.title: Financial and labor reforms
keyAchievements.description: Introduced major regulatory reforms in finance, communications, and labor, such as establishing the SEC and the FDIC, and enacting the National Labor Relations Act.

keyAchievements.title: Repeal of Prohibition
keyAchievements.description: Signed the Cullen-Harrison Act to end the Prohibition era.

keyAchievements.title: Leadership during World War II
keyAchievements.description: Worked closely with other national leaders to lead the Allied Powers against the Axis Powers and oversaw the economic mobilization of the United States.

keyAchievements.title: Initiation of nuclear weapon development
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the development of the world's first nuclear weapons.

keyAchievements.title: Laying the foundation for the United Nations
keyAchievements.description: Cooperated with other Allied leaders to lay the foundation for the United Nations and other postwar institutions.

keyAchievements.title: Promotion of the Good Neighbor Policy
keyAchievements.description: Promoted the Good Neighbor Policy, renouncing the right to unilaterally intervene in the affairs of Latin American countries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Social Security System
keyAchievements.description: Legally established the Social Security System through the Social Security Act, promising economic security to the elderly, the poor, and the disabled.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of labor standards
keyAchievements.description: Passed the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), outlawing child labor and establishing a federal minimum wage.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Executive Office of the President
keyAchievements.description: Established the Executive Office of the President, the backbone of the federal executive branch, through the Reorganization Act of 1939.

keyAchievements.title: Environmental conservation efforts
keyAchievements.description: Encouraged the expansion and funding of national parks and national forests and made environmental conservation efforts through the Civilian Conservation Corps.

faq.question: Which president was Franklin D. Roosevelt?
faq.answer: He served as the 32nd President of the United States.

faq.question: What are the major achievements of Franklin D. Roosevelt?
faq.answer: He implemented the New Deal to overcome the Great Depression and led the Allies in World War II.

faq.question: How many times was Franklin D. Roosevelt elected president?
faq.answer: He was elected president a total of 4 times, the only person in US history to serve more than 3 terms.

faq.question: What disease did Franklin D. Roosevelt suffer from?
faq.answer: He was diagnosed with polio in 1921, which left his lower body paralyzed.

faq.question: Who was Franklin D. Roosevelt's wife?
faq.answer: His wife was Eleanor Roosevelt.

faq.question: When did Franklin D. Roosevelt die?
faq.answer: He died on April 12, 1945.

timeline.event: Born as Marcus Aurelius Antoninus in Rome.
Adopted the dress and lifestyle of a philosopher on the orders of Diognetus.
Adopted by Hadrian's successor, Antoninus Pius.
Antoninus Pius ascended to the throne as emperor following Hadrian's death.
Served as consul under Antoninus Pius.
Became consul with Antoninus Pius as his co-consul.
Became consul for the second time.
Married Faustina, the daughter of Antoninus Pius.
Birth of his first daughter, Domitia Faustina.
Antoninus Pius granted Marcus tribunician power and imperium.
Faustina gave birth to twin brothers Titus Aurelius Antoninus and Tiberius Aelius Aurelius (who did not live long).
Birth of daughter Annia Aurelia Galeria Lucilla.
Estimated death of daughter Domitia Faustina.
Birth of son Tiberius Aelius Antoninus (who did not live long).
Death of Marcus's mother, Domitia Lucilla.
Faced another death of a child (name unknown).
Faustina gave birth to daughters Fadilla and Cornificia.
Following the death of Antoninus Pius, Marcus and Lucius Verus ascended as co-emperors.
Lowered the silver purity of the Roman denarius from 83.5% to 79%.
Vologases IV of Parthia entered the Kingdom of Armenia, expelled its king, and installed Pacorus.
Outbreak of the Parthian War.
Lucius left for the battlefield to personally handle the Parthian War.
Artaxata, the capital of Armenia, was captured.
Lucius gained the title 'Armeniacus'.
A new Armenian capital, Kaine Polis, replaced Artaxata.
The Roman army moved into Mesopotamia, captured Edessa, and reinstalled King Mannus. Nisibis fell.
Cassius's army reached Seleucia and Ctesiphon, captured Ctesiphon, burned the royal palace, and sacked Seleucia.
The Antonine Plague broke out in Mesopotamia and spread to Rome.
Cassius's army crossed the Tigris and headed into Media, returning to the battlefield. Lucius claimed the title 'Medicus'.
Marcus proclaimed his twin sons Annius and Commodus as heirs.
War broke out with Germanic tribes.
Revalued the denarius by increasing its silver purity from 79% to 82%.
Co-emperor Lucius likely died of the plague.
The Roman army routed the Germanic tribes in the province of Dacia, but commander Fronto was killed in action.
Moors in the North African provinces rebelled and invaded as far as Hispania.
Began co-ruling with his son Commodus.
Died at the age of 58 in a military camp near Vindobona or Sirmium.

keyAchievements.title: Emperor of the Roman Empire
keyAchievements.description: He was the 16th emperor of the Roman Empire (161-180), the last of the Five Good Emperors, and the last emperor of the Pax Romana.

keyAchievements.title: Stoic Philosopher
keyAchievements.description: As a Stoic philosopher, he wrote 'Meditations', leaving important material for understanding ancient Stoic philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Military victories
keyAchievements.description: Successfully fought wars in the East against the Parthian Empire and the Kingdom of Armenia, and subdued Germanic tribes in the Marcomannic Wars.

keyAchievements.title: Monetary reform
keyAchievements.description: Took measures to correct the silver purity of the Roman coin, the denarius.

keyAchievements.title: Legal and administrative activities
keyAchievements.description: Showed significant interest in three areas: the manumission of slaves, the protection of orphans and minors, and the choice of city councilors.

keyAchievements.title: Erection of monuments
keyAchievements.description: The Column of Marcus Aurelius and his equestrian statue were erected in Rome to commemorate his military victories.

faq.question: Who was Marcus Aurelius?
faq.answer: He was the 16th emperor of the Roman Empire (161-180) and a Stoic philosopher, being the last of the Five Good Emperors and the last emperor of the Pax Romana.

faq.question: What were his major military achievements?
faq.answer: He successfully fought wars in the East against the Parthian Empire and the Kingdom of Armenia, and subdued the Marcomanni, Quadi, and the Sarmatian Iazyges in the Marcomannic Wars.

faq.question: What is the important work he wrote?
faq.answer: The 'Meditations' he wrote is an important source for understanding ancient Stoic philosophy today and provides a window into his inner life.

faq.question: What difficulties did the Roman Empire face during his reign?
faq.answer: During his reign, the Roman Empire faced massive military conflicts, and the Antonine Plague that broke out in 165 or 166 devastated the Roman Empire's population, causing 5 million deaths out of a population of 10 million.

faq.question: How did Marcus Aurelius choose his successor?
faq.answer: He chose not to adopt an heir, and his son Commodus succeeded him as emperor, which was a subject of debate among both contemporary and modern historians.

timeline.event: Born in Corduba (modern-day Córdoba, Spain).
Returned to Rome after a period in Egypt and began his career in politics and law.
Exiled to the island of Corsica by Emperor Claudius on charges of adultery.
Recalled from exile to serve as the tutor to the future Emperor Nero.
Forced to commit suicide by Nero after being implicated in the Pisonian conspiracy.
Born to his father Shu Lianghe and mother Yan Zhengzai in Zou, Changping, Qufu in the State of Lu.
His father, Shu Lianghe, died.
Married.
His mother, Yan Zhengzai, died.
Married the daughter of the Bingguan family of the State of Song.
Had a son, Li (鯉).
Opened an academy in the State of Lu and began teaching students.
Started his career as an official, passing through positions such as Weili (accountant) and Sizhi (manager of pastures).
Fled to the State of Qi following Duke Zhao of Lu due to the 'Rebellion of the Three Huan' in the State of Lu.
Returned from the State of Qi.
Became Zhongduzai (magistrate of Zhongdu).
Rose to the position of Da Sikou (Minister of Crime).
Earned the enmity of Ji Sunsi, left the State of Lu, and wandered through various states for 14 years.
Suffered grief as he successively lost his son Boyu and his disciples Yan Hui and Zilu.
Returned to his hometown of Qufu and devoted himself to cultivating younger scholars.
Passed away with his disciples watching over him.
Emperor Xuanzong of Tang posthumously honored Confucius as 'King Wenxuan'.
Emperor Zhenzong of Song posthumously titled him 'Zhisheng Wenxuan Wang' (The Most Sage King Wenxuan).
Came to be called 'Dacheng Zhisheng Wenxuan Wang' (The Great, Supreme Sage and King Wenxuan).
Awarded the title 'Zhisheng Xianshi' (The Most Sage and Ancient Teacher).
Awarded the title 'Dacheng Zhisheng Wenxuan Xianshi' (The Great, Supreme Sage and Ancient Teacher Wenxuan).

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of China's first school
keyAchievements.description: Established an academy for the first time in Chinese history and taught students.

keyAchievements.title: Abolition of the evil practice of human sacrifice
keyAchievements.description: Advised the king and abolished the evil practice of human sacrifice (sati) which had continued until then.

keyAchievements.title: Recovery of the lost territory of the State of Lu
keyAchievements.description: Recovered the old territory that the State of Lu had lost through brilliant strategies in negotiations with the State of Qi.

keyAchievements.title: Education on the Six Classics
keyAchievements.description: Educated his disciples using the Six Classics as textbooks: the 'Book of Changes', 'Book of Odes', 'Book of Documents', 'Book of Rites', 'Classic of Music', and 'Spring and Autumn Annals'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of the philosophy of Ren (Benevolence)
keyAchievements.description: Advocated humanism, placing 'Ren' (Benevolence) as the ultimate orientation of human behavior.

keyAchievements.title: Cultivating younger scholars
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he returned to his hometown and devoted himself to cultivating younger scholars, producing many outstanding disciples.

faq.question: Who was Confucius?
faq.answer: He was a politician, philosopher, and educator in the Spring and Autumn period of ancient China, and is known as the founder of Confucianism.

faq.question: What is the core philosophy of Confucius?
faq.answer: Contained in the 'Analects', a collection of his words and deeds, he advocated humanism, placing the ultimate orientation of human behavior on 'Ren' (Benevolence).

faq.question: What educational activities did Confucius engage in?
faq.answer: He established an academy for the first time in Chinese history and strove to cultivate younger scholars by teaching classics such as the Book of Odes, the Book of Documents, and the Book of Changes.

faq.question: What was Confucius's political ideal?
faq.answer: He advocated rule by virtue, governing the people and the country with virtue rather than law, and humanism, which places the highest value on people.

faq.question: When did Confucius die?
faq.answer: He passed away in 479 BC at the age of 72, with his disciples watching over him.

timeline.event: Born in Athens, ancient Greece.
Participated directly in battles as a hoplite in three wars.
Strove to educate and reform the youth as an educator.
Participated in politics for one year as a member of the Council of 500.
Indicted on charges of 'impiety' and 'corrupting the youth', and executed by drinking hemlock at the age of 71.

keyAchievements.title: The first figure in Western philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Consistently devoted his life to discussing problems of philosophy, and is regarded as the first figure in Western philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Maieutic method (Socratic method)
keyAchievements.description: Utilizing Zeno's dialectic, he developed the maieutic method, which awakens one to the contradictions of false judgments and guides them to correct ones.

keyAchievements.title: Emphasis on the connection between virtue and ignorance
keyAchievements.description: Emphasized the ethical aspect, viewing vice as stemming from ignorance and virtue as arising from the foundation of rational thinking.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of soul-centric thinking
keyAchievements.description: As a body-soul dualist, he argued that the soul is immortal and all knowledge is innate within the soul.

keyAchievements.title: Called the 'Greatest Teacher of Mankind'
keyAchievements.description: Influenced countless people with his outstanding intellectual and moral character, earning him the title of the 'Greatest Teacher of Mankind'.

faq.question: Did Socrates leave behind any writings?
faq.answer: Socrates did not leave behind any writings, and his thoughts have been passed down through the records of his disciples and contemporaries.

faq.question: Did Socrates say 'A bad law is still a law'?
faq.answer: There is no evidence that Socrates directly said 'A bad law is still a law'; this aphorism first appeared in the Roman era by Domitius Ulpianus.

faq.question: How did Socrates meet his death?
faq.answer: He was indicted on charges of 'impiety' and 'corrupting the youth', sentenced to death, and met his end by drinking a cup of poison (hemlock).

timeline.event: Believed to have been born in the State of Chu during the Zhou dynasty.
Served as the Keeper of the Archives at the imperial court of the Zhou dynasty.
According to legend, met with a young Confucius to discuss philosophy and rituals.
Departed from the declining Zhou state on a water buffalo, heading west.
Wrote the 'Tao Te Ching' at the western frontier pass before disappearing into history.
Born in Stagira in the region of Thrace on the Chalcidice peninsula in northern Greece.
Entered Plato's Academy at the age of 17.
Went to Atarneus at the invitation of the tyrant Hermias shortly before Plato's death.
Went to Mieza as a tutor to Prince Alexander at the request of King Philip II of Macedonia.
Retreated to Stagira and continued his research life when Alexander became regent.
Returned to Athens again, founded the Lyceum school, and spent 12 years lecturing and researching.
Fled to Chalcis, his mother's hometown, to escape anti-Macedonian sentiment following news of Alexander the Great's death.
Ended his 63-year life due to a stomach ailment.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Western philosophical system
keyAchievements.description: Created the first comprehensive system of Western philosophy, encompassing morality and aesthetics, logic and science, politics and metaphysics.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Lyceum school library and zoo
keyAchievements.description: Built a library at the Lyceum school with the help of Alexander the Great, and also created the world's first zoo.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of logic
keyAchievements.description: Gained a pioneering position in formal logic with the compilation of 'Prior Analytics', and invented the syllogism.

keyAchievements.title: Zoological research
keyAchievements.description: Observed, recorded, and classified over 500 species of animals, leaving precise records such as the discovery that whales and sharks give birth to live young.

keyAchievements.title: Proposal of the theory of the four causes
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the theory of the four causes, which explains the causes of moving and changing sensory objects from four aspects: material cause, formal cause, efficient cause, and final cause.

keyAchievements.title: Description of the camera obscura
keyAchievements.description: His book 'Problems' contains the oldest surviving description of the camera obscura, which corresponds to early cameras.

faq.question: Whose disciple and whose teacher was Aristotle?
faq.answer: Aristotle was a disciple of Plato and the teacher of Alexander the Great.

faq.question: What fields did Aristotle write books about?
