faq.answer: He wrote books on a wide range of subjects including physics, metaphysics, poetry, biology, zoology, logic, rhetoric, politics, ethics, and moral science.

faq.question: What is the most famous example in Aristotle's logic?
faq.answer: The most famous example in Aristotle's logic is the 'syllogism', an argument based on deductive reasoning.

faq.question: What is Aristotle's theory of the four elements?
faq.answer: Aristotle proposed four elements: hot and dry fire, cold and dry earth, hot and wet air, and cold and wet water.

faq.question: What did Aristotle see as the purpose of education?
faq.answer: Aristotle placed the purpose of education on leading a rational life and achieving a happy life.

faq.question: What was Aristotle's view on women?
faq.answer: Aristotle's view on women had a profound influence on Western thinkers until the late Middle Ages and is treated as an important subject in women's history.

timeline.event: Birth.
Went to Sicily at the earnest request of Dionysius II to try and implement ideal politics.
Death.
The final year that the Academy founded by Plato continued.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of objective idealism
keyAchievements.description: A Greek philosopher and thinker who founded objective idealism.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of and teaching at the Academy
keyAchievements.description: Founded and taught at the 'Academy', a higher education institution that was the prototype for a university.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of dialogues
keyAchievements.description: Wrote dialogues on many philosophical topics including politics, ethics, metaphysics, and epistemology.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of the Theory of Ideas (Forms)
keyAchievements.description: Became independent from the thoughts of his teacher Socrates and advocated his own doctrine called the Theory of Ideas.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Philosopher King theory
keyAchievements.description: Developed the famous Philosopher King theory that 'philosophers must become kings, or kings must philosophize'.

keyAchievements.title: Founding the basis of early ideal communism
keyAchievements.description: Founded the basis of early ideal communism through a teleological view of humanity as beings endowed with the 'duty' to become 'rational beings' to build a better society.

faq.question: Who was Plato?
faq.answer: Plato was a disciple of Socrates, the teacher of Aristotle, and an influential Greek philosopher and thinker who founded objective idealism.

faq.question: What are Plato's main achievements?
faq.answer: He founded objective idealism, established the 'Academy' which was the prototype for a university, and advocated the Theory of Ideas and the Philosopher King theory.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Plato's writings?
faq.answer: Most of his extant works take the form of dialogues, featuring his teacher Socrates as the main interlocutor.

faq.question: What was Plato's view on education?
faq.answer: Plato divided education into 5 stages and aimed to cultivate rulers by teaching literature, physical education, mathematics, and dialectics at each stage.

faq.question: What was Plato's view on child development?
faq.answer: Plato believed that human development had three aspects: desire, spirit, and divine; he emphasized the importance of early childhood experiences and advocated for gender separation at age 6.

timeline.event: Born in the mountain village of Vinci in the Tuscany region.
According to tax records, he was living in the same house as his father.
His stepmother Albiera died.
At the age of 14, moved to Florence with his family and entered the workshop of Andrea del Verrocchio.
Registered with the painters' guild of Florence and became known as a painter.
Assisted in his master Verrocchio's work 'The Baptism of Christ' and painted the angel on the left side.
Invited as a painter by the noble Sforza family of Milan.
Produced 'The Virgin of the Rocks' (Louvre Museum).
Immersed himself in anatomical studies of the human body and horses, and devoted himself to treatises on human anatomy and the flight of birds, as well as research on optics.
Produced 'The Last Supper'.
Returned to Florence in 1500 due to the invasion of Milan by King Louis XII of France.
Invited by Cesare Borgia for civil engineering projects, canal development, and urban planning.
Returned to Florence and began painting the 'Mona Lisa'.
His father Piero died at the age of 77.
Became the court painter for King Louis XII of France in Milan.
Returned to Florence, then moved to Rome under the patronage of Giuliano de' Medici.
Met Francis I in Bologna and was invited to France.
His patron Giuliano de' Medici died.
Crossed the Alps and headed to Amboise, France, with his disciple Francesco Melzi.
Drafted his will as if sensing his death.
Died at the age of 67 at his residence in Clos Lucé, France.
With the death of Francesco Melzi, Leonardo da Vinci's massive collection of sketches and drawings was revealed to the world.
His remains were destroyed and no longer exist, as church buildings were dismantled during the French Revolution.

keyAchievements.title: A polymath in multiple fields
keyAchievements.description: A representative Italian polymath who was active in various fields including as a painter, sculptor, inventor, architect, anatomist, geographer, and musician.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as the most creative person
keyAchievements.description: Ranked first as the most creative person among the 10 geniuses who changed human history selected by the journal Nature in 2007.

keyAchievements.title: Mona Lisa
keyAchievements.description: The most famous and widely known painting in the world today, representing the Louvre Museum.

keyAchievements.title: The Last Supper
keyAchievements.description: One of his masterpieces, counted as one of the great paintings of the century.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Sfumato technique
keyAchievements.description: Developed sfumato, an atmospheric perspective technique that paints even the air surrounding an object to create a completely three-dimensional world.

keyAchievements.title: Scientific discoveries and research
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the theory of wave motion, fluid pressure, water pumps, and hydraulic pressure, and researched various scientific fields such as flight principles, aerodynamics, anatomy, and botany.

keyAchievements.title: Book 'Treatise on Painting'
keyAchievements.description: Left behind many treatises, including 'Treatise on Painting', an important document of Renaissance art theory regarding painting.

faq.question: When and where was Leonardo da Vinci born?
faq.answer: Leonardo da Vinci was born at 3 AM on Saturday, April 15, 1452, in the mountain village of Vinci in the Tuscany region.

faq.question: What are Leonardo da Vinci's representative works?
faq.answer: Among his many works, the 'Mona Lisa' is the most famous today, and 'The Last Supper' is also counted as one of his masterpieces.

faq.question: In what fields did Leonardo da Vinci work?
faq.answer: He worked in and held various professions across many fields, including as a painter, sculptor, inventor, architect, anatomist, geographer, and musician.

faq.question: Did Leonardo da Vinci receive formal education?
faq.answer: Because he was illegitimate, he did not learn to read and write in a formal school, and he did not receive normal education in various subjects such as Latin and Greek.

faq.question: Where did Leonardo da Vinci die?
faq.answer: On May 2, 1519, he died at the age of 67 at his residence in Clos Lucé, France.

faq.question: Do Leonardo da Vinci's remains still exist today?
faq.answer: During the chaotic period of the French Revolution in 1802, church buildings were dismantled and tombs were vandalized, so his remains do not exist today.

faq.question: Did Leonardo da Vinci marry?
faq.answer: Leonardo da Vinci remained single his whole life and left no children.

timeline.event: Born in Salzburg, Austria.
Proficient in playing the keyboard and violin, and began composing.
Traveled on concert tours to various parts of Europe, including Germany, Paris, and London.
Studied composition under Johann Christian Bach in London, England.
Studied music theory under Giovanni Battista Martini during a trip to Italy.
Received decisive stimulation from the music of Haydn, Wagenseil, Monn, etc. during a trip to Vienna.
Premiered the Piano Concerto (K.242) with an instrument provided by Stein.
Was dismissed from his position as a court musician in Salzburg, left for Vienna, and stayed there until his death.
Married Constanze.
Joined a Masonic 'Charity' lodge.
Passed away.
His wife Constanze remarried the Danish diplomat Georg Nikolaus von Nissen.

keyAchievements.title: Composing a multitude of works
keyAchievements.description: Despite his short life, his rapid pace of creation left behind over 800 works across almost all genres of Western classical music at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Pinnacle works by genre
keyAchievements.description: Many of his works are considered the pinnacle of the symphony, concerto, chamber music, opera, and choral repertoire.

keyAchievements.title: Composition of major operas
keyAchievements.description: Composed operas such as 'Idomeneo', 'The Abduction from the Seraglio', 'The Marriage of Figaro', 'Don Giovanni', 'Così fan tutte', and 'The Magic Flute'.

keyAchievements.title: Piano concertos
keyAchievements.description: Composed more than 12 piano concertos in Vienna, which are considered some of his greatest achievements.

keyAchievements.title: Composition of masterpieces
keyAchievements.description: Composed masterpieces such as his last three symphonies including Symphony No. 41, the serenade 'Eine kleine Nachtmusik', the Clarinet Concerto, and the unfinished 'Requiem'.

faq.question: What was the cause of Mozart's death?
faq.answer: The records cite 'severe miliary fever', but there is insufficient information to diagnose it with modern medicine, and there are various theories such as trichinosis, poisoning, and rheumatic fever.

faq.question: Did Mozart die in poverty?
faq.answer: It is a common story that Mozart died in poverty and obscurity, but arguments that he had a reasonably satisfactory income are also persuasive.

faq.question: Did Mozart meet Beethoven?
faq.answer: An anecdote says they met in Vienna in 1784, but its reliability is not widely accepted today as the sole source of this story is Otto Jahn's biography of Mozart.

faq.question: Is it true that Mozart was poisoned by Antonio Salieri?
faq.answer: The story of the rivalry and poisoning between Mozart and Salieri is a legend that has been the subject of various works, including Alexander Pushkin's play, Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov's opera, and Peter Shaffer's play.

faq.question: Did Mozart compose everything at once by inspiration?
faq.answer: It is a legend that Mozart completed the music in his head by inspiration and then wrote it down without a single revision; there is an argument that he was actually a careful and hardworking composer.

timeline.event: Born in Stratford-upon-Avon, England.
His family's fortunes declined, leading him to abandon his studies and help with the family business.
Married Anne Hathaway, who was eight years his senior, at the age of 18.
Received a marriage license from the consistory court of the Diocese of Worcester.
Birth of his daughter Susanna.
Birth of twins Hamnet and Judith.
Arrived in the capital city of London.
Worked as a playwright for about 24 years, publishing a total of 38 plays, including comedies and tragedies.
Can be presumed to have been a famous playwright in London.
Published the narrative poem 'Venus and Adonis'.
Published the narrative poem 'The Rape of Lucrece' and became an exclusive playwright for the Lord Chamberlain's Men.
Staged the romantic comedy 'A Midsummer Night's Dream' to great acclaim.
The Lord Chamberlain's Men built the Globe Theatre on the south bank of the River Thames.
Following the death of Queen Elizabeth I, the Lord Chamberlain's Men changed its name to the King's Men.
His eldest daughter Susanna married physician John Hall.
Published 'Shakespeare's Sonnets'.
Reportedly returned to his hometown of Stratford a few years before his death.
Called to testify as a witness in a court case involving the marriage of Mountjoy's daughter Mary.
Purchased the gatehouse of the former Blackfriars priory in London.
His younger daughter Judith married vintner Thomas Quiney.
Passed away, leaving behind his wife and two daughters.
His collected plays (including 36 plays) were published through the efforts of his fellow actors.

keyAchievements.title: Status as England's Greatest Playwright
keyAchievements.description: Rose to the status of 'England's greatest playwright' during his lifetime and was hailed as the greatest dramatist of his era.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Classics for Humanity
keyAchievements.description: Left behind masterpiece plays that offer profound insights into the human mind, such as 'Romeo and Juliet' and 'Hamlet', which remain classics of humanity.

keyAchievements.title: Mastery of Various Genres and Prolific Output
keyAchievements.description: Mastered all genres of theater, including histories, comedies, tragedies, and tragicomedies, writing a total of 36 plays, 154 sonnets, and two narrative poems.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to the Development of Modern English
keyAchievements.description: Brought out the potential of modern English to create the pinnacle of poetic drama, greatly influencing the rich expressive power of the English language today.

keyAchievements.title: Writing the Four Great Tragedies
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the Four Great Tragedies—'Hamlet', 'Othello', 'King Lear', and 'Macbeth'—which hold immortal fame in the history of world literature.

faq.question: Did William Shakespeare receive a university education?
faq.answer: Unlike other writers of his time, he received no higher education, yet he became a great writer through his innate linguistic ability and sense of stagecraft.

faq.question: Is there a controversy surrounding the actual authorship of Shakespeare's works?
faq.answer: Questions have been raised about whether Shakespeare, who lacked a university education, could have written all the works, but there is no evidence to support speculations that other figures, such as Francis Bacon, were the actual authors.

faq.question: What kind of works did Shakespeare write?
faq.answer: He mastered all theatrical genres including histories, comedies, tragedies, and tragicomedies, and wrote a total of 36 plays, 154 sonnets, and two narrative poems.

faq.question: What are Shakespeare's Four Great Tragedies?
faq.answer: His four great tragedies are 'Hamlet', 'Othello', 'King Lear', and 'Macbeth', which established him as a writer of immortal fame in world literature.

william-shakespeare.missingDataNote: 셰익스피어의 생애에 대한 자료는 불충분하여 추측과 빈약한 기록으로만 헤아려 볼 수 있으며, 특히 유랑기 행적과 작품 활동 시작 시기는 명확하게 밝혀지지 않았다. 또한, 유년기 부모님 이름에 대한 원문 내 불일치가 있어 해당 정보는 생략되었다.

timeline.event: Born in Ulm, in the Kingdom of Württemberg, German Empire.
Moved to Switzerland.
Renounced his German citizenship to avoid military service and enrolled in the mathematics and physics teaching diploma program at the Zurich Federal Polytechnic School.
Passed his mathematics and physics exams and received a federal teaching diploma.
Acquired Swiss citizenship.
Married Mileva Marić and secured a permanent position at the Swiss Patent Office in Bern.

Received his doctorate from the University of Zurich and published four groundbreaking papers on the photoelectric effect, Brownian motion, special relativity, and mass-energy equivalence during his 'miracle year'.
Appointed as a lecturer at the University of Bern.
Appointed associate professor of theoretical physics at the University of Zurich.
Became a full professor at the German Charles-Ferdinand University in Prague.
Moved to Berlin to join the Prussian Academy of Sciences and Humboldt University.
Successfully completed the general theory of relativity.
Became director of the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Physics and became a German citizen again.
Married Elsa Löwenthal after divorcing Mileva Marić, and became world-famous after Sir Arthur Eddington confirmed his prediction of the deflection of light.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for his services to theoretical physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect.
Traveled to Asia, including Singapore, Ceylon, and Japan, as well as to Palestine.

Unable to return to Germany due to Adolf Hitler's rise to power while visiting the United States, he officially renounced his German citizenship and became a resident scholar at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton.
Signed a letter warning President Roosevelt of a potential German nuclear weapons program and recommending that the US begin similar research.
Became a US citizen.
Died at the age of 76.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Theory of Relativity
keyAchievements.description: Revolutionized our understanding of space, time, and gravity by developing the special and general theories of relativity, the two pillars of modern physics.

keyAchievements.title: Mass-Energy Equivalence Formula (E=mc²)
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the 'world's most famous equation', showing that mass and energy are interchangeable.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Law of the Photoelectric Effect
keyAchievements.description: Made a pivotal contribution to the development of quantum theory by proposing the concept that light acts as a stream of particles (photons), for which he won the Nobel Prize in Physics.

keyAchievements.title: Explanation of Brownian Motion
keyAchievements.description: Provided definitive evidence for the existence of atoms and molecules by explaining the random motion of particles suspended in a liquid.

keyAchievements.title: Laying the Foundation for Modern Cosmology
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for modern cosmology, the study of the structure and evolution of the universe based on general relativity.

keyAchievements.title: Prediction of Gravitational Waves
keyAchievements.description: Predicted the existence of gravitational waves—ripples in the curvature of spacetime propagating as waves—which were confirmed by observation nearly 100 years later.

faq.question: For which major achievement did Albert Einstein receive the Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: He received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 'for his services to theoretical physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect'.

faq.question: What is Einstein's most famous scientific contribution?
faq.answer: He developed the theory of relativity and made significant contributions to the development of quantum mechanics, and his mass-energy equivalence formula E=mc² is known as the 'world's most famous equation'.

faq.question: Why did Einstein leave Germany and move to the United States?
faq.answer: When Adolf Hitler came to power in Germany in 1933, he settled in the United States in opposition to the Nazi government's policies, and became a US citizen in 1940.

faq.question: What was Einstein's stance on the development of nuclear weapons?
faq.answer: Just before World War II, he signed a letter warning President Roosevelt about the German nuclear weapons program, but he generally condemned the idea of nuclear weapons.

faq.question: What were Einstein's religious views?
faq.answer: He expressed sympathy for the impersonal pantheistic God of Baruch Spinoza's philosophy and called himself a 'deeply religious nonbeliever' rather than an atheist.

timeline.event: Born in Warsaw as the fifth of one boy and four girls.
Lost her mother to pulmonary tuberculosis when she was 10 years old.
Worked as a governess from around the age of 17 and resolved to study abroad in Paris, France.
Enrolled at the Sorbonne in Paris, majoring in mathematics and physics.
Married the scientist Pierre Curie.
With Pierre Curie, first isolated and discovered radium chloride from pitchblende, a uranium ore.
Processed 8 tons of waste uranium ore to obtain 0.1g of pure radium chloride.
Jointly awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics with her husband Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel.
Jointly awarded the Davy Medal with Pierre Curie.
Jointly awarded the Matteucci Medal with Pierre Curie.
Following her husband Pierre Curie's death, she began lecturing at the Sorbonne on November 5, becoming its first female professor.
Awarded the Actonian Prize.
Succeeded in precisely measuring the atomic weight of radium.
Awarded the Elliott Cresson Medal.
Obtained metallic radium by electrolyzing radium chloride.
Awarded the unshared Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the discovery of radium and polonium, and for her study of the nature and compounds of radium.
Her health noticeably deteriorated at the age of 56.
Admitted to the Sancellemoz Sanatorium in Switzerland.
Died at the age of 67 from a combination of bone marrow cancer, leukemia, and aplastic anemia caused by excessive radiation exposure.
In recognition of her achievements by the French government 61 years after her death, she became the first woman to be entombed in the Panthéon.

keyAchievements.title: Two-time Nobel Laureate and First Female Laureate
keyAchievements.description: She was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize and is the only person to win Nobel Prizes in both Physics and Chemistry.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Polonium and Radium
keyAchievements.description: Discovered and named two new elements, polonium and radium, together with Pierre Curie.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Concept of Radioactivity
keyAchievements.description: Coined the term 'radioactivity' and revealed that radiation phenomena occur at the atomic level, offering a new direction for scientific research.

keyAchievements.title: First Female Professor at the Sorbonne
keyAchievements.description: Following her husband's death, she began lecturing at the Sorbonne, becoming its first female professor.

faq.question: Which Nobel Prizes did Marie Curie win?
faq.answer: Marie Curie jointly won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1903 with her husband Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel, and solely won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1911.

faq.question: What are the main elements discovered by Marie Curie?
faq.answer: Marie Curie discovered two new elements, polonium and radium, along with her husband Pierre Curie.

faq.question: How did Marie Curie die?
faq.answer: Marie Curie died on July 4, 1934, at the age of 67, from a combination of bone marrow cancer, leukemia, and aplastic anemia caused by excessive radiation exposure.

faq.question: Is Marie Curie entombed in the Panthéon in France?
faq.answer: Yes, Marie Curie became the first woman to be entombed in the Panthéon, the French national mausoleum, in April 1995, 61 years after her death.

timeline.event: Birth of Nikola Tesla.
Enrolled at the Technische Universität Graz in Austria.
Obtained a job at the Paris branch of the Edison Telephone Company through the arrangement of the Puskás brothers.
Left for the United States with a letter of recommendation from Charles Batchelor and met Thomas Edison.
Met A. K. Brown, a manager at the Western Union Telegraph Company, and founded a company bearing Tesla's name.
The Tesla Electric Company was newly founded.
Westinghouse built a power plant using Tesla's alternating current (AC) system to supply electricity to a mining area.
Lectured at the electrical engineering congress and presented a mechanical oscillator and electrical oscillator.
Demonstrated wireless communication for the first time during speeches at the Franklin Institute in Philadelphia and the National Electric Light Association in St. Louis.
The Niagara Falls Commission signed a contract with Westinghouse, bringing the War of the Currents to an end.
First realized wireless communication via radio.
A major fire occurred at his laboratory at 33-35 South Fifth Avenue.
Reported the dangers of X-rays to the New York Academy.
Applied for a wireless communication patent.
Left New York and demonstrated a remote-controlled boat in Chicago.
Accepted Leonard Curtis's offer and arrived in Colorado Springs.
Measured lightning during storms, discovering its regular periodicity and realizing it was a standing wave.
Completed his work in Colorado and prepared to leave.
His wireless communication patent was approved.
The New York Times published an article stating that Edison and Tesla had been chosen as joint recipients of the Nobel Prize in Physics.
Reuters reported that the Nobel Committee had selected Professors William Henry Bragg and William Lawrence Bragg as the laureates.
Originated the basic concept of radar for military purposes.
Death of Nikola Tesla.
The Conférence Générale des Poids et Mesures in Paris established the 'tesla' as the SI unit of magnetic flux density.
A statue commemorating the 150th anniversary of Nikola Tesla's birth was officially unveiled in Queen Victoria Park in Niagara Falls, Ontario, Canada.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Alternating Current (AC) System
keyAchievements.description: Invented transformers and the alternating current system, making significant contributions to commercial electricity.

keyAchievements.title: Advancement of Electromagnetism
keyAchievements.description: Well known as the figure who enabled revolutionary advancements in electromagnetism in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

keyAchievements.title: Forming the Foundation of the Modern AC System
keyAchievements.description: His patents and theoretical work formed the foundation of the modern AC system, including the polyphase power distribution system and the AC motor.

keyAchievements.title: Realization of Wireless Communication
keyAchievements.description: Called the father of wireless communication for first realizing wireless communication via radio in 1894.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Rotating Magnetic Field Principle
keyAchievements.description: Developed a completely new system utilizing the principle of the rotating magnetic field based on an idea that came to him while walking in a park with a friend.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Polyphase System
keyAchievements.description: Developed the polyphase system, which includes the polyphase induction motor, split-phase induction motor, and polyphase synchronous motor, enabling the generation, transmission, distribution, and mechanical utilization of electricity.

keyAchievements.title: X-ray Research
keyAchievements.description: Took X-ray photographs prior to Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen's discovery, realized the harmful effects of X-rays on the human body, and reported the dangers to the New York Academy in 1897.

keyAchievements.title: Ideas on Wireless Energy Transmission and Communication
keyAchievements.description: Conceived ideas for controlling the world's weather using magnetic fields, and methods for transmitting energy and communicating wirelessly.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Fluidic Diode
keyAchievements.description: While developing a large turbine at the Waterside Power Station, he developed a special valved conduit that allowed the use of combustible fuel, also known as the 'fluidic diode'.

keyAchievements.title: Origination of the Basic Concept of Radar
keyAchievements.description: Originated the basic concept of radar for military purposes to track German submarines during World War I around 1917.

keyAchievements.title: Proposal of the Theory of Electrogravitics
keyAchievements.description: Proposed a new theory of gravity called electrogravitics, refuting Einstein's theory by arguing that the curving of space is impossible and the universe is filled with ether.

faq.question: Who was Nikola Tesla?
faq.answer: Nikola Tesla was a Serbian-American inventor, physicist, and electrical engineer who spearheaded the Second Industrial Revolution by inventing the transformer and AC system and laying the foundation for the modern AC system.

faq.question: What are Tesla's major achievements?
faq.answer: He first realized the AC system, polyphase induction motor, and wireless communication, and made illustrious contributions to the field of electromagnetism, including X-ray research, ideas on wireless energy transmission, and originating the basic concept of radar.

faq.question: What was Tesla's relationship with Edison?
faq.answer: Tesla worked at Edison's company and improved the performance of generators, but left the company after not receiving a promised bonus. He subsequently engaged in the 'War of the Currents' with his AC system against Edison's DC system.

faq.question: Did Tesla win a Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: There was an article in 1915 mentioning him as a joint recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics, but he was never officially notified, and the final laureates were other individuals.

faq.question: Was Tesla's method of invention unique?
faq.answer: He had his own world of imagination in his mind, and he could mentally resolve the entire process required for an invention without any special blueprints or physical experiments.

faq.question: What are some examples of Tesla's name being used?
faq.answer: His name is used for the SI unit of magnetic flux density 'tesla (T)', a crater on the far side of the Moon, the asteroid '2244 Tesla', a power plant and airport in Serbia, and the electric car company 'Tesla Motors'.

faq.question: What kind of personality did Tesla have?
faq.answer: He had a peculiar personality from a young age, enjoying writing poetry and inventing, having a habit of measuring the volume of food during meals, and showing a fascination with sparkling crystals. He also suffered from pathological phobias and compulsions.

timeline.event: Born in Zundert, Netherlands.
His younger brother, Theodorus 'Theo', was born.
Was sent to the village school in Zundert.
Enrolled in a boarding school in Zevenbergen.
Was sent to a middle school in Tilburg.
Dropped out of school and returned home.
Found employment at the Goupil & Cie gallery in The Hague.
Transferred to the Goupil gallery's London branch on Southampton Street.
Was arranged a transfer to Paris.
Was dismissed from the Goupil gallery; worked as an unpaid supply teacher at a boarding school in Ramsgate, England; lived as an assistant to a Methodist minister.
Prepared for the theology entrance exam with his uncle, Johannes Stricker, in Amsterdam.
Failed the theology entrance exam and left his uncle's house; took a 3-month course at a Protestant missionary school in Laeken near Brussels.
Took up a post as a lay missionary in the mining village of Petit Wasmes in the Borinage, Belgium; was dismissed by the missionary organization.
Returned to Cuesmes and stayed with a miner; took up art in earnest at Theo's recommendation.
Left for Brussels and enrolled at the Académie Royale des Beaux-Arts.
Returned to Etten for a long stay with his family; proposed to his uncle's daughter, Cornelia 'Kee' Vos-Stricker, but was rejected.

Learned oil painting from Anton Mauve and set up a studio; began a relationship with Clasina Maria 'Sien' Hoornik, an alcoholic prostitute; spent three weeks in the hospital with an attack of gonorrhea.
Left Sien and her children; moved to Drenthe province in the northern Netherlands; returned to his parents' home in Nuenen.
Fell in love with his neighbor Margot Begemann, but she attempted suicide by poison due to their families' opposition to marriage.
His father died on March 26; completed 'The Potato Eaters'; 'The Potato Eaters' was first publicly exhibited in The Hague.
Moved to Antwerp.

Took the entrance exam for the higher level of the Royal Academy of Fine Arts in Antwerp and was admitted; was hospitalized due to overwork, poor diet, and excessive smoking; moved to Paris, lived in his brother Theo's apartment, and studied art at Fernand Cormon's studio.
Moved to Asnières-sur-Seine, a northwestern suburb of Paris; befriended Paul Gauguin; held an exhibition in Montmartre with Bernard, Anquetin, and Toulouse-Lautrec.

Left Paris for rest and moved to Arles in southern France; rented four rooms at 2 Place Lamartine in Arles; Paul Gauguin arrived in Arles; following an argument with Gauguin, he cut off his left ear and was hospitalized.

Returned to the Yellow House in Arles; a petition by townspeople led the police to close his house, sending him back to the hospital; voluntarily admitted himself to the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum in Saint-Rémy-de-Provence.

Albert Aurier praised his work in the 'Mercure de France'; received an invitation from Les XX in Brussels to participate in their annual exhibition; participated in the 6th Salon des Indépendants on the Champs-Élysées in Paris; left the clinic in Saint-Rémy and moved to Auvers-sur-Oise, a suburb of Paris.
Attempted suicide by shooting himself in the chest with a revolver on July 27.
Died on July 29.
Was buried in the municipal cemetery of Auvers-sur-Oise on July 30.
His brother Theo died on January 25.
Theo's widow, Johanna van Gogh-Bonger, had Theo's body exhumed and reburied next to Vincent's grave.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Post-Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: One of the most famous and influential Post-Impressionist painters in the history of Western art, contributing to the revival of Expressionism in modern art.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Artist
keyAchievements.description: Created about 2,100 artworks, including around 860 oil paintings, in just over a decade.

keyAchievements.title: Bold Colors and Brushwork
keyAchievements.description: Established a unique style characterized by bold colors and dramatic brushwork in landscapes, still lifes, portraits, and self-portraits.

keyAchievements.title: Symbolic Use of Color
keyAchievements.description: Believed that color carried psychological and moral weight beyond simple depiction, especially using yellow as a symbol of sunlight, life, and the divine.

keyAchievements.title: Self-Portrait Series
keyAchievements.description: Produced over 43 self-portraits between 1885 and 1889, expressing a high level of introspection and various mental and physical states.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Global Fame
keyAchievements.description: Though he sold only one painting during his lifetime, after his death, his art and life story captured the public imagination as a symbol of the misunderstood genius, and his works are among the most expensive paintings ever sold.

faq.question: How many paintings did Vincent van Gogh sell during his lifetime?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, Van Gogh sold only one painting: 'The Red Vineyard at Arles'.

faq.question: How many artworks did Vincent van Gogh create in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He created about 2,100 artworks, including around 860 oil paintings, in just over a decade.

faq.question: Why did Vincent van Gogh cut off his ear?
faq.answer: Following an argument with Paul Gauguin, he seemingly hallucinated and severed all or part of his left ear with a razor, indicating that he suffered an acute mental breakdown.

faq.question: How did Vincent van Gogh die?
faq.answer: On July 27, 1890, at the age of 37, he attempted suicide by shooting himself in the chest with a revolver in a wheat field, and died of an infection from the wound two days later on July 29.

faq.question: What was the relationship like between Vincent van Gogh and his brother Theo?
faq.answer: His younger brother Theo supported Vincent financially and emotionally, and the two maintained a deep friendship, exchanging hundreds of letters throughout their lives.

faq.question: Which of Van Gogh's paintings sold for the highest price?
faq.answer: On May 15, 1990, his 'Portrait of Dr. Gachet' (first version) sold for $82.5 million at Christie's, setting a new record high price.

timeline.event: His grandfather, Abraham Lincoln, was killed in an Indian raid.
Birth of Abraham Lincoln.
The Lincoln family lost their land due to title disputes.
His mother, Nancy Lincoln, died.
His father, Thomas Lincoln, remarried Sarah Johnston.
His older sister, Sarah Lincoln, died in childbirth.
The Lincoln family moved to public land in Macon County, Illinois.
Lincoln arrived in the village of New Salem, Illinois, and began working for Denton Offutt.
Ran for the Illinois General Assembly but was defeated.
Obtained his qualifications as a lawyer through self-study.
Elected to the Illinois State Legislature.
Met Ann Rutledge in New Salem.
Ann Rutledge died.
Ended his relationship with Mary Owens.
Became engaged to Mary Todd.
Married Mary Todd.
Birth of his eldest son, Robert Todd Lincoln.
The Lincolns bought a house in Springfield.
Elected to the US House of Representatives.
Birth of his second son, Edward Baker Lincoln.
Death of his second son, Edward Baker Lincoln.
Birth of his third son, William Wallace Lincoln.
Birth of his fourth son, Tad Lincoln.
Ran for the US Senate but was defeated.
Nominated as the presidential candidate at the Republican National Convention.
Elected president in the 16th US presidential election.
South Carolina declared its secession from the Union.
Representatives from seven Southern states met in Montgomery and formed the Confederate States of America.
Inaugurated as President.
The American Civil War broke out, starting with the Battle of Fort Sumter.
Resolved the crisis of war with Britain over the Trent Affair.
Death of his third son, William Wallace Lincoln.
Congress passed a bill emancipating the slaves of those engaged in rebellion against the Union.
The Union Army won the Battle of Antietam.
Issued the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation.
Issued the final Emancipation Proclamation.
Successfully re-elected in the presidential election after founding the National Union Party.
The American Civil War essentially ended with the fall of Richmond, the Confederate capital.
Assassinated by John Wilkes Booth while watching a play at Ford's Theatre.
Died.
John Wilkes Booth was killed.
His fourth son, Tad Lincoln, died young in Chicago.
Robert Lincoln committed his mother, Mary Lincoln, to an asylum.

keyAchievements.title: Served as the 16th President of the United States
keyAchievements.description: Led the nation through the immense internal crisis of the American Civil War, preserving the Union and abolishing slavery.

keyAchievements.title: Spearheaded the Abolition of Slavery
keyAchievements.description: Led the abolition of slavery by issuing the Emancipation Proclamation in 1863 and advocating for the passage of the 13th Amendment to the US Constitution.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in the American Civil War
keyAchievements.description: Focused on winning the civil war both militarily and politically, and deeply involved himself in selecting top commanders like General Ulysses S. Grant, leading to victory in the Civil War.

keyAchievements.title: Resolution of the Trent Affair
keyAchievements.description: Resolved the Trent Affair in late 1861, averting a crisis of war with Britain.

keyAchievements.title: The Gettysburg Address
keyAchievements.description: Delivered a speech that became a symbol of America's traditional liberalism—liberty, equality, and democracy—and is considered the most quoted speech in US history.

keyAchievements.title: Self-Taught Qualification as a Lawyer
keyAchievements.description: Completed his studies by teaching himself rather than learning at school, and passed the bar exam to become a lawyer.

faq.question: How was Abraham Lincoln educated?
faq.answer: He completed his studies by teaching himself rather than learning at school, and his formal education consisted of only about 18 months of classes taught by several itinerant teachers.

faq.question: What was Lincoln's stance on slavery?
faq.answer: Lincoln was a vocal opponent of the expansion of slavery and criticized it as a 'monstrous injustice'.

faq.question: Why was Lincoln assassinated?
faq.answer: John Wilkes Booth, a famous actor and Confederate sympathizer, was angered by Lincoln's speech supporting voting rights for African Americans and assassinated him at Ford's Theatre on April 14, 1865.

faq.question: Was Lincoln a wrestler?
faq.answer: He made a living as a professional wrestler when the sport was in its infancy, and was a strong competitor who lost only one of many matches over a 12-year period.

faq.question: What was Lincoln's personality like?
faq.answer: He had a cheerful personality and was good at telling jokes, but he suffered from clinical depression for a long time due to memories of losing his mother, sister, and sweetheart at a young age.

timeline.event: Born to Augustine Washington and Mary Ball Washington in Westmoreland County, Colony of Virginia.
Assisted in surveying Lord Fairfax's lands west of the Blue Ridge.
Acted as the first surveyor of the newly created Culpeper County.
Convalesced in Barbados with his half-brother Lawrence for Lawrence's tuberculosis treatment.
After Lawrence's death, inherited some of his estates and assumed some of his duties as adjutant of the colony. Appointed as a major in the Virginia militia as an adjutant general.
Joined the Virginia militia at the age of 21. In December, at the request of Governor Dinwiddie, went to the Ohio frontier to deliver a British ultimatum to the French.
Married Martha Dandridge Custis, becoming one of the wealthiest men in Virginia.
Appointed by the Continental Congress as Commander-in-Chief of the Continental Army formed to fight Britain.
After the end of the American Revolutionary War, returned to his plantation to lead a private life.
Presided over the Philadelphia Constitutional Convention held to draft the US Constitution to replace the Articles of Confederation.
Elected unanimously in the US presidential election and inaugurated as the first President of the United States.

Signed the Jay Treaty with Britain to avoid war. When the Whiskey Rebellion broke out, called up state militias and personally led the army to suppress the rebellion. Signed the Naval Act of 1794 into law on March 27.
A peace treaty was signed between the United States and Algiers.
Passed a bill to fund the construction of the three ships closest to completion.
Retired after serving two terms, setting the precedent of a two-term limit for presidents.
Died at the age of 67.
President Abraham Lincoln posthumously promoted him to the honorary rank of General in the United States Army.
President Ulysses S. Grant posthumously promoted him to the honorary rank of General of the Army.
President Gerald Ford posthumously promoted him to the honorary rank of General of the Armies of the United States.

keyAchievements.title: Served as the First President of the United States
keyAchievements.description: Served as the first President of the United States from 1789 to 1797, laying the foundation for the conventions and duties of the various departments of the federal government.

keyAchievements.title: Commander-in-Chief during the American Revolutionary War
keyAchievements.description: Achieved American independence by serving as the Commander-in-Chief of the Continental Army in the American Revolutionary War from 1775 to 1783.

keyAchievements.title: Presided over the Philadelphia Constitutional Convention
keyAchievements.description: Presided over the Philadelphia Constitutional Convention in 1787, held to draft the United States Constitution to replace the Articles of Confederation.

keyAchievements.title: Adoption of a Non-Interventionist Foreign Policy
keyAchievements.description: Adopted a policy of non-intervention in foreign affairs, particularly maintaining neutrality in wars that broke out in Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Promoted the Establishment of a Strong Central Government
keyAchievements.description: Pushed forward plans to establish a strong central government by restructuring national debt, implementing an efficient tax system, and creating a national bank.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Two-Term Precedent for Presidents
keyAchievements.description: Even though the Constitution had no term limits, he declined suggestions to run for a third term and retired after his second term, establishing a precedent.

keyAchievements.title: Suppression of the Whiskey Rebellion
keyAchievements.description: Established the public power and authority of the federal government by personally leading the army to suppress the Whiskey Rebellion in 1794.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to the Creation of the US Navy
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the practical creation of the US Navy by signing the 'Naval Act' in 1794.

keyAchievements.title: Delivery of the Farewell Address
keyAchievements.description: Delivered the famous 'Farewell Address' upon retirement, leaving advice on the importance of unity, warnings against partisanship, and foreign policy.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Shaping the Presidency and Creating Mass Democracy
keyAchievements.description: Is evaluated to have played a major role in shaping the presidency and creating mass democracy in the United States.

faq.question: Which president of the United States was George Washington?
faq.answer: George Washington was the first President of the United States, serving from 1789 to 1797.

faq.question: What role did George Washington play in the American Revolutionary War?
faq.answer: He served as the Commander-in-Chief of the Continental Army in the American Revolutionary War from 1775 to 1783, achieving American independence.

faq.question: How was George Washington elected president?
faq.answer: He was elected unanimously in the 1789 United States presidential election, the only such occurrence in American history.

faq.question: How many terms did George Washington serve as president?
faq.answer: He retired after serving two terms, setting the precedent of a two-term limit for presidents.

faq.question: When did George Washington die?
faq.answer: George Washington died in 1799 at the age of 67.

faq.question: Did George Washington contribute to the creation of the US Navy?
faq.answer: He contributed to the practical creation of the US Navy by signing the 'Naval Act of 1794'.

faq.question: What kind of foreign policy did George Washington implement?
faq.answer: He adopted a policy of non-intervention in foreign affairs, particularly maintaining neutrality in wars that broke out in Europe.

timeline.event: Born on April 28 (March 8 by the lunar calendar) as the third son of Yi Jeong in Geoncheon-dong, Southern Ward, Hanseong-bu.
Married Lady Bang and prepared for the military examination while studying military arts under the sponsorship of his father-in-law, Bang Jin.
Took the special military examination at the age of 27, but fell from his horse and failed.

Passed the triennial military examination on March 22 at the age of 32, placing fourth in the Pyonggwa (丙科) category, and began his official career as an acting instructor (Gwonji Hullyeonwon Bongsa).
Became the border defense commander (Gwongwan) of Donggubibo in December.
Served as a military training official (Hullyeonwon Bongsa) in Haemi for 10 months.
Transferred in December to serve as a military officer for the Army Commander of Chungcheong Province.
Transferred in July to serve as the naval commander (Manho) of Balpojin in Jeolla Province.
While serving as the commander of Balpojin in January, he was dismissed by Seo Ik for allegedly failing to properly maintain military weapons, but was soon reinstated.
Became a military training official (Hullyeonwon Bongsa) in May.
Recommended by Yi Yong, Army Commander of South Hamgyong Province, in July, and became his military officer.
Recommended and sent out as the border defense commander (Gwongwan) of Geonwonbo in August.
Suffered the death of his father on November 15.
Became an assistant master (Jubu) in the Royal Stables in January, followed by concurrent appointments as commander (Manho) of Josan and military farming supervisor (Dunjeonsaui) of Nokdo.
In September, the Battle of Nokdundo broke out following a surprise attack by the Jurchens, and Yi Sun-shin stayed behind with Yi Gyeong-rok to fight and achieve victory.
In October, the Army Commander of the North, Yi Il, blamed him for the defeat at Nokdundo, resulting in his dismissal, imprisonment, and conscription into the army as a commoner (Baeguijonggun).
Was pardoned and reinstated after capturing the Jurchen leader Ueulginae during the second expedition to Nokdundo.
Recommended by Yi San-hae, Jeong Eon-sin, and others for exceptional appointment by the Border Defense Council (Bibyeonsa) in January.
Became a military officer for the Inspector-General of Jeolla Province in February.
Became a military royal messenger (Mugyeom Seonjeongwan) in November.
Appointed as the magistrate of Jeongeup in December upon the recommendation of Ryu Seong-ryong, and governed the town with benevolent administration, receiving widespread praise from the people.

By order of King Seonjo in February, he was promoted from magistrate of Jeongeup to county magistrate of Jindo, commander of Garipo, and then appointed as the Naval Commander of the Left Jeolla Province.
Received news of the Japanese invasion at Busanpo through a dispatch from Won Gyun at 10 PM on May 26 (April 16 by the lunar calendar).
Achieved his first victory at the Battle of Okpo on June 16 (May 7 by the lunar calendar), sinking 26 Japanese fleet ships.
Deployed the Turtle Ship for the first time at the Battle of Sacheon on July 8 (May 29 by the lunar calendar), destroying 13 Japanese ships.
Severely defeated the Japanese forces using the 'Crane Wing' formation (Hakikjin) at the Battle of Hansan Island on August 14 (July 8 by the lunar calendar).
Promoted to the rank of Jaheondaebu on August 16.
Destroyed over 100 Japanese ships at the Battle of Busan on September 1 (lunar calendar).
Advanced to Ungpo in Ungcheon on March 12 (February 10 by the lunar calendar) and engaged in the Battle of Ungpo.
Appointed by the Joseon royal court as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces (Samdo Sugun Tongjesa) on August 1 (lunar calendar).
Dismissed from the post of Commander-in-Chief on April 11 (February 25 by the lunar calendar), escorted to Hanseong, and imprisoned.
Spared from the death penalty on May 16 (April 1 by the lunar calendar) and ordered to serve as a common soldier (Baeguijonggun) under Commander-in-Chief Gwon Yul.
His mother passed away on a boat on May 26 (April 11 by the lunar calendar) while he was serving as a common soldier.
Reappointed as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces on August 28 (July 16 by the lunar calendar) after Won Gyun's devastating defeat at the Battle of Chilcheollyang.
Achieved a great victory at the Battle of Myeongnyang on October 26 (September 16 by the lunar calendar), destroying over 130 of the hundreds of Japanese ships with only 13 warships.
Killed in action by a stray bullet while pursuing retreating Japanese forces at the Battle of Noryang on November 19 (lunar calendar).
Posthumously promoted to Senior First Rank (Jeong 1-pum), Daegwang Boguk Sungnok Daebu, Right State Councilor of the State Council, and concurrent Royal Lecturer in December.
Posthumously honored as a First Class Seonmu Merit Subject and Deokpung Buwongun in June, and additionally granted the title of Left State Councilor and concurrent Royal Lecturer in July.
Received the posthumous title 'Chungmu' (Loyalty and Valor) from King Injo in March, becoming Chungmugong.
Additionally posthumously promoted to Senior First Rank, Chief State Councilor of the State Council, by King Jeongjo in July.

keyAchievements.title: Passed the Military Examination
keyAchievements.description: Passed the triennial military examination in 1576 at the age of 32, placing fourth in the Pyonggwa category, and began his official career.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in the Battle of Nokdundo
keyAchievements.description: In 1587, during the Battle of Nokdundo caused by a surprise attack by Jurchens, he stayed behind with Yi Gyeong-rok, fought and won, rescuing over 60 Joseon citizens.

keyAchievements.title: Development and Construction of the Turtle Ship
keyAchievements.description: Immediately after his appointment as Left Naval Commander of Jeolla, he strengthened military preparations by expanding weapons and provisions and developing and building the Turtle Ship in preparation for war.

keyAchievements.title: Victories in Major Naval Battles of the Imjin War
keyAchievements.description: After the outbreak of the Imjin War, he successively defeated the Japanese navy in major naval battles including Okpo, Sacheon, Hansan Island, and Busan, seizing control of the sea and cutting off Japanese supply lines.

keyAchievements.title: Served as Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces
keyAchievements.description: Appointed as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces (Samdo Sugun Tongjesa) in 1593, taking supreme command and rebuilding the Joseon navy.

keyAchievements.title: Great Victory at the Battle of Myeongnyang
keyAchievements.description: After the defeat at Chilcheollyang, he achieved a great victory in the Myeongnyang Strait with 12 ships against hundreds of Japanese ships, reversing the tide of the Jeongyu War (Second Invasion).

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Battle of Noryang and Death in Action
keyAchievements.description: In the Battle of Noryang in 1598, he joined forces with the Ming navy to heavily defeat the retreating Japanese forces, and was killed in action by a stray bullet during the battle.

keyAchievements.title: Revered as a Hero-Saint
keyAchievements.description: Revered as a martial saint who saved the nation through successive victories in naval battles against the Japanese navy with brilliant intellect and outstanding strategy as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces during the Imjin War.

faq.question: When and where was Yi Sun-shin born?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin was born on April 28, 1545 (March 8 by the lunar calendar) in Geoncheon-dong, Southern Ward, Hanseong-bu (near the current Sindo Building at 19 Eulji-ro 18-gil, Jung-gu, Seoul).

faq.question: When did Yi Sun-shin pass the military examination?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin passed the triennial military examination on March 22, 1576 (the 9th year of King Seonjo) at the age of 32, placing fourth in the Pyonggwa category.

faq.question: Why was Yi Sun-shin stripped of his rank and made to serve as a common soldier (Baeguijonggun) twice?
faq.answer: The first time was in 1587 when the Northern Army Commander Yi Il wrongly blamed him for the defeat at the Battle of Nokdundo, leading to his imprisonment and subsequent conscription as a common soldier. The second time was in 1597 when he was dismissed from his post as Commander-in-Chief and ordered to serve as a common soldier on charges of disobeying the court's orders.

faq.question: In which naval battle did Yi Sun-shin die in action?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin was killed in action by a stray bullet on November 19, 1598 (lunar calendar), during the Battle of Noryang while pursuing the retreating Japanese forces.

faq.question: What are the famous writings left by Yi Sun-shin?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin left behind 'Nanjung Ilgi' (War Diary), which records his experiences during the Imjin War, and 'Chungmugong Yi Sun-shin Jeonseo', a complete collection of his posthumous works.

faq.question: What posthumous title did Yi Sun-shin receive?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin received the posthumous title 'Chungmu' (Loyalty and Valor) from King Injo in March 1643 (the 21st year of King Injo), thus becoming Chungmugong.

faq.question: What was Yi Sun-shin's family structure?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin had three sons (Yi Hoe, Yi Ye, Yi Myeon) and one daughter with his wife, Lady Bang of Sangju (Onyang Bang clan), and two sons (Yi Hun, Yi Sin) and two daughters with his concubine, Lady Oh of Haeju.

timeline.event: Born as the daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn.
Execution of her mother, Anne Boleyn.
Restored to the status of a princess through a change in the Acts of Succession.
Death of Henry VIII, accession of her younger half-brother, Edward VI.
Expelled from her home by her stepmother, Catherine Parr.
Beheading of Thomas Seymour.
Death of Edward VI.
Accession of her older half-sister, Mary I.
Imprisoned in the Tower of London after being suspected of masterminding Wyatt's rebellion.
Following the death of Mary I, ascended to the throne as Queen of England and Ireland.
Coronation held at Westminster Abbey.
Promulgation of the new Act of Supremacy.
Invasion of Le Havre, France (the only preemptive attack).
Mary, Queen of Scots, fled to England and was placed under house arrest.
Pope Pius V issued a papal bull declaring Elizabeth a heretic.
Humphrey Gilbert established a colony in North America.
Outbreak of the Anglo-Spanish War.
Beheading of Mary, Queen of Scots.
The Spanish Armada was heavily defeated by the English navy.
England suffered a major defeat after launching a large-scale expeditionary fleet (the Drake-Norris Expedition) against Spain.
Continued war with Irish rebels led by Hugh O'Neill.
Elizabeth declared that she was 'married to the nation'.
Granted a royal charter to the English East India Company.
Execution of Robert Devereux, Earl of Essex.
Died at Richmond Palace.

keyAchievements.title: 44 Years of Stable Rule
keyAchievements.description: Ascending to the throne in 1558 and ruling until her death in 1603, she reigned as the monarch of the Kingdoms of England and Ireland for 44 years, leading the 'Elizabethan Era'.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Church of England
keyAchievements.description: Reversed the religious policies of Mary I, reaffirming the break with Catholicism and ensuring the firm establishment of the Church of England.

keyAchievements.title: Cultivation of the Virgin Queen Image
keyAchievements.description: Despite numerous proposals, she insisted on remaining unmarried and was known as the 'Virgin Queen', which combined with a public cult of virginity, led to a personality cult.

keyAchievements.title: Defeat of the Spanish Armada
keyAchievements.description: Overcame a national threat in 1588 when the English navy severely defeated the Spanish Armada off the coast of Gravelines.

keyAchievements.title: Overseas Colonization and Trade Expansion
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for overseas colonization and trade expansion by authorizing Humphrey Gilbert's colonization in North America and chartering the English East India Company.

faq.question: Whose daughter was Elizabeth I?
faq.answer: She was the daughter of Anne Boleyn, the second wife of Henry VIII.

faq.question: Why was Elizabeth I called the 'Virgin Queen'?
faq.answer: She was called the 'Virgin Queen' because she insisted on remaining unmarried despite numerous proposals.

faq.question: What was the greatest military achievement during the reign of Elizabeth I?
faq.answer: The defeat of the Spanish Armada in 1588 is considered her greatest military achievement.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Elizabeth I and Mary, Queen of Scots?
faq.answer: Mary I claimed the right to the English throne and was considered a potential threat to Elizabeth; ultimately, Mary was implicated in a plot and beheaded by Elizabeth's order.

timeline.event: Birth of Gwanggaeto the Great, the 19th monarch of Goguryeo.
Accession of his father, King Gogugyang, following the death of his uncle, King Sosurim.
Invested as Crown Prince.
Ascended to the throne as the 19th monarch of Goguryeo following King Gogugyang, and began using the era name Yeongnak.
Led 40,000 troops to attack the northern border of Baekje, capturing over 10 fortresses including Seokhyeonseong.
When Baekje attacked Goguryeo's southern border, ordered his generals to repel them.
Conquered the Khitan, recovering over 10,000 people who had been taken captive in the 8th year of King Sosurim (378).
Captured Gwanmiseong, a northern stronghold of Baekje, after a 20-day siege.
Death of King Jinsa of Baekje and accession of King Asin.
Repelled a Baekje attack at Gwanmiseong. Founded 9 Buddhist temples in Pyongyang.
Repelled a Baekje attack at Sugokseong. Built 7 fortresses on the southern border to defend against Baekje invasions.
Captured over 8,000 Baekje troops alive and achieved a great victory at Paesu. Advanced to Yeomsu (Salt Water), conquered and subjugated the Khitan tribe Paeryeo.

Launched a massive attack on Baekje, capturing 58 fortresses and over 700 villages north of the Han River, and laid siege to Wiryeseong. Received the surrender of King Asin of Baekje and returned triumphantly, taking the king's brother and 10 ministers as hostages.
Conquered the Sushen to stabilize the northeastern border region.
Conducted a royal tour to Pyongyang. When Baekje and Wa (Japan) attacked Silla, Silla sent envoys to Pyongyang to plead for help.

Dispatched a large army of 50,000 to Silla to defeat the Wa forces. Pursued and repelled the Wa forces as far as Jongbalseong in Geumgwan Gaya. Replaced the king of Silla from Naemul Maripgan to Silseong Maripgan. King Murong Sheng of Later Yan attacked Sinseong and Namsosŏng.
Attacked and captured Sukgunseong of Later Yan.
Annihilated Wa forces that invaded the Daifang region. Invaded Later Yan and attacked as far as Yan Commandery, capturing it.
Defeated the Later Yan army at Yodongseong (Liaodong Fortress).
Defeated the Later Yan army at Mokjeoseong, firmly securing the occupation of Liaodong.
Mobilized 50,000 men to defeat the Later Yan army and captured massive spoils of war. Captured 6 fortresses of Later Yan on the way back.
When Go Un, who was of Goguryeo descent, founded Northern Yan, he established friendly relations, thereby stabilizing the western border.
Built 6 fortresses including Doksanseong in the east of the country and relocated households from Pyongyang to live there. Conducted a royal tour to the south.
Attacked and subjugated Eastern Buyeo.
Passed away.
King Jangsu erected a stele at his tomb and, in accordance with his will, appointed tomb keepers to manage the royal mausoleum.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Baekje
keyAchievements.description: Launched a massive attack on Baekje in 396, capturing 58 fortresses and over 700 villages north of the Han River, and receiving the surrender of King Asin.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Khitan and Sushen
keyAchievements.description: Conquered the Khitan in 392 to recover captured subjects, and conquered the Sushen in 398 to stabilize the northeastern border region.

keyAchievements.title: Rescue of Silla and Defeat of Wa
keyAchievements.description: Dispatched an army of 50,000 to Silla in 400 to defeat the allied forces of Baekje and Wa, and established influence over Silla by doing things such as replacing its king.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in War Against Later Yan
keyAchievements.description: Secured the occupation of Liaodong by winning fierce wars against Later Yan, and stabilized the western border through friendly relations with Northern Yan.

keyAchievements.title: Subjugation of Eastern Buyeo
keyAchievements.description: Strengthened Goguryeo's northern influence by attacking and subjugating Eastern Buyeo in 410.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening Internal Affairs
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation of the state by emphasizing Pyongyang, building 9 temples and fortresses there, and establishing a system for maintaining previous royal tombs and tomb keepers.

faq.question: What was the name of Gwanggaeto the Great?
faq.answer: His name was Damdeok or An.

faq.question: Which monarch of Goguryeo was Gwanggaeto the Great?
faq.answer: He was the 19th monarch of Goguryeo.

faq.question: When did Gwanggaeto the Great reign?
faq.answer: He reigned from the 5th lunar month of 391 to the 10th lunar month of 412.

faq.question: What was the era name of Gwanggaeto the Great?
faq.answer: His era name was Yeongnak.

faq.question: What results did Gwanggaeto the Great achieve in the war against Baekje?
faq.answer: In 396, he launched a massive attack on Baekje, capturing 58 fortresses and over 700 villages north of the Han River, and received the surrender of King Asin of Baekje.

faq.question: How did Gwanggaeto the Great rescue Silla?
faq.answer: In 400, he dispatched an army of 50,000 to Silla, defeated the allied forces of Baekje and Wa, and exerted influence over Silla by replacing its king.

faq.question: When was the Gwanggaeto Stele erected?
faq.answer: The Stele of Gwanggaeto the Great was erected by King Jangsu in 414.

gwanggaeto-the-great.missingDataNote: 즉위 및 사망 연도에 대해 《삼국사기》와 광개토대왕릉비 간의 기록 차이가 원문에 명시되어 있습니다.

timeline.event: Born at Blenheim Palace, Oxfordshire
Joined the British Army
Elected as a Member of Parliament for the Conservative Party
Crossed the floor to the Liberal Party
Appointed President of the Board of Trade in H. H. Asquith's cabinet
Married Clementine Hozier
Birth of his first daughter, Diana
Promoted to Home Secretary
Birth of his second child, Randolph
Appointed First Lord of the Admiralty
Outbreak of World War I, served as the civilian head of the Royal Navy
Resigned from the cabinet
Temporarily promoted to Lieutenant Colonel and commanded the 6th Battalion, Royal Scots Fusiliers
Appointed Minister of Munitions in David Lloyd George's government
Birth of his fourth child, Marigold
Appointed Secretary of State for War and Secretary of State for Air
Appointed Secretary of State for the Colonies
Death of his daughter Marigold
Birth of his fifth and last child, Mary
Lost his parliamentary seat in Dundee
Appointed Chancellor of the Exchequer
Restored the gold standard to its pre-1914 parity
Edited the government's anti-strike propaganda newspaper 'The British Gazette' during the UK General Strike
Resigned as Chancellor of the Exchequer following the Conservative defeat in the UK general election
Published 'My Early Life'
Resigned from the Conservative Shadow Cabinet over Stanley Baldwin's support for Dominion status for India
Recognized the threat of Nazi Germany after Hitler came to power
Published the fourth volume of 'Marlborough: His Life and Times'
Condemned the signing of the Munich Agreement as a 'total and unmitigated defeat'
Re-appointed First Lord of the Admiralty after the declaration of war on Germany
Appointed Prime Minister of the United Kingdom
Delivered his first speech as Prime Minister, 'Blood, Toil, Tears and Sweat'
Delivered the 'We shall fight on the beaches' speech after the conclusion of the Dunkirk evacuation
Delivered the 'This was their finest hour' speech
The UK and US governments signed the Destroyers for Bases Agreement
The Lend-Lease Act was formally enacted
Hitler launched the invasion of the Soviet Union (Operation Barbarossa)
Issued the Atlantic Charter with Roosevelt
Declared war on Japan immediately following the attack on Pearl Harbor
Described the fall of Singapore to Japanese forces as 'the worst disaster and largest capitulation in British history'
Occurrence of the Bengal famine
Became Leader of the Opposition after the Conservative Party was defeated in the general election
Re-elected as Prime Minister
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature
Resigned as Prime Minister
Retained his seat as a Member of Parliament
Death

keyAchievements.title: Prime Minister during World War II
keyAchievements.description: Served as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom from 1940 to 1945, leading the Allied war effort against the Axis powers and securing victory in 1945.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Literature Laureate
keyAchievements.description: Wrote numerous books and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1953.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Social Reforms
keyAchievements.description: As President of the Board of Trade and Home Secretary, he championed prison reform and workers' social security, and pushed for the introduction of a minimum wage system and unemployment insurance.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening Naval Power
keyAchievements.description: As First Lord of the Admiralty, he contributed to the strengthening of naval power by creating a Naval War Staff, building submarines, refocusing the Royal Naval Air Service, and supporting the development of the tank.

keyAchievements.title: Wartime Orator
keyAchievements.description: Boosted national morale and rallied the will to fight through powerful speeches such as 'Blood, Toil, Tears and Sweat' and 'We shall fight on the beaches'.

keyAchievements.title: Co-authoring the Atlantic Charter
keyAchievements.description: Issued the Atlantic Charter with Roosevelt in 1941, outlining both nations' goals for the post-war world.

faq.question: When was Winston Churchill born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Winston Churchill was born on November 30, 1874, and died on January 24, 1965.

faq.question: How many times did Winston Churchill serve as Prime Minister?
faq.answer: Winston Churchill served as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom twice: during World War II from 1940 to 1945, and again from 1951 to 1955.

faq.question: Which political party did Winston Churchill belong to?
faq.answer: Churchill spent most of his political career as a member of the Conservative Party, but he was a member of the Liberal Party from 1904 to 1924.

faq.question: Did Winston Churchill win a Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: Yes, Churchill wrote several books between his terms in office and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1953.

faq.question: What role did Churchill play during World War II?
faq.answer: Appointed Prime Minister in May 1940 during World War II, Churchill formed a national government and led the Allied war effort against the Axis powers, achieving victory in 1945.

faq.question: Did Churchill coin the term 'Iron Curtain'?
faq.answer: Yes. After the Conservative defeat in the 1945 general election, as the Cold War with the Soviet Union intensified, he used the term 'Iron Curtain' to warn of the expanding communist influence in Europe and urged European unity.

timeline.event: Born in the State of Zhao to Prince Yiren and Zhao Ji
Began reign as the 31st King of the State of Qin (Lü Buwei began regency)
Began personal rule
Conquered the State of Han
Conquered the State of Zhao
Conquered the State of Wei
Conquered the State of Chu
Conquered the State of Yan
Conquered the State of Qi and unified the Chinese mainland
Carried out the Burning of Books (Fen Shu)
Carried out the Burying of Scholars (Keng Ru)
Died at the age of 50 in Shaqiu during his final inspection tour

keyAchievements.title: Unification of China
keyAchievements.description: Unified a divided China and established the imperial system and the commandery-county system (Junxian system), laying the basic framework for Chinese dynasties over the next 2,000 years.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Imperial System
keyAchievements.description: First to use the title 'Emperor' (Huangdi) instead of 'King' and proclaimed himself 'First Emperor' (Shi Huangdi).

keyAchievements.title: Implementation of the Commandery-County System
keyAchievements.description: Following the advice of Chancellor Li Si, he divided the entire country into 36 commanderies and appointed an administrator, a military commander, and an inspector to each, establishing a centralized system of governance.

keyAchievements.title: Standardization of Weights, Measures, Currency, and Script
keyAchievements.description: To rule the empire efficiently, he standardized weights and measures, currency, and writing systems, and improved the road networks.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the Great Wall
keyAchievements.description: Connected the defensive walls of the Warring States period to complete the Great Wall to prevent invasions by the Xiongnu.

faq.question: When was Qin Shi Huang born?
faq.answer: Qin Shi Huang was born in 259 BC in the State of Zhao to Prince Yiren, who was sent there as a hostage from the State of Qin, and Zhao Ji.

faq.question: Why did Qin Shi Huang use the title 'Emperor' (Huangdi)?
faq.answer: Considering the title of 'King' inadequate for himself, Qin Shi Huang took the characters 'Huang' and 'Di' from the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors to form 'Huangdi' (Emperor), and styled himself the 'First Emperor' (Shi Huangdi).

faq.question: What were the major tyrannical acts of Qin Shi Huang?
faq.answer: Qin Shi Huang carried out the 'Burning of Books and Burying of Scholars', a massive cultural suppression in which he burned all books except those concerning the history of Qin, medicine, and agriculture, and buried alive over 460 scholars.

faq.question: What efforts did Qin Shi Huang make to achieve immortality?
faq.answer: In his quest for the elixir of life, Qin Shi Huang sent the alchemist Xu Fu with 3,000 boys and girls and a ship laden with treasures to Mount Penglai. He also ingested mercury, mistakenly believing it to be the elixir of immortality.

faq.question: How did Qin Shi Huang die?
faq.answer: During his final inspection tour in 210 BC, Qin Shi Huang fell severely ill in the region of Shaqiu and passed away at the age of 50.

timeline.event: Born in the city of Rome.
Death of his father, Octavius.
Delivered the funeral oration for his maternal grandmother, Julia Caesaris.
Donned the toga virilis and was elected to the College of Pontiffs.
Presided over the Greek games in honor of the Temple of Venus Genetrix, built by Caesar.
Assassination of his adoptive father, Julius Caesar.
Arrived in Rome and met Mark Antony.
Defeated two legions under Antony's command.
Admitted to the Senate and granted military command.
Defeated Antony's forces at the Battle of Forum Gallorum and the Battle of Mutina.
Appointed consul.
Formed the Second Triumvirate with Antony and Lepidus near Bologna.
The Roman Senate declared Julius Caesar a god.
Defeated the forces of Brutus and Cassius at the Battle of Philippi.
Ended the siege of Lucius Antonius at Perusia and received his surrender.
Executed 300 senators and equestrians who had collaborated with Lucius Antonius.
Signed the Treaty of Brundisium with Antony.
Married his older sister, Octavia Minor, to Antony.
Concluded a temporary peace agreement, the Pact of Misenum, with Sextus Pompey.
Divorced Scribonia and married Livia Drusilla.
Agreed to extend the Triumvirate for another five years.
Completely destroyed Sextus Pompey's fleet at the Battle of Naulochus.
The Senate stripped Antony of his consular powers and declared war on Egypt.
Defeated the fleets of Antony and Cleopatra at the Battle of Actium.
Began holding the consulship every year.
Achieved victory in Alexandria, leading to the suicides of Antony and Cleopatra.
Granted the title of 'First Citizen of the State' (princeps civitatis) by the Senate.
After ending the civil wars, declared the return of extraordinary powers to the Senate and was awarded the title 'Augustus'.
Married his daughter Julia to his sister's son, Marcellus.
Marcellus died, and Augustus resigned from the consulship.
Personally undertook the construction of roads.
Finally suppressed the rebellion in the Cantabria region of Spain.
Assumed the office of Pontifex Maximus after the death of Lepidus.
His stepson Drusus died after falling from a horse in Germania.
Elected consul, and his maternal grandson Gaius Caesar began his political career.
Elected consul, his maternal grandson Lucius Caesar began his political career, and he was granted the title of 'Father of the Country' (pater patriae) by the Senate and the Roman people.
His maternal grandson Lucius Caesar died young.
His maternal grandson Gaius Caesar died young, and he adopted Tiberius and Agrippa Postumus as his sons.
Established the 'Military Treasury' (aerarium militare) to fund pensions for active and retired soldiers.
Three Roman legions were annihilated by Germanic tribes in the Teutoburg Forest.
Tiberius was granted all the prerogatives held by Augustus.
Died in Nola, and the Senate and the popular assembly declared him a god.

keyAchievements.title: First Emperor of the Roman Empire
keyAchievements.description: Reigned as the first Emperor of the Roman Empire and the founder of the Julio-Claudian dynasty.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Pax Romana
keyAchievements.description: His rule ushered in an era of peace and prosperity known as the 'Pax Romana' (Roman Peace), which sustained peace in the Mediterranean world for over two centuries.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of Imperial Territory and Border Protection
keyAchievements.description: Expanded the territory of the Roman Empire, protected its borders and allies, and concluded a peace treaty with Parthia.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of the State System
keyAchievements.description: Reformed Rome's taxation system, built an overland transportation network, and established a standing army, a small navy, and the Praetorian Guard.

keyAchievements.title: Redevelopment of the City of Rome
keyAchievements.description: Established police and fire-fighting forces in Rome and extensively redeveloped large parts of the city.

keyAchievements.title: Recording the 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti'
keyAchievements.description: Before his death, he left behind a record of his accomplishments known as the 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti'.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Title 'Augustus'
keyAchievements.description: Received the title 'Augustus', meaning 'the revered one', from the Senate.

keyAchievements.title: Granted the Title 'First Citizen of the State'
keyAchievements.description: Was granted the title 'princeps civitatis' (First Citizen of the State) by the Senate, meaning the leading figure.

keyAchievements.title: Securing Military Command
keyAchievements.description: Became the supreme commander of the Roman military and held the authority to celebrate triumphs.

keyAchievements.title: Tax Reform
keyAchievements.description: Executed tax reforms, including a fixed-rate direct tax on provincials and the introduction of a tax collection system managed by civil servants.

keyAchievements.title: Naming of the Month 'August'
keyAchievements.description: The former 'sixth month' (Sextilis) was renamed 'Augustus' (August) in his honor.

keyAchievements.title: Construction Projects
keyAchievements.description: Contributed so significantly to the architectural development of Rome that he famously boasted, 'I found Rome a city of bricks and left it a city of marble'.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Military Pension Fund
keyAchievements.description: Established the 'Military Treasury' (aerarium militare) to provide pensions for active duty and retired soldiers.

faq.question: What was the real name of Augustus?
faq.answer: Before being adopted by Caesar, his name was Gaius Octavius, and after adoption, he became known as Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus.

faq.question: How did Augustus become the first emperor of the Roman Empire?
faq.answer: After the collapse of the Second Triumvirate, he monopolized power and, upon receiving the title 'Augustus' from the Senate in 27 BC, became the first emperor of the Roman Empire.

faq.question: What was the greatest achievement of the Augustan era?
faq.answer: His rule brought about an era of prosperity known as the 'Pax Romana' (Roman Peace), sustaining peace in the Mediterranean world for more than two centuries.

faq.question: Did Augustus enjoy gambling?
faq.answer: Contrary to his stoic image, he enjoyed gambling, particularly dice and odd-even games, and often built friendships by intentionally losing money.

faq.question: Why was the month of August named after Augustus?
