Rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.
Published the poetry collection Les Châtiments.
Published the poetry collection Les Contemplations.
Published the first series of the poetry collection La Légende des siècles.
Published the novel Les Misérables.
Published the novel Les Travailleurs de la mer.
Wrote Les Années funestes, depicting the reality of French workers.
Returned to Paris as a defender of republicanism with the fall of Napoleon III due to the Franco-Prussian War.
Published L'Année terrible.
Published the novel Quatrevingt-treize.
Published the second series of La Légende des siècles.
Wrote his will.
Published the third series of La Légende des siècles.
Died, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon in a state funeral.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Romantic Literature
keyAchievements.description: He presented the theory of romantic theater in the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Masterpiece Novels
keyAchievements.description: He solidified his position as a novelist with Notre-Dame de Paris and wrote numerous masterpieces including Les Misérables during his exile.

keyAchievements.title: Social Criticism and Humanitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Through his works, he expressed love for poor workers, emphasized popular art, and solidified his humanitarian tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Major Figure in the French Literary World
keyAchievements.description: He occupied an important position in the French literary world, receiving the Knight of the Legion of Honour in 1825 and becoming a member of the Académie Française in 1841.

faq.question: Who was Victor Hugo?
faq.answer: He was a French poet, novelist, and playwright.

faq.question: What impact did Victor Hugo have on romanticism?
faq.answer: He presented the theory of romantic theater through the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

faq.question: What are Victor Hugo's representative works?
faq.answer: They include the novels Notre-Dame de Paris and Les Misérables.

faq.question: Why was Victor Hugo in exile?
faq.answer: He rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état in 1851, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.

faq.question: What was Victor Hugo's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: His works were banned by the Catholic Church, and he could not reconcile with Catholicism, even refusing the church's prayers.

faq.question: When did Victor Hugo die, and where is he buried?
faq.answer: He died on May 22, 1885, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon.

timeline.event: Born as the third son in the Black Sea port city of Taganrog.
Attended a preparatory school in his hometown that taught ancient Greek.
Entered the Taganrog Gymnasium, which aimed for a classical education.
Repeated the third-year course due to poor grades.
Failed his ancient Greek exam and repeated the fifth-year course.
Graduated from the Taganrog school and entered the medical faculty of Moscow University.
Took charge of a column sketching daily life in Moscow every two weeks for the literary magazine Oskolki.
Passed his medical graduation exams and published his first short story collection, The Tales of Melpomene.
The play Ivanov premiered and was a huge success in Moscow and St. Petersburg.
Left Moscow to travel to Siberia and Sakhalin Island.
Bought an estate in the Moscow suburbs, lived with his parents and younger sister, and worked as a doctor.
Published the play The Seagull, marking the beginning of his later dramatic period.
Moved his residence to the Crimean Peninsula facing Yalta due to deteriorating health.
Elected as a member of the Russian Academy.
Married Olga Knipper, an actress of the Moscow Art Theatre.
Resigned his membership in the Academy in protest when the government revoked Maxim Gorky's membership.
Ended his 44-year life due to pulmonary tuberculosis.

keyAchievements.title: Medical Graduation and Activities
keyAchievements.description: Graduating from the medical faculty of Moscow University in 1884, he worked as a doctor, caring for neighboring farmers and building schools in villages.

keyAchievements.title: Reputation as a Short Story Writer
keyAchievements.description: Around his college graduation, he was already highly renowned as a rising novelist, contributing humorous short stories and essays to various magazines and publishing his first short story collection, The Tales of Melpomene.

keyAchievements.title: Success as a Playwright
keyAchievements.description: Starting with the massive success of the play Ivanov, he established a new form of conversational drama through modern masterpieces such as The Seagull, Uncle Vanya, Three Sisters, and The Cherry Orchard.

keyAchievements.title: Socially Critical Activities
keyAchievements.description: He wrote a travelogue recording the miserable lives of prisoners exiled to Sakhalin Island and returned his Russian Academy membership in protest of the government's unfair actions.

faq.question: What was Anton Chekhov's profession?
faq.answer: He was a Russian doctor, short story writer, and playwright.

faq.question: What are Anton Chekhov's representative plays?
faq.answer: His representative plays include The Seagull, Uncle Vanya, Three Sisters, and The Cherry Orchard.

faq.question: When did Anton Chekhov die, and what was the cause of death?
faq.answer: Anton Chekhov died of pulmonary tuberculosis at the age of 44 on July 15, 1904.

faq.question: What activities did Anton Chekhov do as a doctor?
faq.answer: Living in the Moscow suburbs, he served as a doctor by taking care of the health of neighboring farmers and building schools in the village.

timeline.event: Frédéric François Chopin was born.
Chopin's family moved to Warsaw.
Warsaw became part of the constitutional Kingdom of Poland.
Received his first professional music education from Wojciech Żywny.
Began holding public concerts at age 7 and composed two polonaises.
Composed the Polonaise in A-flat major and dedicated it to Żywny.
Performed his own improvisations and part of Moscheles's concerto on the aeolomelodicon.
Performed his Rondo Op. 1 (his first commercially published work) at an aeolopantaleon concert.
Attended the Warsaw Lyceum and studied music theory, figured bass, and composition at the Warsaw Conservatory for 3 years.
Shortly after his youngest sister Emilia died, the family moved to Krakowskie Przedmieście.
Visited Berlin with a family friend.
Composed Souvenir de Paganini after hearing Niccolò Paganini play the violin.
Debuted in Vienna, holding two piano concerts and receiving favorable reviews.
Premiered Piano Concerto No. 2 in F minor, Op. 21.
Left Warsaw.
The November Uprising broke out in Warsaw.
Expressed anguish in his diary upon hearing news of the uprising's suppression while traveling from Vienna to Paris.
Arrived in Paris.
Robert Schumann declared 'Hats off, gentlemen, a genius' in a review of the Op. 2 Variations.
Held his Paris debut concert to universal acclaim.
Published the Études Op. 10.
Attended the Lower Rhenish Music Festival with Hiller and met Felix Mendelssohn.
Went to Karlovy Vary to spend his last time with his parents.
Obtained French citizenship.
Met George Sand at a party hosted by Marie d'Agoult.
Stayed with the Wodziński family in Marienbad and Dresden, and proposed to Maria.
Visited London secretly with piano manufacturer Camille Pleyel.
Published the Études Op. 25.
Became lovers with George Sand.
Spent the winter with Sand in Majorca.
Published the Preludes Op. 28.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 2, Op. 35.
Headed to Sand's estate in Nohant to spend the summer.
Last public joint appearance with Liszt.
Wrote a letter to Grzymała about his deteriorating health after a Paris recital.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 3, Op. 58.
The relationship between Sand and Chopin deteriorated.
His relationship with Sand ended after a heated exchange of letters.
Held his last Paris concert with cellist Auguste Franchomme.
Left for London during the French Revolution of 1848.
Queen Victoria and Prince Albert attended his first London performance at Stafford House.
Wrote his last will in Edinburgh.
Held his last public concert at the Guildhall in London.
His older sister Ludwika came to Paris with her husband and daughter.
Died in Paris at the age of 39.
Funeral held at the Madeleine Church in Paris.
Chopin's tombstone was installed.
His older sister Ludwika took Chopin's heart back to Poland.
Julian Fontana compiled and published 23 unpublished piano pieces with 8 additional opus numbers (Op. 66–73).
17 Polish songs were published as Op. 74.
Chopin's music was used in the ballet Les Sylphides (Chopiniana).
Claude Debussy dedicated his piano études to the memory of Chopin.
Results of a visual examination of Chopin's preserved heart were published in the American Journal of Medicine.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Composer
keyAchievements.description: Maintained worldwide fame with his poetic genius based on a professional technique unmatched by anyone of his time.

keyAchievements.title: Specializing in Solo Piano Music
keyAchievements.description: Composed primarily solo piano music, and all his known works feature the piano.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Instrumental Ballade
keyAchievements.description: Created the instrumental ballade as a new musical genre.

keyAchievements.title: Making the Scherzo a Concert Piece
keyAchievements.description: Was the first to compose scherzos as individual concert pieces.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Prelude Genre
keyAchievements.description: Established essentially a new genre with his free cycle of preludes (Op. 28).

keyAchievements.title: Utilizing the Poetic Potential of Études
keyAchievements.description: Utilized the poetic potential of the concept of the concert étude in his two sets of études (Op. 10, Op. 25).

keyAchievements.title: Elevating the Status of the Mazurka
keyAchievements.description: Put the mazurka on the European musical map.

keyAchievements.title: New Standards for the Polonaise Form
keyAchievements.description: Set new standards for music in the polonaise form.

keyAchievements.title: Deepening the Nocturne Genre
keyAchievements.description: Elevated the nocturne to a deeper level of sophistication.

keyAchievements.title: Introducing Nationalist Consciousness into Music
keyAchievements.description: Is evaluated as having introduced a new nationalist consciousness into music.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Piano and Composition Schools
keyAchievements.description: Was called the founder of piano and composition schools.

faq.question: When was Chopin born?
faq.answer: Frédéric François Chopin was born on March 1, 1810.

faq.question: What was Chopin's real name?
faq.answer: His real name was Fryderyk Franciszek Chopin.

faq.question: What instrument did Chopin primarily compose for?
faq.answer: Chopin composed primarily solo piano music, and the piano appears in all his works.

faq.question: How many public performances did Chopin give in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He gave a minimal number of public performances, slightly over 30, preferring the more intimate atmosphere of salons.

faq.question: How did Chopin make a living in Paris?
faq.answer: He made a living by selling his compositions and giving piano lessons, which were highly popular at the time.

faq.question: What was George Sand's relationship to Chopin?
faq.answer: George Sand was a French author and Chopin's lover; they became lovers in July 1838.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Chopin and Franz Liszt?
faq.answer: The two were friends and respected each other, but their friendship was unstable and had the characteristics of a love-hate relationship.

faq.question: What was the cause of Chopin's death?
faq.answer: His death certificate listed the cause of death as tuberculosis, while recent research suggests it was highly likely a rare pericarditis caused by chronic tuberculosis complications.

faq.question: Where is Chopin's heart preserved?
faq.answer: His heart was removed by a doctor, preserved in alcohol in an urn, and taken back to Poland by his older sister Ludwika in 1850.

faq.question: Did Chopin give descriptive titles to his works?
faq.answer: With the exception of his Funeral March, the composer never named his instrumental pieces beyond genre and number.

timeline.event: Born into an artisan family in the Katsushika district of Edo (Childhood name: Tokitaro).
Began drawing around age 6.
Worked at a bookstore and lending library at age 12.
Worked as an apprentice woodcarver at age 14.
Entered the studio of Katsukawa Shunshō at age 18.
Received the name Shunrō from his master and published his first series of prints of Kabuki actors.
Produced Fireworks at Ryōgoku Bridge in Edo.
Death of his master Katsukawa Shunshō.
Second marriage.
Passed the name Tawaraya Sōri to his pupil, began working as an independent artist, and used the name "Hokusai Tomisa".
Began using the name "Katsushika Hokusai", published the landscape print series Famous Views of the Eastern Capital and Eight Views of Edo, and started taking on pupils.
Painted a massive portrait of the Buddhist monk Daruma, about 200 square meters in size, during an Edo festival.
Collaborated with popular novelist Kyokutei Bakin to produce a series of illustrated novels.
Produced illustrations for the fantasy novel Strange Tales of the Crescent Moon.
Changed his name to Taito at age 51 and began producing Hokusai Manga and various etehon (art manuals).
Produced Quick Lessons in Simplified Drawing.
The first volume of Hokusai Manga was published and became a huge success.
Painted a giant portrait of Daruma outside the Hongan-ji Nagoya Betsuin in Nagoya.
Changed his name to Iitsu and began gaining a solid reputation as an artist throughout Japan.
Produced his masterpiece, Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced Oceans of Wisdom (Shika Shashin-kyō).
Began working under the name "Gakyō Rōjin Manji" (The Old Man Mad About Art) and produced One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced his last print series, One Hundred Poems Explained by the Nurse.
A fire destroyed his studio and much of his work.
Visited Obuse in Shinano Province at age 83 and stayed there for several years.
Painted Chinese lions (shishi) every morning as a talisman to ward off bad luck.
Painted Dragon Flying in Clouds above Mount Fuji and Tiger in Snow.
Died and was buried at Seikyō-ji Temple in Taito Ward, Tokyo.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced this ukiyo-e series, which includes the iconic The Great Wave off Kanagawa, earning immense fame both domestically and internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Expanding Ukiyo-e Subjects
keyAchievements.description: He played a crucial role in broadening the subjects of ukiyo-e, which had primarily focused on courtesans and actors, to include landscapes, plants, and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Output
keyAchievements.description: Working tirelessly from childhood until his death, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, woodblock prints, and images for picture books.

keyAchievements.title: Innovative Composition and Depiction Skills
keyAchievements.description: He is considered one of the greatest masters in art history due to his innovative compositions and outstanding depictive skills.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Hokusai Manga
keyAchievements.description: He produced Hokusai Manga, containing thousands of drawings, which immediately became a huge success and was also used as a drawing manual.

keyAchievements.title: Experimentation with Western Perspective
keyAchievements.description: Among Japanese artists, he early on experimented with Western perspective, applying techniques to express depth and volume in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European Art
keyAchievements.description: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

keyAchievements.title: Production of One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced another important series, One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji, evaluated as "the greatest masterpiece among his landscape collections."

faq.question: Who was Katsushika Hokusai?
faq.answer: He was a Japanese ukiyo-e painter and printmaker of the Edo period, who played a crucial role in expanding the subjects of ukiyo-e to landscapes, plants, and animals.

faq.question: What is Hokusai's most famous work?
faq.answer: The Great Wave off Kanagawa, included in his ukiyo-e series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, is his most iconic work.

faq.question: What influence did Hokusai have on Western art?
faq.answer: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

faq.question: How many names did Hokusai use in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He is known to have used at least 30 names during his lifetime, closely linked to changes in his artwork and style.

faq.question: When did Hokusai die?
faq.answer: He died on May 10, 1849, showing a passion for creating better works even on his deathbed.

timeline.event: Born in Torquay, Devon, England.
Went to Paris at age 16 to study singing and piano.
Married her first husband, Archibald Christie, a colonel in the Royal Flying Corps.
Worked in a hospital dispensary during World War I.
Her only daughter, Rosalind Hicks, was born.
Disappeared for 10 days on December 8, attracting media attention.
Remarried to archaeologist Max Mallowan.
Received the Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE).
Received the Dame Commander of the Order of the British Empire (DBE).
Died at the age of 85.

keyAchievements.title: Reputation as a Mystery Writer
keyAchievements.description: Nicknamed the "Queen of Crime", she wrote over 80 mystery novels and gained immense popularity by creating the great Belgian detective Hercule Poirot and Miss Marple.

keyAchievements.title: Global Sales Records
keyAchievements.description: Her works are listed in the Guinness World Records, having sold over 1 billion copies in English and another 1 billion copies translated into 103 other languages.

keyAchievements.title: Most Translated Author
keyAchievements.description: She is recorded as the most translated author in the world by the Index Translationum.

keyAchievements.title: Longest-Running Play
keyAchievements.description: Her play The Mousetrap ran from its premiere in London in 1955 until November 2012.

faq.question: What was Agatha Christie's full name?
faq.answer: Her full name was Dame Agatha Christie, Lady Mallowan, DBE.

faq.question: What pen name did Agatha Christie use?
faq.answer: She used the pen name Mary Westmacott when writing romance novels.

faq.question: Who are the famous detective characters created by Agatha Christie?
faq.answer: She created the great Belgian detective Hercule Poirot and Miss Marple.

faq.question: How many copies of Agatha Christie's works have been sold?
faq.answer: Her works are listed in the Guinness World Records, having sold over 1 billion copies in the English-speaking world and another 1 billion copies in editions translated into 103 languages.

faq.question: What is Agatha Christie's most famous play, and how long was it performed?
faq.answer: Her play The Mousetrap ran from its premiere in London in 1955 until November 2012.

timeline.event: Samuel Langhorne Clemens (Mark Twain) was born.
Debuted in the literary world with "The Celebrated Jumping Frog".
Samuel Langhorne Clemens (Mark Twain) died.

keyAchievements.title: American Author
keyAchievements.description: An American author more famously known by his pen name Mark Twain.

keyAchievements.title: The Adventures of Tom Sawyer
keyAchievements.description: A children's book depicting the adventures of mischievous boys Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn, set along the Mississippi River.

keyAchievements.title: The Celebrated Jumping Frog
keyAchievements.description: The work that made him debut in the literary world in 1865.

keyAchievements.title: The Innocents Abroad
keyAchievements.description: A work that gained popularity.

keyAchievements.title: Mississippi Trilogy
keyAchievements.description: Consists of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, Life on the Mississippi, and The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, expressing a hymn to the American and free spirit.

keyAchievements.title: Social Satires
keyAchievements.description: Works set in medieval feudal Europe, such as A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court, The Prince and the Pauper, and The Mysterious Stranger.

keyAchievements.title: Criticism of Christian Fundamentalism
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he published Letters from the Earth, criticizing Christian fundamentalism in the United States at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Criticism of Slavery
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he strove to expose and rectify the contradictions of a "free country with slavery."

faq.question: What was Mark Twain's real name?
faq.answer: Mark Twain's real name was Samuel Langhorne Clemens.

faq.question: What is Mark Twain's representative work?
faq.answer: His major works include The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, a children's book depicting the adventures of mischievous boys Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn, set along the Mississippi River.

faq.question: Where did Mark Twain's pen name come from?
faq.answer: He used the unit of water depth, which he used during his time as a steamboat pilot on the Mississippi River, as his pen name.

faq.question: What was Mark Twain's religious stance?
faq.answer: Mark Twain was a progressive Presbyterian, but he criticized Christian fundamentalism in the United States at the time.

mark-twain.missingDataNote: 가족의 해니벌 이사 시기, 아버지 사망 및 견습공 시작 시기, 금 탐사 시기 등 일부 사건의 정확한 연도가 원문에 명시되지 않아 타임라인에서 생략되었습니다. 또한, 주요 작품들의 출판 연도 정보가 부족하여 포함하지 않았습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany.
Harbored doubts about his faith triggered by the Lisbon earthquake.
Harbored doubts about his faith triggered by the Seven Years' War.
His romance with Gretchen was broken off.
Had a romance with Anna Katharina Schönkopf while studying at Leipzig University.
Wrote his first play, Die Laune des Verliebten (The Lover's Caprice).
Fostered pious religious feelings through his association with Susanne von Klettenberg while recovering at home.
Obtained a doctorate in law from the University of Strasbourg, reunited with Herder to open his eyes to the essence of literature, and laid the groundwork for the Sturm und Drang movement.
Opened a law practice.
Went to the Imperial Chamber Court in Wetzlar for legal training, where he met Charlotte Buff.
Published the play Götz von Berlichingen.
Published the novel The Sorrows of Young Werther.
Became engaged to Lili Schönemann, but broke it off shortly after.
Arrived in Weimar at the invitation of Duke Karl August of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach.
Handled state affairs for about 10 years in the early Weimar period, building a record of achievements as a privy councillor, senior privy councillor, and head of the cabinet.
Wrote the tragedy Iphigenia in Tauris (prose version).
Wrote Torquato Tasso.
Stated, "I am not an anti-Christian or an un-Christian, but a non-Christian."
Established his classical view of literature during his journey to Italy, and wrote Iphigenia in Tauris (verse version).
Embarked on a journey to Italy lasting a year and a half.
Published Egmont.
Returned to Germany.
Began living with Christiane von Vulpius.
Had his eldest son, August.
Torquato Tasso was first published.
Followed Duke August in the War of the First Coalition and participated in the Battle of Valmy.
Participated in the Siege of Mainz.
Reunited with Schiller on his way back from a natural science conference in Jena and began their friendship.
Wrote the Bildungsroman Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship.
Wrote the epic poem Hermann and Dorothea.
Fell into great shock due to Schiller's death.
Completed Part I of Faust.
Completed Theory of Colours.
Wrote Italian Journey.
Wrote West-östlicher Divan.
Completed Wilhelm Meister's Journeyman Years.
Wrote the Marienbad Elegy.
Wrote Second Stay in Rome.
Completed Part II of Faust.
Passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: He completed German classical literature by creating numerous literary works such as The Sorrows of Young Werther, Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship, and Faust.

keyAchievements.title: Fostering Weimar as a Cultural Center
keyAchievements.description: Moving to Weimar in 1775, he played a decisive role in elevating the city to a cultural center.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as an Administrator and Scientist
keyAchievements.description: He participated in state affairs serving as a minister in the Grand Duchy of Weimar, and left vast achievements showing interest in various natural science fields such as botany, anatomy, mineralogy, geology, and the theory of colors.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing German Classical Literature
keyAchievements.description: He established his classical view of literature during his journey to Italy and met Schiller, blossoming German Weimar Classicism together.

keyAchievements.title: Court Theatre Director
keyAchievements.description: As the director of the court theatre, he was active across the board in management, directing, and actor training, elevating the theatre to a world-class level.

faq.question: Who was Goethe?
faq.answer: He was a German classical writer, philosopher, and scientist, who also served as a minister in the Grand Duchy of Weimar.

faq.question: What are Goethe's representative works?
faq.answer: His representative works include The Sorrows of Young Werther, Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship, and Faust.

faq.question: What was Goethe's stance on religion?
faq.answer: Born into a Lutheran family, he developed doubts about his faith due to the Lisbon earthquake and the Seven Years' War, and referred to himself as a "non-Christian".

faq.question: What role did Goethe play in Weimar?
faq.answer: Moving to Weimar in 1775, he played a decisive role in elevating the city to a cultural center and participated in state affairs as an administrator.

timeline.event: Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley was born.
Lost her mother just 11 days after birth.
Began a romance with Percy Bysshe Shelley, one of her father's political followers.
Conceived the idea for the novel Frankenstein while spending the summer with Lord Byron and John William Polidori near Geneva, Switzerland.
Married Percy Bysshe Shelley following the suicide of Percy's wife Harriet.
The Shelleys left Britain for Italy.
Published the novel Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus.
Her husband Percy Bysshe Shelley drowned when his sailing boat sank in a storm near Viareggio.
Returned to Britain.
Published the historical novel Valperga.
Published the apocalyptic novel The Last Man.
Wrote biographical articles for Dionysius Lardner's Cabinet Cyclopaedia.
Published the historical novel The Fortunes of Perkin Warbeck.
Published the novel Lodore.
Published the novel Falkner.
Published the travelogue Rambles in Germany and Italy.
Died of an illness suspected to be a brain tumor.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Frankenstein
keyAchievements.description: She wrote the Gothic novel Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus (1818), which is considered an early example of science fiction.

keyAchievements.title: Editing and Promoting Her Husband's Works
keyAchievements.description: She edited and promoted the works of her husband, the romantic poet and philosopher Percy Bysshe Shelley.

faq.question: What work is Mary Shelley best known for?
faq.answer: Until the 1970s, she was known primarily for her novel Frankenstein and her efforts to publish her husband's works.

faq.question: Who were Mary Shelley's parents?
faq.answer: Her father was the political philosopher William Godwin, and her mother was the philosopher and women's rights advocate Mary Wollstonecraft.

faq.question: What themes are mainly dealt with in Mary Shelley's works?
faq.answer: Her works often argue that cooperation and sympathy, particularly as practiced by women in the family, are the ways to reform civil society.

mary-shelley.missingDataNote: 메리 셸리의 아버지가 메리 제인 클레어몬트와 결혼한 연도는 원문에 명시되지 않아 생략되었습니다.

timeline.event: Born on August 6 in Ayrshire, Scotland.
Qualified for medicine from St Mary's Hospital Medical School in London.
Discovered the enzyme lysozyme, his first major breakthrough in antibacterial research.
Accidentally discovered the antibiotic substance penicillin in a contaminated mold culture.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine alongside Florey and Chain.
Born on the Caribbean island of Nevis.
Joined George Washington's staff as a trusted aide-de-camp during the Revolutionary War.
Played a pivotal role in drafting the U.S. Constitution and authoring the Federalist Papers.
Appointed as the first U.S. Secretary of the Treasury by President Washington.
Died following a fatal duel with political rival Aaron Burr.
Born on January 17 in Boston, Massachusetts.
Published the first edition of 'Poor Richard's Almanack', which became highly successful.
Conducted his famous kite experiment, proving that lightning is electricity.
Helped draft the Declaration of Independence as part of the Committee of Five.
Negotiated and signed the Treaty of Paris, officially ending the American Revolutionary War.
Born at York House near the Strand in London.
Entered Trinity College, Cambridge.
Started his legal career by enrolling at Gray's Inn.
Returned home after his father's death.
Elected as a Member of Parliament for Bossiney in Cornwall.
Became an outer barrister.
Became a bencher, a senior member of the Inn.
Elected as a Reader, a law lecturer of the Inn.
Became a clerk of the Star Chamber.
Acted as a secret advisor to Robert Devereux, Earl of Essex.
Wrote Observations on a Libel, refuting the Jesuit Robert Parsons.
Elected as a Member of Parliament for Middlesex.
Became Queen's Counsel after being named legal advisor to Elizabeth I.
Arrested for failing to pay off debts.
Participated in the interrogation of the Earl of Essex for treason.
Received a knighthood as a Knight Bachelor.
Married Alice Barnham, daughter of a London alderman.
Published The Advancement of Learning.
Took the post of Solicitor General.
Officially served as a clerk of the Star Chamber.
Became Attorney General.
Prosecuted Robert Carr, Earl of Somerset, and his wife Frances Howard for murder, leading to a guilty verdict.
Sat as a regent for a month.
Appointed Lord Chancellor and became Baron Verulam.
Published Novum Organum.
Elevated to Viscount St Albans.
Impeached by Parliament on charges of bribery and stripped of all public offices and titles.
Died of pneumonia.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Modern Empiricism and Laying the Groundwork for the Scientific Revolution
keyAchievements.description: He researched natural philosophy and led the development of the scientific method, laying the groundwork for the later Scientific Revolution, and is evaluated alongside Descartes as a pioneer of modern philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Advocating Empiricist Philosophy and Writing Novum Organum
keyAchievements.description: He argued for empiricism, stating that the nature of the natural world could be revealed through inductive reasoning, and wrote Novum Organum, which criticized Aristotle's methodology and advocated empiricism.

keyAchievements.title: Publishing The Advancement of Learning
keyAchievements.description: In The Advancement of Learning, he classified the system of knowledge and preached the importance of inductive observation for future pursuits of truth.

keyAchievements.title: Famous Maxim 'Knowledge is Power'
keyAchievements.description: He is well known for the phrase "Knowledge is power" (scientia est potentia).

keyAchievements.title: Proposing a Library Classification System
keyAchievements.description: He patronized libraries and proposed a classification system dividing books into three categories: history, poetry, and philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of the Act of Union
keyAchievements.description: Arguing for the annexation of Scotland, he was evaluated as a pioneer of the Act of Union that later laid the foundation for the creation of the Kingdom of Great Britain.

faq.question: How is Francis Bacon evaluated?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as the founder of modern empiricism and, alongside Descartes, a pioneer of modern philosophy, leading the development of the scientific method to lay the groundwork for the Scientific Revolution.

faq.question: What are Francis Bacon's representative philosophical claims?
faq.answer: He advocated empiricism, claiming that the nature of the natural world could be revealed through inductive reasoning, and proposed a method of arriving at truth through careful observation and experimentation by eliminating preconceptions.

faq.question: What are Francis Bacon's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include Novum Organum, which advocated empiricism, and The Advancement of Learning, which classified the system of knowledge and preached the importance of inductive observation.

faq.question: What famous quote did Francis Bacon leave?
faq.answer: He is well known for the phrase "Knowledge is power" (scientia est potentia).

faq.question: What public offices did Francis Bacon hold?
faq.answer: He served as Attorney General and Lord Chancellor of England during the reign of James I, and at that time, the Lord Chancellor doubled as the Prime Minister.

francis-bacon.missingDataNote: 원문에서 앨리스 번햄과의 결혼 시기가 1604년과 1606년으로 두 차례 언급되어 있으나, '세인트알반스 여자작 엘리스 반햄과 결혼하여 명실상부한 귀족이 되었다'는 구체적인 내용과 함께 제시된 1604년을 채택했습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Weil der Stadt, Holy Roman Empire.
Published 'Mysterium Cosmographicum', his first defense of the Copernican system.
Moved to Prague to work as an assistant to the renowned astronomer Tycho Brahe.
Succeeded Brahe as the Imperial Mathematician to Emperor Rudolf II after Brahe's death.
Published 'Astronomia Nova', containing his first two laws of planetary motion.
Born on May 29 at 83 Beals Street in Brookline, Massachusetts, USA.
Majored in international relations at Harvard University and graduated cum laude with excellent grades.
Began serving in the U.S. House of Representatives.
Began serving in the U.S. Senate.
Defeated Republican candidate Nixon in the presidential election, becoming the youngest elected president in U.S. history.
Served as president starting January 20.
Launched the Peace Corps in March by executive order.
The Bay of Pigs invasion by exiled Cuban rebels ended in failure on April 17.
Established the Military Assistance Command, Vietnam (MACV) in the capital city of Saigon in February, with Paul Harkins as commander.
In September, Congress passed Kennedy's Trade Expansion Act.
The Cuban Missile Crisis occurred in October when the US discovered the Soviet Union installing missiles in Cuba.
Visited West Berlin on June 26 and delivered a speech criticizing communism.
Assassinated by Lee Harvey Oswald in Dallas on November 22.

keyAchievements.title: Securing the Nuclear Test Ban Treaty
keyAchievements.description: Overcame the Berlin Crisis in Europe and the Cuban Missile Crisis in the Caribbean to secure the Nuclear Test Ban Treaty.

keyAchievements.title: Advocating the New Frontier
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the "New Frontier," a new pioneer spirit, in his acceptance speech as the Democratic presidential candidate.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Peace Corps
keyAchievements.description: Launched the Peace Corps by executive order in March 1961, sending thousands of Americans to developing countries to raise their standard of living.

keyAchievements.title: Resolving the Cuban Missile Crisis
keyAchievements.description: Responding to the Soviet Union's missile installation in Cuba, he ordered the Navy to blockade Cuba and resolved the crisis.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening the Policy of Intervention in the Vietnam War
keyAchievements.description: Increased the number of US military advisors stationed in South Vietnam and established the MACV to protect the Ngo Dinh Diem regime.

keyAchievements.title: Attempt to Implement Coinage Power
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to replace the central bank with a new national central bank possessing constitutional coinage power to solidify the foundation of national finances.

faq.question: Which president of the United States was John F. Kennedy?
faq.answer: John F. Kennedy was the 35th President of the United States.

faq.question: When was John F. Kennedy assassinated?
faq.answer: John F. Kennedy was assassinated by Lee Harvey Oswald in Dallas on November 22, 1963.

faq.question: What was one of John F. Kennedy's major policies?
faq.answer: One of his major policies was advocating the "New Frontier," a new pioneer spirit.

faq.question: What was John F. Kennedy's religion?
faq.answer: John F. Kennedy was the only Roman Catholic among the US presidents at the time.

faq.question: What were the major international crises that occurred during John F. Kennedy's presidency?
faq.answer: During his term, many events occurred, including the Bay of Pigs invasion, the Cuban Missile Crisis, and the construction of the Berlin Wall.

faq.question: Is John F. Kennedy the only president to break the Ohio curse?
faq.answer: Yes, he is the only president to break the "Ohio curse" by being elected president without securing Ohio's electoral votes.

timeline.event: Born on May 24 at Kensington Palace in London.
Ascended to the British throne at the age of 18 following the death of King William IV.
Married her first cousin, Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha.
Assumed the title of Empress of India, symbolizing the height of the British Empire.
Died on January 22, ending the Victorian era after a 63-year reign.
Born on April 13 in Shadwell, Virginia.
Drafted the United States Declaration of Independence as the principal author.
Appointed as the first United States Secretary of State under President George Washington.
Inaugurated as the third President of the United States.
Orchestrated the Louisiana Purchase, significantly expanding the territory of the United States.
Born in Gwangseok-dong, Haeju-bu, Hwanghae-do as the eldest son of father Ahn Tae-hun and mother Jo Maria
Married Kim Ah-ryeo and had his first meeting with Kim Gu
His father Ahn Tae-hun converted to Catholicism
Converted to Catholicism at age 19 and received the baptismal name Thomas
Promoted Catholic missionary work and the establishment of a Catholic university
A dispute arose between his father Ahn Tae-hun and Seo Won-hun, a Qing Dynasty physician
Battered Seo Won-hun along with about 10 others including Lee Yong-il
Upon hearing the news of the Eulsa Treaty, he went to Shanghai for the movement to restore national sovereignty, but was refused cooperation and returned; his father Ahn Tae-hun passed away
After moving to Jinnampo, Pyongan-namdo, he managed a coal trading company before liquidating it and starting an education movement by establishing Samheung School

Took over Nampo Donui School and worked as a teacher; responded to the National Debt Repayment Movement and was appointed as the head of the Gwanseo branch of the National Debt Repayment Association; went to Primorsky Krai and joined the righteous army
Went into exile in Manchuria after the dissolution of the Korean Empire's army

Under Jeon Je-deok's command, as the Lieutenant General of the Korean Righteous Army, Special Independent Commander, and Commander of the A-ryeong District, he and Eom In-seop led about 100 subordinates across the Tumen River to infiltrate the country and launch a surprise attack on the Japanese garrison
Formed the Dongui Danjihoe (Society of Cut Fingers) with 11 like-minded comrades and cut off a joint of his left ring finger to make a blood pledge
Left Vladivostok and moved to Harbin
Assassinated Ito Hirobumi in Harbin and was arrested by the Russian army
Sentenced to death at the Kwantung Government-General District Court in Lushun, Kwantung Leased Territory
Met with the Chief Justice of the High Court and raised an objection to being tried as a murderer for his actions as a righteous army commander
Received the sacrament of penance from Father Joseph Wilhelm
Offered his final Mass officiated by Father Wilhelm
His execution was postponed and he left his last requests to his two younger brothers
Executed at 10:00 AM at the age of 32
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Lieutenant General (later revised to the Republic of Korea Medal)

keyAchievements.title: Assassination of Ito Hirobumi
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the anti-Japanese independence movement by assassinating Japanese politician Ito Hirobumi in Harbin on October 26, 1909.

keyAchievements.title: Education Movement
keyAchievements.description: Strived for the movement to restore national sovereignty through education by establishing Samheung School and taking over Nampo Donui School.

keyAchievements.title: Righteous Army Activities
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the righteous army movement after moving to Primorsky Krai in 1907, and engaged in combat with the Japanese army as the Lieutenant General of the Korean Righteous Army in 1908.

keyAchievements.title: Formation of Dongui Danjihoe
keyAchievements.description: Formed the Dongui Danjihoe with 11 comrades in 1909 and cut off his left ring finger to strengthen his will for independence with a blood pledge.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: Left an ideological legacy by writing the unfinished book "On Peace in the East" and the autobiography "The History of Ahn Eung-chil" while in prison.

keyAchievements.title: Leaving Calligraphy
keyAchievements.description: Left many calligraphic works while imprisoned in Lushun Prison, some of which have been designated as national treasures.

faq.question: When and where was Ahn Jung-geun born?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun was born on September 2, 1879, in Gwangseok-dong, Haeju-bu, Hwanghae-do, as the eldest son of father Ahn Tae-hun and mother Jo Maria.

faq.question: Whom did Ahn Jung-geun assassinate?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun assassinated Japanese politician Ito Hirobumi in Harbin on October 26, 1909.

faq.question: Why did Ahn Jung-geun assassinate Ito Hirobumi?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun assassinated Ito Hirobumi because he judged that Ito had committed 15 crimes, including the assassination of Empress Myeongseong, the signing of the Eulsa Treaty, and the dethronement of Emperor Gojong.

faq.question: When was Ahn Jung-geun executed?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun was executed on March 26, 1910, following the assassination of Ito Hirobumi.

faq.question: Where are Ahn Jung-geun's remains?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun's remains were buried in the prisoners' cemetery near Lushun Prison, but their exact location has not been found to this day.

faq.question: What are the major works of Ahn Jung-geun?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun wrote "On Peace in the East" and the autobiography "The History of Ahn Eung-chil" while in prison.

faq.question: What is Ahn Jung-geun's baptismal name?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun's baptismal name is Thomas.

timeline.event: Born in Wenshui County, Tang dynasty China.
Elevated to the position of Empress Consort after Emperor Gaozong deposed Empress Wang.
Became Empress Dowager and the de facto ruler of China following Emperor Gaozong's death.
Proclaimed herself Empress Regnant, establishing the short-lived Wu Zhou dynasty.
Forced to abdicate in a coup, restoring the Tang dynasty shortly before her death.
Born in Majae, Chobu-myeon, Gwangju-bu, Gyeonggi-do.
After his father Jeong Jae-won resigned from his government post and returned to his rural home, he named Jeong Yak-yong's childhood name Gwinong.
Married Pungsan Hong, the daughter of military official Hong Hwa-bo.
His father Jeong Jae-won became the assistant secretary of the Board of Taxation and rented a house in Seoul, where he met Lee Ga-hwan and Lee Seung-hun.
Participated in the Western Learning doctrine training session at Jueosa Temple and Cheonjinam Hermitage in Yangju, Gyeonggi-do, led by Gwon Cheol-sin.
Passed the Jeungwangsi examination celebrating the investiture of the Crown Prince and became a Saengwon (licentiate) through the Hoesi examination.
Heard an explanation of Catholic doctrines from Lee Byeok, the brother-in-law of his eldest brother Jeong Yak-hyeon.
The Myeongnyebang Incident occurred, where the Myeongnyebang community run by Lee Seung-hun was discovered and he was dragged to the Board of Punishments.
King Jeongjo defined Catholicism as an evil cult and issued a ban, and ordered the search and burning of Western Learning books related to Catholicism.
Passed the highest-level state examination at the age of 27 and entered government service.
When the Jinsan Incident occurred, where Yun Ji-chung held his mother's funeral in a Catholic manner in Jinsan, Jeolla-do, he was exiled to Haemi, Seosan, but was released after 11 days.
Participated in the design of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress and utilized the Geojunggi (traditional crane).
His father Jeong Jae-won passed away.
Lectured at Seonggyungwan and patrolled Yeoncheon, Saknyeong, etc. as a secret royal inspector of Gyeonggi-do.
Demoted to Geumjeong Chalbang (station master) due to the Eulmyo Persecution incident.
Due to suspicions raised by Busagwa Bak Jang-seol, Lee Seung-hun was demoted to Yesan, Lee Ga-hwan to Chungju Moksa, and Jeong Yak-yong to Hongju Geumjeong Chalbang.
While serving as the Third Minister of the Board of Punishments, he was impeached by his older brother Jeong Yak-jeon, so he submitted a "Jamyeongso" (petition of self-vindication) and resigned.
While living in retirement in his hometown, he heard the news of King Jeongjo's death and went up to Seoul.
Queen Dowager Jeongsun issued an order suppressing Catholicism and began a purge of the Namin faction.
Arrested and imprisoned, then his sentence was reduced to exile and he was released.
Began 18 years of exile in places like Janggi, Gyeongsang-do, and Gangjin, Jeolla-do.
His son Jeong Nong-jang (Nong-a) died of smallpox.
His exile was lifted and he rose to the position of Seungji (Royal Secretary).
Returned to his hometown.
While in exile in Gangjin-gun, Jeolla-do, he conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality.
Passed away at his home in Mahyeon-ri.
Posthumously promoted to Jeongheon Daebu Gyujanggak Jehak and granted the posthumous title 'Mundo'.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Writing Activities
keyAchievements.description: Known as the person who wrote the most books since the invention of Chinese characters, authoring over 500 volumes.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Mokminsimseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book that details the principles and mindset of local magistrates who govern the people.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Heumheumsinseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book detailing the principles and norms of judgments, punishments, and prison administration based on actual cases he investigated while serving as the magistrate of Goksan.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Gyeongseyupyo"
keyAchievements.description: A book discussing appropriate and standard institutional regulations for state management.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Magwahoetong"
keyAchievements.description: Authored the measles treatment book "Magwahoetong" based on Lee Heon-gil's "Majingibang," saving the lives of countless Joseon people.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Geojunggi
keyAchievements.description: Created the pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) for use during the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of Silhak
keyAchievements.description: Synthesized the Silhak (Practical Learning) ideology handed down through Yu Hyeong-won and Seongho Yi Ik into a comprehensive system.

keyAchievements.title: Conception of Jeongjeon-ron
keyAchievements.description: Conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality through the free distribution of land and communal labor-communal distribution.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure
keyAchievements.description: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012 to commemorate the 250th anniversary of his birth.

faq.question: When was Jeong Yak-yong born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was born on August 5, 1762, and passed away on April 7, 1836.

faq.question: What are the major works of Jeong Yak-yong?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong's three major works are "Mokminsimseo", "Heumheumsinseo", and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: What was Jeong Yak-yong's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was once deeply engrossed in Catholicism but apostatized after the Sinhae Persecution (1791), and actively participated in searching for Catholic believers during the Sinyu Persecution (1801).

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong do during his exile?
faq.answer: During his 18-year exile, Jeong Yak-yong authored extensive writings, including "Mokminsimseo" and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: How did Jeong Yak-yong contribute to the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress?
faq.answer: During the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress, Jeong Yak-yong created a pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) to utilize it.

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong's ideology aim for?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong aimed to establish Silhak of statecraft and practical use, correct distorted theories by returning to the essence of Confucianism of Confucius and Mencius, and build a welfare state that cares for the socially and economically weak.

faq.question: Was Jeong Yak-yong selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure?
faq.answer: Yes, Jeong Yak-yong was selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012, marking the 250th anniversary of his birth.

timeline.event: Born (estimated)
Died (estimated)

keyAchievements.title: Development of Confucian Thought
keyAchievements.description: Developed Confucius's thought and greatly influenced the transmission of Confucianism to future generations.

keyAchievements.title: The Book "Mencius"
keyAchievements.description: The book "Mencius" contains Mencius's thoughts, and it is believed to have been compiled by his disciples.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Innate Human Goodness
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the theory of innate human goodness, arguing that human nature is inherently good and that the human mind is equipped with the "Four Beginnings" of the four virtues: benevolence, righteousness, propriety, and wisdom.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Benevolent Government and Royal Politics
keyAchievements.description: After the age of 40, he traveled across the world advocating benevolent government and royal politics.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Four Beginnings
keyAchievements.description: Explained that the human mind contains the Four Beginnings: a feeling of compassion, a feeling of shame and dislike, a feeling of deference and compliance, and a feeling of right and wrong.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Five Relationships
keyAchievements.description: Presented the Theory of the Five Relationships, organizing five human relationships: intimacy between father and son, righteousness between ruler and minister, distinction between husband and wife, order between elder and junior, and trust between friends.

keyAchievements.title: Confirmation of the Right of Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Confirmed the right of revolution, stating that a monarch who betrays the will of the people and violates benevolence and righteousness is nothing but an ordinary fellow, and subduing such a monarch is not an act of regicide.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Cyclical View of History
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to prove the legitimacy of Confucius based on the cyclical view of history that a sage appears every 500 years.

faq.question: Is the story of Mencius's mother moving three times true?
faq.answer: The story of Mencius's mother moving three times is an anecdote from the "Biographies of Exemplary Women" written by the Han Dynasty scholar Liu Xiang, and the general consensus is that it is hard to view it as a historical fact.

faq.question: From whom did Mencius learn?
faq.answer: Mencius studied the Six Classics under the tutelage of Zisi, the grandson of Confucius.

faq.question: What was the name of Mencius's mother?
faq.answer: The name of Mencius's mother is Ji (伋).

faq.question: Who compiled the book "Mencius"?
faq.answer: There is a theory that the book "Mencius" was written in his later years, but it is more accurate to view it as having been compiled by his disciples.

timeline.event: Born
Became a Langzhong (palace attendant) and traveled to regions such as Jiangnan, Shandong, and Henan while accompanying Emperor Wu
Before his father Sima Tan died of illness, he was asked to complete the compilation of the history book
Succeeded his father and became the Grand Astrologer (Taishiling)
Revised the calendar and completed the Taichu Calendar
Incurred the wrath of Emperor Wu while defending General Li Ling and was sentenced to castration
Died

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian)
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a 130-volume history book that chronicled the history from the Yellow Emperor, the first sovereign of China, to Emperor Wu, organized by prominent figures.

keyAchievements.title: Revision of the Taichu Calendar
keyAchievements.description: Revised the calendar in 104 BC and completed the Taichu Calendar.

faq.question: Who is Sima Qian?
faq.answer: A historian of the Former Han Dynasty in China, the author of the "Shiji" (Records of the Grand Historian), and considered one of the greatest historians of the East.

faq.question: Why was Sima Qian castrated?
faq.answer: In 99 BC, when General Li Ling was defeated by the Xiongnu, Sima Qian defended Li Ling, incurring the wrath of Emperor Wu and resulting in his castration.

faq.question: What are the major works of Sima Qian?
faq.answer: His representative work is the "Shiji" (Records of the Grand Historian), and other works include "Letter to Ren An" and "Lament for Not Meeting One's Time".

faq.question: What government positions did Sima Qian hold?
faq.answer: He succeeded his father as the Grand Astrologer (Taishiling), and after undergoing castration, he was appointed as the Palace Secretary (Zhongshuling).

timeline.event: Died

keyAchievements.title: Succession and Development of Daoist Thought
keyAchievements.description: Succeeded and developed Laozi's thought, becoming a representative figure of Daoism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Myriad Things Monism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the monism of all things.

keyAchievements.title: Argument for Return to Nature and Nothingness
keyAchievements.description: Inheriting Laozi's thoughts, he argued for a return to nature and a return to nothingness.

keyAchievements.title: Pursuit of Individual Peace of Mind
keyAchievements.description: Unlike Laozi, he devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Philosophy for an Ideal Life
keyAchievements.description: He viewed the ideal life as one that discards the physical and mental roots of anxiety, reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility' to achieve freedom and independence.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Epistemology
keyAchievements.description: Established an epistemology that conceptual recognition and value judgments are meaningless, and that only thorough ignorance is correct.

faq.question: What was Zhuangzi's real name?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi's real name was Zhou (周).

faq.question: Whose thoughts did Zhuangzi succeed and develop?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi succeeded and developed the thoughts of Laozi.

faq.question: What government position did Zhuangzi hold?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi became an official of the lacquer tree garden in Meng.

faq.question: Did Zhuangzi accept the offer of King Wei of Chu?
faq.answer: King Wei of Chu requested him to assist in politics, but Zhuangzi refused.

faq.question: What is Zhuangzi's ideal life?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi viewed the ideal life as discarding the physical and mental roots of anxiety and reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility'. Through this, he argued, one could follow the laws of nature and attain freedom and independence without being infringed upon by anything.

faq.question: How do Zhuangzi's thoughts differ from Laozi's?
faq.answer: While Laozi had some interest in the reality of politics and society, Zhuangzi devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

zhuangzi.missingDataNote: 장자의 출생 연도(기원전 369년?)는 불확실하며, 관직 생활이나 초나라 위왕의 제안 거절 등 주요 생애 사건들의 구체적인 연도가 원문에 명시되어 있지 않습니다. 또한, 장자의 저작 목록이 원문에 제공되지 않았습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Yangdu County, Langya Commandery during the late Eastern Han dynasty.
Recruited by the warlord Liu Bei after the famous 'three visits to the thatched cottage'.
Played a critical diplomatic and strategic role leading to the allied victory at the Battle of Red Cliffs.
Appointed Chancellor (Prime Minister) of the newly established state of Shu Han.
Died of illness at the Wuzhang Plains during his final Northern Expedition against Wei.
Period of living as the 3rd Mauryan Emperor of Magadha
Possibility of birth
Possibility of accession to the throne
Conquest of the Kalinga region
Record of visiting Sambodhi (the sacred Bodhi tree of Bodh Gaya)
Became closer to the Buddhist Sangha and toured the empire
Began inscribing edicts to spread Dharma
Established the position of Dhamma-Mahamatra
Commissioned the expansion of the Stupa of Buddha Kanakamuni
Donated the Khalatika Cave

keyAchievements.title: Ruling the Empire
keyAchievements.description: As the 3rd Mauryan Emperor of Magadha, he ruled an empire that covered the largest part of the Indian subcontinent.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage and Spread of Buddhism
keyAchievements.description: As a patron of Buddhism, he played a crucial role in spreading Buddhism throughout ancient Asia.

keyAchievements.title: Spread of Dhamma
keyAchievements.description: After the conquest of Kalinga, he dedicated himself to the spread of 'Dhamma', or righteous behavior.

keyAchievements.title: Construction and Support of Buddhist Facilities
keyAchievements.description: Buddhist legends praise him for building a large number of stupas, patronizing the Third Buddhist Council, supporting Buddhist missionaries, and making generous donations to the Sangha.

keyAchievements.title: Historical Stature in India
keyAchievements.description: Since the decipherment of the Brahmi script in the 19th century, he has been renowned as one of India's greatest emperors.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Modern India
keyAchievements.description: The emblem of the Republic of India is an adaptation of the Lion Capital of Ashoka, and the Ashoka Chakra, the wheel of Ashoka, is depicted in the center of the Indian flag.

keyAchievements.title: Social Welfare Activities
keyAchievements.description: Carried out social welfare activities such as establishing medical facilities for humans and animals, cultivating medicinal herbs, digging wells, and planting trees along roads.

keyAchievements.title: Promotion of Animal Welfare
keyAchievements.description: Promoted animal welfare by abolishing the death penalty, banning the slaughter of animals for sacrifice, and abolishing royal hunts.

faq.question: What does Ashoka's name mean?
faq.answer: 'A-shoka' literally means 'without sorrow'.

faq.question: What were Ashoka's main titles?
faq.answer: His main titles were 'Priyadasi' ('he who regards amiably' or 'of gracious mien') and 'Devanampiya' ('beloved of the gods').

faq.question: Was Ashoka a Buddhist?
faq.answer: In his Minor Rock Edict 1, he clearly identified himself as a Buddhist by calling himself an upasaka (Buddhist lay follower) and a Shakya (Buddhist).

faq.question: Did Ashoka persecute other religions?
faq.answer: His edicts emphasize tolerance for all sects, donating caves to non-Buddhist ascetics and stating that both Brahmins and Shramanas deserve respect.

faq.question: What did 'Dhamma' mean to Ashoka?
faq.answer: To Ashoka, Dhamma meant a moral policy encompassing active social concern, religious tolerance, ecological awareness, observance of common ethical precepts, and the renunciation of war.

faq.question: Was Ashoka a violent person before his conversion to Buddhism?
faq.answer: Both the Sri Lankan and North Indian traditions claim that Ashoka was a violent person before embracing Buddhism.

faq.question: How did the Kalinga War affect Ashoka?
faq.answer: The destruction that occurred during the war evoked remorse for violence in him, and in the years that followed, he was drawn to Buddhism.

faq.question: What efforts did Ashoka make for animal welfare?
faq.answer: He promoted animal welfare by abolishing the death penalty, banning the slaughter of animals for sacrifice, abolishing royal hunts, and establishing animal hospitals.

timeline.event: Born as Yi San, the son of Crown Prince Sado, during the Joseon Dynasty.
Ascended the throne as the 22nd monarch of the Joseon Dynasty.
Established the Kyujanggak royal library to promote cultural and political reform.
Began the construction of the Hwaseong Fortress in Suwon to move the royal court.
Died suddenly, leaving his ambitious reform plans incomplete.
Born during the Tang Dynasty era.
In his hometown of Shu, he practiced Daoism while climbing the mountains of Sichuan with a hermit named Dongyanzi, and later left his hometown to travel around Jiangnan.
At the age of 43, he received an edict from Emperor Xuanzong and stayed in Chang'an as a Hanlin Gongfeng (academician).
Feeling bored with his life as an official, he argued with Gao Lishi, a close aide to Emperor Xuanzong, and resigned.
Returned to Jiangnan.
Joined the army of Prince Yong Lin, the sixteenth son of Emperor Xuanzong, but Prince Yong's army was designated as a rebel force, leading to his exile to Yelang (present-day Guizhou).
Died at the age of 61 at the home of his relative Li Yangbing, who was the magistrate of Dangtu County in Anhui Province.

keyAchievements.title: Great Poet in Chinese History
keyAchievements.description: A poet of the Tang Dynasty era, he is considered one of the greatest poets in Chinese history along with Du Fu.

keyAchievements.title: Poet Immortal (Shixian)
keyAchievements.description: Li Bai was called the 'Poet Immortal', and his poetic talent was evaluated as 'genius'.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Poet
keyAchievements.description: Currently, about 1,100 of his poems survive, and his poetic literature themed around Daoism and wine is famous.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Gushi (Ancient Style Poetry) and Jueju (Quatrains)
keyAchievements.description: He specialized in gushi and jueju, and his jueju were evaluated as 'divine works'.

keyAchievements.title: Free and Fantastic Poetic Style
keyAchievements.description: His poems are grand in scale and full of vitality, sometimes featuring fantastic and extremely free characteristics.

faq.question: What were Li Bai's courtesy name and pseudonym?
faq.answer: Li Bai's courtesy name was Taibai, and his pseudonym was Qinglian Jushi (Householder of the Azure Lotus).

faq.question: By what nickname was Li Bai called?
faq.answer: Considered one of the greatest poets in Chinese history, Li Bai was called the 'Poet Immortal (Shixian)'.

faq.question: How many of Li Bai's poems survive?
faq.answer: Currently, about 1,100 of his poems survive.

faq.question: What are the main themes in Li Bai's poetry?
faq.answer: The main themes related to his poetic literature are Daoism and wine.

faq.question: Where was Li Bai from?
faq.answer: Currently, it is widely accepted that he was an Central Asian from Suyab of the Tang Dynasty.

faq.question: Did Li Bai receive formal education?
faq.answer: Because his father was a merchant, Li Bai did not receive formal education.

faq.question: When did Li Bai die?
faq.answer: Li Bai died in 762 at the age of 61 at the home of his relative Li Yangbing, who was the magistrate of Dangtu County in Anhui Province.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Li Bai's poetry?
faq.answer: His poems are grand in scale and full of vitality, sometimes featuring fantastic and extremely free characteristics.

li-bai.missingDataNote: 사직 후 두보와의 여행, 유배 중 은사(恩赦)를 받은 시점은 원문에 특정 연도나 나이가 명시되어 있지 않아 타임라인에서 생략되었습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Harima Province, Japan, during the tumultuous Sengoku period.
Fought in the pivotal Battle of Sekigahara, likely on the losing side of the Western Army.
Defeated his renowned rival Sasaki Kojiro in a legendary duel on Ganryujima island.
Retreated to Reigando cave to reflect on his life and perfect his philosophy.
Completed his philosophical martial arts masterpiece, 'The Book of Five Rings', shortly before his death.
Born in Owari Province, the heir to the Oda clan.
Defeated the much larger forces of Imagawa Yoshimoto at the Battle of Okehazama.
Marched into Kyoto and installed Ashikaga Yoshiaki as a puppet shogun, establishing control over the capital.
Revolutionized Japanese warfare by utilizing firearms to decisively win the Battle of Nagashino.
Betrayed by his general Akechi Mitsuhide and forced to commit seppuku at the Honno-ji incident.
Born on November 12 in Xiangshan County, Guangdong Province.
Founded the Revive China Society in Honolulu to push for the overthrow of the Qing dynasty.
The Wuchang Uprising succeeded, leading to the collapse of the imperial Qing dynasty.
Served briefly as the first Provisional President of the newly declared Republic of China.
Died of liver cancer in Beijing, leaving behind his unfulfilled 'Three Principles of the People'.
Born as Matsudaira Takechiyo in Mikawa Province.
Gained independence for his clan following the death of Imagawa Yoshimoto at Okehazama.
Secured supremacy over Japan by leading the Eastern Army to victory at the Battle of Sekigahara.
Appointed Shogun by the Emperor, officially establishing the Tokugawa Shogunate.
Eliminated the Toyotomi clan at the Siege of Osaka, finalizing his absolute control over Japan.
Born as the son of a poor peasant in Nakamura, Aichi District, Owari Province (February 6, Tenbun 6, lunar calendar).
Began working as a servant for Oda Nobunaga.
Married Nene, the adopted daughter of Asano Nagakatsu (Eiroku 4).

Played an active role in the capture of Mitsukuri Castle in Omi, and when Oda Nobunaga installed Ashikaga Yoshiaki as Shogun, he managed political affairs in Kyoto alongside Akechi Mitsuhide and Niwa Nagahide (September, Eiroku 11, lunar calendar).
Joined the subjugation of Asakura Yoshikage of Echizen Province, and distinguished himself by commanding the rearguard at Kanegasaki (Genki 1).
After the annihilation of the Azai clan, he was granted three districts in Omi, took up his post as the lord of Nagahama Castle, and changed his surname to Hashiba (Tensho 1).
Returned without permission while being dispatched to rescue Shibata Katsuie, and subjugated Matsunaga Hisahide of Yamato Province (Battle of Shigisan Castle) (Tensho 4).
Subjugated Ukita Naoie, a daimyo of Bizen and Mimasaka Provinces (Tensho 7).
Attacked and received the surrender of Bessho Nagaharu, the lord of Miki Castle in Harima (Battle of Miki). Captured Arikoyama Castle of Yamana Akihiro of Tajima Province (Tensho 8).
Captured Tottori Castle (Battle of Tottori Castle). Placed Awaji Province under his control by capturing Iwaya Castle (Tensho 9).
Requested reinforcements from Oda Nobunaga during the water attack on Takamatsu Castle in Bitchu (Tensho 10).
Received the news that his lord Oda Nobunaga had committed suicide in the Honno-ji Incident (June 2, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Defeated Akechi Mitsuhide at the Battle of Yamazaki (June 13, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
At the Kiyosu Conference, Oda Nobunaga's legitimate grandson Sanboshi was decided as the successor (June 27, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Held Oda Nobunaga's funeral with his adopted son Hashiba Hidekatsu as the chief mourner (October 15, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Conflict with Shibata Katsuie intensified, leading him to besiege Nagahama Castle, capture Kajiki Castle, and receive Sanboshi from Oda Nobutaka (Tensho 11).
Defeated Shibata Katsuie at the Battle of Shizugatake (April 21, Tensho 11, lunar calendar).
Began the construction of Osaka Castle on the site of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji (Tensho 11).
Conflicted with Oda Nobukatsu, and Tokugawa Ieyasu sided with Nobukatsu (Battle of Komaki and Nagakute) (Tensho 12).
Ikeda Tsuneoki, his son Motosuke, and Mori Nagayoshi were killed in action at the Battle of Nagakute (April 9, Tensho 12, lunar calendar).
Made peace with Oda Nobukatsu (November 11, Tensho 12, lunar calendar).
Appointed to the court rank of Senior Second Rank and the office of Palace Minister (Naidaijin) (March 10, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Executed the conquest of Kishu (March 21, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Executed the conquest of Shikoku (July 25, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Ascended to the position of Kampaku (Imperial Regent) (July 11, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Sassa Narimasa of Etchu surrendered to Hideyoshi (August 25, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Forced his younger sister Asahi to divorce and married her off to Ieyasu, and sent his mother Omandokoro as a hostage to Ieyasu (Tensho 14).
Was granted the surname Toyotomi by the Imperial Court (September 9, Tensho 14, lunar calendar).
Ascended to the position of Daijo-daijin (Chancellor of the Realm) and established his regime (December 25, Tensho 14, lunar calendar).
Fought and was defeated by the Shimazu army led by Shimazu Iehisa at the Hetsugi River in Bungo (Battle of Hetsugigawa) (Tensho 14).
Led a large army of 200,000 to conquer Kyushu, resulting in the surrender of the Shimazu clan (Kyushu Campaign) (Tensho 15).
Issued the Bateren Edict expelling Christian missionaries (June, Tensho 15).
Implemented the Sword Hunt (Katanagari) (Tensho 16).
Tsurumatsu was born to his concubine Yodo-dono and was designated as his successor (Tensho 17).
Led a large army of 210,000 to dispatch troops to Kanto to suppress the Go-Hojo clan, and besieged Odawara Castle (Siege of Odawara) (Tensho 18).

After the death from illness of Tsurumatsu, whom he had designated as his successor, he adopted his nephew Hidetsugu, transferred the position of Kampaku to him, and was himself called Taiko (Tensho 19).
Ordered the tea master Sen no Rikyu to commit seppuku (Tensho 19).
Suppressed the rebellion led by Kunohe Masazane in the Tohoku region (Tensho 19).
The Imjin War (Japanese invasions of Korea) began (Bunroku 1).
Peace negotiations with Ming China began. His concubine Yodo-dono gave birth to Hideyori (Bunroku 2).
Disinherited Kampaku Toyotomi Hidetsugu, exiled him to Mount Koya, and later forced him to commit seppuku on charges of treason (Bunroku 4).
Peace negotiations with Ming China broke down (Bunroku 5).
Re-invaded Joseon Korea with an army of 140,000 (Jeongyu War) (Keicho 2).
Died at Fushimi Castle (August 18, Keicho 3, lunar calendar).

keyAchievements.title: Three Great Unifiers of the Sengoku Period
keyAchievements.description: Along with Oda Nobunaga and Tokugawa Ieyasu, he is known as one of the Three Great Unifiers of the Sengoku period.

keyAchievements.title: Succeeding Oda Nobunaga's Enterprise
keyAchievements.description: After the Honno-ji Incident, he defeated Akechi Mitsuhide and succeeded Oda Nobunaga's enterprise.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Japan
keyAchievements.description: Unified Japan by subduing the Go-Hojo clan and brought an end to the long-lasting Sengoku period.

keyAchievements.title: Appointment as Kampaku and Daijo-daijin
keyAchievements.description: Established his regime by being appointed Kampaku and Daijo-daijin, and was granted the surname Toyotomi by the Imperial Court.

keyAchievements.title: Taiko Land Survey and Sword Hunt
keyAchievements.description: Stabilized the domestic situation in Japan by implementing the Taiko Land Survey (Taikou Kenchi) and the Sword Hunt (Katanagari) policies.

keyAchievements.title: Rise from the Lowest Class to Ruler
keyAchievements.description: Ascended to become the ruler of Japan despite being a person from the lowest social class in the country.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of Osaka Castle
keyAchievements.description: Constructed Osaka Castle on the site of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji.

keyAchievements.title: Leading the Imjin War
keyAchievements.description: He was the main instigator who led the Imjin War in the first year of Bunroku (1592) and invaded Joseon Korea.

faq.question: What was Toyotomi Hideyoshi's background?
faq.answer: He is known to have been born as the son of a poor peasant in Nakamura, Aichi District, Owari Province. Because of his very low social status, he was conscious of his humble origins, leading to suspicions that records from his time and later periods were often distorted or glorified.

faq.question: Why couldn't Toyotomi Hideyoshi become a Shogun?
faq.answer: Coming from a commoner background and not being of the Minamoto bloodline, he could not become a Shogun due to the rule that 'only a Minamoto can become a Shogun'. Instead, he rose to the positions of Kampaku and Daijo-daijin.

faq.question: What were the main reasons Toyotomi Hideyoshi started the Imjin War?
faq.answer: General analysis suggests that he sought to acquire the justification to become Shogun by recovering the southern part of Joseon, which he claimed as former territory, and annihilating the Ming army. Furthermore, there is a theory that he intended to solidify the unstable vassal relationships by granting new territories to the samurai.

faq.question: What were Toyotomi Hideyoshi's physical characteristics?
faq.answer: He looked like a monkey and was called 'little monkey'. According to the records of the missionary Luis Frois, he was short, had an ugly face, and was polydactylous, having six fingers on his right hand.

faq.question: How was Toyotomi Hideyoshi's death handled?
faq.answer: He died at Fushimi Castle in the third year of Keicho (1598). Due to concerns about the army's morale, no funeral was held, and his death was kept a secret. His tomb was blown up and destroyed after the Battle of Sekigahara.

timeline.event: Born as Prince Chulalongkorn, the son of King Mongkut of Siam.
Ascended the throne as King Rama V at the age of 15 following his father's death.
Initiated the gradual abolition of slavery and the corvée labor system in Siam.
Navigated the perilous Franco-Siamese War, yielding territory to preserve Siam's independence.
Died on October 23, remembered as a great modernizing monarch who kept Siam uncolonized.
Born in Carthage (modern-day Tunisia) into a prominent military family.
Sparked the Second Punic War and famously crossed the Alps with war elephants to invade Italy.
Achieved one of history's greatest tactical victories against Rome at the Battle of Cannae.
Defeated by the Roman general Scipio Africanus at the decisive Battle of Zama.
Committed suicide by poison in Bithynia to avoid being captured and extradited to Rome.
Born into a family of Ulama (Islamic scholars) in Tangier, Morocco.
Left his hometown at the age of 21 and embarked on the Hajj, a pilgrimage to Mecca.
Arrived in Alexandria.
After spending a month in Mecca, he joined a caravan returning to Iraq.
Arrived in Baghdad.
Left Baghdad and headed north along the Tigris River.
Arrived in Aden.
Visited Sultan Al-Hasan ibn Sulaiman of Kilwa.
Visited Mogadishu.
Decided to seek employment at the court of Muhammad bin Tughluq, the ruler of the Delhi Sultanate, and joined a caravan departing from Anatolia towards Delhi.
Arrived in Constantinople and had an audience with Andronikos III Palaiologos.
Arrived at the Samudera Pasai Sultanate located in the Aceh region of northern Sumatra.
Left Guangzhou and embarked on his journey back home.
Arrived in Damascus and heard the news that his father had died 15 years earlier.
When Alfonso XI of Castile attacked the Tangier region, he rallied with other Muslims to defend the port.
Left Fez and headed to Sijilmasa.
Arrived in Taghaza with a caravan.
Joined a caravan leaving Mali and headed to Timbuktu.
Returned to Sijilmasa.
Returned to Morocco.
Dictated his travelogue to Ibn Juzayy by the order of the Sultan, and Ibn Juzayy compiled and wrote the "Rihla of Ibn Battuta".
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Authoring the Famous Travelogue (Rihla)
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'Rihla', a famous travelogue that documented the medieval Islamic world and extensive regions beyond.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Travels
keyAchievements.description: Traveled extensively over 120,000 km across contemporary Islamic countries, China, Sumatra, etc., leaving behind records of great cultural anthropological value.

keyAchievements.title: Appointment as Judge of the Delhi Sultanate
keyAchievements.description: Was appointed as a judge (Qadi) by Sultan Muhammad bin Tughluq of the Delhi Sultanate and served in that role.

faq.question: When and where was Ibn Battuta born?
faq.answer: Ibn Battuta was born on February 24, 1304, into a family of Ulama (Islamic scholars) in Tangier, Morocco.

faq.question: What value does Ibn Battuta's travelogue hold?
faq.answer: His travelogue describes an extensive journey of 120,000 km across almost all contemporary Islamic countries, China, and down to Sumatra, holding great cultural anthropological value.

faq.question: What professions did Ibn Battuta have?
faq.answer: He was a traveler, explorer, and author of the famous travelogue (Rihla). He also served as an appointed judge (Qadi) in the Delhi Sultanate and lived as a judge in Morocco during his later years.

faq.question: Who wrote Ibn Battuta's travelogue?
faq.answer: By the order of the Sultan, Ibn Battuta dictated his travelogue to Ibn Juzayy, who compiled and wrote the "Rihla of Ibn Battuta".

timeline.event: Born into the royal family of the Khmer Empire.
Witnessed the devastating sack of the Khmer capital, Angkor, by the neighboring Champa kingdom.
Successfully expelled the Cham invaders and was crowned King of the Khmer Empire.
Commissioned the construction of Ta Prohm temple in honor of his mother.
Died after completing massive architectural projects, including Angkor Thom and the Bayon temple.
Ascended to the throne as the Mansa of the Mali Empire and began his rule.
A visitor to Mali recorded the safety of the Mali Empire and the king's strict law enforcement.
Ended his rule of the Mali Empire.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Building the Golden Age of the Mali Empire
keyAchievements.description: Created a golden age while ruling the Mali Empire from 1312 to 1332.

keyAchievements.title: Pilgrimage to Mecca and Charitable Activities
keyAchievements.description: During his pilgrimage to Mecca, he took 12,000 slaves, 800 wives, and 11 tons of gold, distributing the gold to the poor along the way.

keyAchievements.title: Inviting Scholars and Construction Projects
