keyAchievements.description: Brought back many scholars upon his return and had an architect from Spain build a royal palace and mosques in the capital, Timbuktu.

faq.question: When did Mansa Musa rule the Mali Empire?
faq.answer: Mansa Musa ruled the Mali Empire from 1312 to 1332.

faq.question: How was Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca?
faq.answer: During his pilgrimage to Mecca, he was accompanied by 12,000 slaves, 800 wives, and 11 tons of gold, and he distributed the gold to the poor along the way.

faq.question: How was the Mali Empire during Mansa Musa's era evaluated?
faq.answer: According to a visitor's record in 1325, the Mali Empire was a country where unjust actions were rare, and travelers could travel safely due to the king's strict law enforcement.

timeline.event: Born near present-day Melmoth, KwaZulu-Natal, as the illegitimate son of Zulu Chief Senzangakhona.
Took the Zulu throne following the death of his father, with the backing of the Mthethwa paramount chief Dingiswayo.
After Dingiswayo's death, Shaka defeated the rival Ndwandwe clan at the Battle of Gqokli Hill, establishing Zulu dominance.
His mother, Nandi, died, triggering a period of extreme and brutal mourning enforced by Shaka.
Assassinated by his half-brothers Dingane and Mhlangana at his capital, kwaDukuza.
Assassinated by his half-brothers

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Zulu Empire
keyAchievements.description: Transformed the small Zulu clan into a massive, militaristic empire that dominated southern Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Military Innovations
keyAchievements.description: Revolutionized regional warfare by introducing the iklwa (a short stabbing spear), the large cowhide shield, and the highly effective 'buffalo horns' formation.

keyAchievements.title: Social Integration
keyAchievements.description: Integrated conquered tribes into the Zulu nation, replacing traditional tribal loyalties with loyalty to the king and the age-grade military regiments (amabutho).

keyAchievements.title: Development of the 'bull horn' tactic
keyAchievements.description: Developed the 'bull horn' formation tactic that annihilated Zwide's army at the Battle of Gqokli Hill.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of hegemony based on military power
keyAchievements.description: Expanded his hegemony by defeating rivals and incorporating their scattered remnants into his own army based on military power.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Nguni shield
keyAchievements.description: It is believed that he introduced a larger and heavier version of the Nguni shield.

keyAchievements.title: Training in shield tactics
keyAchievements.description: Believed to have taught warriors the tactic of using the left side of their shield to hook the enemy's shield to the right, exposing their ribs.

keyAchievements.title: Forced march training
keyAchievements.description: Frequently subjected his troops to forced marches across hot, rocky terrain at a rapid pace.

keyAchievements.title: Youth logistical support system
keyAchievements.description: Recruited boys over the age of six as apprentice warriors (udibi) to handle logistical support such as carrying food, pots, mats, and spare weapons.

keyAchievements.title: Organization of age-grade regiments
keyAchievements.description: Organized different age groups into regiments, stationed them in special military kraals, and gave them unique names and insignia.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of military discipline
keyAchievements.description: Instilled ruthless determination in his army by drilling into them that a cruel fate awaited those who lost courage or were defeated in battle.

keyAchievements.title: Incorporation of conquered tribes
keyAchievements.description: Incorporated the remnants of conquered tribes into his army and used them to conquer other tribes.

faq.question: What is the 'Mfecane'?
faq.answer: The Mfecane (The Crushing) refers to a period of widespread chaos and warfare among indigenous ethnic communities in southern Africa in the early 19th century. While Shaka's expansions contributed to this, modern historians debate the extent to which he was the primary cause versus other factors like drought and colonial pressures.

faq.question: Was Shaka as brutal as he is often portrayed?
faq.answer: He was a brilliant but ruthless leader. Much of his 'bloodthirsty' reputation was exaggerated by early European writers to justify colonialism. However, his harsh discipline and the mass executions following his mother's death demonstrate he could be extremely cruel.

faq.question: What was the 'buffalo horns' formation?
faq.answer: It was a military tactic Shaka perfected. It consisted of a strong center (the 'chest') to pin the enemy, while two flanking wings (the 'horns') encircled them, and a reserve force (the 'loins') waited behind to pursue fleeing enemies or reinforce weak spots.

faq.question: How did Shaka Zulu die?
faq.answer: He was assassinated in September 1828 by his half-brothers Dingane kaSenzangakhona and Mhlangana, and Mbopa kaSithayi.

faq.question: What was Shaka Zulu's role in the Mfecane?
faq.answer: Shaka's reign coincided with the Mfecane period, and although his military expansion caused massive migrations and turmoil, his exact role remains controversial.

timeline.event: Born in Baghdad (estimated)
Wrote the book 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals'
Wrote his major work 'al-Kitab al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr wa'l-muqabala' (The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing)
Death (estimated)
His book on arithmetic was translated into Latin
His achievements spread to Europe through Latin translations
The book 'The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing' was translated into Latin

keyAchievements.title: Algebraic work 'The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the book that introduced the general solution of linear and quadratic equations, considered the origin of modern algebra.

keyAchievements.title: Arithmetic work 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the book that played a major role in introducing Hindu-Arabic numerals and arithmetic to the Western world.

keyAchievements.title: Astronomical tables 'Zij al-Sindhind'
keyAchievements.description: Created astronomical tables including calendars, astronomical calculations, and sine and cosine trigonometric functions.

keyAchievements.title: Geographical work 'The Image of the Earth'
keyAchievements.description: Revised Ptolemy's Geography and wrote a book containing the coordinates and geographical features of 2402 cities.

keyAchievements.title: Study of mechanical devices
keyAchievements.description: Studied mechanical devices such as the astrolabe and sundial.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in mapmaking and measuring the Earth's circumference
keyAchievements.description: Assisted in mapmaking for Caliph al-Ma'mun and participated in the project to measure the circumference of the Earth.

faq.question: What is Al-Khwarizmi known for?
faq.answer: He was a Persian mathematician who established the four fundamental operations of arithmetic using Arabic numerals derived from India, utilized the concept of zero and positional value, and is known as the 'father of algebra'.

faq.question: How did the terms 'algorithm' and 'algebra' originate?
faq.answer: 'Algorithm' derived from his name, and 'algebra' originated from the title of his book <al-jabr wa al-muqabala>.

faq.question: What are Al-Khwarizmi's major works, and what do they contain?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing', which covers solutions for linear and quadratic equations, 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals', which introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals to the West, and 'The Image of the Earth', a revision of Ptolemy's Geography.

timeline.event: Born on April 14 into a Dalit (untouchable) family in Mhow, British India.
Called to the Bar in London and began his lifelong campaign against social discrimination in India.
Appointed as the first Minister of Law and Justice of independent India.
Successfully steered the drafting of the Constitution of India, which formally abolished untouchability.
Converted to Buddhism along with hundreds of thousands of followers shortly before his death.
Born in ancient India, though his exact origins remain a subject of historical debate.
Overthrew the Nanda dynasty and established the Mauryan Empire with the help of his mentor Chanakya.
Defeated Seleucus I Nicator, a former general of Alexander the Great, expanding his empire westward.
Abdicated the throne in favor of his son Bindusara and became a Jain ascetic.
Fasted to death (Sallekhana) at Shravanabelagola in accordance with Jain spiritual practices.
Born on May 18 in Nishapur, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Wrote the influential 'Treatise on Demonstration of Problems of Algebra'.
Invited by the Seljuk Sultan Malik-Shah I to build an observatory in Isfahan.
Finalized the Jalali calendar, an exceptionally accurate solar calendar.
Died in Nishapur, leaving behind a profound legacy in mathematics, astronomy, and poetry (the Rubaiyat).
Born to a wealthy Jewish family in New York
Graduated summa cum laude in chemistry from Harvard University
Studied abroad at the Cavendish Laboratory, University of Cambridge, UK
Studied theoretical physics at the University of Göttingen, Germany, and authored 16 papers
Began teaching at the University of California, Berkeley
Married Katherine Harrison, the former wife of a Communist Party member
Directed the Manhattan Project while serving as director of the Los Alamos Laboratory
Removed from all public office after opposing the development of the hydrogen bomb
Elected as a Foreign Member of the Royal Society of London
Diagnosed with head and neck cancer
Died in his sleep at his home in Princeton at the age of 62

keyAchievements.title: Led the Manhattan Project
keyAchievements.description: During World War II, as the director of the Los Alamos National Laboratory, he led the Manhattan Project to develop the atomic bomb.

keyAchievements.title: Research and teaching in theoretical physics
keyAchievements.description: Researched theoretical physics and published numerous papers at the University of Göttingen, Germany, and served as a popular professor at the University of California, Berkeley.

keyAchievements.title: Elected as a Foreign Member of the Royal Society
keyAchievements.description: Elected as a Foreign Member of the Royal Society of London in 1962.

faq.question: When was Robert Oppenheimer born?
faq.answer: Robert Oppenheimer was born on April 22, 1904, in New York.

faq.question: What is Robert Oppenheimer's most well-known achievement?
faq.answer: During World War II, as the director of the Los Alamos National Laboratory, he led the Manhattan Project to develop the atomic bomb.

faq.question: Why was Robert Oppenheimer removed from public office?
faq.answer: In 1950, after opposing the development of the hydrogen bomb, he was stripped of all public offices.

faq.question: What languages did Robert Oppenheimer learn?
faq.answer: He learned Italian to read Dante's <Divine Comedy>, Sanskrit to read Indian philosophical texts, and mastered Greek during his high school years.

timeline.event: Born in Tikrit, modern-day Iraq, into a prominent Kurdish family.
Appointed vizier of the Fatimid Caliphate in Egypt, consolidating his political power.
Founded the Ayyubid dynasty and began expanding his territorial control over Egypt and Syria.
Decisively defeated the Crusader army at the Battle of Hattin and recaptured Jerusalem.
Died of a fever in Damascus, giving away his personal wealth to his subjects before his passing.
Born on December 22 in Erode, Madras Presidency, British India.
Obtained a copy of G. S. Carr's synopsis of mathematics, which awakened his profound mathematical genius.
Began a historic correspondence with the prominent British mathematician G. H. Hardy.
Elected as a Fellow of the Royal Society, becoming one of its youngest members.
Died in India at the young age of 32, leaving behind thousands of unproven theorems.
Born on November 6 in Trabzon, Ottoman Empire.
Ascended the Ottoman throne as the tenth Sultan of the empire.
Defeated the Kingdom of Hungary at the pivotal Battle of Mohács.
Led the first Ottoman Siege of Vienna, marking the peak of the empire's expansion into Europe.
Died during the Siege of Szigetvár, ending the longest reign in Ottoman history.
Born as Narendranath Datta on January 12 in Calcutta, British India.
Met his guru, Sri Ramakrishna, which profoundly shifted his spiritual path.
Delivered his famous speech at the Parliament of the World's Religions in Chicago, introducing Hinduism to the West.
Founded the Ramakrishna Mission to provide extensive social and spiritual services.
Passed away on July 4 at Belur Math while meditating.
Opened their first bicycle shop, the Wright Cycle Exchange, which funded their early aeronautical experiments.
Began their serious pursuit of flight by building a biplane kite to test their wing-warping control concept.
Achieved the first sustained, controlled, powered heavier-than-air manned flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina.
Flew the Wright Flyer III, considered the world's first fully practical and reliable airplane.
Founded the Wright Company to manufacture airplanes and provide flight training, commercializing their invention.
Born in Kirkcaldy, Scotland, where he would later develop his foundational ideas on political economy.
Appointed professor of logic and later moral philosophy at the University of Glasgow.
Published The Theory of Moral Sentiments, exploring the psychological foundations of human morality and sympathy.
Published The Wealth of Nations, establishing the fundamental principles of classical free-market economics.
Appointed Commissioner of Customs in Scotland, spending his final years regulating the trade he had theorized about.
Born in Syracuse
Died during the Second Punic War
Cicero discovered Archimedes' tomb near Syracuse
Danish historian of mathematics Johan Ludvig Heiberg discovered 'The Method of Mechanical Theorems' in Istanbul
Greek scientist Ioannis Sakkas successfully recreated the Archimedes heat ray
'The Method of Mechanical Theorems' was auctioned at Christie's in New York
'The Method of Mechanical Theorems' was revealed to the public
Students at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology conducted an experiment to recreate the Archimedes heat ray

keyAchievements.title: Research in statics and hydrostatics
keyAchievements.description: Researched statics and hydrostatics in the field of physics.

keyAchievements.title: Explanation of the principles of levers and pulleys
keyAchievements.description: Explained the principles of levers and pulleys.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of machines
keyAchievements.description: Invented machines such as the first elevator, the Archimedes' screw, and naval attack mirrors.

keyAchievements.title: Mathematical achievements
keyAchievements.description: Left behind mathematical achievements such as the introduction of the decimal system, calculating the area enclosed by a parabola, and calculating pi.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Archimedes' principle
keyAchievements.description: Discovered a method for determining the volume of irregular objects to prove the purity of a gold crown.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Archimedes' screw
keyAchievements.description: Developed a device to pump water by rotating a spiral fitted inside a hollow cylinder.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Claw of Archimedes
keyAchievements.description: Invented a defensive weapon using heavy metal hooks to capsize enemy ships.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Archimedes' heat ray
keyAchievements.description: Reportedly invented a type of burning glass that focused sunlight to ignite fires.

keyAchievements.title: Elucidation of the principle of the lever
keyAchievements.description: Elucidated various principles of the lever in 'On the Equilibrium of Planes'.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a planetarium
keyAchievements.description: According to Cicero, he created a planetarium showing the movements of the sun, moon, and five planets.

keyAchievements.title: Calculation of the approximation of pi
keyAchievements.description: Used polygons to calculate the approximation of pi (π) as 3.1408 < π < 3.1429.

keyAchievements.title: Proof of the quadrature of the parabola
keyAchievements.description: Proved that the area enclosed by a parabola and a straight line is 4/3 of the area of its inscribed triangle.

keyAchievements.title: Devised a method to express extremely large numbers
keyAchievements.description: Introduced a method for expressing extremely large numbers, such as the number of grains of sand, in 'The Sand Reckoner'.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of major works
keyAchievements.description: Wrote several mathematical and scientific treatises, including 'On the Equilibrium of Planes', 'Measurement of a Circle', and 'On the Sphere and Cylinder'.

faq.question: When and where was Archimedes born?
faq.answer: Archimedes was born around 287 BC in Syracuse, which was part of Magna Graecia in ancient Greece.

faq.question: How did Archimedes die?
faq.answer: He was killed by a Roman soldier around 212 BC when Syracuse fell to Rome during the Second Punic War.

faq.question: What is Archimedes' most famous anecdote?
faq.answer: The anecdote where he discovered a method to measure the volume of an irregular object to verify the purity of a gold crown and shouted 'Eureka!'.

faq.question: What was inscribed on Archimedes' tombstone?
faq.answer: An illustration demonstrating his proof of the relationship between the volumes of a cylinder and a sphere of the same height was inscribed on it.

faq.question: What was the Archimedes' screw used for?
faq.answer: It was a device consisting of a spiral fitted inside a long hollow cylinder that pumped water when rotated, used for pumping bilge water out of ships.

faq.question: Was the Archimedes' heat ray actually used?
faq.answer: The 2nd-century author Lucian wrote that it burned Roman warships during the Siege of Syracuse, but its actual use has been debated since the Renaissance.

faq.question: Did Archimedes really say 'Do not disturb my circles'?
faq.answer: This phrase was a later invention and is not mentioned in Plutarch's Parallel Lives.

faq.question: Have any of Archimedes' works been rediscovered in modern times?
faq.answer: In 1906, Danish historian of mathematics Johan Ludvig Heiberg discovered 'The Method of Mechanical Theorems', which included 'On Floating Bodies', in Istanbul.

timeline.event: Born as the youngest of four children in Toruń, Poland
Lost his father at the age of ten
Enrolled at the University of Kraków and began studying mathematics and astronomy
Completed his studies in mathematics and astronomy at the University of Kraków
Left the University of Kraków for Bologna, Italy, and enrolled in the Faculty of Theology
Boarded at the home of Domenico Maria Novara, an astronomy professor at the University of Bologna, and assisted with his astronomical observations
Completed four years of law studies at the University of Bologna and traveled to Rome
Received permission from the Catholic Diocese Chapter of Warmia in Frombork to study for two more years
Enrolled in the medical program at the University of Padua
Received a doctorate in canon law from the University of Ferrara
Returned to Warmia and began serving as a member of the chapter
Observed and recorded the great conjunction of planets in Cancer
Translated Theophylact Simocatta's 'Epistles: Moral, Rustic, and Amatory' into Latin
Completed the basic framework of a heliocentric astronomical system and circulated the 'Commentariolus' (Little Commentary) among his acquaintances
Performed administrative duties as a chancellor of the chapter
Moved to Frauenburg (Frombork) in Royal Prussia and worked as a cathedral canon after the death of his maternal uncle Lucas Watzenrode
Tasked with administering the lands of the Warmia chapter and resolving issues with tenant farmers
Wrote a treatise on monetary reform
Endeavored to defend Frombork when the Teutonic Knights attacked, requesting aid from the King of Poland and fellow chapter members
Attended the Prussian Diet to discuss the reform of silver coinage
Elected as interim bishop by the chapter members following the death of Bishop Fabian Luzjanski
Poland and the Teutonic Knights signed a final peace treaty
Began writing 'De revolutionibus orbium coelestium' (On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres)
Nearly completed his manuscript on his cosmological model
The Pope's secretary explained Copernicus's theory to the Pope and cardinals
Cardinal Schönberg wrote a letter to Copernicus urging the publication of his book
Printing of 'On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres' began
'De revolutionibus orbium coelestium' was published, and he died on May 24 after suffering a stroke
Remains believed to be those of Copernicus were found beneath the altar of Frombork Cathedral
A genetics expert from Uppsala University in Sweden announced that the DNA from the remains matched the DNA of hair found in his books
A reburial ceremony was held in Poland nearly 500 years after his death

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Heliocentrism
keyAchievements.description: Brought about a groundbreaking shift in modern natural science by pointing out the errors of the universally accepted geocentric model and proposing the heliocentric model.

keyAchievements.title: Published the 'Commentariolus'
keyAchievements.description: Completed the basic framework of a heliocentric astronomical system around 1510 and circulated a manuscript summary to his acquaintances.

keyAchievements.title: Published 'On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres'
keyAchievements.description: Systematically presented the heliocentric theory by publishing a comprehensive book on his cosmological model in 1543.

keyAchievements.title: Copernican Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Brought about a paradigm shift from an anthropocentric geocentric model to an objective heliocentric model, ultimately leading to the rejection of the medieval worldview.

faq.question: Who was Copernicus?
faq.answer: He was a Polish Roman Catholic cleric and astronomer who brought about a groundbreaking shift in modern natural science by pointing out the errors of the geocentric model and advocating heliocentrism.

faq.question: What is Copernicus's heliocentric theory?
faq.answer: It is the theory that the sun is at the center of the universe and that the planets, including the Earth, revolve around the sun, which directly challenged the Ptolemaic system of the time.

faq.question: What are Copernicus's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include the 'Commentariolus', circulated among acquaintances around 1510, and his masterpiece 'De revolutionibus orbium coelestium', published in 1543.

faq.question: How were Copernicus's remains discovered?
faq.answer: In 2005, skeletal remains were found beneath the altar of Frombork Cathedral, and in 2008, DNA analysis confirmed them to be his remains.

faq.question: How was Copernicus's theory received at the time?
faq.answer: Immediately after the publication of 'On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres', the response was muted, but as time passed, it spread widely, and in 1616, it was placed on the Index of Forbidden Books by the Roman Catholic Church.

timeline.event: Born in Florence, Italy.
First met Beatrice Portinari, the woman who would become his lifelong muse, though they barely spoke.
Exiled from Florence for life due to his involvement with the White Guelphs political faction.
Began writing 'The Divine Comedy' (La Divina Commedia) while wandering in exile.
Completed 'Inferno' (Hell).
Completed 'Purgatorio' (Purgatory).
Completed 'Paradiso' (Paradise), shortly before his death.
Died in Ravenna, Italy, where he remains buried today.

keyAchievements.title: The Divine Comedy
keyAchievements.description: Authored one of the supreme works of world literature, an epic poem detailing the soul's journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Heaven.

keyAchievements.title: Father of the Italian Language
keyAchievements.description: He wrote his masterpiece in the Tuscan vernacular instead of traditional Latin, establishing it as the standard for the modern Italian language.

keyAchievements.title: Synthesis of Medieval Thought
keyAchievements.description: His work seamlessly integrates classical philosophy, Catholic theology, and contemporary politics, serving as the ultimate expression of the medieval worldview.

faq.question: Who was Beatrice?
faq.answer: Beatrice was a real woman (likely Beatrice Portinari) whom Dante met only a few times but loved deeply from afar. She died young, and Dante immortalized her in his poetry, making her his guide through Heaven in 'The Divine Comedy'.

faq.question: Why was he exiled?
faq.answer: Dante was heavily involved in Florentine politics as a member of the White Guelphs, a faction that opposed the Pope's temporal power. When the rival Black Guelphs seized power with the Pope's backing, Dante was accused of corruption and exiled on pain of death.

faq.question: Why is it called a 'Comedy' if it's about Hell?
faq.answer: In Dante's time, 'comedy' did not mean a funny story. It referred to a narrative that begins in despair or tragedy (Hell) and ends in happiness or grace (Heaven), as opposed to a 'tragedy' which ends poorly.

faq.question: Why did Dante write his works in Italian instead of Latin?
faq.answer: It was his intention to widely spread the medieval message of catharsis and salvation contained in the 'Divine Comedy' to the general public.

dante-alighieri.missingDataNote: 베아트리체를 처음 만난 해, 단테의 망명 시작 연도, 단테에게 사형이 선고된 궐석 재판의 연도는 원문에 명시되어 있지 않아 생략했습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Mainz.
Gutenberg's father was exiled by the citizens and fled to Strasbourg.
After his father passed away, Gutenberg also moved to Strasbourg.
Used movable metal type printing.
Returned to Mainz.
Succeeded in metal type printing and opened a printing press in Mainz.
Improved to smaller and more advanced type, and printed a 42-line Old Testament in two volumes.
Printed the 'Biblia Sacra' with 42 lines per page.
Fust filed a lawsuit against Gutenberg with the Archbishop of Mainz.
The 'Mainz Psalter' was published by the Fust-Schöffer printing press.
Death.

keyAchievements.title: Use of movable metal type printing
keyAchievements.description: A German goldsmith who used movable metal type printing around 1440.

keyAchievements.title: Type design and spread of mass production technology
keyAchievements.description: Spread type design and type mass production technology throughout Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Combination of printing technologies
keyAchievements.description: Combined movable metal type printing technology with the use of oil-based ink and wooden printing presses.

keyAchievements.title: Adoption of type manufacturing materials and methods
keyAchievements.description: Used an alloy as the material for making type and adopted a casting method.

keyAchievements.title: Printing of the Gutenberg Bible
keyAchievements.description: Printed the first 36-line Latin Bible, the <Gutenberg Bible>, using Gothic type.

keyAchievements.title: Printing of the 42-line Old Testament
keyAchievements.description: Around 1453, after improving the type, he printed a 42-line Old Testament in two volumes, which was highly acclaimed for its excellent printing quality.

keyAchievements.title: Printing of the 'Biblia Sacra'
keyAchievements.description: In 1455, printed the 'Biblia Sacra' with 42 lines on each page.

faq.question: What is Gutenberg's real name?
faq.answer: His real name is Johannes Gensfleisch, and Gutenberg is a common appellation.

faq.question: When was Gutenberg born?
faq.answer: Gutenberg is believed to have been born in Mainz in either 1397 or 1398.

faq.question: What are Gutenberg's major achievements?
faq.answer: His major achievements include spreading type design and mass production technology in Europe, and combining this technology with oil-based ink and the use of the wooden printing press.

faq.question: What books did Gutenberg print?
faq.answer: He printed Catholic indulgences, the first 36-line Latin <Gutenberg Bible>, and the 42-line 'Biblia Sacra' in 1455.

faq.question: What impact did Gutenberg's printing press have on human history?
faq.answer: His invention allowed books to spread massively across Europe, triggering an information revolution and laying the foundation for the Renaissance, the Reformation, and the humanist movement.

timeline.event: Discovered molecular asymmetry by resolving tartaric acid crystals, laying the foundation for stereochemistry.
Conducted the famous swan-neck flask experiments that definitively disproved the theory of spontaneous generation.
Patented the process of pasteurization to prevent the spoilage of wine and milk by destroying harmful microbes.
Successfully developed and demonstrated a vaccine against anthrax using artificially attenuated bacilli.
Administered the first successful rabies vaccine to a human patient, nine-year-old Joseph Meister.
Born in Florence, Italy, into a wealthy and influential family during the height of the Renaissance.
Appointed as the Second Chancellor of the Republic of Florence, handling diplomatic and military affairs.
Dismissed from office and briefly imprisoned and tortured when the Medici family returned to power.
Wrote The Prince, his seminal treatise on political power and statecraft, while living in exile.
Posthumous publication of Discourses on Livy, revealing his deeper republican political sympathies.
Born François-Marie Arouet in Paris, later adopting the pen name Voltaire.
Imprisoned in the Bastille for eleven months due to his satirical verses mocking the French government.
Published Letters on the English, which popularized British empirical philosophy and sparked outrage in France.
Published his masterpiece Candide, a brilliant satirical novella criticizing philosophical optimism.
Began his campaign to clear the name of Jean Calas, emerging as a leading champion for religious tolerance.
Born in Edinburgh, Scotland, into a family devoted to elocution and the correction of speech.
Founded a school in Boston to train teachers of the deaf, deeply influencing his research on sound.
Granted a patent for the telephone and successfully transmitted the first intelligible human speech.
Became one of the founding members of the National Geographic Society, later serving as its president.
Died in Nova Scotia, causing all telephones in North America to be silenced for a minute in tribute.
Took her first flying lesson and subsequently purchased her first aircraft, a Kinner Airster.
Became the first woman to fly across the Atlantic Ocean as a passenger, gaining international fame.
Successfully completed a solo nonstop flight across the Atlantic Ocean, the first woman to do so.
Became the first person to fly solo from Hawaii to the United States mainland.
Disappeared over the central Pacific Ocean during an attempt to make a circumnavigational flight of the globe.
Emigrated from Scotland to the United States with his family, settling in Allegheny, Pennsylvania.
Left his job at the Pennsylvania Railroad to focus on his growing investments in the iron works industry.
Published The Gospel of Wealth, an influential essay urging the rich to use their wealth to improve society.
Formed the Carnegie Steel Company, establishing an industrial empire that dominated American steel production.
Sold his steel company to J.P. Morgan for $480 million, becoming a full-time philanthropist.
Born in Boston, Massachusetts, and orphaned shortly after, leading him to be taken in by the Allan family.
Published his first book of poetry, Tamerlane and Other Poems, under the pseudonym A Bostonian.
Published The Murders in the Rue Morgue, widely considered to be the first modern detective story.
Achieved instant literary success and widespread acclaim with the publication of his poem The Raven.
Died mysteriously in Baltimore at the age of 40 under circumstances that remain unexplained.
Born in Oak Park, Illinois.
Attended Oak Park and River Forest High School.
Published his first article in the school newspaper, 'The Trapeze'.
Severely wounded by mortar fire while serving as an ambulance driver for the Italian Red Cross, and received the Silver Medal of Military Valor.
Returned to the United States and began working as a freelancer and staff writer for the 'Toronto Star Weekly'.
Married Hadley Richardson, moved to Paris, and worked as a foreign correspondent for the 'Toronto Star'.
Met Ezra Pound at Sylvia Beach's bookstore, Shakespeare and Company.
Published his first book 'Three Stories and Ten Poems' and his son Jack Hemingway was born.
Published 'In Our Time' and met F. Scott Fitzgerald.
Published his first novel 'The Sun Also Rises'.
Divorced Hadley Richardson, married Pauline Pfeiffer, and published the short story collection 'Men Without Women'.
His son Patrick was born, and he received news of his father's suicide.
Published the novel 'A Farewell to Arms'.
Broke his right arm in a car accident.
His third son, Gregory Hemingway, was born.
Went on a safari trip to Kenya with Pauline.
Purchased the fishing boat Pilar.
Published 'Green Hills of Africa'.
Traveled to Spain to cover the Spanish Civil War for the North American Newspaper Alliance, and published 'To Have and Have Not'.
Began living at the Hotel Ambos Mundos in Havana, Cuba.
Married Martha Gellhorn and published the novel 'For Whom the Bell Tolls'.
Traveled to China with Martha.
Stayed in London and other parts of Europe, and participated as a journalist in the Normandy landings and the liberation of Paris.
Divorced Martha Gellhorn.
Married Mary Welsh.
Awarded the Bronze Star Medal for his bravery during World War II.
Published the novel 'Across the River and into the Trees'.
Won the Pulitzer Prize for 'The Old Man and the Sea' and embarked on a second trip to Africa.
Survived two plane crashes in Africa and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Left Cuba and purchased a house on the outskirts of Ketchum, Idaho.
Left Cuba for New York and was admitted to the Mayo Clinic.
Returned to Ketchum after being discharged from the Mayo Clinic, and committed suicide with a shotgun on July 2.

keyAchievements.title: Stylistic innovation
keyAchievements.description: Profoundly influenced 20th-century fiction with a concise, understated writing style known as the Iceberg Theory.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Literature
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954.

keyAchievements.title: Pulitzer Prize
keyAchievements.description: Won the Pulitzer Prize for 'The Old Man and the Sea' in 1952.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of major works
keyAchievements.description: Published numerous masterpieces including 'The Sun Also Rises' (1926), 'A Farewell to Arms' (1929), 'For Whom the Bell Tolls' (1940), and 'The Old Man and the Sea' (1951).

keyAchievements.title: Bronze Star Medal
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Bronze Star Medal in 1947 for his bravery during World War II.

keyAchievements.title: Literary influence
keyAchievements.description: Greatly influenced the overall style of American literature and was inducted into the Chicago Literary Hall of Fame in 2012.

faq.question: What is Ernest Hemingway's signature writing style?
faq.answer: Hemingway profoundly influenced 20th-century fiction by using a concise, understated writing style known as the 'Iceberg Theory'.

faq.question: When did Hemingway win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
faq.answer: Hemingway won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954.

faq.question: What are some of Hemingway's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Sun Also Rises', 'A Farewell to Arms', 'For Whom the Bell Tolls', and 'The Old Man and the Sea'.

faq.question: How did Hemingway's life end?
faq.answer: On July 2, 1961, he committed suicide with a shotgun in Ketchum, Idaho.

faq.question: What are the main themes of Hemingway's works?
faq.answer: Love, war, the wilderness, and defeat frequently appear as main themes in his works, with nature often depicted as a place of rebirth and rest.

timeline.event: Graduated from Harvard College and began his lifelong friendship with fellow transcendentalist Ralph Waldo Emerson.
Moved to a small cabin he built near Walden Pond, beginning his two-year experiment in simple living.
Spent a night in jail for refusing to pay his poll tax, a protest against slavery and the Mexican-American War.
Published the essay Civil Disobedience, articulating his philosophy of nonviolent resistance to unjust states.
Published Walden, his masterpiece reflecting upon simple living in natural surroundings.
Partnered with Anthony Drexel to form Drexel, Morgan & Co., the precursor to J.P. Morgan & Co.
Arranged the merger of Edison General Electric and Thomson-Houston Electric Company to form General Electric.
Led a syndicate that supplied the U.S. Treasury with 3.5 million ounces of gold, stabilizing the economy.
Created the United States Steel Corporation, the world's first billion-dollar corporation, by buying out Andrew Carnegie.
Orchestrated the financial rescue of multiple major institutions to halt the Panic of 1907.
Born in Boston, Massachusetts, into a long line of Unitarian ministers.
Published his first essay, Nature, laying the foundational philosophical tenets of American Transcendentalism.
Delivered The American Scholar address, famously calling for intellectual independence from Europe.
Published Essays: First Series, containing the influential essay Self-Reliance.
Emerged as a prominent anti-slavery speaker, lecturing actively for the abolitionist movement.
Met Elizabeth Cady Stanton, forging a powerful, lifelong partnership in the women's rights movement.
Co-founded the National Woman Suffrage Association, dedicated to securing a constitutional amendment for women's voting rights.
Arrested for voting in the presidential election in Rochester, New York, to test the Fourteenth Amendment.
Began publishing the multi-volume History of Woman Suffrage alongside Stanton and Matilda Joslyn Gage.
The 19th Amendment, granting women the right to vote, is ratified 14 years after her death, colloquially known as the Susan B. Anthony Amendment.
Born in West Hills, New York, the second of nine children in a working-class family.
Self-published the first edition of Leaves of Grass, radically transforming the landscape of American poetry.
Traveled to Washington, D.C. to care for his wounded brother, later working as a volunteer nurse during the Civil War.
Published Drum-Taps, a deeply moving poetry collection reflecting his experiences during the American Civil War.
Prepared the final Deathbed Edition of Leaves of Grass shortly before passing away in Camden, New Jersey.
Born in Kiel, Holstein.
Witnessed the march of Prussian and Austrian troops into Kiel during the Second Schleswig War.
Family moved to Munich, and he enrolled in the Maximiliansgymnasium.
Began his studies at the University of Munich.
Studied for a year at the Friedrich Wilhelm University in Berlin.
Defended his doctoral dissertation 'On the Second Law of Thermodynamics'.
Earned his habilitation by presenting the thesis 'Equilibrium States of Isotropic Bodies at Different Temperatures'.
Appointed associate professor of theoretical physics at the University of Kiel.
Married Marie Merck.
Appointed as the successor to Kirchhoff's position at the Friedrich Wilhelm University of Berlin.
Became a full professor.
Turned his attention to the problem of black-body radiation.
Published 'Treatise on Thermodynamics'.
Contributed to the formation of the German Physical Society (DPG).
Presented a new derivation of Planck's law of black-body radiation, including the energy quantization hypothesis.
Served as president of the German Physical Society (DPG) (until 1909).
His first wife, Marie Planck, died.
Married his second wife, Marga von Hoesslin.
Successfully brought Einstein to Berlin and created a new professorship for him.
His eldest son, Karl, was killed in action at the Battle of Verdun.
His daughter, Grete, died while giving birth to her first child.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of energy quanta (actually awarded in 1919).
His daughter, Emma, died.
Founded the Emergency Association of German Science (Notgemeinschaft der Deutschen Wissenschaft) along with Fritz Haber.
Retired in Berlin, and Erwin Schrödinger succeeded him.
Became president of the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (KWG).
Was 74 years old when the Nazis took power, and discussed the treatment of Jewish professors with Hitler.
His term as president of the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (KWG) ended.
Celebrated his 80th birthday, and resigned as president of the Prussian Academy when it was taken over by the Nazis.
His home in Berlin was destroyed in a bombing raid, resulting in the loss of all his scientific records and correspondence.
His son Erwin was executed for his involvement in the assassination attempt on Hitler.
Died in Göttingen.
The Kaiser Wilhelm Society was renamed the Max Planck Society (MPG).

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of energy quanta
keyAchievements.description: Won the 1918 Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of energy quanta, which revolutionized the understanding of atomic and subatomic processes.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of quantum theory
keyAchievements.description: His reputation as a physicist rests primarily on his role as the originator of quantum theory, a revolutionary idea incompatible with classical physics.

keyAchievements.title: Planck's law of black-body radiation
keyAchievements.description: Derived Planck's law of black-body radiation, which accurately described the experimentally observed black-body spectrum, through his research on the black-body radiation problem.

keyAchievements.title: Planck constant (h)
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation of quantum theory by introducing the Planck constant (h) through the hypothesis that electromagnetic energy could only be emitted in quantized form.

keyAchievements.title: Early recognition of and contribution to the theory of relativity
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the few to immediately recognize the significance of Albert Einstein's special theory of relativity, and contributed to its reformulation.

keyAchievements.title: Leadership in German scientific institutions
keyAchievements.description: Held key positions in the German scientific community, serving as president of the German Physical Society (DPG) and the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (KWG).

faq.question: What major scientific discovery did Max Planck make?
faq.answer: Max Planck discovered energy quanta and founded quantum theory, revolutionizing the understanding of atomic and subatomic processes.

faq.question: When did Max Planck win the Nobel Prize in Physics?
faq.answer: He was awarded the 1918 Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of energy quanta, though he actually received it in 1919.

faq.question: What was Max Planck's stance regarding the Nazi regime?
faq.answer: He tried to assist his Jewish colleagues and preserve the independence of science; he met with Hitler to discuss the treatment of Jewish professors and resigned from the presidency of the Prussian Academy in protest against the Nazis.

faq.question: What were Max Planck's religious views?
faq.answer: He was a German Lutheran who believed in an almighty, omniscient, and benevolent God (though not necessarily a personal one), and viewed both science and religion as having the goal of 'Toward God!'.

timeline.event: Born in Alexandria as the daughter of Theon, a philosopher and mathematician
Murdered and her body mutilated by a mob of Christians

keyAchievements.title: Work on Diophantus's astronomical calculations
keyAchievements.description: Parts of her writings were discovered in the Vatican Library around the 15th century.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of a commentary on Diophantus's Arithmetica
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as a significant research achievement for adding several alternative solutions and many new problems to her commentary beyond Diophantus's original solutions.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of a commentary on Apollonius's Conics
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a commentary on Apollonius's Conics.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of a commentary on Ptolemy's Almagest
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a commentary on Ptolemy's major work, the Almagest.

keyAchievements.title: Co-authorship of a commentary on Euclid's Elements
keyAchievements.description: Co-authored a commentary on Euclid's Elements with her father, Theon.

faq.question: Who was Hypatia?
faq.answer: Hypatia was a prominent Greek female philosopher and mathematician representing Neoplatonism, active in Alexandria, ancient Egypt.

faq.question: What are Hypatia's major works?
faq.answer: She wrote commentaries on Diophantus's Arithmetica, Apollonius's Conics, and Ptolemy's Almagest, and co-authored a commentary on Euclid's Elements with her father, Theon.

faq.question: How did Hypatia die?
faq.answer: Considered a political opponent by Archbishop Cyril, she was murdered by a mob of Christians who mutilated her body.

faq.question: Who was Hypatia's father?
faq.answer: Hypatia was born as the daughter of Theon, a philosopher and mathematician.

timeline.event: Born in Basra, in present-day Iraq, during the Islamic Golden Age.
Faked madness to escape the wrath of the Fatimid Caliph after failing to regulate the flooding of the Nile.
Completed his magnum opus, the Book of Optics (Kitab al-Manazir), profoundly impacting the scientific understanding of light.
Produced critical works on astronomy and mathematics that challenged the Ptolemaic models.
Died in Cairo, leaving a legacy as the father of modern optics and a pioneer of the scientific method.
Born in Clermont-Ferrand, France, to Etienne Pascal, a tax official in Rouen
Discovered by himself that the sum of the interior angles of a triangle is 180 degrees
Discovered Pascal's triangle
Participated in the main regular meetings of a group of French mathematicians
Proved Pascal's theorem, which is the foundation of projective geometry
Derived 400 propositions using Pascal's theorem
Invented the Pascaline, the first calculator
Established Pascal's law, which laid the foundation for hydrostatics, through an experiment using a mercury column
Conducted a vacuum experiment using various shaped tubes in Rouen
His experiments were conducted in Paris by Mersenne and other scientists
Published 'Discours sur le vide' (Thoughts on the Vacuum)
Moved from Rouen to Paris due to failing health
Debated with Descartes on the existence of a vacuum
Wrote a paper titled 'Experiences nouvelles touchant le vide' (New Experiments with the Vacuum)
Performed 'Pascal's experiment' observing the change in the height of a mercury column with altitude at Puy de Dome
Wrote a paper summarizing the results of the Puy de Dome experiment
Suspended his mathematics and science research to focus his mind on religious contemplation
Explained the law of pressure (Pascal's principle) in 'Traitez de l'equilibre des liqueurs' (Treatises on the Equilibrium of Liquids)
Involved in a carriage accident
Wrote a confession of faith claiming to have heard a voice from heaven and became deeply involved in Christian theology
Suffered mentally while battling headaches
Gave his house to a poor family suffering from smallpox and moved into his sister's house
Died from convulsions
After his death, 'Traitez de l'equilibre des liqueurs' was published by Perier

keyAchievements.title: Pascal's triangle
keyAchievements.description: A geometric arrangement of binomial coefficients in the shape of a triangle in mathematics.

keyAchievements.title: Pascal's theorem
keyAchievements.description: A theorem that is the foundation of projective geometry, which he proved at the age of 16.

keyAchievements.title: Pascaline (Pascal's calculator)
keyAchievements.description: The world's first calculator, invented at the age of 19, capable of addition and subtraction.

keyAchievements.title: Pascal's principle
keyAchievements.description: A law stating that if pressure is applied to a portion of a confined, incompressible fluid, that pressure is transmitted equally to all parts of the fluid.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for probability theory
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded the foundation of probability theory through correspondence with Fermat.

keyAchievements.title: Cycloid research
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the development of mathematics by studying the cycloid to forget his headaches.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Pensees'
keyAchievements.description: An unfinished book containing his Christian apologetics, including the famous saying, 'Man is a thinking reed.'

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Provincial Letters'
keyAchievements.description: A book written to defend Jansenist clergy oppressed by the Jesuits, evaluated as the starting point of modern French prose.

faq.question: Who was Blaise Pascal?
faq.answer: A French psychologist, mathematician, scientist, theologian, physicist, inventor, writer, philosopher, and statistician who invested a lot of time especially in philosophy and theology.

faq.question: What is the Pascal calculator?
faq.answer: The world's first calculator invented at the age of 19 to help his accountant father, capable only of addition and subtraction.

faq.question: What is Pascal's principle?
faq.answer: A law stating that if pressure is applied to a portion of a confined, incompressible fluid, that pressure is transmitted equally to all parts of the fluid.

faq.question: What is Pascal's famous saying?
faq.answer: He left the famous saying, 'Man is a thinking reed.'

faq.question: Why did Pascal stop his mathematics and science research?
faq.answer: At the age of 27, he focused his mind on religious contemplation and thus suspended his mathematics and science research.

timeline.event: Born in Tobolsk, Siberia, as the youngest of a large Russian family.
Graduated from the Main Pedagogical Institute in Saint Petersburg with a degree in science.
Presented his formulation of the Periodic Law to the Russian Chemical Society, creating the first periodic table of elements.
Accurately predicted the existence and properties of undiscovered elements such as gallium and germanium to fill gaps in his table.
Appointed Director of the Bureau of Weights and Measures, where he standardized Russian measurement systems.
Born Augusta Ada Byron in London, the only legitimate child of the poet Lord Byron.
Introduced to the brilliant polymath Charles Babbage, sparking her profound interest in his calculating machines.
Began translating an Italian engineer's article on Babbage's proposed Analytical Engine.
Published her translation appended with extensive notes, which contained what is considered the first computer algorithm.
Died of uterine cancer at the age of 36, leaving a legacy as the world's first computer programmer.
Born on June 23
Started majoring in mathematics at the University of Cambridge
Took physicist Eddington's scientific methodology class and proved the central limit theorem
Left for Princeton University after providing a negative proof to the decidability problem
Finished studies at Princeton University and returned to Cambridge
Joined the Government Code and Cypher School (GCCS) during World War II and started cryptography research
Devised the Turing machine; pushed for the creation of a mathematics department to build an electronic calculator at the National Physical Laboratory (NPL) on October 1
Awarded the Officer of the Order of the British Empire (OBE)
Published a paper titled 'Can Machines Think?' in the philosophy journal 'Mind'
Arrested and convicted by British police on charges of homosexuality
Published a paper on the Riemann zeta function
Died of potassium cyanide poisoning on June 7
Queen Elizabeth II pardoned Turing's homosexuality conviction on December 24

keyAchievements.title: Devised the Turing machine
keyAchievements.description: Formalized the concepts of algorithm and computation through an abstract model called the Turing machine, making a profound contribution to the development of computer science.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Turing test
keyAchievements.description: Proposed an experiment to evaluate how human-like a machine's answers are with the proposition 'Can machines think?', achieving great success.

keyAchievements.title: Cracked the Enigma code
keyAchievements.description: Successfully cracked the German codes during World War II by introducing a code-breaking machine called the Bombe.

keyAchievements.title: Proved the central limit theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved it in 1933 without knowing that Finnish mathematician Jarl Waldemar Lindeberg had already proved it in 1922.

keyAchievements.title: Negative proof of the decidability problem
keyAchievements.description: Provided a negative proof to the decidability problem in 1936.

faq.question: What fields was Alan Turing a pioneer in?
faq.answer: He was an English computer scientist, mathematician, cryptanalyst, logician, and a pioneering figure in computer science.

faq.question: What is the purpose of the Turing Award?
faq.answer: It is an annual prize given by the ACM to those who have made significant contributions to computer science, established in 1966 to honor Turing's contributions.

faq.question: Why is Alan Turing called the 'father of computer science'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'father of computer science' because he made profound contributions to theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence.

faq.question: When was Alan Turing pardoned for his homosexuality conviction?
faq.answer: Queen Elizabeth II pardoned his homosexuality conviction on December 24, 2013, 59 years after his death.

timeline.event: Born in London, displaying an early and intense fascination with mathematics.
Proposed the construction of a Difference Engine to mechanize the calculation of astronomical and mathematical tables.
Appointed the Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge University.
Conceived the Analytical Engine, a revolutionary design that envisioned a fully programmable mechanical computer.
Died in London, leaving his pioneering mechanical computers incomplete but establishing the conceptual framework for modern computing.
Born in Afshana near Bukhara as the child of Abdullah ibn Al-Hasan, a tax collector of the Samanid Empire, and Sitara.
At the age of five, his family moved to Bukhara, and he entered a private school in Bukhara.
By the age of ten, he could already recite the Quran and many Arabic literary works from memory.
Started treating patients at the age of sixteen.
Studied on his own at eighteen and came to understand Aristotelian philosophy through Al-Farabi's commentaries.
At the age of eighteen, he wrote his first work, 'The Compendium of Various Sciences', for his neighbor Al-Aruzi.
The Samanid Empire fell due to attacks by the Ghaznavids and the Karakhanids.
His father Abdullah passed away.
At twenty-one, he wrote and presented the 20-volume encyclopedia 'The Book of Fair Judgment' for the jurist Al-Barqi.
Left Bukhara at twenty-two and embarked on a wandering journey.
Was employed by Mamun II, the ruler of Urgench in the Khwarezm region, became an official, and started writing 'The Canon of Medicine'.
Moved to Rey near Tehran and wrote about 30 short essays.
Completed 'The Canon of Medicine', which he had been writing for some time.
After Shams al-Dawla passed away, Ibn Sina abandoned his official post and went into seclusion.
Was imprisoned by Sama al-Dawla, the new ruler of Hamadan.
Isfahan was attacked by Mas'ud I, the ruler of the Ghaznavid Empire, and Ibn Sina's belongings, including his collection of books, were looted.
Died in Hamadan.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of philosophy and medicine
keyAchievements.description: Compiled Greek and Arabic philosophy and medicine, and had a profound impact on European medicine and philosophy by integrating Aristotelian philosophy and Neoplatonism.

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Canon of Medicine
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'The Canon of Medicine', which compiled all known medical knowledge of the time and was used as a standard medical text in Europe until the 17th century.

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Book of Healing
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'The Book of Healing', an encyclopedia covering all fields of study except ethics and politics.

keyAchievements.title: Natural science research
keyAchievements.description: Denied the possibility of alchemical transmutation of metals, and researched various fields of natural science including mathematics, physics, astronomy, and mineralogy.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European academia
keyAchievements.description: Influenced Thomas Aquinas and became the catalyst for the revival of Aristotelianism in Europe in the late 12th century.

faq.question: How is Ibn Sina evaluated as a figure?
faq.answer: A Persian philosopher, physician, and scientist, he is regarded as one of the greatest medical scholars of the Middle Ages and the finest intellectual produced by the Islamic world.

faq.question: What is Ibn Sina's major medical work?
faq.answer: He authored 'The Canon of Medicine', which compiled all known medical knowledge of the time, and this book was used as the basic textbook of European medicine until the 17th century.

faq.question: What philosophical contributions did Ibn Sina make?
faq.answer: He compiled Greek and Arabic philosophy, integrated Aristotelian philosophy and Neoplatonism, and greatly influenced European medicine and philosophy.

faq.question: What was Ibn Sina's stance on alchemy?
faq.answer: He denied the possibility of metal transmutation believed by alchemists of the time, arguing that the differences between metals are profound and that precious metals created through alchemy are merely imitations.

timeline.event: Born in Greenock, Scotland, into a family of merchants and shipwrights.
Conceived the idea for a separate condenser to vastly improve the efficiency of the Newcomen steam engine.
Patented his revolutionary steam engine design, introducing the separate condenser that dramatically saved fuel.
Formed a highly successful partnership with Matthew Boulton to manufacture and market his improved steam engines.
Patented the sun and planet gear system, adapting his steam engine to produce rotary motion for mills and factories.
Born in Wylam, Northumberland, England, and began working at the local colliery at a young age.
Constructed his first steam locomotive, the Blücher, to transport coal at the Killingworth colliery.
Opened the Stockton and Darlington Railway, operating the world's first public passenger steam train, Locomotion No. 1.
Won the Rainhill Trials with his famed locomotive Rocket, proving the viability of steam-powered railways.
Oversaw the opening of the Liverpool and Manchester Railway, the world's first inter-city passenger railway.
Born into a farming family in Heinzendorf, part of the Austrian Empire.
Entered the Augustinian St Thomas's Abbey in Brno, where he would later conduct his famous experiments.
Began his systematic, multi-year experiments crossbreeding thousands of pea plants in the monastery garden.
Presented his findings on the laws of inheritance, including dominant and recessive traits, to the Natural History Society of Brno.
Published his paper Experiments on Plant Hybridization, which was largely ignored until being rediscovered decades after his death.
Born in Paris as the first son of Jean-Antoine Lavoisier and Emilie Lavoisier.
Graduated from College Mazarin.
Obtained a bachelor's degree in law.
Became a member of the French Academy of Sciences and joined the Ferme Generale, a tax collection agency.
Became famous by publishing a paper concluding that water cannot be transmuted into earth.
Married Marie-Anne Pierrette Paulze.
Published 'Opuscules Physiques et chimiques' (Physical and Chemical Essays).
Appointed head of the Gunpowder Administration by Finance Minister Anne Robert Jacques Turgot.
Revealed that water is produced when hydrogen is burned, in cooperation with Pierre-Simon Laplace.
Became a director of the French Academy of Sciences.
Published 'Methode de nomenclature chimique' (Method of Chemical Nomenclature) by reforming chemical nomenclature together with Claude Louis Berthollet and others.
Published 'Traite Elementaire de Chimie' (Elementary Treatise on Chemistry).
Participated in the creation of a unified system of weights and measures commissioned by the National Assembly.
Numerous academies, including the French Academy of Sciences, were closed.
Imprisoned along with his father-in-law Jacques Paulze.
Sentenced to death and beheaded at the guillotine in the Place de la Revolution.
Marie-Anne Lavoisier was officially able to receive Lavoisier's confiscated belongings back.

keyAchievements.title: Led the Chemical Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Led the 18th-century chemical revolution, leaving a mark in the history of chemistry and biology.

keyAchievements.title: Abolished the phlogiston theory and established the theory of combustion
keyAchievements.description: Advanced chemistry significantly by advocating a new theory of combustion, discarding the phlogiston theory, and identifying the role of oxygen.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Law of Conservation of Mass
keyAchievements.description: Established the law of conservation of mass, stating that matter is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions, but only changes form.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for modern chemical nomenclature
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation of modern chemistry by distinguishing between elements and compounds and leading a systematic reform of chemical nomenclature.

keyAchievements.title: Introduced quantitative methods and compiled a list of elements
keyAchievements.description: Introduced quantitative methods to chemistry for the first time, compiled the first extensive list of elements, and predicted the existence of silicon.

faq.question: For what reason was Antoine Lavoisier executed?
faq.answer: During the height of the French Revolution, he was indicted for tax fraud and selling adulterated tobacco, and despite his contributions to science, he was executed by the guillotine.

faq.question: What is Lavoisier's most important scientific achievement?
faq.answer: He led the chemical revolution by discarding the phlogiston theory and identifying the role of oxygen in combustion and respiration, established the law of conservation of mass, and laid the foundation for modern chemical nomenclature.

faq.question: How did Lavoisier's wife, Marie-Anne Paulze Lavoisier, contribute to her husband's research?
faq.answer: As a laboratory assistant, Marie-Anne helped by drawing laboratory equipment, recording results, translating English papers, and she also participated in the development of the metric measurement system.

timeline.event: Born on July 3 in the 13th year of Zhengde of the Ming Dynasty
Began the work of compiling standard medical texts at the age of 35
Died in the 21st year of Wanli of the Ming Dynasty (at the age of 76)
Publication of the 'Compendium of Materia Medica' (after Li Shizhen's death)

keyAchievements.title: Authored the Compendium of Materia Medica
keyAchievements.description: Authored the 'Compendium of Materia Medica' (Bencao Gangmu), which integrated herbal medicine and had a profound influence on subsequent Eastern medicine and natural history.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to acupuncture and diagnostics
keyAchievements.description: Also contributed to the fields of acupuncture and diagnostics.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Binhu Maixue
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Binhu Maixue', a book on pulse diagnosis.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Qijing Bamai Kao
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Qijing Bamai Kao', a book on the eight extraordinary meridians.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Wuzang Tulun
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Wuzang Tulun', a book on the five internal organs, which is currently lost.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Mingmen Kao
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Mingmen Kao', a book on the 'Gate of Life' (Mingmen), which is currently lost.

faq.question: When was Li Shizhen born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen was born on July 3, 1518, the 13th year of Zhengde of the Ming Dynasty, and died in 1593, the 21st year of Wanli of the Ming Dynasty, at the age of 76.

faq.question: What are Li Shizhen's major works?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen's major work is the 'Compendium of Materia Medica', which integrated herbal medicine. In addition, he authored 'Binhu Maixue', 'Qijing Bamai Kao', and others.

faq.question: What positions did Li Shizhen hold?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen served as a physician for the Prince of Chu, became an imperial physician in the royal court, and later held the position of magistrate of Huanggang, Hubei in the Ming Dynasty.

faq.question: What was Li Shizhen's family background?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen's grandfather and father were doctors. His father's name was Li Yanwen, his courtesy name was Ziyu, and his pseudonym was Yuechi.

li-shizhen.missingDataNote: 명나라 후베이 황강 태수 직책과 조정의 태의가 된 정확한 연도가 원문에 명시되어 있지 않아 타임라인에 포함하지 않았습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Bologna, Italy, where he developed an early interest in physics and electricity.
Successfully transmitted a wireless signal over a distance of one and a half miles at his family's estate in Italy.
Founded the Wireless Telegraph & Signal Company in London to commercialize his wireless inventions.
Achieved the first successful transatlantic wireless transmission, receiving a signal sent from England in Newfoundland.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics, shared with Karl Ferdinand Braun, for their contributions to the development of wireless telegraphy.
Born in Clausthal, Kingdom of Hanover
Graduated in medicine from the University of Göttingen
Presented the results of anthrax research at the University of Breslau
Clearly identified the anthrax bacillus as the specific causative agent of anthrax
Moved to the Imperial Health Office in Berlin
Discovered the tuberculosis bacillus for the first time
Discovered the cholera pathogen while traveling through Egypt and India
Clearly identified the cholera bacillus as the specific causative agent of cholera
Discovered tuberculin, an antigen against the tuberculosis bacillus
Became the first director of the Institute for Infectious Diseases in Berlin
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for founding bacteriology and discovering the tuberculosis bacillus
Died in Baden-Baden

keyAchievements.title: Identified the causes of anthrax and cholera
keyAchievements.description: Clearly identified pathogenic bacteria as the specific causative agents of anthrax in 1877 and cholera in 1885, earning the evaluation as the 'father of bacteriology'.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered the tuberculosis bacillus
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the tuberculosis bacillus for the first time in 1882 and revealed that it is transmitted through the air.

keyAchievements.title: Founded bacteriological research methodology
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the development of microbiology by pioneering methods for fixing, staining, and photographing bacteria under a microscope.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered tuberculin
keyAchievements.description: Discovered tuberculin, an antigen against the tuberculosis bacillus, in 1890.

faq.question: Who was Robert Koch?
faq.answer: Robert Koch was a German physician and microbiologist born in 1843 and died in 1910, known as the 'father of bacteriology'.

faq.question: What are Robert Koch's major achievements?
faq.answer: He identified the causative bacteria of anthrax in 1877 and cholera in 1885, and discovered the tuberculosis bacillus for the first time in 1882.

faq.question: When did Robert Koch win the Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1905 for his contributions to founding bacteriology and discovering the tuberculosis bacillus.

faq.question: Did Robert Koch discover tuberculin?
faq.answer: Yes, he discovered tuberculin, an antigen against the tuberculosis bacillus, in 1890, but his reputation temporarily declined when it was later found to be ineffective as a cure.

robert-koch.missingDataNote: 프로이센-프랑스 전쟁 당시 군의관 근무 및 시골 의사 근무에 대한 구체적인 연도는 원문에 명시되어 있지 않아 생략했습니다. 베를린 대학교에서의 미생물 연구 시점도 구체적인 연도가 없어 생략했습니다.

timeline.event: Born into noble lineage in Roccasecca, Italy, at the castle of his father, Count Landulf of Aquino.
Joined the Dominican Order despite fierce opposition from his family, who imprisoned him for a year to deter him.
Appointed as a Master of Theology at the University of Paris, becoming a dominant intellectual figure of the medieval period.
Began writing his monumental masterpiece, the Summa Theologica, synthesizing Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology.
Ceased all writing following a profound mystical experience during mass, declaring that all he had written seemed like straw.
Born Jalal al-Din Muhammad Rumi in Balkh, in present-day Afghanistan or Tajikistan.
Settled with his family in Konya, Anatolia, where he would eventually inherit his father's position as an Islamic scholar.
Met the wandering mystic Shams of Tabriz, a life-altering encounter that transformed Rumi into a passionate poet.
Started composing the Masnavi, a sprawling, spiritual masterpiece of Persian poetry encompassing spiritual teaching and mystical stories.
Died in Konya, prompting immense mourning across diverse religious communities who revered his inclusive message of universal love.
Born in Córdoba, Al-Andalus, into a prominent family of Andalusian scholars and judges.
Appointed a judge in Seville, later becoming the chief judge in Córdoba while serving as a court physician.
Authored The Incoherence of the Incoherence, powerfully defending philosophy against the attacks of earlier theologian Al-Ghazali.
Fell from political favor and was exiled to Lucena due to opposition to his rationalist philosophies by conservative clerics.
Passed away in Marrakesh, leaving behind Aristotelian commentaries that would profoundly shape medieval European philosophy.
Born in New York City to a wealthy intellectual family, the older brother of novelist Henry James.
Earned his medical degree from Harvard University, overcoming severe bouts of depression and physical illness.
Published The Principles of Psychology, a groundbreaking 1,200-page masterwork that redefined psychology as a modern science.
Delivered the Gifford Lectures, later published as The Varieties of Religious Experience, exploring the nature of human spirituality.
Published Pragmatism, effectively solidifying the American philosophical tradition that evaluates truths based on practical consequences.
Born in Marbach, Württemberg, Holy Roman Empire.
Published his first play, 'The Robbers' (Die Räuber), anonymously.
Appointed professor of history and philosophy in Jena.
Formed a close and highly productive friendship with Johann Wolfgang von Goethe.
Died in Weimar at the age of 45.
Born into slavery in Diamond, Missouri.
Became the first African American to earn a Bachelor of Science degree.
Invited by Booker T. Washington to head the agriculture department at Tuskegee Institute.
Published the research bulletin, 'How to Grow the Peanut and 105 Ways of Preparing it for Human Consumption'.
Died in Tuskegee, Alabama, leaving his life savings to found the George Washington Carver Research Foundation.
Born in Tus, Khorasan.
Abandoned his prestigious positions due to religious anguish and other causes, and traveled to Mecca and various regions as a Sufi ascetic.
Retired to his hometown and passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Incoherence of the Philosophers'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a book criticizing some philosophers for excessively transplanting Greek philosophy into Islam, holding thoughts that contradict the Quran.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences' and 'Deliverance from Error'
keyAchievements.description: Left behind masterworks such as 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences' and 'Deliverance from Error' which had a great influence on future generations.

keyAchievements.title: Harmonization of orthodox theology and Sufism
keyAchievements.description: Made his greatest contribution by harmonizing orthodox theology and Sufism, breathing new life into the Islamic religious system.

keyAchievements.title: Defended orthodox Islam
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to adhering to orthodox Islam by criticizing Ibn Sina's philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Provided a turning point for Islamic scholarship
keyAchievements.description: Brought a turning point to Islamic religious thought by breaking away Islamic scholarship from scholastic theology and reviving the Quran and Hadith.

keyAchievements.title: Established the status of Sufism
keyAchievements.description: Established the status of Sufism within Islam, making the masses of Muslims accept Sufism.

faq.question: How is Al-Ghazali evaluated?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the greatest Muslim after Muhammad, and is a significant figure in the fields of theology, philosophy, and Sufism.

faq.question: What are Al-Ghazali's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Incoherence of the Philosophers', 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences', and 'Deliverance from Error'.

faq.question: What is Al-Ghazali's greatest contribution?
faq.answer: His greatest contribution is the harmonization of orthodox theology and Sufism, and the defense of orthodox Islam by criticizing the philosophy of Ibn Sina.

faq.question: What turning point did Al-Ghazali bring to Islamic religious thought?
faq.answer: He broke Islamic scholarship away from scholastic theology, reintroduced esoteric elements, established the status of Sufism, and made philosophy more accessible to ordinary Muslims.

timeline.event: Birth
Secluded himself in the village of Qal'at Ibn Salama in the Oran region of Algeria and devoted himself to academic research

Moved to Cairo, Egypt, and engaged in active lecturing, gained the trust of the Mamluk Sultan Barquq, served as a professor in several academic institutions, and was appointed Chief Justice of the Maliki school
Death

keyAchievements.title: Kitab al-'Ibar (Book of Lessons)
keyAchievements.description: A world history that attempts to systematize the Islamic history of West Asia, containing records and examples of the beginnings and progress of Arabs, Persians, Berbers, and other eras.

keyAchievements.title: Muqaddimah (Prolegomena)
keyAchievements.description: An introduction to history included at the beginning of 'Kitab al-'Ibar', considered a world-class masterpiece that examines the circumstances and laws of societal formation and change, and seeks to answer fundamental questions of cultural history.

faq.question: Who is Ibn Khaldun?
faq.answer: Ibn Khaldun is a representative historian, thinker, and politician of the medieval Islamic world who was active from 1332 to 1406. He comes from the prominent Khaldun family, which exiled from Seville, Spain to Tunis.

faq.question: What are Ibn Khaldun's major works?
faq.answer: His major works are the world history 'Kitab al-'Ibar', which attempts to systematize West Asian Islamic history, and its introduction, 'Muqaddimah'. The 'Muqaddimah' is especially evaluated as a world-class masterpiece that examines the laws of societal formation and change.

faq.question: What was Ibn Khaldun's political career like?
faq.answer: He served as a secretary, diplomatic envoy, and chief justice in various Islamic regimes, including the Hafsid, Marinid, and Nasrid dynasties. However, his life was a series of wanderings due to involvement in conspiracies or being ousted by friction. These political experiences later had a huge impact on his ideological system as a scholar.

timeline.event: Born Moses ben Maimon in Córdoba, Almoravid Empire.
Completed the 'Commentary on the Mishnah', a cornerstone of Rabbinic literature.
Finished the 'Mishneh Torah', his monumental code of Jewish law.
Published 'The Guide for the Perplexed', a major philosophical work seeking to harmonize faith and reason.
Died in Fustat (Cairo), Egypt, and was later buried in Tiberias.
Born in eastern Iran or Central Asia (approximate date of existence).
Reportedly received his first divine revelation at the age of 30, seeing the supreme being Ahura Mazda.
Faced significant opposition from local priests, wandering for years to preach his monotheistic vision.
Converted King Vishtaspa, securing political support and ensuring the religion's survival.
Died, reportedly assassinated by a rival priest while praying at an altar.
Born in Alcala de Henares, a university town in Madrid
Followed an Italian cardinal to Rome, became a soldier, and participated in the Battle of Lepanto, where he suffered an injury that left his left hand crippled
Captured by pirates and lived as a slave in Algiers for 5 years
Paid his ransom with the help of the Trinitarian Order, returned to Madrid, and began to write
Published the novel 'La Galatea', but it did not gain popularity
Published Part 1 of 'Don Quixote', which gained immense popularity
Completed Part 2 of 'Don Quixote'
Passed away at the age of 69

keyAchievements.title: Don Quixote
keyAchievements.description: A work evaluated by many as the first modern novel in Europe.

keyAchievements.title: La Galatea
keyAchievements.description: Published the novel 'La Galatea', but it did not attract much popularity.

faq.question: What is Miguel de Cervantes's nickname?
faq.answer: He has the nickname 'Prince of Wits' (el Príncipe de los Ingenios).

faq.question: Where was Miguel de Cervantes born?
faq.answer: He was born in Alcala de Henares, a university town in Madrid.

faq.question: What were Miguel de Cervantes's main professions?
faq.answer: He was a novelist, poet, and playwright.

faq.question: How are Miguel de Cervantes's works evaluated?
faq.answer: His works are mentioned among the most important masterpieces in all of literature, and 'Don Quixote' in particular is evaluated as the first modern novel in Europe.

miguel-de-cervantes.missingDataNote: 어린 시절에 대한 정보가 거의 알려지지 않았습니다.

timeline.event: Born into an aristocratic Russian family at the Yasnaya Polyana estate.
