faq.question: What challenges did Ida B. Wells face because of her anti-lynching campaign?
faq.answer: Her newspaper office in Memphis was destroyed by a white mob, and she received numerous death threats, eventually forcing her to leave the South and relocate to Chicago.

faq.question: What is the Alpha Suffrage Club?
faq.answer: It is the first Black women's suffrage organization in Illinois, founded by Ida B. Wells in Chicago in 1913.

timeline.event: Born in Leiden, Netherlands.
Apprenticed for three years under painter Jacob van Swanenburgh.
Apprenticed for six months under painter Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam.
Opened his own studio in Leiden.
Began accepting students.
Moved to Amsterdam, began working as a professional portrait painter, and married Saskia van Uylenburgh.
Purchased an upscale house in Sint Antoniesbreestraat, Amsterdam.
His wife Saskia passed away shortly after the birth of their son Titus.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Chiaroscuro
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered the technique of chiaroscuro, maximizing the dramatic effect by dramatically contrasting light and dark.

keyAchievements.title: Innovations in Portraiture
keyAchievements.description: Moved away from conventional formal portraits, emphasizing the inner psychology and emotional states of the subjects through his portraits.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Self-Portraits
keyAchievements.description: Left behind numerous self-portraits over his lifetime, documenting the process of his self-reflection and changes in his life.

keyAchievements.title: Excellence in Printmaking
keyAchievements.description: Left a significant mark on the history of printmaking with his outstanding skill in etching, elevating printmaking to an independent art form.

keyAchievements.title: Dutch Golden Age Art
keyAchievements.description: Led the Dutch Golden Age art of the 17th century, creating a wide variety of works encompassing religious themes, history paintings, and landscapes.

faq.question: What is Rembrandt's most famous painting?
faq.answer: His most famous painting is 'The Night Watch' (De Nachtwacht), a group portrait completed in 1642 depicting a militia company moving into action.

faq.question: Why did Rembrandt paint so many self-portraits?
faq.answer: He painted self-portraits as a means to explore his inner self, document his aging process, practice facial expressions and lighting, and sometimes to showcase his skills.

faq.question: What technique is Rembrandt best known for?
faq.answer: He is famous for chiaroscuro, a technique that highlights the subject and creates dramatic tension by strongly contrasting light and shadow.

faq.question: Did Rembrandt enjoy wealth during his lifetime?
faq.answer: He achieved great success and wealth in his early years, but later in life, he suffered from financial difficulties due to lavish spending and changes in artistic trends, eventually declaring bankruptcy.

timeline.event: Born in Portsmouth, England
Father John Dickens is imprisoned for debt; Charles begins working at a blacking factory
Starts career as a journalist
Publishes 'Sketches by Boz'
Gains explosive popularity with the serialization of 'The Pickwick Papers'; marries Catherine Hogarth
Publishes 'Oliver Twist'
First visit to the United States
Publishes 'A Christmas Carol'
Publishes 'David Copperfield'
Purchases Gad's Hill Place, a house he admired since childhood
Separates from his wife Catherine
Publishes 'A Tale of Two Cities'
Publishes 'Great Expectations'
Involved in the Staplehurst rail crash
Second visit to the United States and conducts reading tours
Dies of a stroke; buried in Poet's Corner at Westminster Abbey
《데이비드 코퍼필드》를 출판했다.
《A Child's History of England》를 출판했다.
《황폐한 집》을 출판했다.
《어려운 시절》을 출판했다.
《작은 도릿》을 출판했다.
《두 도시 이야기》를 출판했다.
《위대한 유산》을 출판했다.
《Our Mutual Friend》를 출판했다.
두 번째 미국 방문이 이루어졌다.
《에드윈 드루드의 비밀》을 출판했다.
58세의 나이로 개즈 힐에서 숨을 거두었다.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of memorable characters
keyAchievements.description: Created some of the world's most famous fictional characters, including Ebenezer Scrooge, Oliver Twist, and David Copperfield.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for social reform
keyAchievements.description: Strongly criticized the social contradictions, poverty, and child labor issues brought about by the Industrial Revolution through his novels, contributing to social reform.

keyAchievements.title: Popularization of the serialization format
keyAchievements.description: Greatly contributed to the popularization of the novel serialization format by publishing novels in weekly or monthly installments, receiving tremendous response from readers.

keyAchievements.title: Modernization of Christmas
keyAchievements.description: Often referred to as 'The Man Who Invented Christmas,' his work 'A Christmas Carol' played a significant role in establishing the modern culture and concept of Christmas.

faq.question: What is Charles Dickens's most representative work?
faq.answer: His representative works include 'Oliver Twist', 'A Christmas Carol', 'David Copperfield', 'A Tale of Two Cities', and 'Great Expectations'.

faq.question: What was Dickens's childhood like?
faq.answer: His father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a blacking factory at the age of 12. This harsh experience deeply influenced his later works.

faq.question: Why did Dickens's novels usually come out in serial form?
faq.answer: He published them in weekly or monthly magazines to make his works accessible to the public at an affordable price, and this serialization method became very popular during the Victorian era.

faq.question: Did Dickens's works actually influence society?
faq.answer: Yes, his vivid descriptions of the plight of the poor and the poor conditions of child labor raised public awareness and contributed to real social reforms.

faq.question: How did Dickens die?
faq.answer: He died of a stroke at his home, Gad's Hill Place, on June 9, 1870, at the age of 58.

charles-dickens.missingDataNote: 어린 시절 가족의 이사 및 구두약 공장 취직, 변호사 사무실 사환 등의 사건은 나이만 명시되어 있고 정확한 연도가 원문에 직접 명시되어 있지 않아 타임라인에서 제외했습니다.

timeline.event: Born as the youngest child to Captain Stephen Barton and Sarah Stone Barton in North Oxford, Massachusetts.
Saved the life of her seriously injured brother David over two years.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of the American Red Cross
keyAchievements.description: Founded the American Red Cross in 1881 and served as its first president for 23 years, leading national relief efforts.

keyAchievements.title: Battlefield Nursing Activities
keyAchievements.description: Risked her life to provide medical supplies and nurse wounded soldiers directly on the front lines during the American Civil War, earning the nickname 'Angel of the Battlefield'.

faq.question: Who was Clara Barton?
faq.answer: She was a pioneering American nurse, the founder of the American Red Cross, and an activist for women's suffrage.

faq.question: What is Clara Barton's nickname?
faq.answer: She is known as the 'Angel of the Battlefield' for her dedication to nursing wounded soldiers directly on the front lines during the American Civil War.

clara-barton.missingDataNote: 미국 남북 전쟁 당시 간호 활동 및 전쟁 이후 여성 관련 인도주의 활동에 대한 구체적인 정보가 원문에 없습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Leipzig
Obtained a bachelor's degree in philosophy from the University of Leipzig
Obtained a master's degree in philosophy
The University of Leipzig refused his doctoral application in law due to his young age
Obtained a doctorate in law and was granted the right to practice from the University of Altdorf
Served the Elector of Mainz
Stayed in Paris, expanding his mathematical and scientific knowledge
Devised the binary number system
Entered the service of the House of Hanover as librarian and political advisor
Developed infinitesimal calculus
Discovered the concept of kinetic energy
Traveled through Germany, Austria, and Italy to write the history of the House of Brunswick
Proposed the idea of establishing an academy to the Elector of Brandenburg, leading to the creation of the Prussian Academy of Sciences
The Royal Society recognized Newton's priority in inventing calculus, declaring Leibniz a plagiarist
Wrote 'Monadology' based on his philosophical thoughts
George Louis, Elector of Hanover, ascended the British throne as George I, but Leibniz was forbidden from going to London
Died in Hanover; his funeral was held quietly
유럽 연합이 나올 것이라는 예상을 번복함.
사망함.
《인간오성신론》이 출판됨.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Infinitesimal Calculus
keyAchievements.description: Developed infinitesimal calculus independently of Isaac Newton, and the mathematical notation he devised is the one mainly used today.

keyAchievements.title: Devised the Binary Number System
keyAchievements.description: Refined the binary number system, which forms the basis of modern computer architecture.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of a Mechanical Calculator
keyAchievements.description: Invented the stepped reckoner, the first mechanical calculator capable of performing all four arithmetic operations.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Kinetic Energy (Vis Viva)
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the concept of 'vis viva' (living force), anticipating the law of conservation of energy.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Monads
keyAchievements.description: In philosophy, he proposed the concept of 'monads', explaining the universe as being composed of simple, immaterial substances.

keyAchievements.title: Principle of Sufficient Reason
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the principle of sufficient reason, stating that 'nothing happens without a reason'.

keyAchievements.title: Law of Continuity
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the law of continuity, stating that 'nature makes no leaps'.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the 'Best of All Possible Worlds'
keyAchievements.description: Optimistically argued that our universe is the 'best of all possible worlds' created by God.

keyAchievements.title: 도서관학 발전
keyAchievements.description: 독일 하노버와 볼펜뷔텔의 도서관에서 사서로 일하며 도서관학의 창시자 중 한 명으로, 새로운 도서관 건물을 설계하고 독자적인 색인 체계를 고안했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 베를린 학사원 설립
keyAchievements.description: 1700년에 베를린 학사원 설립을 직접적으로 추진했으며, 죽을 때까지 그 학회의 회장을 맡았습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 중국 철학 연구
keyAchievements.description: 『주역』의 괘가 2진법 숫자와 연관성이 있음을 주목하고, 유학적 개념을 유럽인들의 신앙에 수용시키려고 시도했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 《변신론》 저술
keyAchievements.description: 저서 《변신론》에서 세상에는 반드시 불완전함이 존재함을 '있을 수 있는 세계 중 최상의 세계'라는 용어를 통해 증명하려 했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 유럽 연맹 제안
keyAchievements.description: 1677년에 각 나라 대표가 모여 운영되는 유럽 연맹을 제안하여 유럽 연합의 출현을 예견했습니다.

faq.question: What was the calculus priority dispute?
faq.answer: A fierce dispute arose between Isaac Newton and Leibniz over who first invented calculus. Today, it is recognized that both discovered it independently.

faq.question: Why are Leibniz's mathematical notations important?
faq.answer: The notations used by Leibniz, such as the integral symbol (∫) and the derivative notation (d/dx), were intuitive and convenient, playing a significant role in the development of mathematics.

faq.question: What is Leibniz's Monadology?
faq.answer: It is a philosophy asserting that the universe is composed of simple, immaterial, soul-like entities called 'monads', which do not interact with each other but form a harmony pre-established by God.

faq.question: What role did Leibniz play in the history of computers?
faq.answer: He refined the binary number system, the basis of modern computers, and invented a mechanical calculator capable of performing all four arithmetic operations, establishing him as a pioneer of computer science.

faq.question: How did Leibniz spend his later years?
faq.answer: He suffered a great loss of reputation due to the calculus priority dispute with Newton. His employer, George I, moved to England, but Leibniz was left behind in Hanover, where he died lonely and sick with gout.

faq.question: 라이프니츠는 결혼했었나요?
faq.answer: 라이프니츠는 미혼으로 살았습니다.

faq.question: 라이프니츠의 종교는 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 라이프니츠의 종교는 개신교였으며, 철학적 유신론자였습니다.

faq.question: 라이프니츠는 어떤 학사원 설립에 기여했나요?
faq.answer: 라이프니츠는 1700년에 설립된 베를린 학사원의 설립을 직접적으로 추진했으며, 죽을 때까지 그 학회의 회장을 맡았습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Marton, Yorkshire, England
Began working as an apprentice on a merchant ship
Volunteered for the Royal Navy
Promoted to ship's master; participated in the Seven Years' War and surveyed the St. Lawrence River in Canada
Married Elizabeth Batts
Commanded the HMS Endeavour on his first voyage, tasked with observing the transit of Venus and searching for the 'Unknown Southern Land' (Terra Australis)
Arrived in Tahiti and observed the transit of Venus
Explored the coastline of New Zealand and discovered the Cook Strait
Arrived on the eastern coast of Australia (Botany Bay) and claimed it for Britain
The Endeavour was damaged on the Great Barrier Reef, undergoing repairs
Returned to England, completing the first voyage
Commanded the HMS Resolution on his second voyage, circumnavigating the globe as far south as possible to search for the 'Unknown Southern Land'
Crossed the Antarctic Circle for the first time in history
Returned to England, completing the second voyage; proved that a vast habitable southern continent did not exist
Commanded the HMS Resolution on his third voyage to discover the Northwest Passage
Discovered the Hawaiian Islands and named them the 'Sandwich Islands'
Explored the western coast of North America and reached the Bering Strait but was blocked by ice
Returned to Hawaii, got into a conflict with the indigenous people, and was killed
힉스곶으로 명명했던 육지를 목격하며 오스트레일리아 대륙 동쪽 해안에 도달한 최초의 유럽인이 됨
오스트레일리아 대륙에 처음 상륙 (현재 커널반도)
그레이트 배리어 리프 북쪽에 인데버 호가 부딪혀 난파
포제션섬에 상륙하여 오스트레일리아 동해안의 영국 영유권을 선언
바타비아에서 말라리아와 이질로 많은 일행이 사망한 후 귀국
인데버호가 남 잉글랜드의 다운즈에 닻을 내리고 쿡은 켄트로 하선
유럽인으로서 처음으로 남극권에 돌입 (두 번째 항해)
통가, 이스터섬, 뉴칼레도니아, 바누아투에 도착 후 남아메리카 남단을 돌아 사우스 조지아 및 샌드위치 제도를 발견 (두 번째 항해)
하와이 제도를 방문한 최초의 유럽인이 됨 (세 번째 항해)
사망

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of Australia and New Zealand
keyAchievements.description: Was the first European to encounter the eastern coastline of Australia and the Hawaiian Islands, and completed the first recorded circumnavigation of New Zealand.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of accurate maps
keyAchievements.description: Produced maps of unprecedented accuracy with his excellent seamanship and surveying skills, changing the shape of the world map.

keyAchievements.title: Scurvy prevention
keyAchievements.description: Significantly reduced the incidence of scurvy among his crew by enforcing strict hygiene and providing diets including foods like sauerkraut.

keyAchievements.title: Search for the Northwest Passage and Antarctica
keyAchievements.description: Crossed the Antarctic Circle for the first time on his second voyage, and explored the Bering Strait in search of the Northwest Passage on his third voyage.

keyAchievements.title: 해도 제작
keyAchievements.description: 뉴펀들랜드와 뉴질랜드의 해도를 제작했으며, 특히 뉴펀들랜드 해안의 대규모의 정확한 지도를 처음으로 만들었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 괴혈병 사망자 없는 세계 일주
keyAchievements.description: 첫 번째 항해에서 사상 처음으로 괴혈병에 의한 사망자를 내지 않고 세계 일주를 달성했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 영국 식민지 개척 기여
keyAchievements.description: 태평양을 남쪽 끝에서부터 북쪽 끝까지 탐험하여 영국의 식민지 개척에 크게 이바지했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 대항해시대의 종결
keyAchievements.description: 소위 대항해시대의 끝을 장식한 인물로, 그의 탐험을 전후하여 세계의 거의 모든 지역이 유럽인에게 알려지게 되었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 뉴질랜드 해안 지도 제작 및 해협 발견
keyAchievements.description: 뉴질랜드 해안의 거의 완벽한 지도를 제작하고 뉴질랜드 남북섬을 나누는 쿡 해협을 발견했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 남극권 최초 돌입
keyAchievements.description: 유럽인으로서 처음으로 남극권에 돌입하는 위대한 업적을 달성했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 남방 대륙 탐험 종지부
keyAchievements.description: 남극 대륙을 발견하였지만, 남방 대륙이 인류가 거주 가능한 위도에는 존재하지 않는다는 것을 확인하여 전설의 남방 대륙 탐험에 종지부를 찍었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 정확한 경도 결정
keyAchievements.description: 두 번째 항해에서 크로노미터가 활약하여 정확한 경도를 결정하는 데 크게 기여했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 북서부 해안 탐사 및 해도 제작
keyAchievements.description: 캘리포니아에서 베링 해협에 이르는 미국의 북서부 해안 대부분의 해도를 제작하고 알래스카의 곳곳을 파악했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 세계 일주 항해 일지 작성
keyAchievements.description: 자필 원고로 세계 일주 항해 일지를 남겼습니다(제2 항해).

faq.question: What is Captain James Cook's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: Leading three Pacific voyages, discovering the eastern coast of Australia and the Hawaiian Islands, and mapping the Pacific Ocean in great detail.

faq.question: Did James Cook discover Australia?
faq.answer: He was not the first European to discover Australia. Dutch explorers had reached the western coast earlier, but Cook was the first to explore and map the eastern coast.

faq.question: How did Captain Cook die?
faq.answer: During his third voyage in 1779, after returning to Hawaii, a conflict arose with the indigenous people over a stolen boat, and he was killed during the altercation.

faq.question: Did Captain Cook find the Northwest Passage?
faq.answer: No, he explored up to the Bering Strait but could not find a navigable Northwest Passage because he was blocked by ice.

faq.question: What was Captain Cook's relationship with the indigenous people like?
faq.answer: He generally tried to maintain peaceful relations, but there were also cultural misunderstandings and violent clashes, a legacy that remains controversial today.

faq.question: 그의 첫 번째 항해의 공식적인 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 왕립 학회는 금성 일면통과 관측을 위해 그를 남태평양에 파견했습니다.

faq.question: 그의 두 번째 항해의 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 남방 대륙(테라 아우스트랄리스)을 발견하기 위해 왕립 학회에 의해 다시 파견되었습니다.

faq.question: 그의 세 번째 항해의 공식적인 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 북극해를 빠져나와 태평양과 대서양을 잇는 북서 항로를 탐색하는 것이었습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Hochheim (or Tambach), Thuringia.
Traveled to Paris to study.
Appointed Prior in Erfurt and Vicar of Thuringia.
Obtained a master's degree (Magister) at the University of Paris.
Appointed Provincial of the newly established province of Saxony.
Appointed Vicar General of the province of Bohemia.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of German Mysticism
keyAchievements.description: Known as the father of German mysticism, profoundly influencing later mystics such as Johannes Tauler and Heinrich Seuse.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the German Language
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role in the development of the German language by delivering sermons and writing treatises not only in Latin but also in Middle High German.

keyAchievements.title: Theology of the Birth of God in the Soul
keyAchievements.description: Emphasized that the divine spark (Fünklein) exists in the deepest part of the human soul, and that humans can achieve union with God there.

faq.question: Who was Meister Eckhart?
faq.answer: Meister Eckhart was a German Catholic priest, theologian, philosopher, and mystic belonging to the Dominican Order.

faq.question: What does 'Meister' mean?
faq.answer: 'Meister' is a title referring to the academic degree of Master in Theology he obtained in Paris, indicating his status as an authoritative teacher.

faq.question: Why was Meister Eckhart accused of heresy?
faq.answer: Some of his expressions emphasizing the union of God and the human soul were deemed radical by the church authorities of the time, leading to suspicions of pantheism or self-deification.

meister-eckhart.missingDataNote: 도미니크회 가입의 정확한 연도(15세 때로만 명시), 3부작 저술의 정확한 연도 및 내용, 프란체스코회와의 논쟁 및 이단 비난의 구체적인 상세 내용은 원문에 없어 생략되었습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Horsens, Denmark
Went to sea and engaged in voyages to the East Indies

keyAchievements.title: First Kamchatka Expedition
keyAchievements.description: Through the First Kamchatka Expedition, he confirmed that the Asian and North American continents were separated.

keyAchievements.title: Second Kamchatka Expedition
keyAchievements.description: Led the massive Second Kamchatka Expedition (Great Northern Expedition), charting the Arctic coast of Siberia and discovering the coast of Alaska and the Aleutian Islands.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of the Bering Strait and Bering Sea
keyAchievements.description: Explored the strait and sea separating Asia and North America, which were named the Bering Strait and the Bering Sea in his honor.

faq.question: What is Vitus Bering's nationality?
faq.answer: He was born in Denmark but spent most of his life exploring as an officer in the Russian Navy.

faq.question: What did Vitus Bering discover during his first expedition?
faq.answer: He confirmed that the Asian and North American continents are not connected but separated by a strait.

faq.question: How did Vitus Bering die?
faq.answer: During his second expedition, he was shipwrecked on an uninhabited island on his return journey from Alaska and died of scurvy along with 28 of his crew members.

faq.question: What geographical features are named after Vitus Bering?
faq.answer: The Bering Strait, the Bering Sea, Bering Island, and the Bering Glacier are named after him.

vitus-bering.missingDataNote: 러시아 해군에서 일한 기간에 대한 정보가 부족합니다. 표트르 1세의 명을 받아 아시아와 아메리카 두 대륙 사이의 해협을 탐험한 정확한 연도에 대한 정보가 부족합니다. 캄차카반도, 코만도르스키 제도, 알류샨 열도, 알래스카를 탐험한 정확한 연도에 대한 정보가 부족합니다.

timeline.event: Appointed to lead an expedition tasked by King John II of Portugal to find the southern tip of Africa
Sailed past the coast of Angola
Sailed past present-day Namibia
Caught in a massive storm off the coast of South Africa and drifted for 13 days
Landed at Mossel Bay after turning the Cape of Good Hope
Reached the Great Fish River (Rio do Infante) but was forced to turn back due to crew demands
Discovered the Cape of Good Hope on the return journey and named it the 'Cape of Storms' (Cabo das Tormentas)

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Cape of Good Hope
keyAchievements.description: Became the first European to discover the Cape of Good Hope at the southern tip of Africa in 1488.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered the sea route to India
keyAchievements.description: Proved that the Atlantic and Indian Oceans were connected, opening up the possibility of a direct sea route from Europe to Asia (India).

faq.question: What is Bartolomeu Dias's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: He was the first European to sail to the southern tip of Africa and discover the Cape of Good Hope.

faq.question: Why is the Cape of Good Hope called the Cape of Good Hope?
faq.answer: Dias originally named it the 'Cape of Storms' (Cabo das Tormentas), but King John II of Portugal renamed it the 'Cape of Good Hope' (Cabo da Boa Esperança), signifying the hope of reaching India.

faq.question: Did Bartolomeu Dias reach India?
faq.answer: No. He proved the possibility of a route to India, but had to turn back due to a lack of supplies and his crew's demands. It was Vasco da Gama who actually reached India later.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a vicar in Berkeley, Gloucestershire.
Became an apprentice to surgeon Daniel Ludlow in Sodbury.
Began studying medicine under John Hunter at St George's Hospital, University of London.
Became a general practitioner in his hometown.
Developed an interest in smallpox.
Gathered his observations on smallpox.
Married Catharine Kingscote.
Received his MD from the University of St Andrews in Scotland.
John Hunter died, ending their friendship.
Successfully performed the first vaccination on a local boy.
Reported the results of 23 experiments to the Royal Society and published his paper 'An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae'.
Received a grant from the government (part of a total of £30,000).
The Royal Jennerian Society was founded to promote vaccination in London.
Received the remainder of the grant from the government.
Made his last visit to London.
Died of a stroke in his hometown.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Cowpox Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the cowpox vaccine, earning him the title 'Father of Immunology'.

keyAchievements.title: Coined the Term Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Coined the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from the Latin word 'vacca', meaning cow.

keyAchievements.title: Development and Distribution of Smallpox Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Successfully performed the first vaccination on a boy on May 14, 1796, developing the smallpox vaccine and contributing to its worldwide distribution.

faq.question: Who is Edward Jenner?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was an English physician, the discoverer of the cowpox vaccine, and the father of immunology.

faq.question: When was Edward Jenner born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was born on May 17, 1749, and died on January 26, 1823.

faq.question: What led Edward Jenner to coin the term vaccine?
faq.answer: Jenner created the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from 'vacca', the Latin word for cow.

faq.question: What are the main achievements of Edward Jenner?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner discovered the cowpox vaccine and successfully administered the first vaccination to a boy on May 14, 1796, developing the smallpox vaccine.

timeline.event: December 10: Emily Elizabeth Dickinson was born in Amherst, Massachusetts, USA.
May 15: Died at the age of fifty-five.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry Creation
keyAchievements.description: Wrote nearly 2,000 poems.

keyAchievements.title: Main Themes of Her Poetry
keyAchievements.description: Created poems with main themes including love, death, parting, the soul, and heaven.

keyAchievements.title: Lack of Public Recognition in Life
keyAchievements.description: Was largely unrecognized by the public during her lifetime, with only about 4 of her poems published in collections.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Exchanges in Life
keyAchievements.description: Consistently exchanged poems through correspondence with a few poets and intellectuals who recognized her talent during her lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Recognition of Genius
keyAchievements.description: Her genius was widely recognized after her death when her younger sister, Lavinia Norcross Dickinson, published her collections of poems.

keyAchievements.title: Musical Reinterpretation of Poetry
keyAchievements.description: Her poems have been set to music by renowned composers such as Aaron Copland.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson recognized by the public during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not recognized by the public during her lifetime, and only 4 of her poems were published.

faq.question: What themes did Emily Dickinson's poetry primarily deal with?
faq.answer: Her poetry mainly dealt with themes of love, death, separation, the soul, and heaven.

faq.question: How many poems did Emily Dickinson write?
faq.answer: She wrote nearly 2,000 poems.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson a reclusive poet?
faq.answer: She is known as a reclusive poet who strictly refrained from going out, but as a child, she had a lively personality, going out to the fields every day and playing with neighborhood children.

timeline.event: Born in Fuendetodos, Aragon, Spain.
Goya's family bought a house in Zaragoza and moved there a few years later.
Submitted an application to the Royal Academy of Fine Arts but was rejected.
Was deeply moved by the works of Velázquez and Rembrandt in Madrid.
Submitted an application to the Royal Academy of Fine Arts but was rejected again.
Traveled to Italy and won second prize at a painting exhibition in Parma; returned to Zaragoza later that year to become a painter for the Basilica of Our Lady of the Pillar.
Married Josefa, Bayeu's sister.
Painted the portrait of the Count of Floridablanca, a close associate of Charles III.
Began painting portraits of Charles III.
Charles III died.
Charles IV ascended to the throne, and Goya became a court painter.
Contracted cholera and lost his hearing due to a high fever.
Painted a series of fantastical works.
Painted the frescoes of the Royal Chapel of St. Anthony of La Florida in Madrid.
Published the print series 'Los Caprichos' and became the First Court Painter.
Painted 'Charles IV of Spain and His Family' and 'The Nude Maja'.
Painted 'The Clothed Maja'.
The Peninsular War broke out between France and Spain, and Goya left documentary paintings of it.
Created the print series 'The Disasters of War'.
His wife Josefa died.
The Spanish Inquisition confiscated the Maja series, deeming them obscene.
The French army retreated, and Ferdinand VII returned to the throne.
Moved to Bordeaux, France.
Returned to Spain but went back to Bordeaux due to deteriorating health.
Died at the age of 82.

keyAchievements.title: Status as a Romantic Painter and Printmaker
keyAchievements.description: Spain's representative Romantic painter and printmaker, known as the representative of 18th-century Spanish painting and a genius artist who foreshadowed Impressionism.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a Court Painter
keyAchievements.description: Became a court painter in 1789 and the First Court Painter in 1799, painting portraits of the king, queen, and many royals and nobles.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of the Print Series 'Los Caprichos'
keyAchievements.description: Published 'Los Caprichos' in 1799, created using aquatint, to expose the reality and superstitions of the corrupt Catholic Church.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of War Documentary Paintings and Prints
keyAchievements.description: Left documentary paintings of the Peninsular War, 'The Second of May 1808' and 'The Third of May 1808', and created the print series 'The Disasters of War' depicting the horrors of war.

keyAchievements.title: 'Maja' Series
keyAchievements.description: Painted 'The Nude Maja' and 'The Clothed Maja', with 'The Nude Maja' being considered the 'first life-size female nude in Western art'.

keyAchievements.title: Black Paintings Series
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, at the 'Quinta del Sordo' (House of the Deaf Man), he created the 'Black Paintings' series depicting madness hidden behind reason, famously including 'Saturn Devouring His Son'.

faq.question: How is Francisco Goya evaluated as a painter?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as Spain's leading Romantic painter and printmaker, the representative of 18th-century Spanish painting, and a genius who heralded the beginning of Impressionism.

faq.question: When did Goya lose his hearing?
faq.answer: He lost his hearing in 1792 due to a high fever after contracting cholera.

faq.question: What are Goya's major print series?
faq.answer: His major print series include 'Los Caprichos', published in 1799, and 'The Disasters of War', created between 1810 and 1820.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Goya's 'Maja' series?
faq.answer: 'The Nude Maja' and 'The Clothed Maja' portray the same woman in the identical pose, and are evaluated as 'the first life-size female nudes in Western art'.

faq.question: What was the trend of Goya's works in his later years?
faq.answer: After losing his hearing in 1792, his paintings became darker; following the Peninsular War, he painted works with heavy themes and dark tones; and in his later years, he left the 'Black Paintings' series at the 'House of the Deaf Man'.

timeline.event: Mary Ann Evans (George Eliot) was born.
Family moved to Griff House.
Mother died.
Completed English translation of Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus'.
Father died.
Traveled to Switzerland with the Brays.
Returned to England and moved to London.
Became assistant editor of 'The Westminster Review'.
Began contributing essays and reviews to 'The Westminster Review'.
Translated Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity'.
Resigned from 'The Westminster Review'.
Published the novel 'Adam Bede'.
Published the novel 'The Mill on the Floss'.
Published the novel 'Silas Marner'.
Published the novel 'Romola'.
Published the novel 'Middlemarch'.
Published the novel 'Daniel Deronda'.
George Eliot died.

keyAchievements.title: Important Writer of the Victorian Era
keyAchievements.description: Worked as a novelist, poet, journalist, and translator, being one of the most important writers of the Victorian era.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Major Novels
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a total of 7 novels, including 'Adam Bede', 'The Mill on the Floss', 'Silas Marner', 'Romola', 'Middlemarch', and 'Daniel Deronda', showcasing realism and psychological insight.

keyAchievements.title: Translation of Philosophical Books
keyAchievements.description: Translated Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus' (1846) and Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity' (1854).

keyAchievements.title: Editorial Work at 'The Westminster Review'
keyAchievements.description: Worked as the assistant editor of 'The Westminster Review' from 1851 to mid-1854, carrying out practical publishing tasks and contributing many essays and reviews.

keyAchievements.title: Overcoming Prejudice Through a Pen Name
keyAchievements.description: Used the pen name 'George Eliot' to escape prejudice against female writers and to have her works evaluated objectively.

faq.question: What was George Eliot's real name?
faq.answer: George Eliot's real name was Mary Ann Evans.

faq.question: What was the main reason George Eliot used a pen name?
faq.answer: To escape the prejudice that female writers only write lighthearted romance novels, to be evaluated separately from her role as an editor and critic, and to avoid scandals regarding her private life.

faq.question: What are George Eliot's representative novel works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Adam Bede', 'The Mill on the Floss', 'Silas Marner', 'Romola', 'Middlemarch', and 'Daniel Deronda'.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of her novels?
faq.answer: Most of her novels are set in rural England and are famous for their realism and psychological insight.

faq.question: What major works did George Eliot translate?
faq.answer: She translated Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus' and Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity'.

timeline.event: Most historians believe he was born in England.
Left Gravesend on the Hopewell to begin his first significant voyage.
The Hopewell and its crew returned to England.
Set sail again on the Hopewell to search for the Northeast Passage.
Commissioned by the Dutch East India Company to search for the Northeast Passage on the Halve Maen (Half Moon).
Set sail on the Discovery to discover the Northwest Passage.
Disappeared in Hudson Bay after a mutiny forced him, his son John, and 7 loyal crew members into a small boat.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Hudson River, Hudson Strait, and Hudson Bay
keyAchievements.description: Explored northern Canada in 1610 and discovered the Hudson River, Hudson Strait, and Hudson Bay.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of North American Waterways
keyAchievements.description: Failed to find a passage to Asia but discovered various waterways in North America.

keyAchievements.title: Laying the Foundation for Colonization and Trade
keyAchievements.description: His discoveries laid the groundwork for further European expeditions for colonization and trade in North America.

faq.question: When was Henry Hudson born?
faq.answer: Most historians believe Henry Hudson was born in England around 1565.

faq.question: How did Henry Hudson disappear?
faq.answer: On June 23, 1611, following a mutiny by his crew, he, his son John, and 7 loyal crew members were forced into a small boat and left adrift in Hudson Bay, never to be seen again.

faq.question: What was the main purpose of Henry Hudson's explorations?
faq.answer: The main purpose of Henry Hudson's explorations was to find the Northwest Passage or Northeast Passage to Asia.

faq.question: What legacy did Henry Hudson's explorations leave behind?
faq.answer: His discoveries laid the foundation for North American colonization and trade, and many of the areas he explored and navigated are named after him today.

timeline.event: The approximate period when historians believe Homer lived and composed his epic poems, though dates range widely from the 12th to the 8th century BCE.
The period when the 'Iliad' and the 'Odyssey' are believed to have been composed in the oral tradition.

keyAchievements.title: The Iliad
keyAchievements.description: Composed the epic poem detailing the Trojan War, focusing on the wrath of Achilles and exploring themes of heroism, glory, and fate.

faq.question: Was Homer a real person?
faq.answer: This is known as the 'Homeric Question'. Many modern scholars believe 'Homer' may not have been a single historical person, but rather a name attached to a long, collective oral tradition of bards over centuries.

faq.question: Was he really blind?
faq.answer: The tradition that Homer was a blind bard comes from ancient legends and a self-referential character (Demodocus) in the Odyssey. However, there is no historical evidence to confirm whether he was blind, or even if he existed as a single individual.

faq.question: Did the Trojan War actually happen?
faq.answer: Archaeological excavations at Troy (Hisarlik in modern Turkey) show the city was destroyed around the time of the alleged war (c. 1200 BCE). While the epic involves gods and mythological elements, it is likely based on a kernel of historical truth regarding conflicts in the region.

faq.question: When were the 'Iliad' and the 'Odyssey' written?
faq.answer: These poems are believed to have been composed around the end of the 8th century BC or the early 7th century BC.

timeline.event: Born at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.
Studied at the Reading Abbey Girls' School in Reading, Berkshire.
Started writing novels at the age of 14.
Wrote the epistolary novel 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Met a young Irishman named Tom Lefroy and wrote 'First Impressions'.
Began writing 'Sense and Sensibility' based on 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Her father relinquished his rectory and the family moved to Bath, Somerset.
Received a marriage proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither but rejected it the next day.
Sold 'Lady Susan' to Crosby & Co.
Her father died.
Settled in a cottage in Chawton with the help of her third brother, Edward.
Submitted 'Mansfield Park' and published 'Sense and Sensibility' anonymously.
Published 'Pride and Prejudice', based on 'First Impressions'.
Published 'Mansfield Park'.
Dedicated 'Emma' to the Prince Regent (later George IV) shortly before its publication.
Her health frequently deteriorated, leaving her bedridden for long periods.
Died in Winchester at the age of 42 while writing 'Sanditon'.
'Northanger Abbey' and 'Persuasion' were published posthumously.

keyAchievements.title: Began Writing Novels
keyAchievements.description: Started writing stories at the age of 14 and grew as a novelist with the support of her family.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Sense and Sensibility'
keyAchievements.description: One of her first works published anonymously in 1811.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Pride and Prejudice'
keyAchievements.description: Published in January 1813; the original draft was titled 'First Impressions'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Dedication of 'Emma'
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to the Prince Regent (later George IV) and published in December 1815.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the 'Great Tradition' of the English Novel
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as having founded the 'Great Tradition' of the English novel by addressing the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

keyAchievements.title: Socially Critical Realism Literature
keyAchievements.description: Created a sophisticated literary world based on realism, satirizing the material-oriented trends and hypocrisy of contemporary society with keen observation and a sharp critical perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Literary Re-evaluation and Pop Culture Influence
keyAchievements.description: Elevated to the literary canon through critics' acclaim from the late 19th century onwards, and in the late 20th century, her works became a popular cult phenomenon, being adapted into films and plays.

faq.question: When was Jane Austen born?
faq.answer: She was born on December 16, 1775, at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.

faq.question: What are Jane Austen's representative works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Sense and Sensibility' (1811), 'Pride and Prejudice' (1813), 'Mansfield Park' (1814), and 'Emma' (1816).

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Jane Austen's novels?
faq.answer: Her novels are characterized by depicting the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

faq.question: Was Jane Austen famous during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not very famous during her lifetime, but in the 20th century, her works gained massive popularity and were adapted into films multiple times.

faq.question: Did Jane Austen ever marry?
faq.answer: She had a romance with a young man named Tom Lefroy, but it did not lead to marriage. She received a proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither in 1802 but rejected it the next day.

faq.question: When did Jane Austen die?
faq.answer: She died on July 18, 1817, at the age of 42.

timeline.event: Born in Pentonville, London, England.
Began learning Greek.
Began learning Latin, Euclid, and algebra, and acted as a tutor for his younger siblings.
Reached the level of being able to read original texts of Plato and Demosthenes.
Started studying scholastic logic and original texts of Aristotle's logic.
Began studying political economy.
Stayed in France for a year and took courses in chemistry, zoology, and logic at the Faculty of Sciences in Montpellier.
Suffered from a nervous breakdown.
Founded the Utilitarian Society with his friends.
Published 'A System of Logic'.
Published 'Essays on Some Unsettled Questions of Political Economy'.
Published 'Principles of Political Economy'.
Married Harriet Taylor.
Harriet Taylor died, and he retired from the East India Company.
Published 'On Liberty'.
Published 'Utilitarianism' and 'Considerations on Representative Government'.
Elected as Lord Rector of the University of St Andrews and elected to the House of Commons as a Member of Parliament for the City of Westminster.
Term as Member of Parliament ended.
Became the first person in Parliament to advocate for women's right to vote, and published 'The Subjection of Women'.
Died in Avignon, France, and 'Autobiography' was published.
Published 'Three Essays on Religion'.
Published 'Chapters on Socialism'.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the System of Logic
keyAchievements.description: Published 'A System of Logic' in 1843, completing Bacon's inductive logic and presenting a groundbreaking methodology for natural science research.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Liberal Political Theory
keyAchievements.description: Left behind a classic work on the concept of liberty through 'On Liberty', presenting the basic principles of modern liberal democracy by defending freedom of expression as a fundamental right of citizens.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Utilitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Developed Bentham's utilitarianism, his godfather and teacher, into his own system by adding Saint-Simonianism and Romanticism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for Women's Rights and Social Reform
keyAchievements.description: As a Member of Parliament, he advocated for various social reforms including women's rights, proportional representation, labor unions, and cooperative organization of agriculture, and was the first to argue for women's suffrage in Parliament.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Market Socialism Theory
keyAchievements.description: Considered one of the early theorists of market socialism for advocating the replacement of capitalist enterprises with worker cooperatives.

faq.question: How was John Stuart Mill educated?
faq.answer: He was educated primarily by his father, James Mill, and was a precocious child, learning Greek at the age of three and Latin and advanced mathematics at eight.

faq.question: What were Mill's views on religion?
faq.answer: He was critical of religion itself, never having had religious beliefs, denied Christian doctrines, and viewed religion as the greatest enemy of freedom.

faq.question: How did Harriet Taylor influence John Stuart Mill?
faq.answer: Harriet profoundly influenced Mill's thoughts, particularly strengthening his advocacy for women's rights, and influenced the writing of 'On Liberty' and 'The Subjection of Women'.

faq.question: What did John Stuart Mill think about the 'stationary state'?
faq.answer: He believed that a stationary state of capital and wealth might not be terrible but could actually be a much more advanced and happier state than the present, anticipating that humanity would be less concerned with money and focus more on values like dignity, integrity, and justice.

faq.question: Was John Stuart Mill a socialist?
faq.answer: He called himself a socialist and is considered one of the early market socialist theorists for advocating the replacement of capitalist businesses with worker cooperatives.

timeline.event: Born in a rural French village.
Met Jacques Biétrix de Rozières and entered into a long romantic relationship.
In the year her biological father died, she began her career as a public intellectual and wrote the play 'Zamore and Mirza', an anti-slavery work.
Published the pamphlet 'Letters to the People'.
'Zamore and Mirza' was not performed; wrote another play titled 'The Black Market'.
Became a member of the 'Social Club' or 'Society of the Friends of Truth' and began writing the 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen'.
The Jacobins arrested and imprisoned her allies, members of the Girondins.
The Girondin members were executed at the guillotine.
Was sentenced to death by the Jacobins.
Executed.
An intersection in Paris was named 'Olympe de Gouges' in her honor.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for Women's Suffrage
keyAchievements.description: Made an innovative argument during the French Revolution that women should also be granted the right to vote.

keyAchievements.title: Author of 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen'
keyAchievements.description: Drafted a declaration articulating the equal rights of women in 1791 out of concern that the French Constitution failed to address women's suffrage.

keyAchievements.title: Anti-Slavery Activism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated for the rights of Black people, writing the anti-slavery play 'Zamore and Mirza' in 1784 and 'The Black Market' in 1789.

keyAchievements.title: Proposals for Marriage Contracts and Protection of Women
keyAchievements.description: Advocated for equal division of wealth between husband and wife, equal property division in case of divorce, and laws to protect impoverished women and their children.

keyAchievements.title: Defense of the Monarchy
keyAchievements.description: Opposed the abolition of the monarchy during the French Revolution and took a stance defending King Louis XVI and Queen Marie Antoinette.

faq.question: Who was Olympe de Gouges?
faq.answer: Olympe de Gouges was an innovative civic activist and a radical advocate for women's rights who argued that women should also be given suffrage during the French Revolution.

faq.question: What is her most famous work?
faq.answer: Her most famous work is the 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen', written in 1791.

faq.question: Why was she executed?
faq.answer: She was executed in 1793 by the Jacobins on charges of inappropriate behavior and attempting to restore the monarchy, citing the contents of her flyer 'The Three Urns, or the Salvation of the Fatherland, by an Aerial Traveler' and her unfinished play 'France Saved, or the Tyrant Dethroned'.

faq.question: What was her stance on the rights of Black people?
faq.answer: She spoke out against black slavery by writing the script for the anti-slavery play 'Zamore and Mirza' in 1784 and writing 'The Black Market' in 1789.

faq.question: What was her stance on the French Revolution?
faq.answer: She welcomed the French Revolution but grew disillusioned that the rights of equality it championed were not extended to women. She opposed the revolutionaries' attempts to abolish the monarchy and supported its preservation.

olympe-de-gouges.missingDataNote: 17세에 결혼하고 남편이 일찍 죽었다는 내용은 있으나, 해당 사건의 정확한 연도는 원문에 명시되어 있지 않아 생략했습니다.

timeline.event: Plotinus was born.
Plotinus died.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Neoplatonism
keyAchievements.description: Immersed in and taught Plato's philosophy, he is known as the 'Founder of Neoplatonism'.

keyAchievements.title: Preservation and Editing of Works
keyAchievements.description: His disciple Porphyry preserved 54 of his works, organizing them into six sets of nine tractates.

keyAchievements.title: Author of 'The Enneads'
keyAchievements.description: The works edited by his disciple Porphyry are known today as 'The Enneads'.

keyAchievements.title: Study of 'The One' Concept
keyAchievements.description: Focused his thoughts on the question of how the many emanate from 'The One' (Hen).

keyAchievements.title: Development of the 'Emanation' Concept
keyAchievements.description: Immersed in the concept of 'Emanation' to answer the question of how the many came into existence from 'The One'.

faq.question: When was Plotinus born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Plotinus was born in 205 and died in 270.

faq.question: What philosophical school is Plotinus known to have founded?
faq.answer: Plotinus immersed himself in teaching Plato's ideas and is therefore called the 'founder of Neoplatonism'.

faq.question: What is the major work of Plotinus?
faq.answer: The 54 treatises edited by his disciple Porphyry into six groups of nine are known today as 'The Enneads'.

faq.question: What is the core concept of Plotinus' philosophy?
faq.answer: He developed his ideas around the question of how the many flow from the 'One' (Hen), and was deeply absorbed in the concept of 'Emanation'.

faq.question: Is anything known about Plotinus' family or upbringing?
faq.answer: There are no surviving records regarding his family or his upbringing.

plotinus.missingDataNote: 플로티노스의 가족 및 성장에 관한 이야기는 원문에 전해오는 것이 없다고 명시되어 있습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Borge, near Oslo.
Decided to become an explorer after being inspired by Fridtjof Nansen's crossing of Greenland.
Dropped out of medical school after his mother's death and joined the Royal Norwegian Navy.
Selected as the first mate for an Antarctic expedition.
Became the captain of the Belgica after successfully commanding the ship to break through the ice and escape.
Embarked on an Arctic expedition and determined the position of the North Magnetic Pole.
Arrived in Nome, Alaska, successfully navigating the Northwest Passage and returning.
Decided to conquer the South Pole and embarked on an Antarctic expedition.
Became the first human to reach the South Pole.
Attempted an Arctic Ocean expedition with Umberto Nobile and others on the airship Norge, flying over the North Pole and landing in Alaska.
Went missing while searching for his friend Umberto Nobile; his death was confirmed upon the discovery of airplane parts.

keyAchievements.title: First Human to Reach the South Pole
keyAchievements.description: On December 14, 1911, he became the first person in history to reach the South Pole.

keyAchievements.title: First Human to Reach the North Pole
keyAchievements.description: Became the first human to reach the North Pole using an airship.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered the Northwest Passage
keyAchievements.description: Arrived in Nome, Alaska, in 1905, successfully navigating and pioneering the Northwest Passage.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to the Discovery of the Northeast Passage
keyAchievements.description: Drifted during an Arctic expedition on the Maud, but greatly contributed to the discovery of the Northeast Passage.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to Shortening Global Distances
keyAchievements.description: His 1926 Arctic Ocean expedition helped shorten global distances by connecting Western Europe, Asia, and North America via the Arctic Ocean.

faq.question: When was Roald Amundsen born?
faq.answer: Roald Amundsen was born on July 16, 1872.

faq.question: When did Roald Amundsen die?
faq.answer: Roald Amundsen died on June 18, 1928.

faq.question: Where did Roald Amundsen reach first in human history?
faq.answer: On December 14, 1911, Roald Amundsen became the first human to reach the South Pole, and he also became the first to reach the North Pole via airship.

faq.question: What was the secret to Roald Amundsen's successful Antarctic expedition?
faq.answer: His success was due to meticulous preparation, including superior clothing, setting up supply depots, careful selection of crew members, accepting their advice, ensuring smooth food procurement, discarding unnecessary equipment, and analyzing the causes of failure of other explorers.

timeline.event: Born.
Delivered the Declaration of Sentiments at the first women's rights convention held in Seneca Falls, New York.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Led the First Women's Rights Convention
keyAchievements.description: In 1848, she delivered the Declaration of Sentiments at the first women's rights convention held in Seneca Falls, New York, which is often considered the first organized movement for women's rights and women's suffrage.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated a Philosophy on Women's Roles
keyAchievements.description: She argued that a woman is the arbiter of her own destiny, should have the same rights as all other members as a citizen, and that roles as 'mother, wife, sister, daughter' are merely incidental.

faq.question: Who was Elizabeth Cady Stanton?
faq.answer: Elizabeth Cady Stanton was an American social activist, abolitionist, and leading figure of the early women's rights movement.

faq.question: What is her most significant achievement?
faq.answer: In 1848, she delivered the Declaration of Sentiments at the first women's rights convention in Seneca Falls, New York, which is often regarded as the first organized women's rights and women's suffrage movement.

faq.question: What did she argue regarding women's roles?
faq.answer: She argued that a woman is the arbiter of her own destiny, should possess the same rights as all other citizens, and that her roles as 'mother, wife, sister, daughter' are merely incidental.

timeline.event: Henrik Johan Ibsen was born in Skien, Norway.
At age 18, had a son, Hans Jacob Hendrichsen Birkedalen, from a relationship with Else Sophie Jensdatter Birkedalen.
Published his first play, 'Catiline', under the pseudonym 'Brynjolf Bjarme', and had his first stage production, 'The Burial Mound'.
Became the artistic director of the Christiania Theater and married Suzannah Thoresen.
Son Sigurd was born.
Left Christiania for Sorrento, Italy, beginning 27 years of living abroad.
Published the play 'Brand', which brought him critical acclaim and financial success.
Published the play 'Peer Gynt', for which Edvard Grieg composed the incidental music.
Moved from Italy to Dresden, Germany.
Published his only collection of poetry, 'Poems' (Digte).
Wrote 'Emperor and Galilean', dramatizing the life of the Roman Emperor Julian the Apostate, and was knighted.
Moved to Munich and began work on his first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society'.
His first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society', was published and first performed.
Published the play 'A Doll's House', which brought him international recognition.
Published the play 'Ghosts', containing commentary on the morality of Ibsen's society.
Published the play 'An Enemy of the People', addressing controversial themes.
Published the play 'The Wild Duck', considered by many to be Ibsen's finest work.
Published the later work 'Hedda Gabler', exploring psychological conflict.
Returned to Christiania, Norway, after 27 years of living abroad.
Published the later work 'The Master Builder', exploring psychological conflict, and was made a Commander.
Received the Grand Cross of the Order of St. Olav.
Suffered several strokes.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Died at his home in Christiania at the age of 78.
The 'Ibsen Year' was celebrated in Norway and many other countries to commemorate the 100th anniversary of his death, and 'A Doll's House' was the world's most performed play.
The 'Delhi Ibsen Festival' began to be held annually in Delhi, India.
Håkon Anton Fagerås created two bronze busts of Ibsen to be installed in Ibsen Park in Sorrento, Italy, and in the Skien municipality.
A marble statue of Ibsen was created for the Ibsen Museum in Oslo.
Google featured a Doodle celebrating Henrik Ibsen's 185th birthday.

keyAchievements.title: Father of Modern Drama
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as the world's greatest playwright of the 19th century and referred to as the 'Father of Modern Drama'.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered Realistic Theater
keyAchievements.description: Though he also wrote lyrical epic works, he pioneered realistic theater.

keyAchievements.title: World's Most Performed Playwright
keyAchievements.description: He is the most frequently performed playwright in the world after Shakespeare.

keyAchievements.title: International Recognition
keyAchievements.description: Achieved international recognition with works such as 'Brand', 'Peer Gynt', and 'A Doll's House'.

keyAchievements.title: Redefined the Rules of Drama
keyAchievements.description: Completely rewrote the rules of drama through realism.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Playwright
keyAchievements.description: Influenced figures such as George Bernard Shaw, Oscar Wilde, and James Joyce.

faq.question: When was Henrik Ibsen born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born on March 20, 1828, and died on May 23, 1906.

faq.question: How is Henrik Ibsen evaluated as a figure?
faq.answer: He is considered the world's greatest playwright of the 19th century and is known as the 'Father of Modern Drama'.

faq.question: What are Henrik Ibsen's representative works?
faq.answer: His works include 'A Doll's House', 'Ghosts', 'An Enemy of the People', 'The Wild Duck', and 'Peer Gynt'.

faq.question: Did Henrik Ibsen win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
faq.answer: He was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1902, 1903, and 1904.

faq.question: What were Henrik Ibsen's political views?
faq.answer: He expressed anarchistic views, stating that the state is a curse upon the individual and should be abolished.

timeline.event: October 15, born in what is now Andes, Lombardy.
Received his primary education in his hometown.
September 21, died in Brundisium.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of the 'Aeneid'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the 'Aeneid', the national epic of ancient Rome.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of the 'Georgics'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the 'Georgics'.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of the 'Eclogues'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the 'Eclogues'.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Latin Literature
keyAchievements.description: His works are evaluated as demonstrating the essence of the Latin language and literature.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European Literature
keyAchievements.description: Provided a standard of aesthetic ideals that influenced European writers for generations.

keyAchievements.title: Appearance in Dante's 'Divine Comedy'
keyAchievements.description: Appeared as Dante's guide through Hell in the 'Divine Comedy'.

faq.question: When was Virgil born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born on October 15, 70 BC, and died on September 21, 19 BC.

faq.question: What are Virgil's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include Rome's national epic 'Aeneid', 'Georgics', and 'Eclogues'.

faq.question: What philosophy did Virgil encounter?
faq.answer: He encountered Epicurean philosophy and deeply studied ancient Greek philosophy at the Epicurean school led by Siro.

faq.question: Did Virgil complete the 'Aeneid'?
faq.answer: He worked on the 'Aeneid' for 11 years before his death but died without completing it.

faq.question: What were his parents' occupations?
faq.answer: His parents were of commoner background; his father was a farmer who also worked in pottery making.

virgil.missingDataNote: 원문 내 연도 정보의 불일치(로마 이주 시점과 초등교육 종료 시점) 및 오기(나폴리 이주 시점의 '기원후' 표기)로 인해 일부 사건의 연도는 타임라인에서 제외되었습니다.

timeline.event: Birth (Estimated).
Death of her mother and younger brother William Aigret.
Death of her father William X; succeeded to the Duchy of Aquitaine and the County of Poitiers (age 15).
Married Louis VII.
Death of Louis VI.
Louis VII became King of France.
Affair between Raoul I and Petronilla began; vacancy of the Archbishopric of Bourges occurred.
War between Louis VII and Theobald II.
Louis and Eleanor visited the newly built Basilica of Saint-Denis.
Birth of her first daughter, Marie.
Pope Eugene III requested Louis to lead the Second Crusade.
Crusade assembly at Vézelay; Eleanor also decided to join.
Agreed to divorce Louis VII.
Marriage to Louis VII was annulled.
Married Henry Plantagenet.
Henry became King of England.
Coronation as Queen of England.
Introduced maritime law to Oleron.
Birth of her last child with Henry II.
Stayed in England to arrange the marriage of her daughter Matilda to Henry the Lion, Duke of Saxony; agreed to separate from Henry around Christmas and left for Poitiers.
Stayed in Poitiers.
Henry the Young King led a rebellion.
Arrested by Henry II and her imprisonment began.
Transferred to England and imprisoned in Winchester Castle or Sarum Castle.
Death of Henry II's mistress, Rosamund Clifford.
Henry the Young King led another rebellion.
Death of Henry the Young King.
Summoned to Normandy by Henry II and stayed for six months.
Returned to England.
Death of Henry II; Richard I succeeded to the throne.
Richard I arrived in Portsmouth.
Truce agreement between Philip II and King John; marriage agreement between John's niece and Louis.
Arrived in Castile.
Crossed the Pyrenees with her granddaughter Blanche.
Assassination of Mercadier.
Health deteriorated; departed from Fontevraud to stop Arthur I from invading Poitiers.
Death.

keyAchievements.title: Queen of France and England
keyAchievements.description: She was the Queen of King Louis VII of France, but after their divorce, she became the Queen of King Henry II of England.

keyAchievements.title: Queen Mother of Two English Kings
keyAchievements.description: She became the Queen Mother of Richard I and King John of England.

keyAchievements.title: Cultural Patronage and Dissemination
keyAchievements.description: Patronized troubadours and literary figures, spreading the glamorous and refined courtly culture of southern France to France and England.

keyAchievements.title: Inheritance of Vast Estates
keyAchievements.description: Inherited the vast estates of the Duchy of Aquitaine and the County of Poitiers from her father.

keyAchievements.title: Regency Rule
keyAchievements.description: Ruled as regent when her son Richard I participated in the Third Crusade.

keyAchievements.title: Led Ransom Negotiations
keyAchievements.description: Traveled to the Holy Roman Empire to negotiate the ransom when Richard I was held captive.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Maritime Law
keyAchievements.description: Introduced maritime law to her lands in Oleron and to England.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Trade Customs
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role in developing trade customs between the trading ports of the Crusader states and Constantinople.

keyAchievements.title: Support for Her Children
keyAchievements.description: Lived into the reign of her youngest son, John, continuing to support her sons.

keyAchievements.title: Arranged French Royal Marriage
keyAchievements.description: Selected Blanche of Castile as the bride for Prince Louis of France and arranged their marriage.

faq.question: What is Eleanor's estimated year of birth?
faq.answer: Based on records indicating she was 82 at the time of her death in 1204, she is estimated to have been born in 1122.

faq.question: Who was Eleanor married to?
faq.answer: She was the Queen of King Louis VII of France, but after their divorce, she became the Queen of King Henry II of England.

faq.question: Whom did Eleanor patronize?
faq.answer: Influenced by the Poitiers region, a center of 12th-century romantic literature, she patronized troubadours and literary figures.

faq.question: For how long was Eleanor imprisoned?
faq.answer: She was imprisoned from 1173 until the death of Henry II in 1189.

faq.question: Which of Eleanor's children became Kings of England?
faq.answer: Among the sons she had with Henry II, Richard I and King John became Kings of England.

faq.question: Is it true that Eleanor presided over 'Courts of Love'?
faq.answer: Historian Alison Weir concluded that this was a literary invention by Andreas Capellanus, and it is not mentioned in any sources other than Capellanus's book.

eleanor-of-aquitaine.missingDataNote: 엘레오노르의 정확한 출생연도와 출생지는 밝혀지지 않았습니다. 엘레오노르의 머리색이나 눈의 색과 같은 상세한 외모 묘사는 남아있지 않습니다. 존 스피드의 저작에 1158년 필립이라는 아이를 낳았다는 기록이 있으나, 그 근거가 현재 전해지지 않아 불확실합니다.

timeline.event: Born in Kangaba, Mali (approximate date).
Exiled from his homeland due to political struggles and a prophecy of his greatness.
Defeated the Sosso king Soumaoro Kanté at the historic Battle of Kirina.
Founded the Mali Empire, establishing a unified and powerful West African state.
Died, though his legacy lived on through the Epic of Sundiata passed down by griots.
Proclaimed heiress of the House of Habsburg by the Pragmatic Sanction.
May 13, born as the eldest daughter of Holy Roman Emperor Charles VI and Elisabeth Christine of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel.
Fell in love with Francis Stephen of Lorraine.
February 12, married Francis Stephen of Lorraine.
The War of the Austrian Succession broke out after the death of Charles VI.
March 13, gave birth to son Joseph.
June 25, crowned Queen of Hungary.
September 11, addressed the Hungarian Diet and obtained war funds and troops.
Signed the Treaty of Dresden, recognizing Prussia's possession of Silesia.
October 4, successfully secured the imperial throne for her husband, Francis Stephen.
The War of the Austrian Succession ended with the signing of the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle.
The Seven Years' War broke out between Prussia and Austria.
Russia withdrew from the Seven Years' War after the death of Elizabeth and the accession of Peter III.
Military organizations were consolidated under the Court Council of War, making it the supreme decision-making body.
Peace was made through the Treaty of Hubertusburg (Pressburg in some contexts), finalizing Prussia's possession of Silesia.
After the death of her husband Francis I, her son Joseph II was crowned Holy Roman Emperor.
Freedom of dress was implemented.
Approved the love marriage of her fourth daughter, Maria Christina.
Suppression of the Jesuits was implemented.
November 29, died.

keyAchievements.title: Domestic and Military Reforms
keyAchievements.description: Promoted the modernization of Austria by carrying out domestic reforms with the appointment of Haugwitz and military reforms led by Daun.

keyAchievements.title: Diplomatic Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Appointed Kaunitz to forge an alliance with France, thereby ending the hostilities that had lasted since Maximilian I and achieving the Diplomatic Revolution.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Compulsory Education
keyAchievements.description: Established compulsory education by setting up elementary schools nationwide ahead of other countries, greatly raising the intellectual level of the populace.

keyAchievements.title: Prohibition and Crackdown on Prostitution
keyAchievements.description: Banned prostitution and had sex workers, pimps, and brokers arrested, whipped, and sent to labor camps for forced labor.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening of Military Power
keyAchievements.description: Significantly increased Austria's military strength by establishing a military administration committee, consolidating the Court Council of War, and adopting general conscription.

faq.question: Was Maria Theresa an empress?
faq.answer: Officially, she was the Empress Consort, not the Holy Roman Empress regnant, but since her husband's political power was minimal, she was practically recognized as the Empress by her contemporaries as the de facto ruler.

faq.question: What children did Maria Theresa have?
faq.answer: She had 16 children with Francis Stephen of Lorraine (5 sons and 11 daughters), including Joseph II, Leopold II, and Marie Antoinette.

faq.question: Did Maria Theresa reclaim Silesia?
faq.answer: She attempted to recapture Silesia through the War of the Austrian Succession and the Seven Years' War, but ultimately failed and recognized Prussia's possession of it.

faq.question: How did Maria Theresa become the ruler of the Habsburg dynasty?
faq.answer: Because her father Charles VI did not have a son, she was proclaimed the heiress of the House of Habsburg by the Pragmatic Sanction of 1713 and inherited his territories upon his death.

faq.question: What was Maria Theresa's education policy?
faq.answer: She established compulsory education by setting up elementary schools across the country before other nations, and distributed uniform textbooks to raise the intellectual level of the people.

timeline.event: As the queen of the ancient British Iceni tribe, she led an uprising against the Roman Empire.
Her husband Prasutagus's will was ignored, his kingdom annexed, their property confiscated, Boudicca was flogged, and her daughters were raped.
Led the Iceni, Trinovantes, and other tribes in a rebellion.
Captured and destroyed Camulodunum (modern-day Colchester), the former capital of the Trinovantes.
Defeated the Roman Empire's Legio IX Hispana when they attempted to retake Camulodunum.
Governor Suetonius abandoned the defense of Londinium (modern-day London) and burned the entire city to the ground.
Verulamium (modern-day St Albans) was abandoned and reduced to ashes.
Boudicca's rebel forces were defeated by the Roman army led by Suetonius at the Battle of Watling Street.

keyAchievements.title: Led the Uprising Against the Roman Empire
keyAchievements.description: In 60 or 61 AD, she led the Iceni, Trinovantes, and other tribes in a massive uprising against the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Captured Camulodunum and Defeated the Roman Army
keyAchievements.description: During the rebellion, she captured and destroyed Camulodunum, the former capital of the Trinovantes, and defeated the Roman Empire's Legio IX Hispana which sought to recapture it.

faq.question: Who was Boudicca?
faq.answer: Boudicca was a queen of the ancient British Iceni tribe who led a failed uprising against the conquering forces of the Roman Empire in AD 60 or 61.

faq.question: Why did Boudicca start a rebellion?
faq.answer: To resist Roman oppression after her husband Prasutagus's will was ignored, his kingdom annexed, their property seized, herself flogged, and her daughters raped.

faq.question: How is the end of Boudicca known?
faq.answer: There are conflicting claims about Boudicca's death, including theories that she committed suicide, was captured alive by the Roman army, or died of illness.

timeline.event: Born in the Guangdong province of China.
Married Zheng Yi, a notorious pirate commander of the Red Flag Fleet.
Assumed full command of the Red Flag Fleet following her husband's death.
Negotiated a peaceful surrender with the Qing dynasty, securing amnesty for her crew.
Died peacefully as a wealthy and free woman in Guangzhou.
Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr al-Turi (Askia the Great) was born.
Challenged the succession of Sonni Baru, son of Sonni Ali.
Ascended as the first ruler of the Askia dynasty of the Songhai Empire.
Was deposed by his son, Askia Musa.
Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr al-Turi (Askia the Great) died.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening and Expansion of the Empire
keyAchievements.description: Strengthened his empire to make it the largest in West African history, expanding its territory from Taghaza in the north to the borders of Yatenga in the south, and from Air in the northeast to Futa Jallon in Guinea.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of Trade
keyAchievements.description: Rapidly expanded trade with Europe and Asia.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Educational Institutions
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the development of education by establishing many schools within the empire.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Islam
keyAchievements.description: Established Islam as an integral part of the empire.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Bureaucracy and Improvement of Government System
keyAchievements.description: Introduced a bureaucratic government system unparalleled in West Africa, strengthening and improving the traditional model.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Economic and Tax Systems
keyAchievements.description: Established standardized trade measures and regulations, initiated policing of trade routes, and established an organized tax system.

faq.question: Who is Askia the Great?
faq.answer: Askia the Great is Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr al-Turi, the first ruler of the Askia dynasty who ruled the Songhai Empire from 1493 to 1528. He is known for building the largest empire in West African history and establishing Islam.

faq.question: When did Askia the Great rule?
faq.answer: Askia the Great ruled the Songhai Empire from 1493 to 1528.

faq.question: How did Askia the Great come to power?
faq.answer: In 1492, he challenged the succession of Sonni Ali's son, Sonni Baru, defeated him, and became the ruler of the Songhai Empire in 1493.

faq.question: What are the main achievements of Askia the Great?
faq.answer: He expanded and strengthened the Songhai Empire into the largest in West African history, expanded trade with Europe and Asia, and established many schools. He also established Islam as an integral part of the empire and introduced a bureaucratic government system and an organized tax system.

faq.question: How did the reign of Askia the Great end?
faq.answer: Askia the Great was deposed by his son, Askia Musa, in 1528.

timeline.event: Abu Rayhan al-Biruni was born.
Abu Rayhan al-Biruni died.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Earth Sciences and Geology
keyAchievements.description: Made significant contributions to the fields of geology and earth sciences.

keyAchievements.title: Father of Geodesy
keyAchievements.description: Referred to as the father of geodesy due to his significant contributions to earth sciences.

al-biruni.missingDataNote: FAQ 항목은 원문에 명시된 질문과 답변이 없어 생략되었습니다. 구체적인 수학, 천문학, 물리학, 자연과학, 역사학, 연대학, 언어학 분야의 개별 업적은 원문에 명시되어 있지 않아 생략되었습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Folkestone, Kent, England.
Started studying medicine and liberal arts at Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge University.
Graduated from Cambridge University.
Went to study abroad at the University of Padua, Italy.
Returned home after completing his studies and opened a clinic in London.
Married Elizabeth Browne.
Elected as a Fellow of the Royal College of Physicians.
Appointed as a physician at St Bartholomew's Hospital.
Appointed as a lecturer in anatomy and surgery at the College.
Began giving lectures explaining his concepts and discoveries regarding the heart and blood.
Became Physician to King James I.
Became Physician to King Charles I.
Published 'Anatomical Exercise on the Motion of the Heart and Blood in Animals' (De Motu Cordis).
Wrote 'On the Generation of Animals' (De Generatione Animalium).
Elected President of the Royal College of Physicians but declined.
Died in London, England, on June 3.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Theory of Blood Circulation
keyAchievements.description: Published 'Anatomical Exercise on the Motion of the Heart and Blood in Animals', proposing the theory of blood circulation and proving it through modern scientific methods.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Embryology
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'On the Generation of Animals' and argued that all animals originate from an egg.

keyAchievements.title: Research on the Heart and Blood Vessels
keyAchievements.description: Confirmed that the valves between the atria and ventricles, and in the veins, allow blood to flow in only one direction, and discovered that the ventricles act as a pump.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Pulmonary Circulation
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the pulmonary circulation starting from the vena cava and leading to the arterial system.

faq.question: What was William Harvey's primary research method?
faq.answer: Harvey followed a modern scientific research framework by presenting and refuting the contradictions of existing theories, formulating new hypotheses, and verifying them through experiments.

faq.question: Was William Harvey's theory of blood circulation accepted at the time of publication?
faq.answer: His theory of blood circulation was not accepted at the time; even his mentor, Fabricius, allegedly criticized him.

faq.question: What were the main factors that influenced William Harvey's research?
faq.answer: His research was influenced by the public anatomy lectures at the University of Padua, his teacher Fabricius's experiment-based research methods, and Aristotle's microcosm theory.

timeline.event: Born in Birstall Fieldhead near Leeds, West Riding of Yorkshire, England.
Mother Mary Priestley died.
After his father remarried, he was entrusted to his aunt Sarah Keighley and uncle John Keighley.
Entered the Dissenting Academy in Daventry, Northamptonshire.
Graduated from Daventry Academy and began his ministry in Needham Market, Suffolk, southern England.
Moved to a church in Nantwich, Cheshire, where he established a school and taught students.
Wrote the grammar textbook 'Rudiments of English Grammar' and was employed as a tutor in languages and literature at Warrington Academy.
Became a Dissenting minister in Warrington and married Mary Wilkinson.
Received an honorary Doctor of Laws degree from the University of Edinburgh.
Elected as a Fellow of the Royal Society for his research on electricity.
Published 'The History and Present State of Electricity' and was appointed minister of Mill Hill Chapel in Leeds, Yorkshire.
Published his method for making soda water as 'Directions for Impregnating Water with Fixed Air', and published the first volume of 'Institutes of Natural and Revealed Religion'.
Awarded the Copley Medal for his discovery of how to make soda water and settled in Calne, Wiltshire.
Obtained oxygen by heating red mercuric oxide and met Antoine Lavoisier in Paris to discuss his discovery of oxygen.
Announced the discovery of 'dephlogisticated air' (oxygen).
Published 'Disquisitions Relating to Matter and Spirit' and 'The Doctrine of Philosophical Necessity Illustrated'.
Left Calne and moved to Birmingham to serve as a minister at the New Meeting House.
Published 'An History of the Corruptions of Christianity'.
Wrote a piece criticizing Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France' and moved to Hackney near London after his home and church were destroyed in the Birmingham riots.
Left England and emigrated to the United States, settling in Northumberland, Pennsylvania.
Published his last scientific work, 'The Doctrine of Phlogiston Established and That of the Composition of Water Refuted'.
Died in Northumberland, Pennsylvania, USA.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Oxygen
keyAchievements.description: In 1774, he obtained a gas by heating red mercuric oxide, which he named 'dephlogisticated air', discovered oxygen, and characterized its properties.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Carbonated Water
