keyAchievements.description: Developed a method to produce soda water by dissolving carbon dioxide in water, published it in 1772, and was awarded the Copley Medal.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Electricity
keyAchievements.description: Became a Fellow of the Royal Society through his research on electricity and published 'The History and Present State of Electricity' in 1767.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Various Gases
keyAchievements.description: Discovered several gases including nitrous oxide, ammonia, hydrogen chloride, sulfur dioxide, silicon tetrafluoride, nitrogen, and carbon monoxide.

keyAchievements.title: Elucidation of Oxygen Production by Plants
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that light is important for plant growth and that plants produce oxygen, laying the foundation for research on photosynthesis.

keyAchievements.title: Pedagogical Contributions
keyAchievements.description: Wrote educational works such as 'Rudiments of English Grammar' and 'Biographical Chart', and raised the educational standards of Warrington Academy by proposing a practical curriculum.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Political Theory
keyAchievements.description: Through 'An Essay on the First Principles of Government', he argued for citizens' rights and the right to revolution, influencing Utilitarian thought.

faq.question: Who was Joseph Priestley?
faq.answer: He was an English chemist, clergyman, theologian, educator, political theorist, and natural philosopher who contributed to various fields including liberalism, politics, religion, and experimental philosophy.

faq.question: What is his most widely known achievement?
faq.answer: He is most widely known as the discoverer of oxygen, which he named 'dephlogisticated air' in 1774.

faq.question: How did he discover oxygen?
faq.answer: On August 1, 1774, he discovered that a gas obtained by heating red mercuric oxide using solar heat focused through a convex lens caused a candle to burn brighter.

faq.question: What were his religious and political beliefs?
faq.answer: Theologically, he was a Unitarian; politically, a liberal; and philosophically, he advocated for materialism.

faq.question: What was his relationship with Lavoisier?
faq.answer: Priestley told Lavoisier about his discovery of oxygen in 1774, but he himself clung to the phlogiston theory until the end and rejected Lavoisier's chemical revolution.

faq.question: What were his later years like?
faq.answer: He became isolated in England due to the Birmingham Riots of 1791, emigrated to the United States in 1794, and spent the rest of his life as a minister in Northumberland, Pennsylvania.

timeline.event: Born in Stenbrohult, Sweden.
Enrolled at Lund University.
Transferred to Uppsala University.
Wrote an essay on the sexuality of plants titled 'Praeludia Sponsaliorum Plantarum'.
Expedition to Lapland was approved and initiated.
Obtained a degree in medicine from the University of Harderwijk in the Netherlands.
Published the first edition of 'Systema Naturae'.
Became the botanical curator and personal physician at Hartekamp.
Published 'Genera Plantarum'.
Returned to Sweden and became engaged to Sara Elisabeth Moraea.
Married Sara Elisabeth Moraea.
Became a professor of medicine at Uppsala University.
Reversed the Celsius temperature scale to the standard scale used today.
Received the title of 'Archiater' (chief physician) from King Adolf Frederick of Sweden.
Became the rector of Uppsala University.
Published the first edition of 'Species Plantarum'.
Published the 10th edition of 'Systema Naturae'.
Was granted nobility and took the name Carl von Linné.
Resigned from Uppsala University due to health reasons.
Died on January 10.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Foundations of Biological Taxonomy
keyAchievements.description: Made decisive contributions to establishing the foundations of biological taxonomy, earning the title 'Father of modern botany'.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Binomial Nomenclature
keyAchievements.description: Established binomial nomenclature consisting of genus and species names and formalized it by using it consistently in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Sexual System of Classification
keyAchievements.description: Created a sexual system for plants, categorizing them into classes and orders based on the number of stamens and pistils.

keyAchievements.title: Author of Major Taxonomic Works
keyAchievements.description: Authored major works such as 'Systema Naturae' and 'Species Plantarum', which contained vast classification systems for plants and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Hierarchical Classification System
keyAchievements.description: Introduced the three kingdoms of Animalia, Plantae, and Mineralia, along with ranks such as class, order, genus, species, and variety (and division/phylum).

keyAchievements.title: Proposal of the 'Economy of Nature' Concept
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the concept of the 'economy of nature' to explain the self-renewal and cyclical processes of nature, laying the foundation for ecological research.

faq.question: Who is Carl Linnaeus?
faq.answer: He was a Swedish botanist who made decisive contributions to establishing the foundations of biological taxonomy, often called the 'father of modern botany'.

faq.question: What is Carl Linnaeus's most important taxonomic achievement?
faq.answer: He established binomial nomenclature, which combines a genus name and a species name, and formalized it by consistently applying it in his works.

faq.question: How did Linnaeus classify living organisms?
faq.answer: He classified living organisms by introducing three kingdoms—Animal, Plant, and Mineral—and hierarchical ranks such as class, order, family, genus, species, and variety.

faq.question: Is Linnaeus's classification system still used today?
faq.answer: While Linnaean taxonomy became the cornerstone of modern taxonomy, it contained several errors, such as classifying plants based solely on their reproductive organs, and has been heavily modified since.

faq.question: What role did Linnaeus's students play?
faq.answer: His students, known as the 'Apostles', traveled the world on expeditions to collect new biological samples, contributing to the creation of Linnaeus's global taxonomy.

timeline.event: Born in the 2nd district of Vienna, Austrian Empire.
Passed the Matura (graduation examination) at the Akademisches Gymnasium.
Submitted her paper on alpha particles to the Physikalische Zeitschrift.
Converted to Lutheranism and was baptized.
Published two papers on beta radiation.
Moved to the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute (KWI) and worked unpaid in Otto Hahn's radiochemistry department.
Employed as a regular staff member at the KWI.
Co-discovered the first stable isotope of protactinium with Otto Hahn and was awarded the Leibniz Medal.
Obtained her own laboratory at the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Chemistry.

Elucidated the phenomenon where the emission of an inner-shell electron causes an outer-shell electron to fill the vacancy, transferring the energy difference to another outer-shell electron which is then ejected from the atom (Auger effect).
Became a full professor of physics at the University of Berlin, the first female full professor in Germany.
Started 'research beyond uranium' with Otto Hahn.
Her situation became dangerous when Germany annexed Austria (Anschluss), bringing her under the Nuremberg Race Laws.
Fled Berlin for Sweden to escape Nazi persecution.
Published the results of nuclear fission research in Nature with Otto Robert Frisch.
Was excluded from the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the discovery of nuclear fission.
Retired from the Siegbahn Institute and moved to a new laboratory created by the Swedish Atomic Energy Commission at the Royal Institute of Technology.
Acquired Swedish citizenship.
Retired completely and moved to the UK where most of her relatives lived.
Died in her sleep (aged 89).
Element 109, meitnerium, was named after Lise Meitner.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Nuclear Fission
keyAchievements.description: Together with Otto Hahn, first discovered that uranium undergoes nuclear fission when it absorbs neutrons.

keyAchievements.title: Elucidation of the Principle of Nuclear Fission Energy Release
keyAchievements.description: Along with Otto Robert Frisch, realized that nuclear fission involves a mass defect, resulting in the release of an enormous amount of energy according to mass-energy equivalence.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Stable Isotope of Protactinium
keyAchievements.description: Co-discovered the first stable isotope of protactinium with Otto Hahn.

keyAchievements.title: Elucidation of the Auger Effect
keyAchievements.description: Elucidated the phenomenon where the ejection of an inner-shell electron leads to an outer electron filling the gap, transferring the energy difference to another outer electron that is then expelled from the atom.

keyAchievements.title: First Female Full Professor in Germany
keyAchievements.description: Became a full professor of physics at the University of Berlin, making her the first female full professor in Germany.

faq.question: What kind of scientist was Lise Meitner?
faq.answer: She was a Jewish Austrian-Swedish physicist who specialized in radioactivity and nuclear physics, making crucial contributions to the discovery of nuclear fission alongside Otto Hahn.

faq.question: Did Lise Meitner receive the Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: No, the 1944 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the discovery of nuclear fission was awarded solely to Otto Hahn, and Meitner was excluded.

faq.question: Was Meitnerium named after Lise Meitner?
faq.answer: Yes, element 109, discovered in 1997, was named meitnerium after Lise Meitner.

faq.question: Did Lise Meitner participate in the development of nuclear weapons?
faq.answer: No, she refused the offer to join the Manhattan Project, stating clearly, 'I will have nothing to do with a bomb!'

timeline.event: Born into a Jewish family in Erlangen, Bavaria, Germany.
Enrolled as a mathematics student when the University of Erlangen allowed women to matriculate.
Received her doctorate under Paul Gordan.
Moved to Göttingen, Germany.
Appointed to a teaching position at the University of Göttingen.
Published the paper 'Idealtheorie in Ringbereichen' (Theory of Ideals in Ring Domains).
Published the paper 'Abstrakter Aufbau der Idealtheorie in algebraischen Zahl- und Funktionenkörpern' (Abstract Structure of the Theory of Ideals in Algebraic Number and Function Fields).
Fled Germany and became a professor at Bryn Mawr College in the US after Nazi racial laws barred her from university teaching.
Died on April 14.

keyAchievements.title: Noetherian Ring
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of calling a ring where all ideals are finitely generated a Noetherian ring.

keyAchievements.title: Lasker-Noether Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Presented a major result in commutative algebra regarding the primary decomposition of ideals.

keyAchievements.title: Noether's Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Published a fundamental theorem in mathematical physics that establishes a one-to-one correspondence between symmetries and conservation laws.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Central Simple Algebras
keyAchievements.description: Established the theory of central simple algebras, alongside Emil Artin and Helmut Hasse.

keyAchievements.title: 'Idealtheorie in Ringbereichen'
keyAchievements.description: In this paper, she analyzed the ascending chain condition on ideals.

keyAchievements.title: 'Abstrakter Aufbau der Idealtheorie in algebraischen Zahl- und Funktionenkörpern'
keyAchievements.description: In this paper, she proved that commutative rings in which unique prime factorization of ideals holds are Dedekind domains and proved the isomorphism theorems for rings.

faq.question: Where was Emmy Noether born?
faq.answer: Emmy Noether was born into a Jewish family in Erlangen, Bavaria, Germany.

faq.question: What was Emmy Noether's main field of research?
faq.answer: Emmy Noether primarily researched algebra, leaving behind many achievements such as Noetherian rings and the Lasker-Noether theorem.

faq.question: When did Emmy Noether receive her PhD?
faq.answer: Emmy Noether received her PhD in 1907 under the supervision of Paul Gordan.

faq.question: Why did Emmy Noether leave Germany?
faq.answer: When the Nazi regime's racial laws banned her from university teaching in 1933, she fled Germany and emigrated to the United States.

faq.question: What is Noether's theorem about?
faq.answer: Noether's theorem is a profound achievement in mathematical physics that demonstrates a one-to-one correspondence between symmetries and conservation laws.

timeline.event: Born into a noble Prussian family in Berlin.
Began his education after his father died.
Educated by a tutor alongside his older brother.
Graduated from the Freiberg School of Mines and became a civil servant in the Prussian Department of Mines.
Toured rock salt mining regions in various Central European countries.
Inherited a large fortune upon his mother's death.
Began his expedition to Spanish America with the support of the Spanish Prime Minister.
Returned from South America.
Published 23 volumes of 'Personal Narrative of Travels to the Equinoctial Regions of the New Continent'.
Published 'Views of Nature' (Ansichten der Natur) in Berlin.
Demonstrated the idea and method of comparing climatic conditions across different countries through the 'Creation of Isothermal Lines'.
Taught physical geography at the University of Vienna.
Died at the age of 90 and was honored with a state funeral.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Kosmos'
keyAchievements.description: After observing physical geography and ecology during his expeditions to South America and Central Asia, he compiled vast amounts of data to write 'Kosmos', a masterpiece regarded as a monumental work in modern geography.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Modern Geography
keyAchievements.description: Became a founder of modern geography along with Carl Ritter.

keyAchievements.title: Naming of the Humboldt Current
keyAchievements.description: The ocean current flowing along the coast of Peru was named the Humboldt Current in honor of his investigations.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundations for Physical Geography and Geophysics
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundations of physical geography and geophysics through his investigations of the mountain ranges of tropical America.

keyAchievements.title: Observation and Study of Meteor Showers
keyAchievements.description: Conducted observations of meteor showers from Tenerife in the Canary Islands; his research on their periodicity became a foundation for modern astronomical observation.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Views of Nature'
keyAchievements.description: Investigated and studied Mount Vesuvius in Italy and published 'Views of Nature' in Berlin in 1807.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Isothermal Lines
keyAchievements.description: Demonstrated the method of comparing climatic conditions of various countries in 'Des Lignes Isothermes' (1817) and discovered the rate of temperature decrease with increasing altitude.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Earth's Magnetism
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that the strength of the Earth's magnetic field decreases from the poles toward the equator.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of International Magnetic Observatories and Meteorological Institute
keyAchievements.description: Created an international network of magnetic observatories and established a meteorological institute in Prussia.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Specimen Collection and Research
keyAchievements.description: Investigated the relationship between plants and their environment, collecting a massive herbarium of 60,000 specimens, which included thousands of new species and genera.

faq.question: Who was Alexander von Humboldt?
faq.answer: He was a German geographer, natural scientist, naturalist, and explorer, who along with Carl Ritter, became a founder of modern geography.

faq.question: What is his representative work?
faq.answer: His magnum opus is 'Kosmos', based on data from his expeditions in South America and Central Asia, which is regarded as a monumental achievement in modern geography.

faq.question: How is the Humboldt Current related to him?
faq.answer: The Humboldt Current was named in his honor to commemorate his investigation of the ocean current flowing along the coast of Peru.

faq.question: In what fields did he leave a legacy?
faq.answer: He made tremendous contributions to a variety of fields including flora and fauna, astronomy, climate, mineralogy, and oceanography, in addition to geography, natural science, natural history, and exploration.

timeline.event: Born as the eldest son of a Danish noble family.
Became interested in astronomy after observing a total solar eclipse and enrolled at the University of Copenhagen.
Realized the inaccuracy of the cross-staff and created a table of instrument corrections.
Returned to Copenhagen due to the war between Sweden and Denmark.
Studied at the University of Wittenberg and the University of Rostock, and graduated.
Lost part of his nose in a duel with his cousin Manderup Parsberg.
Became famous in astrology for predicting the death of the Ottoman Empire's Sultan.
Received a promise from Frederick II to be appointed as a canon of Roskilde Cathedral.
Poured his passion into astronomical observations while staying in Augsburg.
Discovered a new star (nova) in the constellation Cassiopeia and recorded observations for 14 months.
Married his lover, Kirsten.
Published the book 'De nova stella' (On the New Star).
His improved observation equipment in Augsburg was destroyed by a storm.
Stayed at the Hesse-Kassel observatory to meet William IV.
Took up the position of canon and established the Uraniborg observatory on the island of Hven.
Discovered a comet and recorded its observations.
Built the additional observatory, Stjerneborg.
Published the book 'Astronomiae Instauratae Progymnasmata' (Introduction to the New Astronomy).
His position became precarious following the death of Frederick II.
Published the book 'De Mundi Aetherei Recentioribus Phaenomenis'.
Experienced friction with the royal court after Christian IV's accession led to pension cuts, prompting him to leave the island of Hven.
Published the book 'Astronomiae Instauratae Mechanica'.
Moved to Prague at the invitation of Holy Roman Emperor Rudolf II.
Died.
The book 'Astronomiae Instauratae Progymnasmata' was published (posthumously).

keyAchievements.title: Discovery and Observation of Supernova
keyAchievements.description: Discovered a nova in the constellation Cassiopeia in 1572 and kept observational records for 14 months.

keyAchievements.title: Precise Astronomical Observational Records
keyAchievements.description: Left behind massive and accurate astronomical records, achieving the most precise naked-eye observations without a telescope.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Tychonic System
keyAchievements.description: Proposed his own 'Tychonic system' (a modified geocentric model), combining the Copernican and Ptolemaic systems.

keyAchievements.title: Proof of the Comet Phenomenon
keyAchievements.description: Proved that comet phenomena occur further away than the moon, disproving the prevailing geocentric theories of the time.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of Observatories
keyAchievements.description: Built the Uraniborg and Stjerneborg observatories on the island of Hven, utilizing them as centers for astronomical observation.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Lunar Libration
keyAchievements.description: Observed the variation in the moon's longitude and discovered lunar libration, an oscillation of the moon's axis of rotation.

keyAchievements.title: Foundation for Kepler's Laws
keyAchievements.description: His precise observational data became the crucial foundation for his assistant Johannes Kepler to discover the laws of planetary motion.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Medicine and Pedagogy
keyAchievements.description: Advanced the study of herbalism, impacting medical progress, and contributed to education in astronomy and herbalism through the Uraniborg research institute.

faq.question: Why did Tycho Brahe lose his nose?
faq.answer: A portion of his nose was severed by a sword during a duel with his cousin Manderup Parsberg in 1566.

faq.question: How did Tycho Brahe die?
faq.answer: He is believed to have died from a ruptured bladder in 1601 after holding his urine out of politeness following heavy drinking at a banquet.

faq.question: What cosmological model did Tycho Brahe propose?
faq.answer: He proposed the unique 'Tychonic system', in which the Earth is fixed at the center of the universe, the Sun and Moon revolve around the Earth, and the other planets revolve around the Sun.

faq.question: Did Tycho Brahe use a telescope?
faq.answer: No, he conducted the most precise observations possible without using a telescope.

faq.question: What impact did Tycho Brahe's observational data have?
faq.answer: His extensive and precise observational data served as the decisive foundation for his student Johannes Kepler to discover Kepler's laws of planetary motion.

timeline.event: John Muir was born (April 21).
John Muir died (December 24).

keyAchievements.title: Expert in Various Fields
keyAchievements.description: Active as a naturalist, writer, environmental philosopher, botanist, zoologist, and glaciologist.

keyAchievements.title: Advocate for US Wilderness Preservation
keyAchievements.description: Was an early figure advocating for the preservation of wilderness in the United States.

keyAchievements.title: Father of the National Parks
keyAchievements.description: Referred to as the 'Father of the National Parks'.

keyAchievements.title: John Muir Trail
keyAchievements.description: A hiking trail in the Sierra Nevada mountains is named the 'John Muir Trail'.

john-muir.missingDataNote: FAQ 항목은 원문에 정보가 없어 생략되었습니다.

timeline.event: Born in the village of Llanbadoc near Usk, Wales, UK.
Dropped out of Hertford Grammar School due to family financial difficulties.
Left London and moved to Kington, near the Welsh border.
Worked as a surveyor in the West of England and Wales.
Set sail for Brazil on the ship 'Mischief' with Henry Bates.
Explored jointly with botanist Richard Spruce and his younger brother Herbert.
Lost his collected specimens in a ship fire while returning to England on the 'Helen'.
Traveled through the Malay Archipelago and the East Indies, collecting specimens and studying nature.
Conceived the concept of evolution through natural selection independently of Charles Darwin.
Returned to England, organized his collections, and gave lectures.
Became interested in spiritualism.
Married Annie Mitten and had three children.
His wife Annie Mitten was diagnosed with stomach cancer due to alcoholism.
Published 'The Malay Archipelago', chronicling his research and explorations.
Wrote 25 papers with the help of Lyell and Darwin.
Started receiving a government pension in recognition of his contributions to science, with Darwin's help.
Published the book 'Man's Place in the Universe'.
Received the Order of Merit (OM).
Died at the age of 90 at his country house, Old Orchard.

keyAchievements.title: Co-discoverer of Evolution by Natural Selection
keyAchievements.description: Independently conceived the concept of evolution by natural selection in 1858, which prompted Charles Darwin to publish 'On the Origin of Species'.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Wallace Line
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the Wallace Line, a faunal discontinuity separating the fauna of Asia and Oceania, during his explorations of the Amazon River basin and the Malay Archipelago.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Wallace Effect
keyAchievements.description: Significantly contributed to the development of 19th-century evolutionary theory by proposing the Wallace Effect, which explains warning coloration in animals and speciation.

keyAchievements.title: Father of Biogeography
keyAchievements.description: Often called the 'Father of Biogeography' for his contributions to the study of the relationship between animal species distribution and geography.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Malay Archipelago'
keyAchievements.description: 'The Malay Archipelago', which details his research and explorations, became one of the most famous books on 19th-century scientific exploration.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of Extraterrestrial Life
keyAchievements.description: Made the first serious attempt to evaluate the possibility of life on other planets in his 1904 book 'Man's Place in the Universe', and was one of the first scientists to write about the search for life on Mars.

faq.question: Who is Alfred Russel Wallace?
faq.answer: He was a British naturalist, explorer, geographer, anthropologist, and biologist who independently conceived the theory of evolution through natural selection alongside Charles Darwin.

faq.question: What is the Wallace Line?
faq.answer: The Wallace Line is a boundary he discovered during his explorations in the Amazon basin and the Malay Archipelago that marks a sharp faunal discontinuity between the animal species of Asia and Oceania.

faq.question: What important books did Wallace write?
faq.answer: He authored books such as 'Palm Trees of the Amazon and Their Uses', 'A Narrative of Travels on the Amazon and Rio Negro', 'The Malay Archipelago', and 'Man's Place in the Universe'.

faq.question: Why is Wallace called the 'father of biogeography'?
faq.answer: He earned the title 'father of biogeography' for his contributions to the study of the relationship between the geographic distribution of animal species.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Alfred Russel Wallace and Charles Darwin?
faq.answer: Wallace independently formulated the concept of evolution by natural selection in 1858, which served as the catalyst for Charles Darwin to publish 'On the Origin of Species'.

timeline.event: May 15, born in France.
Co-awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics with his wife Marie Curie.
Awarded the Davy Medal.
Awarded the Matteucci Medal.
April 19, died in a traffic accident when he was struck by a horse-drawn carriage on his way to his workplace, the Sorbonne.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer in Science
keyAchievements.description: A pioneer in crystallography, magnetism, piezoelectricity, and radioactivity.

keyAchievements.title: Joint Winner of the Nobel Prize in Physics
keyAchievements.description: Co-awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics with his wife Marie Curie in 1903.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Davy Medal
keyAchievements.description: Received the Davy Medal in 1903.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Matteucci Medal
keyAchievements.description: Received the Matteucci Medal in 1904.

faq.question: When was Pierre Curie born?
faq.answer: Pierre Curie was born on May 15, 1859.

faq.question: When did Pierre Curie die?
faq.answer: Pierre Curie died on April 19, 1906, in a traffic accident after being run over by a horse-drawn carriage.

faq.question: What awards did Pierre Curie receive?
faq.answer: Pierre Curie was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics and the Davy Medal in 1903, and the Matteucci Medal in 1904.

faq.question: What were Pierre Curie's main fields of research?
faq.answer: Pierre Curie was a pioneer in the fields of crystallography, magnetism, piezoelectricity, and radioactivity.

faq.question: Who did Pierre Curie marry?
faq.answer: Pierre Curie married Marie Curie.

timeline.event: Born as the eldest of 10 children in Kinnordy, Angus, Scotland.
Graduated from the University of Oxford.
Published his first paper, 'On a Recent Formation of Freshwater Limestone in Forfarshire'.
Became a Fellow of the Royal Society.
Abandoned his legal career to pursue geology full-time.
Appointed Professor of Geology at King's College London.
Authored the first three volumes of 'Principles of Geology'.
Traveled to the United States and Canada.
Published 'Travels in North America'.
Knighted (Knight Bachelor).
Published 'A Second Visit to the United States'.
Awarded the Copley Medal (Royal Society).
Created a Baronet.
Awarded the Wollaston Medal (Geological Society of London).
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the Percentage Method
keyAchievements.description: Together with Roderick Murchison, he studied the Italian marine Tertiary strata and completed the percentage method with the help of Gérard Paul Deshayes.

keyAchievements.title: Author of 'Principles of Geology'
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Principles of Geology', which established the modern framework of geology and comprehensively explained geological phenomena under the concept that 'the present is the key to the past'.

keyAchievements.title: Popularization of Uniformitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Widely popularized uniformitarianism, a concept introduced by James Hutton, through his book 'Principles of Geology'.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote Travelogues Based on Geological Theory
keyAchievements.description: Based on his travel experiences in the US and Canada, he wrote travelogues incorporating geological theories, such as 'Travels in North America' and 'A Second Visit to the United States'.

faq.question: Who was Charles Lyell?
faq.answer: He was a British geologist who founded modern geology and is often called the father of geology.

faq.question: What is Charles Lyell's most famous work?
faq.answer: His most famous work is 'Principles of Geology', written between 1830 and 1833, which established the modern framework of geology and widely popularized uniformitarianism.

faq.question: How did Charles Lyell influence Charles Darwin?
faq.answer: Before the voyage of the Beagle, Charles Darwin received and eagerly read the first volume of Lyell's 'Principles of Geology', which greatly aided the development and emergence of Darwin's theory of evolution.

faq.question: What awards did Charles Lyell receive?
faq.answer: He received the Copley Medal in 1858 and the Wollaston Medal in 1866; he was knighted in 1848 and made a baronet in 1864.

timeline.event: Born near Farab in Central Asia.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of a Philosophical System
keyAchievements.description: Established a philosophical system based on Aristotle and centered around Neoplatonism.

keyAchievements.title: Left Numerous Writings
keyAchievements.description: Left numerous writings, including 'Treatise on the Intellect' and 'On the Ideal City'.

keyAchievements.title: Multidisciplinary Scholar
keyAchievements.description: Specialized in science, philosophy, logic, sociology, medicine, mathematics, and music.

keyAchievements.title: First Iranian Philosopher of the Islamic Era
keyAchievements.description: He is the first Iranian philosopher to emerge in the Islamic era.

keyAchievements.title: Revered as the Second Teacher
keyAchievements.description: Revered as the 'Second Teacher' following Aristotle.

faq.question: Who was Al-Farabi?
faq.answer: He was an Islamic (Turkic) philosopher active from 872 to 950 who specialized in various fields including science, philosophy, logic, sociology, medicine, mathematics, and music.

faq.question: What are Al-Farabi's major achievements?
faq.answer: He established a philosophical system based on Aristotle and Neoplatonism, and left behind numerous works including 'Treatise on the Intellect' and 'On the Ideal City'.

faq.question: What was Al-Farabi's epithet?
faq.answer: He was revered as the 'Second Teacher', following Aristotle.

faq.question: Where was Al-Farabi born?
faq.answer: He was born near Farab in Central Asia.

faq.question: Which languages was Al-Farabi fluent in?
faq.answer: He was proficient in dozens of languages, including Arabic, Persian, and Turkic.

timeline.event: Born in the city of London, England.
Enrolled at Canterbury College, Oxford University.
Elected as a Member of Parliament.
Married Jane Colt.
Served as Under-Sheriff of London and Speaker of the House of Commons.
First wife, Jane Colt, died.
Authored the book 'Utopia'.
Became a temporary regent and 'personal servant' assisting the King.
Knighted.
Became Speaker of the House of Commons of England.
Appointed Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster.
Appointed Lord Chancellor.
Refused to sign the letter requesting the annulment of Henry VIII's marriage.
Resigned as Lord Chancellor.
Ordered to swear an oath of allegiance to the Act of Succession passed by Parliament.
Imprisoned in the Tower of London.
Executed for treason.
Beatified by Pope Leo XIII.
Canonized by Pope Pius XI.
Declared the patron saint of statesmen and politicians by Pope John Paul II.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Utopia
keyAchievements.description: Coined the word 'Utopia', the name he gave to an imaginary island nation with an ideal political system depicted in his 1516 book.

keyAchievements.title: Served as Lord Chancellor
keyAchievements.description: Earned a reputation as a fair and competent public servant while holding various offices, including Lord Chancellor from 1529 to 1532.

keyAchievements.title: Martyrdom for his Beliefs
keyAchievements.description: Executed for treason after refusing to accept Henry VIII's claim to be the Supreme Head of the Church of England.

keyAchievements.title: Canonization as a Saint
keyAchievements.description: Canonized by Pope Pius XI in 1935, 400 years after his death, and declared the patron saint of statesmen and politicians by Pope John Paul II in 2000.

faq.question: Who was Thomas More?
faq.answer: Thomas More was a lawyer, writer, thinker, statesman of the Kingdom of England, and a Christian saint.

faq.question: What is Thomas More's major work?
faq.answer: He authored 'Utopia' in 1516, which is the name he gave to an imaginary island nation possessing an ideal political system.

faq.question: Why was Thomas More executed?
faq.answer: He was executed for treason for refusing to accept Henry VIII's claim to be the Supreme Head of the Church of England.

faq.question: When was Thomas More canonized as a saint?
faq.answer: He was canonized by Pope Pius XI in 1935, 400 years after his death, and declared the patron saint of statesmen and politicians by Pope John Paul II in 2000.

faq.question: What was Thomas More's stance on the Reformation?
faq.answer: Thomas More opposed the Reformation, actively suppressed proponents of Western church reform, supported the Pope, and advocated for peace and social justice through the existing system.

timeline.event: Born in Paris, France, into a secular Jewish family.
Graduated from the École Normale Supérieure and began teaching philosophy.
Took a leave from teaching to work in car factories to understand the plight of the working class.
Joined the anarchist militia during the Spanish Civil War.
Died of tuberculosis in England after refusing to eat more than the ration given to her compatriots in occupied France.
Born on January 22nd in Ales, Sardinia, Italy, as the fourth of seven children.
His father was released from prison.
Re-enrolled at the Cagliari high school.
Graduated from high school in September and entered the University of Turin on a regional scholarship.
Under the influence of his friend Tasca, joined the Italian Socialist Party (PSI) and began his activities as a socialist activist.
Dropped out of university in April due to poverty and health issues, and began working on the editorial board of the Turin edition of the PSI's organ, 'Avanti'.
Contributed 'The Revolution Against Capital' to 'Avanti'.
Founded the magazine 'L'Ordine Nuovo' (The New Order) with university alumni in Turin.
Founded the Italian Communist Party in Livorno.
Entered the Chamber of Deputies as a representative of the Italian Communist Party.
Confirmed as the General Secretary of the Communist Party at the Lyon Congress in January, and arrested by the police under Mussolini's government in November.
Passed away on April 27th.

keyAchievements.title: Founding and Leading the Italian Communist Party
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the founders and a former leader of the Italian Communist Party.

keyAchievements.title: Proposing the Concept of Cultural Hegemony
keyAchievements.description: Widely known for the concept of cultural hegemony, which critiques the state in capitalist society.

keyAchievements.title: Authoring 'Prison Notebooks'
keyAchievements.description: Left behind over 30 notebooks recording his historical and political analyses during his imprisonment, known as the 'Prison Notebooks'.

keyAchievements.title: Expanding Marxist Theory
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the first theorists to attempt to overcome the economistic interpretation of Marxism, emphasizing the importance and autonomy of the superstructure.

faq.question: When and where was Antonio Gramsci born?
faq.answer: Antonio Gramsci was born on January 22, 1891, in Ales, Sardinia, Italy.

faq.question: Why did Antonio Gramsci found the Italian Communist Party?
faq.answer: He founded the Italian Communist Party out of regret that the Socialist Party had lost its fighting spirit and was becoming a yellow party compromising with capitalists and the ruling class.

faq.question: What is Antonio Gramsci's representative ideological concept?
faq.answer: He is widely known for the concept of 'cultural hegemony', which critiques the state in capitalist society.

faq.question: What is Antonio Gramsci's major work?
faq.answer: His major work is the 'Prison Notebooks', a collection of notebooks containing his historical and political analyses written during his imprisonment.

faq.question: Why was Antonio Gramsci imprisoned, and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was imprisoned by the fascist regime during Mussolini's rule, arrested in November 1926, and died on April 27, 1937, due to deteriorating health while serving his sentence.

timeline.event: Born Rozalia Luksenburg in Zamość, a region in southeastern Poland.
Joined the Social Democratic Party of Germany (SPD), the largest party of the Second International, and stood out at the Stuttgart Congress.
Radicalized by the first Russian Revolution and joined the struggle in Warsaw.
Sentenced to two months imprisonment by the Russian government.
Became a member of the International Socialist Bureau.
Published her work 'The Accumulation of Capital'.
Sentenced to one year in prison by the German government on charges of giving an anti-militarist speech.
Began serving her one-year prison sentence.
Temporarily released from prison.
Placed under protective custody.
Protective custody ended.
Delivered a speech at the founding congress of the Communist Party of Germany.
Participated behind the scenes in the Spartacist uprising.
Arrested and murdered by the Freikorps, and her body was thrown into the Landwehr Canal.
Her body remained submerged in the Landwehr Canal.

keyAchievements.title: Social Democratic Theorist
keyAchievements.description: Active as a social democratic theorist for the Social Democratic Party of Germany (SPD) and the Independent Social Democratic Party of Germany (USPD).

keyAchievements.title: Founded the Newspaper 'Die Rote Fahne' (The Red Flag)
keyAchievements.description: Founded the newspaper 'Die Rote Fahne' (The Red Flag).

keyAchievements.title: Co-organized the Spartacus League
keyAchievements.description: Co-organized the Spartacus League, a Marxist group that later became the Communist Party of Germany (KPD).

keyAchievements.title: Doctoral Dissertation
keyAchievements.description: Wrote her doctoral dissertation on the industrial development of Poland.

keyAchievements.title: Major Works
keyAchievements.description: Left behind works such as 'The Accumulation of Capital' (1913), 'Social Reform or Revolution?', 'The Crisis of Social Democracy', and 'The Russian Revolution'.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Revisionism Debate
keyAchievements.description: Criticized Bernstein's revisionism and attempted to explain the vitality of capitalism along with Karl Kautsky.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated the Theory of Capital Accumulation
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the theory of capital accumulation, proposing reasons why capitalism, while surviving in its current stage, is ultimately doomed to collapse.

faq.question: Who was Rosa Luxemburg?
faq.answer: She was a Polish-born German Marxist, political theorist, socialist, philosopher, or revolutionary, and a critic of Leninism.

faq.question: What political activities did she engage in?
faq.answer: She was a theorist for the Social Democratic Party of Germany (SPD) and the Independent Social Democratic Party of Germany (USPD), founded the newspaper 'Die Rote Fahne' (The Red Flag), and co-organized the Spartacus League.

faq.question: How did Rosa Luxemburg die?
faq.answer: On January 15, 1919, after participating in the Spartacist uprising, she was arrested and murdered by the Freikorps, and her body was thrown into the Landwehr Canal.

faq.question: What are her major works?
faq.answer: Her major works include 'The Accumulation of Capital' (1913), 'Social Reform or Revolution?', 'The Crisis of Social Democracy', and 'The Russian Revolution'.

faq.question: What were her ideological contributions?
faq.answer: She criticized Bernstein's revisionism and advocated the theory of capital accumulation explaining the downfall of capitalism.

rosa-luxemburg.missingDataNote: 가족의 바르샤바 이주 시기, 스위스 망명 시기, 취리히 대학 입학 시기, 고등학교 졸업 시기, 스파르타쿠스 연맹 및 독일 공산당 창립 시기, 스파르타쿠스 봉기 시기 등 일부 연도 정보가 원문에 명시적으로 나와 있지 않아 생략되었습니다.

timeline.event: Birth.
Moved to Boston at age 8, where his mother was.
Resigned from his professorship at Harvard University.
Settled in Rome.
Lived as a Catholic freethinker in a convent in Rome.
Death.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of a Unique Philosophical System
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to combine realism and idealism, establishing a unique philosophy which argued that while the existence of material reality is based on irrational 'animal faith', certain knowledge of 'essences' is possible.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Major Book
keyAchievements.description: Authored the novel 'The Last Puritan'.

keyAchievements.title: Coined Famous Aphorism
keyAchievements.description: Left behind the famous aphorism, 'Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.'

faq.question: What was Santayana's main philosophy?
faq.answer: Santayana held a unique philosophy attempting to combine realism and idealism, asserting that the existence of material reality is based on irrational 'animal faith' but arguing for certain knowledge of 'essences'. His thought is characterized by an attempt to merge robust naturalism with beautiful romanticism.

faq.question: What is Santayana's famous aphorism?
faq.answer: Santayana left the famous quote, 'Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.'

faq.question: What is Santayana's representative work?
faq.answer: Santayana's representative work is the novel 'The Last Puritan'.

george-santayana.missingDataNote: 하버드 대학 졸업 연도, 교수직 시작 연도, 영국과 프랑스 거주 기간의 정확한 연도 정보가 원문에 명시되어 있지 않습니다.

timeline.event: Born on October 18 in Paris to a Polish-Jewish father and an Irish-Jewish mother of British nationality.
Enrolled and studied at the Lycée Condorcet.
His answer submitted to the mathematics section of a national academic competition was published in the 'Annales de Mathématiques'.
Entered the École Normale Supérieure in Paris.
Passed the agrégation in philosophy upon graduating from the École Normale Supérieure and became a teacher at the lycée in Angers.
Published 'La philosophie de la poésie: le génie de Lucrèce'.
Published his doctoral thesis, 'Time and Free Will: An Essay on the Immediate Data of Consciousness' (Essai sur les données immédiates de la conscience).
Published 'Matter and Memory' (Matière et mémoire).
Obtained a position as a full-time lecturer (maître de conférences) at the École Normale Supérieure.
Appointed to the Chair of Greek and Latin Philosophy at the Collège de France.
Published 'Laughter' (Le Rire).
Transferred to the Chair of Modern Philosophy at the Collège de France, combining lecturing and research until his retirement in 1921.
Published 'Creative Evolution' (L'Évolution créatrice).
Elected as a member of the Académie Française; in the same year, most of his works were placed on the Index Librorum Prohibitorum (List of Prohibited Books) by the Vatican.
Met with President Woodrow Wilson as a member of a French delegation to request military and financial support.
Published 'Mind-Energy' (L'Énergie spirituelle).
Retired from the Collège de France.
Published 'Duration and Simultaneity' (Durée et simultanéité).
Began working at the International Committee on Intellectual Cooperation (CICI).
Ended his work at the International Committee on Intellectual Cooperation (CICI).
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Awarded the Grand Cross (grand croix) of the Legion of Honour.
Published 'The Two Sources of Morality and Religion' (Les deux sources de la morale et de la religion).
Published 'The Creative Mind' (La Pensée et le mouvant).
Died of pneumonia on January 4 at the age of 81.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Modern Philosophical Trends
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as a pioneer of modern philosophical trends, often referred to as process philosophy or the philosophy of becoming.

keyAchievements.title: Reformation of Western Traditional Metaphysics
keyAchievements.description: By reforming the traditional Western metaphysics descending from Plato, he laid the groundwork for the development of ontology in the 20th century and beyond.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to the Formation of French Phenomenology
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role in forming the distinct current of French phenomenology by elucidating human existence centering on concepts of life, consciousness, and the body.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Literature
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1928.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Grand Cross of the Legion of Honour
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Grand Cross (grand croix) of the Legion of Honour in 1930.

keyAchievements.title: Activity in the International Committee on Intellectual Cooperation
keyAchievements.description: Active from 1922 to 1925 in the International Committee on Intellectual Cooperation (CICI), the predecessor to UNESCO.

faq.question: What kind of philosopher was Henri Bergson?
faq.answer: He was a French philosopher who delved deeply into the problems of duration, consciousness, and life, embraced the empirical achievements of contemporary natural science, and developed a philosophy based on experience and intuition.

faq.question: What is the core concept of Bergson's philosophy?
faq.answer: The core of his philosophy is the concept of 'duration' (durée), which was introduced to distinguish between two aspects of time that had traditionally been confused.

faq.question: How did Bergson view the relationship between science and metaphysics?
faq.answer: He distinguished intuition and analysis as two ways the mind approaches reality, arguing that analysis is the method of science, while metaphysics should aim for intuition.

faq.question: What social activities did Bergson participate in?
faq.answer: He met with President Woodrow Wilson in 1917 as a member of a French delegation and was active in the International Committee on Intellectual Cooperation from 1922 to 1925.

faq.question: What awards did Bergson receive?
faq.answer: He won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1928 and was awarded the Grand Cross of the Legion of Honour in 1930.

timeline.event: Born in Ockham, Surrey, England.
Believed to have studied theology at the University of Oxford.
Summoned to the papal court at Avignon after his commentary on the 'Sentences' was condemned as unorthodox at a synod.
Fled Avignon with Michael of Cesena and other Franciscan leaders to seek asylum with Holy Roman Emperor Louis IV.
Officially excommunicated for leaving Avignon without permission.
Became the leader of the radical Franciscan friars who had sought asylum at the court of Louis IV after the death of Michael of Cesena.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Pursuit of Scholastic Reform
keyAchievements.description: Pursued the reform of Scholasticism in both method and content, with the goal of simplification.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Nominalism
keyAchievements.description: Considered the founding father of modern epistemology for asserting that only individuals exist and that universals are products of human mental activity.

keyAchievements.title: Proposal of Ockham's Razor
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to modern science and intellectual culture by proposing efficient reasoning utilizing the principle of parsimony in explanation and theory building.

keyAchievements.title: Application of Ontological Parsimony to Natural Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Demonstrated an antecedent to the modern scientific revolution by applying ontological parsimony to Aristotle's categories.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Political Treatises
keyAchievements.description: Wrote treatises arguing that secular power is superior to papal power and that the Emperor holds supreme command.

faq.question: Who was William of Ockham?
faq.answer: William of Ockham was a 14th-century English Franciscan friar and theologian, considered one of the major figures in the history of medieval thought.

faq.question: What is 'Ockham's Razor'?
faq.answer: 'Ockham's Razor' is the principle that one should use the principle of parsimony when building explanations and theories to achieve efficient reasoning, which made a profound contribution to modern science.

faq.question: Why was William of Ockham summoned to the papal court at Avignon?
faq.answer: He was summoned because his commentary on the 'Sentences' was condemned as unorthodox at a synod in 1324, and he subsequently became embroiled in the Apostolic poverty controversy.

faq.question: Was William of Ockham a nominalist?
faq.answer: William of Ockham is considered a pioneer of nominalism, arguing that only individuals truly exist and that universals are merely products of human mental activity.

william-of-ockham.missingDataNote: 원문에서 '지식론', '정치학', '논리학' 섹션은 제목만 있고 내용이 없어 해당 분야의 구체적인 업적을 기술할 수 없었습니다. 출생 연도는 '1287년경'으로 명시되어 정확한 날짜는 알 수 없으며, 옥스퍼드 대학교 신학 전공 기간은 '추정'으로 명시되어 있습니다.

timeline.event: Born in the Austrian Empire.
Authored 'The Science of Mechanics: A Critical and Historical Account of Its Development'.
Wrote the philosophical work 'The Analysis of Sensations'.
Wrote the philosophical work 'Knowledge and Error'.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Critique of Mechanics
keyAchievements.description: Developed a critique of mechanics by writing 'The Science of Mechanics: A Critical and Historical Account of Its Development' (1883).

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered a Unique Epistemology
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered a unique epistemology based on the standpoint of positivism.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Supersonic Jets and the 'Mach Number'
keyAchievements.description: Conducted research on supersonic jets, etc., and the 'Mach number' is named after him.

keyAchievements.title: Influenced Einstein's Theory of Relativity
keyAchievements.description: Influenced the formulation of Einstein's theory of relativity through his critique of Newtonian mechanics in 'The Science of Mechanics' (1883).

keyAchievements.title: Advocated a Positivist Stance
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a positivist stance that only acknowledges what is given to the senses in scientific cognition.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Idea of Unified Science
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the idea of unified science, arguing that all sciences deal with the same sensations and are therefore fundamentally in agreement.

keyAchievements.title: Starting Point of Logical Positivism
keyAchievements.description: His positivism and idea of unified science became the starting point for modern logical positivism, which was initially named the 'Ernst Mach Society'.

faq.question: What was Ernst Mach's field of study?
faq.answer: He was a physicist born in the Austrian Empire who taught the history and philosophy of science, and pioneered the critique of mechanics and a unique epistemology.

faq.question: How is the 'Mach number' related to Ernst Mach?
faq.answer: The 'Mach number' is named after Ernst Mach, in connection with his research on supersonic jets and aerodynamics.

faq.question: What was Ernst Mach's main philosophical position?
faq.answer: He advocated a positivist position that only recognized what is given to the senses, and proposed the idea of unified science, asserting that all sciences fundamentally align because they deal with the same sensations.

faq.question: What impact did Ernst Mach's ideas have on later generations?
faq.answer: His critique of mechanics influenced the development of Einstein's theory of relativity, and his positivism and unified science concepts became the starting point of modern logical positivism.

ernst-mach.missingDataNote: 일부 생애 정보(예: 대학 활동 시기)는 연도가 명시되지 않아 타임라인에 포함되지 않았습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Gloucestershire.
Enrolled at the University of Oxford.
Received a Master of Arts degree.
Moved to Cambridge to study the Bible.
Completed an English translation of the New Testament based on the original Hebrew and Greek texts and had it printed in Germany.
Translated the Pentateuch in Hamburg.
Wrote 'An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue', refuting Thomas More's 'A Dialogue Concerning Heresies'.
Arrested in the Netherlands on charges of translating the Bible into English.
Sentenced to death by an imperial decree.
Condemned as a heretic and burned at the stake.

keyAchievements.title: English Translation of the Koine Greek Bible
keyAchievements.description: Influenced by John Wycliffe, he translated the Koine Greek Bible into English.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of New Words
keyAchievements.description: Created new words that did not previously exist for the purpose of English translation.

keyAchievements.title: Translation and Printing of the English New Testament Based on Original Hebrew and Greek Texts
keyAchievements.description: Completed the English New Testament in 1526 through translation work based on original Hebrew and Greek texts and had it printed in Germany.

keyAchievements.title: Translation of the Pentateuch
keyAchievements.description: Translated the Pentateuch in Hamburg in 1529.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a piece in 1531 refuting Thomas More's 'A Dialogue Concerning Heresies'.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundation for the King James Bible
keyAchievements.description: Seventy percent of the English version of the King James Bible is based on Tyndale's Bible.

keyAchievements.title: Spoke Seven Languages
keyAchievements.description: Was able to speak seven languages including English, Hebrew, Greek, Latin, Italian, Spanish, and French.

faq.question: When was William Tyndale born?
faq.answer: William Tyndale was born in Gloucestershire in 1494.

faq.question: What are William Tyndale's main achievements?
faq.answer: He translated the Koine Greek Bible into English, completed and printed the English New Testament based on the original Hebrew and Greek texts. He also laid the foundation for the King James Bible.

faq.question: How did William Tyndale die?
faq.answer: He was arrested in 1535 on charges of translating the Bible into English and was burned at the stake as a heretic on October 6, 1536.

faq.question: What languages could William Tyndale speak?
faq.answer: He could speak seven languages: English, Hebrew, Greek, Latin, Italian, Spanish, and French.

faq.question: What was William Tyndale's relationship with Thomas More?
faq.answer: In 1531, William Tyndale harshly criticized Thomas More by writing 'An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue', a rebuttal to More's 'A Dialogue Concerning Heresies'.

timeline.event: His father, John Chaucer, was kidnapped by his aunt in an attempt to marry the 12-year-old boy to her daughter in order to keep property in Ipswich.
Born in London as the son of a wealthy wine merchant.
His father, John Chaucer, married Agnes Copton, who inherited properties, including 24 shops in London, from her uncle, Hamo de Copton.
First mentioned as a page to Elizabeth de Burgh, Countess of Ulster.
Traveled to France as a member of the English army with Elizabeth's husband, Lionel of Antwerp, during Edward III's invasion of France in the Hundred Years' War.
Captured and taken as a prisoner of war during the siege of Rheims, and released after Edward III paid a ransom of £16.
Married Philippa Roet.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Canterbury Tales'
keyAchievements.description: Best known as the author of the story collection 'The Canterbury Tales'.

keyAchievements.title: Called the 'Father of English literature' or 'Father of English poetry'
keyAchievements.description: Considered the first writer to create works in the vernacular English, rather than French or Latin.

faq.question: Who was Geoffrey Chaucer?
faq.answer: An English author, poet, bureaucrat, courtier, and diplomat, best known as the author of the story collection 'The Canterbury Tales'.

faq.question: What is Geoffrey Chaucer's representative work?
faq.answer: His most famous work is the unfinished 'The Canterbury Tales', a story collection influenced by Giovanni Boccaccio's 'Decameron'.

faq.question: Is there sufficient information about Geoffrey Chaucer's early life?
faq.answer: The text states that there is very little documentary evidence remaining about Chaucer's early life and education.

chaucer.missingDataNote: 초서의 초기 생애와 교육에 대한 자료는 거의 남아있지 않으며, 1360년 석방 이후의 생애는 분명하지 않다고 원문에 언급되어 있습니다.

timeline.event: Born posthumously to Anglo-Irish parents in Dublin, Ireland.
Started becoming active in politics and established his position as an author by publishing two satirical works anonymously.
Appointed as a clergyman but soon retired.
Died and was buried in St Patrick's Cathedral.

keyAchievements.title: Start of Writing Career
keyAchievements.description: Secured his position as a writer by publishing two satirical works anonymously in 1704.

keyAchievements.title: Activity as a Political Commentator
keyAchievements.description: Acted as a political commentator representing the Tory party, which was in power at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of 'Gulliver's Travels'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'Gulliver's Travels', considered the most outstanding satirical novel that sharply criticized human nature and society.

keyAchievements.title: Earned the Title of Irish Patriot
keyAchievements.description: Received the title of 'Irish Patriot' for writing articles blaming England for Ireland's starvation and poverty and advocating for Irish independence.

faq.question: What was Jonathan Swift's profession?
faq.answer: He was an Anglo-Irish novelist and an Anglican clergyman.

faq.question: What is Jonathan Swift's representative literary work?
faq.answer: His most famous work is 'Gulliver's Travels', which is considered the most outstanding satirical novel offering sharp critiques of humanity and society.

faq.question: How was Jonathan Swift evaluated in Ireland?
faq.answer: He was hailed as an 'Irish Patriot' for writing pieces that condemned British responsibility for Ireland's famine and poverty and advocated for Irish independence.

jonathan-swift.missingDataNote: 《걸리버 여행기》의 정확한 출판 연도, 윌리엄 템플 경 비서로 일한 기간, 잉글랜드 이주 연도 등 일부 사건의 구체적인 연도가 원문에 명시되어 있지 않습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Paris.
Lost her father at age 4 and was raised by her grandmother.
Married Baron Dudevant, a provincial nobleman.
Left her husband, taking her two children, and moved to Paris.
Became famous with the publication of her serialized novel 'Indiana'.
Published the autobiographical romance novel 'Lélia'.
Published the humanitarian social novel 'Consuelo'.
Published the pastoral novel 'La Mare au Diable' (The Devil's Pool).
Published 'François le Champi' (The Country Waif).
Published 'La Petite Fadette' (Little Fadette).
Published 'Les Maîtres sonneurs' (The Master Pipers).
Published her memoirs 'Histoire de ma vie' (Story of My Life).
Death.

keyAchievements.title: Activity as a Novelist
keyAchievements.description: A French novelist who became famous with her serialized novel 'Indiana' and continued her literary career.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of European Women's Literature
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated along with Madame de Staël as a founder of European women's literature.

keyAchievements.title: Champion of the Women's Liberation Movement
keyAchievements.description: Re-evaluated as a pioneering champion of the women's liberation movement.

keyAchievements.title: Pinnacle of Epistolary Literature
keyAchievements.description: Her letters (26 volumes) are considered the pinnacle of epistolary literature.

faq.question: What is George Sand's real name?
faq.answer: Her real name is Amantine Lucile Aurore Dupin.

faq.question: Who was George Sand?
faq.answer: She was a French novelist also known for her free-spirited romantic relationships.

faq.question: Where did George Sand's pen name 'Sand' come from?
faq.answer: It is an abbreviation of 'Sandeau', the surname of Jules Sandeau, who was once her lover.

faq.question: Who were George Sand's famous romantic partners?
faq.answer: They include the poet Alfred de Musset and the composer Frédéric Chopin.

faq.question: How is George Sand's literary career divided?
faq.answer: Her literary career is broadly divided into four periods.

timeline.event: Born in Aix-en-Provence.
Enrolled at the Collège Bourbon in Aix and became friends with Émile Zola.
Enrolled in the free municipal drawing school in Aix to study painting.
Entered the law school at the University of Aix-en-Provence but immersed himself in drawing practice.
Moved to Paris for his artistic development but failed the entrance exam to the École des Beaux-Arts.
His paintings were exhibited at the 'Salon des Refusés' established by Napoleon III.
His son Paul was born to Hortense Fiquet.
Participated in the first group exhibition of the 'Anonymous Society of Painters, Sculptors, Engravers, etc.'.
Submitted 16 works to the third Impressionist exhibition.
With the help of Antoine Guillemet, exhibited 'Portrait of M. L. A.' at the Salon, marking his first and last Salon exhibition.
Married Hortense Fiquet.
His father died, and he inherited a fortune, achieving financial stability.
Converted to Catholicism.
Ambroise Vollard held Cézanne's first solo exhibition at his Paris gallery, leading to a re-evaluation of his work.
Death of his mother.
Built his own studio in Aix-en-Provence and moved there in 1903.
Exhibited for the first time at the newly established Salon d'Automne.
An entire room at the Salon d'Automne was dedicated to Cézanne's works.
Died of pneumonia at the age of 67.
Two posthumous retrospective exhibitions (a watercolor exhibition at Galerie Bernheim-Jeune and a retrospective at the Salon d'Automne) inspired many artists.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Post-Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: A French Post-Impressionist painter who served as a bridge between late 19th-century Impressionism and early 20th-century Cubism.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a New Painting Language
keyAchievements.description: Created a new painting language by altering traditional perspective and emphasizing the fundamental structure and formal characteristics of objects.

keyAchievements.title: Color Plane and Brushstroke Techniques
keyAchievements.description: Characterized by unique and exploratory brushwork, using color planes and small brushstrokes to form complex fields.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of Geometric Forms
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the pictorial idea of treating nature in terms of simple geometric shapes like the cylinder, sphere, and cone, and placing objects within perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Expression of Depth through Binocular Vision
keyAchievements.description: Explored binocular vision to express visual perception of the same phenomenon, providing a new aesthetic experience of depth.

keyAchievements.title: Montagne Sainte-Victoire Series
keyAchievements.description: Demonstrated precise observation of nature and pictorial exploration through his series on Montagne Sainte-Victoire in his hometown of Aix-en-Provence.

keyAchievements.title: Profound Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: Provided decisive insight and inspiration to early 20th-century avant-garde art movements, including Picasso and Matisse, earning him the title 'the father of us all'.

faq.question: What kind of painter was Paul Cézanne?
faq.answer: He was a French Post-Impressionist painter who bridged late 19th-century Impressionism and early 20th-century Cubism.

faq.question: How were Cézanne's works received during his lifetime?
faq.answer: His paintings were initially misunderstood and ridiculed by contemporary art critics, but they were re-evaluated after his first solo exhibition in 1895, and in his later years, he was revered as a master by younger artists.

faq.question: What was Cézanne's impact on modern art?
faq.answer: He introduced new modes of expression that influenced early 20th-century avant-garde art movements, notably providing decisive inspiration to Cubist and Fauvist painters like Picasso and Matisse.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Cézanne's painting technique?
faq.answer: He altered traditional perspective, emphasized the fundamental structure and formal traits of objects, and pursued geometric simplification, treating nature as cylinders, spheres, and cones.

timeline.event: Born in Paris, France.
Studied drawing and painting at the Petite École.
Failed the entrance exam to the École des Beaux-Arts and left the Petite École.
His sister Maria passed away from peritonitis.
Met his lifelong companion, the seamstress Rose Beuret.
Submitted his first sculpture, 'The Man with the Broken Nose', to the Salon but it was rejected.
Experienced financial difficulties due to the Franco-Prussian War.
Traveled to Italy.
Stayed in Italy and returned to France in 1877.
Presented 'The Age of Bronze', causing a social sensation.
Received the commission for 'The Gates of Hell'.
Began his relationship with Camille Claudel, his student and collaborator.
Presented 'The Burghers of Calais'.
Presented the 'Monument to Balzac'.
Achieved great success as a sculptor and presented 'The Kiss'.
Presented 'The Thinker'.
The poet Rainer Maria Rilke worked as his secretary.
Presented 'The Walking Man'.
Urged by Rainer Maria Rilke to rent the Hôtel Biron, which is now the Musée Rodin.
Presented 'Clemenceau'.
Donated his works and collections to the state.
Passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Modern Sculpture
keyAchievements.description: Known as the founder of modern sculpture, he elevated the perception of sculpture to the same level as painting.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of 'The Age of Bronze'
keyAchievements.description: Created a social sensation when he presented 'The Age of Bronze' in 1876.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of 'The Gates of Hell'
keyAchievements.description: Created his masterpiece 'The Gates of Hell', which he was commissioned to do in 1880 and worked on for the rest of his life.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of 'The Burghers of Calais'
keyAchievements.description: Created one of his major works, 'The Burghers of Calais'.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of 'The Thinker'
keyAchievements.description: Created the famous sculpture 'The Thinker'.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of 'Monument to Balzac'
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered a new path in the field of sculpture by presenting the 'Monument to Balzac' in 1898.

faq.question: When was Auguste Rodin born?
faq.answer: Auguste Rodin was born in Paris, France, on November 12, 1840.

faq.question: What are Auguste Rodin's representative works?
faq.answer: His representative works include 'The Burghers of Calais', 'The Gates of Hell', and 'The Thinker'.

faq.question: What impact did Auguste Rodin have on the world of sculpture?
faq.answer: He broke the stereotypes of sculpture and elevated the perception of sculpture to the same level as painting, and is thus considered the founder of modern sculpture.

faq.question: When did Auguste Rodin pass away?
faq.answer: He passed away on November 17, 1917.

timeline.event: Born in Saint-Germain-en-Laye, Yvelines, Île-de-France.
Received attention from Madame Marie Mauté de Fleurville.
Entered the Paris Conservatoire at the age of 11.
Composed the Piano Trio in G major.
Composed 'Nuit d'étoiles' and 'Beau soir'.
Composed an Intermezzo for cello and orchestra.
Won the second prize of the Prix de Rome with his cantata 'Le Gladiateur'.
Won the first prize of the Prix de Rome with his cantata 'L'enfant prodigue'.
Composed the cantata 'L'enfant prodigue'.
Started composing 'Ariettes oubliées'.
Started composing 'Petite Suite'.
Composed the symphonic suite 'Printemps'.
Attended Mallarmé's salon.
Started composing 'Cinq poèmes de Charles Baudelaire'.
Started composing 'La Damoiselle élue'.
Started composing 'Deux Arabesques'.
Started composing 'Fantaisie' for piano and orchestra.
Composed 'Valse romantique' and 'Rêverie'.
Started composing 'Suite bergamasque'.
Composed the String Quartet in G minor.
Started composing 'Pelléas et Mélisande'.
Composed the symphonic poem 'Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune'.
Started composing 'Pour le piano'.
Started composing 'Nocturnes'.
Started composing 'Trois Chansons de Bilitis'.
Married Rosalie Texier.
Started composing 'Rapsodie' for alto saxophone and orchestra.
Gained international fame with the premiere of the opera 'Pelléas et Mélisande'.
Started composing 'Images' for orchestra.
Composed 'Estampes'.
Started composing the symphonic poem 'La Mer'.
Fell in love with Emma Bardac and abandoned his wife.
Composed 'Le Roi Lear' and 'L'Isle joyeuse'.
Started composing 'Images' (Books I and II).
Started composing 'Children's Corner'.
Dedicated the piano suite 'Children's Corner' to his daughter Claude-Emma.
Started composing 'Première rhapsodie' for clarinet and orchestra.
Started composing 'Préludes' (Books I and II).
Started composing 'Jeux'.
Composed 'Syrinx', 'La Boîte à joujoux', and 'Trois poèmes de Stéphane Mallarmé'.
Composed 'En blanc et noir', 'Études', 'Élégie', Sonata for cello and piano in D minor, and Sonata for flute, viola, and harp in F major.
Started composing Sonata for violin and piano in G minor.
Died of cancer in Paris on March 25.
Funeral held on March 28.

keyAchievements.title: Entered the Paris Conservatoire
keyAchievements.description: Entered the Paris Conservatoire, France's premier institution for musical education, at the age of 10, gaining recognition for his musical talent.

keyAchievements.title: Won First Prize in the Prix de Rome
keyAchievements.description: Won the first prize of the Prix de Rome in 1884 with his cantata 'L'enfant prodigue'.

keyAchievements.title: Gained International Fame with 'Pelléas et Mélisande'
keyAchievements.description: Gained international fame in 1902 with the premiere of 'Pelléas et Mélisande', his only completed opera.

keyAchievements.title: Established Impressionist Compositional Style
keyAchievements.description: Solidified his Impressionist compositional style with 'Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune', based on a poem by Mallarmé.

keyAchievements.title: Developed a Unique Musical Style
keyAchievements.description: Influenced by Russian and Eastern music, as well as the works of Chopin, he developed his own unique harmonic and orchestral colors.

keyAchievements.title: Influenced Future Composers
keyAchievements.description: Exerted a strong influence on numerous composers, including Béla Bartók and Olivier Messiaen.

faq.question: Which musical movement is Claude Debussy a representative composer of?
faq.answer: Claude Debussy is a representative composer of French Impressionist music.

faq.question: What are Debussy's major orchestral works?
faq.answer: Debussy's major orchestral works include 'Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune', 'Nocturnes' (1897-1899), and 'Images' (1905-1912).

faq.question: When was Debussy born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Debussy was born on August 22, 1862, and died on March 25, 1918.

faq.question: What education did Debussy receive, and what awards did he win?
faq.answer: He entered the Paris Conservatoire at the age of 10 and won the first prize in the Prix de Rome in 1884 with his cantata 'L'enfant prodigue'.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Debussy's music?
faq.answer: Debussy's music originated from a reaction against Wagner and the German musical tradition, seeking new forms instead of the classical symphony format.

faq.question: Which literary movement influenced Debussy?
faq.answer: Debussy was strongly influenced by the late 19th-century Symbolist poetry movement.

timeline.event: Born as Adeline Virginia Stephen in London.
Experienced her first mental breakdown following the death of her mother.
Began studying history and Greek at King's College London.
Attempted suicide by throwing herself out a window after a second mental breakdown following her father's death, but failed.
Married Leonard Woolf.
Published 'The Voyage Out'.
Published 'Night and Day'.
Gained great popularity with the publication of 'Mrs Dalloway'.
Received critical acclaim for 'To the Lighthouse'.
Received critical acclaim for 'Orlando'.
Went missing after going for a walk by the River Ouse, and her body was found two days later.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered the Stream of Consciousness Genre
keyAchievements.description: One of the writers who created and perfected the stream of consciousness genre in novels.

faq.question: Who was Virginia Woolf?
faq.answer: She was an English novelist and essayist, and one of the writers who pioneered and perfected the stream of consciousness genre.

faq.question: What are Virginia Woolf's major works?
faq.answer: Her major works include 'Mrs Dalloway' (1925), 'To the Lighthouse' (1927), and 'Orlando' (1928), all of which enjoyed great popularity and critical acclaim.

faq.question: When did Virginia Woolf die?
faq.answer: She went missing on March 28, 1941, after going for a walk by the River Ouse, and her body was discovered two days later.

faq.question: Which group did Virginia Woolf belong to?
faq.answer: She was a member of the Bloomsbury Group, which was active in the Bloomsbury district of London from 1907 to 1930.

timeline.event: Franz Kafka, a Jewish novelist of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, was born.
Obtained a doctorate in law.
Found employment at an insurance company in Prague.
Joined the Assicurazioni Generali insurance company.
Resigned from Assicurazioni Generali.
Joined the Worker's Accident Insurance Institute for the Kingdom of Bohemia.
Received a proposal from Karl Hermann, the husband of his sister Elli, to start an asbestos factory business.
Met Felice Bauer at Max Brod's house.
Started writing 'The Missing Person' (later retitled 'Amerika') and 'The Metamorphosis'.
Received a medal for inventing a safety helmet.
Started writing 'In the Penal Colony' and 'The Trial'.
Finished the short story collection 'A Country Doctor'.
Diagnosed with tuberculosis.
Separated from Felice Bauer.
Retired from the insurance company.
Started writing 'The Castle'.
Moved to Berlin to concentrate on writing.
Died at the Kierling tuberculosis sanatorium near Vienna, Austria.
Buried in the Jewish cemetery in the Žižkov district of Prague.

keyAchievements.title: Invented a Safety Helmet and Contributed to Mortality Reduction
keyAchievements.description: Received a medal in 1912 for inventing a safety helmet and contributed to reducing the mortality rate of steel mill machinery in the Kingdom of Bohemia to 25 per 1,000 people.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Major Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: Wrote numerous literary works including 'The Metamorphosis', 'The Trial', 'The Castle', 'The Missing Person' ('Amerika'), 'In the Penal Colony', and 'A Country Doctor'.

faq.question: In what language did Kafka write his works?
faq.answer: With the exception of a few letters written in Czech to Milena Jesenská, his works were mostly written in Prague German.

faq.question: How did Kafka want his works to be handled?
faq.answer: In his final testament sent to his friend facing his death, he requested that all his writings be burned.

faq.question: How did Kafka's works become known after his death?
faq.answer: His friend Max Brod defied Kafka's dying wish and oversaw the publication of many of the works he possessed, making them known worldwide.

timeline.event: Born as the first son of John Stanislaus Joyce and Mary Jane Murray at 41 Brighton Square West, Rathgar, Dublin, Ireland.
Published the poetry collection 'Chamber Music'.
Published the short story collection 'Dubliners'.
Published the play 'Exiles'.
Published the novel 'A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man'.
Published the novel 'Ulysses'.
Published the novel 'Finnegans Wake'.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Ulysses
keyAchievements.description: A famous novel that contributed to the avant-garde movement of Modernism.

keyAchievements.title: Finnegans Wake
keyAchievements.description: The highly challenging successor to 'Ulysses'.

keyAchievements.title: Dubliners
keyAchievements.description: A collection of short stories depicting the cynical and gloomy lives of Dubliners in the early 20th century and the harsh social conditions of the time.

keyAchievements.title: A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man
keyAchievements.description: A semi-autobiographical novel.

keyAchievements.title: Chamber Music
keyAchievements.description: A poetry collection published in 1907.

keyAchievements.title: Exiles
keyAchievements.description: A play published in 1915.

faq.question: What kind of writer was James Joyce?
faq.answer: He was a writer from Dublin, Ireland, who worked in various genres including novels, poetry, and plays, and is recognized as a leading novelist of modernism.

faq.question: What are his representative works?
faq.answer: His famous novels include 'Ulysses', 'Finnegans Wake', 'Dubliners', and 'A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man'.

faq.question: What was the main setting that influenced his literary world?
faq.answer: His spiritual and fictional world is deeply rooted in his hometown of Dublin, Ireland, which provided much of the subject matter and settings for his novels.

faq.question: What was James Joyce's stance on the Catholic religion?
faq.answer: Through his Jesuit education, he learned about the injustices and cruelty of priests, leading him to distance himself from the Catholic religion and portray it critically in his works.

timeline.event: Born on December 22 in Lucca, Tuscany, Italy.
Entered the Milan Conservatory at age 23, studying under Ponchielli and other teachers.
Graduated from the Milan Conservatory at age 26.
The year after graduating, he premiered his first opera, which was well-received.
