The premiere of 'Manon Lescaut', an opera based on the original work by Abbé Prévost, was a huge success, solidifying his status as an opera composer.
Started his era of masterpieces by premiering the opera 'La bohème'.
Premiered the opera 'Tosca'.
Premiered the opera 'Madama Butterfly'.
Premiered the opera 'La fanciulla del West'.
Nearing the age of 61, he premiered the trio of operas 'Il tabarro', 'Suor Angelica', and 'Gianni Schicchi'.
Died of a heart attack on November 29 in Brussels, where he had gone for throat cancer surgery just before his 66th birthday.

keyAchievements.title: Achieved Global Fame
keyAchievements.description: Holds worldwide fame as the greatest opera composer produced by Italy since Giuseppe Verdi.

keyAchievements.title: Success of the Opera 'Manon Lescaut'
keyAchievements.description: The massive success of the opera 'Manon Lescaut' decisively established his position as an opera composer.

keyAchievements.title: Beginning of the Masterpiece Era
keyAchievements.description: Began his era of masterpieces with the opera 'La bohème' in 1896, and released iconic works such as 'Tosca' and 'Madama Butterfly'.

keyAchievements.title: Composed Widely Known Arias
keyAchievements.description: Composed widely known arias such as 'O mio babbino caro' from Gianni Schicchi and 'Nessun Dorma' from Turandot.

keyAchievements.title: Utilized German and Italian Opera Techniques
keyAchievements.description: Considered one of the composers who successfully employed both German and Italian opera techniques.

faq.question: How is Puccini evaluated as an opera composer?
faq.answer: He holds worldwide fame as the greatest Italian opera composer since Giuseppe Verdi and is considered one of the composers who successfully employed both German and Italian opera techniques.

faq.question: What are Puccini's representative opera works?
faq.answer: His representative works include La bohème, Tosca, and Madama Butterfly.

faq.question: Which of Puccini's arias are widely known?
faq.answer: Arias such as 'O mio babbino caro' from Gianni Schicchi and 'Nessun Dorma' from Turandot are widely known.

faq.question: When did Puccini die?
faq.answer: He died of a heart attack in Brussels on November 29, 1924, just before his 66th birthday.

faq.question: Who completed Puccini's unfinished opera 'Turandot'?
faq.answer: His unfinished work 'Turandot' was completed by Franco Alfano, a younger alumnus of the Milan Conservatory who was acquainted with Puccini.

timeline.event: Born in Paris, France.
His father, François Baudelaire, died when he was 6 years old.
Sent to Kolkata, India, per his stepfather's wishes, but only traveled as far as Réunion Island before returning to France.
Known as a dandy in artistic circles, though he had not yet published any writings.
Squandered half of the inheritance from his biological father and asked his mother for money to achieve success as a writer.
Published his first art critique, 'Salon of 1845'.
Wrote his second Salon review, gaining a reputation as an advocate and critic of Romanticism.
Published the novella 'La Fanfarlo'.
Participated in the revolution.
Suffered from deteriorating health, heavy debts, and irregular creative output.
Published his first poetry collection and most famous work, 'Les Fleurs du mal' (The Flowers of Evil).
Attempted to leave Paris and settle in Honfleur, but it did not go well.
Continued his writing activities, translating Edgar Allan Poe's 'The Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym of Nantucket' and writing critiques.
Left Jeanne Duval when another of her lovers moved in with them.
Published another edition of 'Les Fleurs du mal' with 6 poems removed but a substantial number of new poems added.
Attempted to run for the Académie Française but withdrew due to criticism from critic Charles Augustin Sainte-Beuve.
Traveled to Brussels to publish books and give lectures due to his debts, but the publishing industry was indifferent.
Died in Paris in August.
On May 11, the 6 censored poems from 'Les Fleurs du mal' were acquitted and republished in France.

keyAchievements.title: Activity as a Critic and Poet
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the literary world by working as a French critic and poet.

keyAchievements.title: Won Literature Competitions
keyAchievements.description: Showed early literary talent by winning an honorable mention in Latin poetry and 2nd prize in French poetry at national competitions.

keyAchievements.title: Published 'Salon of 1845'
keyAchievements.description: Published his first art critique, 'Salon of 1845', gaining attention for its audacity.

keyAchievements.title: Published the Novella 'La Fanfarlo'
keyAchievements.description: Continued his career as a writer by publishing the novella 'La Fanfarlo'.

keyAchievements.title: Translated the Works of Edgar Allan Poe
keyAchievements.description: Translated Edgar Allan Poe's works and introduced them to French readers.

keyAchievements.title: Published 'Les Fleurs du mal'
keyAchievements.description: Published his first poetry collection and most famous work, 'Les Fleurs du mal' (The Flowers of Evil), in 1857.

keyAchievements.title: Late Career Works
keyAchievements.description: Published works such as the prose poems to be included in 'Le Spleen de Paris', 'L'Œuvre et la vie d'Eugène Delacroix', and 'Le Peintre de la vie moderne'.

keyAchievements.title: Revered as the Father of Symbolism
keyAchievements.description: Praised by Stéphane Mallarmé and Paul Verlaine, he was revered as the father of Symbolism.

faq.question: What was Charles Baudelaire's profession?
faq.answer: He was a French critic and poet.

faq.question: What is Charles Baudelaire's most famous work?
faq.answer: His most famous work is his first poetry collection, 'Les Fleurs du mal' (The Flowers of Evil), published in 1857.

faq.question: What controversy surrounded the publication of 'Les Fleurs du mal'?
faq.answer: 'Les Fleurs du mal' was prosecuted for an offense against public morals for being indecent, resulting in a fine, but it was acquitted in 1949.

faq.question: Which author's works did Charles Baudelaire translate?
faq.answer: He translated the works of Edgar Allan Poe, whose short stories had a profound influence on him.

faq.question: What artistic movement did Charles Baudelaire influence?
faq.answer: He was praised by Stéphane Mallarmé and Paul Verlaine, and revered as the father of Symbolism.

timeline.event: Born in Moscow
Settled in Munich at age 30 and began studying at a private school and the Academy of Fine Arts
Ended cohabitation with German Expressionist female painter Gabriele Münter
Returned to Moscow after the Russian Revolution
Left his professorship at the Moscow Academy of Fine Arts and returned to Germany
Began teaching students at the Bauhaus
Published his second theoretical work, 'Point and Line to Plane'
Traveled to Egypt, Greece, and Turkey
Moved to France after the Bauhaus was closed due to political pressure
Acquired French citizenship
Died in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Abstract Art
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as the founder of abstract art, he is known as the artist who painted the first modern abstract works.

keyAchievements.title: Theoretical work 'Point and Line to Plane'
keyAchievements.description: He published his second theoretical work, 'Point and Line to Plane', in 1926, describing the basic relationships of formative elements in painting.

keyAchievements.title: Role at the Bauhaus
keyAchievements.description: He was in charge of the mural workshop at the Bauhaus, and in his later years, as deputy director, he gained trust by cultivating solid judgment in students.

keyAchievements.title: Moment of transition to abstract art
keyAchievements.description: Experiencing synesthesia, seeing colors while listening to the music of Wagner's opera 'Lohengrin', was an important turning point in his transition to abstract art.

faq.question: What kind of artist is Kandinsky considered to be?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as the founder of abstract art and is one of the important artists of the 20th century compared to Picasso and Matisse.

faq.question: When and where was Kandinsky born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born in Moscow on December 4, 1866, and died in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France, on December 13, 1944.

faq.question: What is Kandinsky's major theoretical work?
faq.answer: His major theoretical work is 'Point and Line to Plane', published in 1926.

faq.question: What motivated Kandinsky to transition to abstract art?
faq.answer: Experiencing synesthesia, seeing colors while listening to the music of Wagner's opera 'Lohengrin', was an important turning point in his transition to abstract art.

wassily-kandinsky.missingDataNote: 가브리엘레 뮌터와의 동거 시작 연도와 제1 이론적 저작 《예술에 있어서 정신적인 것》의 출판 연도는 원문에 명시되어 있지 않아 생략되었습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Paris on June 7
Moved to Peru with his family
Left Peru after his guardian fell from power and moved to Orléans, France
Entered the naval preparatory school and served as a pilot and in the navy
Returned to Paris and got a job at a stock brokerage
Married Danish woman Mette-Sophie Gad and started painting as a hobby
Moved to a house with a studio in the Vaugirard district
Submitted works to the Impressionist exhibition
Quit his career as a stockbroker due to the financial crisis and turned into a full-time painter
In October, asked Camille Pissarro for help as a full-time painter
Moved to Rouen with his family, and in November, the whole family moved to Copenhagen
In June, returned to Paris with his son Clovis
In May, submitted 19 paintings and 1 wood relief to the 8th Impressionist Exhibition
Traveled to Panama and Martinique with Charles Laval
In October, visited Arles and lived with Vincent van Gogh, and returned to Paris in late December
Invited to Les XX (The Twenty) exhibition
In February, raised funds through the Hôtel Drouot auction, sailed to Tahiti on April 1, and married Teha'amana in November
At the end of the year, exhibited Tahitian works at the Goupil Gallery
Left Tahiti on June 14, returned to France in August, and held the Durand-Ruel exhibition in November
In early year, prepared woodcuts for the travelogue 'Noa Noa' and separated from his wife Mette
In February, attempted the Hôtel Drouot auction but failed, submitted the ceramic sculpture Oviri to the Salon in April, departed for Tahiti again on June 28, and arrived in September
In April, heard the news of his daughter Aline's death and suffered from poor health and financial difficulties
In November, held an exhibition at the Vollard Gallery
Signed a supply contract with art dealer Ambroise Vollard
In February, became the editor of the local newspaper 'Les Guêpes'
In September, resigned as editor of 'Les Guêpes' and moved to Atuona on Hiva Oa island in the Marquesas Islands
Died on May 8 in the Marquesas Islands

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Post-Impressionism and Symbolism Movements
keyAchievements.description: As a French painter, sculptor, printmaker, ceramist, and writer, he made significant contributions to the Post-Impressionism and Symbolism movements.

keyAchievements.title: Practice of Wood Engraving and Woodcut
keyAchievements.description: He was an influential practitioner of wood engraving and woodcut as art forms.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering the Synthetism Style
keyAchievements.description: He was recognized by later generations for his experimental use of color and Synthetist style, distinct from Impressionism.

keyAchievements.title: Representative Cloisonnism Work 'The Yellow Christ'
keyAchievements.description: He created 'The Yellow Christ', a representative work of Cloisonnism, simplified with bold black outlines and areas of pure color.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Primitivism
keyAchievements.description: He was the first artist to systematically use and achieve popular success with Primitivism, characterized by exaggerated body proportions, animal totems, geometric designs, and stark contrasts.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: His bold, colorful, and design-oriented paintings inspired many modern artists, including Vincent van Gogh, Henri Matisse, and Pablo Picasso.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the Masterpiece 'Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?'
keyAchievements.description: He completed the massive painting 'Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?', considered his masterpiece and artistic testament.

faq.question: What art movements is Paul Gauguin associated with?
faq.answer: He is primarily associated with the Post-Impressionism and Symbolism movements and is regarded as a pioneer of the Primitivist style.

faq.question: What is the typical style of Paul Gauguin's works?
faq.answer: His works feature experimental colors and a Synthetist style, using bold outlines and flat areas of color under the influence of Cloisonnism.

faq.question: What was Paul Gauguin's relationship with Vincent van Gogh?
faq.answer: In 1888, he painted with Vincent van Gogh in Arles for 9 weeks, but their relationship deteriorated, and they parted ways after the incident where Vincent cut off his own ear.

faq.question: Why did Paul Gauguin leave for Tahiti?
faq.answer: He left for Tahiti to escape European civilization and 'everything that is artificial and conventional' to find new artistic inspiration.

faq.question: Was Paul Gauguin a successful painter during his lifetime?
faq.answer: He did not achieve great success during his lifetime, but after his death, he gained popularity through the efforts of art dealer Ambroise Vollard and others, and had a profound influence on modern art.

timeline.event: Birth of English painter and poet William Blake (November 28)
Became an apprentice to an engraver at age 14
Married at age 25
Published 'Poetical Sketches'
Published 'Songs of Innocence'
Published the illuminated book 'The Marriage of Heaven and Hell'
Published the illuminated book 'Songs of Experience'
Published 'Illustrations of the Book of Job', adding mystical speculation to the biblical content
Attempted 100 illustrations for Dante's 'Divine Comedy' but left it unfinished
Death of William Blake (August 12)

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a Poet and Painter
keyAchievements.description: He was active as a major British artist, presenting poetry and paintings intertwined with mystery and fantasy.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Original Illuminated Books
keyAchievements.description: He published his own unique illuminated books by inventing relief etching, placing illustrations on every page.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Major Works
keyAchievements.description: He left behind several famous illuminated books, including 'The Marriage of Heaven and Hell' (1790), 'Songs of Experience' (1794), and 'Illustrations of the Book of Job' (1825).

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Artistic Status of Illustration
keyAchievements.description: He is evaluated as the figure who elevated illustration to a level comparable to other paintings.

faq.question: What was William Blake's profession?
faq.answer: He was an English painter and poet.

faq.question: What kind of painting did William Blake despise?
faq.answer: He despised paintings that merely copied the external appearance of nature, such as portraits or landscapes.

faq.question: What was William Blake's preferred medium for painting?
faq.answer: He avoided oil painting and considered watercolor to be the highest form of expression.

faq.question: What are William Blake's representative illuminated books?
faq.answer: His representative illuminated books include 'The Marriage of Heaven and Hell' (1790), 'Songs of Experience' (1794), and 'Illustrations of the Book of Job' (1825).

faq.question: How did William Blake produce his illuminated books?
faq.answer: He published illuminated books by placing illustrations on every page and inventing his own method of relief etching.

william-blake.missingDataNote: 결혼 및 판화가 제자가 된 정확한 날짜는 원문에 명시되어 있지 않고, 나이만 언급되어 연도를 추정했습니다. 일부 작품의 정확한 출간일이 아닌 연도 범위만 제시되어 있습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Odense, Denmark
Baptized in St. Hans Church, Odense
His father died
His mother remarried
Published his first story, 'The Ghost at Palnatoke's Grave'
His birth certificate was drawn up
Attended school in Elsinore as well
Achieved considerable success with the short story 'A Journey on Foot from Holmen's Canal to the East Point of Amager'
Experimented with 'The Traveling Companion'
Published 'Shadow Pictures of a Journey to the Harz, Swiss Saxony, etc. in the Summer of 1831', containing travel sketches
Received a small travel grant from the King and embarked on his first journey traveling across Europe
Arrived in Rome
His first novel, 'The Improvisatore', was published and received immediate acclaim
Published the first installment of 'Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.'
Published the second booklet of 'Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.'
Danish reviews of the first two booklets appeared for the first time
Published the third booklet of 'Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.'
Started the second series of fairy tales
Published 'Picture Book Without Pictures'
Jenny Lind wrote a letter considering Andersen as a 'brother'
Started the third series of fairy tales
Lived at Nyhavn 67 in Copenhagen
Visited England for the first time and met Charles Dickens
Published 'In Sweden', containing travel sketches
Visited England again mainly to meet Dickens
Referred to this period in his diary as his 'erotic period'
Wrote in his diary, 'Scharff has not visited me for 8 days; it is over with him'
Published 'A Visit to Portugal in 1866'
Received a proposal for 12 new stories from Horace Scudder, editor of the 'Riverside Magazine for Young People'
Fell out of bed at age 67 and was severely injured
Died at the age of 70 at Rolighed, a villa near Copenhagen
The Collin family grave was moved to another cemetery where younger Collin family members were buried

keyAchievements.title: Fame as a Fairy Tale Writer
keyAchievements.description: He was a prolific writer of plays, travelogues, novels, and poems, but is best known for his fairy tales.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Writing and Translation
keyAchievements.description: His fairy tales consist of 156 stories across 9 volumes and have been translated into more than 125 languages.

keyAchievements.title: Cultural Influence
keyAchievements.description: His fairy tales are deeply ingrained in the collective consciousness of Western culture.

keyAchievements.title: Success of 'A Journey on Foot from Holmen's Canal to the East Point of Amager'
keyAchievements.description: He achieved considerable success in 1829 with the short story 'A Journey on Foot from Holmen's Canal to the East Point of Amager'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'The Improvisatore'
keyAchievements.description: His first novel, 'The Improvisatore', published in 1835, received immediate acclaim.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.'
keyAchievements.description: He first attempted the fairy tale genre with this collection, published in installments between 1835 and 1837.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing International Fame with 'The Little Mermaid'
keyAchievements.description: 'The Little Mermaid', included in the third booklet, established Andersen's international fame.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Travelogues
keyAchievements.description: He published several long travelogues, such as 'In Sweden' (1851) and 'A Poet's Bazaar', which received favorable reviews.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Picture Book Without Pictures'
keyAchievements.description: He achieved good results with 'Picture Book Without Pictures', published in 1840.

keyAchievements.title: International Acclaim
keyAchievements.description: He received acclaim across Europe, starting fairy tale series in 1838 and 1845.

faq.question: What kind of writer is Hans Christian Andersen best known as?
faq.answer: He was a prolific writer of plays, travelogues, novels, and poems, but is best known for his fairy tales.

faq.question: How many languages have Andersen's fairy tales been translated into?
faq.answer: His fairy tales consist of a total of 156 stories across 9 volumes and have been translated into more than 125 languages.

faq.question: What are Andersen's most famous fairy tales?
faq.answer: His most famous fairy tales include 'The Emperor's New Clothes', 'The Red Shoes', 'The Little Match Girl', 'The Ugly Duckling', 'The Little Mermaid', 'Thumbelina', and 'The Snow Queen'.

faq.question: When and where was Andersen born?
faq.answer: Andersen was born on April 2, 1805, in Odense, Denmark.

faq.question: Who were Andersen's parents?
faq.answer: His father received elementary education, and his mother was an illiterate washerwoman.

faq.question: Did Andersen ever meet Charles Dickens?
faq.answer: Yes, Andersen first met Charles Dickens during his visit to England in June 1847, and visited England again in 1857 to meet Dickens.

faq.question: When did Andersen die?
faq.answer: Andersen died on August 4, 1875, at the age of 70, at Rolighed, a villa near Copenhagen.

faq.question: How was his childhood reflected in Andersen's works?
faq.answer: 'The Little Match Girl' was based on his poor mother, 'The Snow Queen' on his father's death, and 'The Ugly Duckling' on the mistreatment he suffered due to his background.

timeline.event: Born in Bombay, India.
Returned to India.
Served as an editor for the <Civil & Military Gazette> and <Pioneer> in Lahore.
Published the poetry collection <Departmental Ditties>.
Published the short story collection <Plain Tales from the Hills>.
Traveled to the Far East, America, etc.
Published the story <The Man Who Would Be King>.
Returned to England.
Published the poems <Mandalay> and <Gunga Din>.
Published the short story collection <Life's Handicap>.
Welcomed for <Barrack-Room Ballads>, depicting military life in India.
Revered as a patriotic poet for <The Seven Seas>.
Published the novel <Kim>.
Published <Just So Stories>.
Became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Wrote the poem <If—> for his 12-year-old son.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Literature
keyAchievements.description: In 1907, he became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, and remains the youngest recipient to date.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Children's Literature
keyAchievements.description: He contributed to the field of children's literature by writing tales for children, such as 'The Jungle Book' and 'Just So Stories'.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of the Short Story
keyAchievements.description: He is regarded as a major innovator in the art of the short story.

keyAchievements.title: Works Across Various Genres
keyAchievements.description: He was recognized for his literary talent, publishing many works across various genres including novels, poetry, and short stories.

faq.question: What kind of writer is Rudyard Kipling known as?
faq.answer: He is an English novelist and poet, well known as a children's author of works like 'The Jungle Book'.

faq.question: When did Rudyard Kipling win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
faq.answer: He was the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1907.

faq.question: What are Rudyard Kipling's representative works?
faq.answer: They include novels like 'The Jungle Book', 'Just So Stories', and 'Kim', and poems like <Mandalay> and <Gunga Din>.

faq.question: What literary field is Rudyard Kipling considered a pioneer of?
faq.answer: He is regarded as a major innovator in the art of the short story.

timeline.event: Born in England (June 13)
Entered Oxford University to study modern languages and medieval literature
Earned a Master of Arts degree from Oxford University, becoming one of the first women to receive a degree from Oxford at the time
Published her first novel 'Whose Body?'
Formed the Detection Club in England with G.K. Chesterton, Agatha Christie, Ronald Knox, and others
Died (December 17)

keyAchievements.title: Published first novel 'Whose Body?'
keyAchievements.description: In 1923, she published her first novel 'Whose Body?', featuring her persona Lord Peter Wimsey as a detective.

keyAchievements.title: First women to receive an Oxford University degree
keyAchievements.description: In 1920, she earned a Master of Arts degree from Oxford University, becoming one of the first women to receive a degree from Oxford at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Writing the Lord Peter Wimsey series
keyAchievements.description: She wrote the detective novel series featuring Lord Peter Wimsey as a detective for 15 years, evaluated as masterpieces representing the Golden Age of Detective Fiction.

keyAchievements.title: Formation of the Detection Club
keyAchievements.description: In 1929, she formed the British Detection Club with G.K. Chesterton, Agatha Christie, and others.

keyAchievements.title: Writing activities in various fields
keyAchievements.description: Until shortly before her death, she engaged in writing activities across a truly broad range of fields, including detective novels, poetry, plays, literary criticism, translations, and essays.

keyAchievements.title: Writing <The Zeal for Thy House>
keyAchievements.description: She wrote her representative play <The Zeal for Thy House>, containing Christian theological lessons.

faq.question: In what fields was Dorothy L. Sayers active?
faq.answer: She was active as a detective novelist, poet, and advertising copywriter, and engaged in writing across a broad range of areas including plays, literary criticism, essays, and translations.

faq.question: What are her representative works?
faq.answer: Her representative works include the detective series featuring her persona Lord Peter Wimsey, and <The Zeal for Thy House>, containing Christian lessons.

faq.question: What is Dorothy L. Sayers' connection to Oxford University?
faq.answer: She entered Oxford University in 1912 to study modern languages and medieval literature, and in 1920, she earned a Master of Arts degree, becoming one of the first women to receive a degree from Oxford at the time.

faq.question: Which authors did she interact with?
faq.answer: She built friendships with leading authors of her time, such as C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and T.S. Eliot, and formed the British Detection Club alongside Agatha Christie and others.

timeline.event: Born in Leipzig as Clara Josephine Wieck.
Her parents divorced.
Made her official debut at the Gewandhaus in Leipzig.
Met Robert Schumann.
Composed Four Polonaises for Piano, Op. 1.
Toured Paris and other European cities with her father.
Performed her Piano Concerto in A minor with the Leipzig Gewandhaus Orchestra conducted by Mendelssohn.
Accepted Robert Schumann's marriage proposal.
Appointed 'Royal and Imperial Austrian Chamber Virtuoso', Austria's highest musical honor.
Married the composer Robert Schumann.
Collaborated with Robert Schumann to publish a work based on Friedrich Rückert's poem cycle 'Liebesfrühling' (Spring of Love).
Met violinist Joseph Joachim for the first time.
Premiered Robert Schumann's Piano Concerto in Dresden.
Composed Piano Trio in G minor, Op. 17.
Rescued her children during the May Uprising in Dresden.
Met Johannes Brahms.
Composed Three Romances for Violin and Piano, Op. 22.
Robert Schumann suffered a mental breakdown, attempted suicide, and was admitted to an asylum.
Toured England for the first time.
Her husband Robert Schumann died.
Gave the first performance of Brahms' piano solo work, Variations and Fugue on a Theme by Handel.
Continued her sixteen annual winter-spring tours of England.
Worked on arrangements of 41 of Robert Schumann's lieder commissioned by a publisher.
Appointed as the first piano teacher at the newly founded Dr. Hoch's Konservatorium in Frankfurt.
Performed Beethoven's Choral Fantasy with the newly founded Berlin Philharmonic Orchestra.
Edited her husband Robert Schumann's letters (Jugendbriefe).
Edited her husband Robert Schumann's piano works by adding fingerings and other instructions.
Gave her last performance in Frankfurt.
Maintained her teaching position.
Passed away at the age of 76.
A portrait created by Andreas Staub in 1835 was featured on the 100 Deutsche Mark banknote.

keyAchievements.title: Renowned Concert Pianist
keyAchievements.description: Considered one of the most distinguished pianists of the Romantic era, she exerted her influence through a 61-year concert career.

keyAchievements.title: Innovator of Piano Recital Format
keyAchievements.description: She changed the format and repertoire of piano recitals by reducing the importance of purely virtuosic works, and made playing from memory the standard for concerts.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Composer
keyAchievements.description: She composed works in various genres, including piano solo pieces, piano concertos, chamber music, choral works, and lieder.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Piano Educator
keyAchievements.description: From 1878, she worked as an influential piano educator at Dr. Hoch's Konservatorium in Frankfurt, attracting international students.

keyAchievements.title: Editor of Robert Schumann's Works
keyAchievements.description: She edited the publications of her husband Robert Schumann's works, and added instructions to his letters and piano works.

keyAchievements.title: Promoter of Robert Schumann's Music
keyAchievements.description: Throughout her life, she tirelessly promoted her husband Robert Schumann's works to ensure they were recognized and appreciated.

faq.question: Who was Clara Schumann?
faq.answer: Clara Schumann was a German pianist, composer, and piano teacher, evaluated as one of the most distinguished pianists of the Romantic era.

faq.question: When did Clara Schumann marry Robert Schumann?
faq.answer: Clara Schumann married the composer Robert Schumann on September 12, 1840.

faq.question: How did Clara Schumann influence piano performance?
faq.answer: She made playing from memory the standard for concerts, and changed the format and repertoire of piano recitals by reducing the importance of purely virtuosic works.

faq.question: When did Clara Schumann die?
faq.answer: Clara Schumann died on May 20, 1896, at the age of 76, and was buried next to her husband Robert Schumann in Bonn.

faq.question: Is there a banknote featuring Clara Schumann's portrait?
faq.answer: Yes, Clara Schumann's portrait, created by Andreas Staub in 1835, was used on the 100 Deutsche Mark banknote from 1989 to 2002.

timeline.event: Born in Cedarville, Illinois
Graduated from Rockford College
Founded Hull House in Chicago with Ellen Gates Starr
Elected President of the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom
Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize alongside Nicholas Murray Butler
Died

keyAchievements.title: Foundation of Hull House
keyAchievements.description: In 1889, she founded Hull House in Chicago, the first settlement facility in the United States, alongside Ellen Gates Starr.

keyAchievements.title: Leading the Anti-War Peace Movement
keyAchievements.description: She led the anti-war peace movement during World War I.

keyAchievements.title: President of the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom
keyAchievements.description: She was elected President of the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom in 1915.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize
keyAchievements.description: In 1931, she was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize alongside Nicholas Murray Butler.

faq.question: When was Jane Addams born?
faq.answer: Jane Addams was born on September 6, 1860, in Cedarville, Illinois.

faq.question: When did Jane Addams win the Nobel Peace Prize?
faq.answer: Jane Addams won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1931 alongside Nicholas Murray Butler.

faq.question: What kind of facility was Hull House founded by Jane Addams?
faq.answer: Hull House was the first settlement facility in the United States, featuring night school for adults, a kindergarten, children's clubs, a charity kitchen, an art gallery, and more.

timeline.event: Born in Italy
Became director of the State Orthophrenic School
Devised the 'Montessori method' while taking charge of 60 children from a slum in Rome
Published the book 'The Secret of Childhood (Il segreto dell'infanzia)'
Published the book 'The Absorbent Mind (La mente del bambino)'
Died

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Montessori Method
keyAchievements.description: Based on the potential for children's self-development, she devised an educational method that helps free self-expression and disseminated it worldwide.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Sensory Training Toys
keyAchievements.description: By applying the toys she used for intellectually disabled children to non-disabled children, she discovered toys that aid sensory development.

faq.question: Who was Maria Montessori?
faq.answer: She was an Italian educator and physician who developed the Montessori educational method and disseminated it widely around the world.

faq.question: What is the core principle of the Montessori method?
faq.answer: She viewed that children inherently have the potential for self-development mentally, and that education is about letting children freely express and develop themselves.

faq.question: What is the role of the teacher in the Montessori method?
faq.answer: She stated that the teacher should observe and appropriately direct the children's activities, provide an environment that aids the children's self-activity, and intervene only when necessary.

timeline.event: Born on December 10 in Newburyport, Massachusetts, USA
Founded the newspaper 'The Liberator', advocating the abolition of slavery
Ceased publication of 'The Liberator' and abolished slavery with the 13th Amendment to the US Constitution
Advocated for the expansion of women's suffrage
Died on May 24 in New York City, New York, USA

keyAchievements.title: Founding and Publishing the Newspaper 'The Liberator'
keyAchievements.description: He founded the newspaper 'The Liberator' advocating the abolition of slavery in 1831 and published it until 1865, when slavery was abolished by the 13th Amendment to the US Constitution.

keyAchievements.title: Founding the American Anti-Slavery Society
keyAchievements.description: As one of the founders of the American Anti-Slavery Society, he advocated for the immediate and uncompensated abolition of slavery.

keyAchievements.title: Women's Suffrage Movement
keyAchievements.description: In the 1870s, he advocated for the expansion of women's suffrage.

faq.question: Who was William Lloyd Garrison?
faq.answer: He was an American abolitionist, journalist, and social reformer who founded the newspaper 'The Liberator' advocating the abolition of slavery.

faq.question: What is the name of the newspaper William Lloyd Garrison founded?
faq.answer: The newspaper he founded is 'The Liberator', which was published from 1831 to 1865.

faq.question: What was William Lloyd Garrison's stance on the abolition of slavery?
faq.answer: He advocated for immediate and uncompensated abolition of slavery, initially based on Christian pacifism, but supported armed struggle after the outbreak of the Civil War.

timeline.event: Born in Kaysersberg, Alsace, as the eldest son of a Lutheran pastor.
Studied at the village school.
Began studying at the Realschule (secondary school).
Earned a PhD in philosophy from the University of Strasbourg with a study on Kant.
Became a pastor in Strasbourg.
Earned a PhD in theology with a study on the life of Jesus and early Christianity.
Became a lecturer at the theological faculty of his alma mater.
Entered medical school at the age of 30.
Cooperated in founding the Paris Bach Society and also served as an organist.
Began medical volunteer work with his wife, Helene Bresslau, in Lambaréné, French Equatorial Africa.
Published his theological book 'The Quest of the Historical Jesus'.
His mother died after being hit by military horses of French soldiers.
Imprisoned in a French prisoner-of-war camp.
Changed his nationality from German to French.
Returned to Lambaréné, Africa after fundraising in Europe.
Published his autobiography 'Out of My Life and Thought'.
Received the Nobel Peace Prize.
Received the British honorary Order of Merit (OM).
Engaged in an anti-nuclear movement opposing nuclear weapons through radio broadcasts and newspaper articles.
Died at the age of 90 in Lambaréné.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Peace Prize
keyAchievements.description: He received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1952 for his contributions to medical volunteer work in Africa and the development of human brotherhood, based on his philosophy of 'Reverence for Life'.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Albert Schweitzer Hospital
keyAchievements.description: He established the Albert Schweitzer Hospital in Lambaréné, Gabon, in the western region of Central Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a Musician and Organist
keyAchievements.description: He studied the German composer Johann Sebastian Bach, influenced the organ reform movement in the early 20th century, and served as an organist for the Paris Bach Society.

keyAchievements.title: Earned PhDs in Philosophy and Theology
keyAchievements.description: He earned a PhD in philosophy for his study on Kant and a PhD in theology for his study on the life of Jesus and early Christianity from the University of Strasbourg.

keyAchievements.title: Dedication to Medical Volunteer Work in Africa
keyAchievements.description: From 1913, he dedicated his life to medical work for Black people in Lambaréné, French Equatorial Africa, alongside his wife.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated the Philosophy of 'Reverence for Life'
keyAchievements.description: He advocated the philosophy of 'Reverence for Life', stating that all life is sacred and no life should be sacrificed, in his book 'Out of My Life and Thought'.

keyAchievements.title: Anti-nuclear Peace Movement
keyAchievements.description: In 1957, he engaged in an anti-nuclear movement opposing nuclear weapons through radio broadcasts and newspaper articles, greatly influencing the peace movement.

faq.question: Who was Albert Schweitzer?
faq.answer: Albert Schweitzer was a German-born French physician, musician, philosopher, theologian, and Lutheran pastor who dedicated himself to medical volunteer work in Africa based on his philosophy of 'Reverence for Life' and received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1952.

faq.question: What is Schweitzer's philosophy of 'Reverence for Life'?
faq.answer: 'Reverence for Life' is a Christian idea that all life is sacred and no life should be sacrificed, which he advocated and practiced in his book 'Out of My Life and Thought'.

faq.question: Why did Schweitzer start medical volunteer work in Africa?
faq.answer: Schweitzer believed it was right to share happiness with everyone, and maturing with the realization that 'I cannot live happily alone,' he entered medical school at age 30 and subsequently began medical volunteer work in Africa.

faq.question: What kind of musical activities did Schweitzer do?
faq.answer: Schweitzer was an organist and Bach researcher; he helped establish the Paris Bach Society, served as an organist, and held concert tours in Europe to raise funds for his volunteer work in Africa.

faq.question: When did Schweitzer receive the Nobel Peace Prize?
faq.answer: Schweitzer received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1952 for his philosophy of 'Reverence for Life' and his contributions to medical volunteer work in Africa and the advancement of human brotherhood.

faq.question: How did Schweitzer's nationality change?
faq.answer: The Alsace region where Schweitzer was born was a site of intense territorial disputes between Germany and France, and he changed his nationality from German to French in 1919.

timeline.event: Converted to Christianity while staying in England.
Was one of the 10 female delegates at the Congress session.
Established the Mukti Mission, a Christian charity, in the village of Kedgaon, 40 miles east of Pune.

keyAchievements.title: Acquiring Titles
keyAchievements.description: After passing examinations by the faculty of Calcutta University, she became the first woman to receive the titles of Pandita and Sarasvati as a Sanskrit scholar.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Sharada Sadan
keyAchievements.description: She started Sharada Sadan, a home for widows, using raised funds.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Mukti Mission
keyAchievements.description: She established the Mukti Mission, a Christian charity, in the village of Kedgaon, 40 miles east of Pune.

faq.question: What is Pandita Ramabai's real name?
faq.answer: Her real name is Pandita Ramabai Sarasvati.

faq.question: What titles did Pandita Ramabai receive?
faq.answer: After passing examinations by the faculty of Calcutta University, she received the titles of Pandita and Sarasvati as a Sanskrit scholar.

faq.question: What are the major institutions established by Pandita Ramabai?
faq.answer: She established Sharada Sadan for widows and the Mukti Mission, a Christian charity.

timeline.event: Born in Bristol, Gloucestershire, England.
Entire family immigrated to New York, USA.
Family settled in Cincinnati, Ohio.
Admitted to Geneva Medical College in New York state.
Graduated top of her class from Geneva Medical College.
Dedicated herself to nursing and treating wounded soldiers upon the outbreak of the American Civil War.
Founded a medical college in New York state.
Founded a medical college in London.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Graduated Top of Her Class from Geneva Medical College
keyAchievements.description: In 1849, she graduated at the top of her class from Geneva Medical College in New York, becoming a doctor.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Medical Colleges
keyAchievements.description: She established a medical college in New York in 1865 and in London in 1874.

keyAchievements.title: Medical Activities During the Civil War
keyAchievements.description: When the American Civil War broke out in 1861, she dedicated herself to nursing and treating wounded soldiers.

faq.question: Who was Elizabeth Blackwell?
faq.answer: Elizabeth Blackwell was an English physician born in 1821 and died in 1910.

faq.question: When did Elizabeth Blackwell decide to become a doctor?
faq.answer: She decided to become a doctor in Cincinnati, Ohio, under the influence of Harriet Beecher Stowe.

faq.question: What kind of education did Elizabeth Blackwell receive?
faq.answer: She entered Geneva Medical College in New York state in 1847 and graduated at the top of her class in 1849.

faq.question: What medical institutions did Elizabeth Blackwell establish?
faq.answer: She established a medical college in New York in 1865 and in London in 1874.

timeline.event: Born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Joined Jane Addams and other reformers at Hull House in Chicago.
Appointed the first Chief Factory Inspector for the State of Illinois.
Became the first general secretary of the National Consumers League (NCL).
Died in Germantown, Philadelphia, after decades of advocating for labor rights and children's welfare.
Born in Scotland as the son of a working-class family.
Departed for Africa as a medical missionary for the London Missionary Society.
Arrived in Botswana, South Africa, and began missionary work.
Discovered Lake Ngami during his first expedition.
Discovered the Zambezi River during his first expedition.
Discovered Victoria Falls during his first expedition and successfully crossed Africa.
Became a British consul and led an expedition to Central Africa, exploring the Zambezi River basin and liberating slaves.
Commissioned by the Royal Geographical Society, he left for Africa again and discovered Lake Mweru and Lake Bangweulu.
Contracted a fever in Ujiji on the shores of Lake Tanganyika but was rescued by Henry Morton Stanley's search expedition.
Died of extreme exhaustion and illness on the shores of Lake Bangweulu.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration and Discoveries in Africa
keyAchievements.description: From 1849 to 1856, he crossed the Kalahari Desert, discovered Lake Ngami, the Zambezi River, and Victoria Falls, successfully crossing the African continent.

keyAchievements.title: Anti-Slavery Activities
keyAchievements.description: In 1858, he intervened in the slave trade by the Portuguese in the Zambezi River basin, liberating hundreds of slaves.

keyAchievements.title: Geographical Exploration of Central Africa
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned by the Royal Geographical Society, he continued his exploration of Central Africa, discovering lakes such as Mweru and Bangweulu.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Exploration Records
keyAchievements.description: After returning from his African explorations, he published 'Missionary Travels', detailing his experiences.

faq.question: When and where was David Livingstone born?
faq.answer: David Livingstone was born in Scotland on March 19, 1813.

faq.question: What was David Livingstone's main profession?
faq.answer: He was a Scottish Congregationalist missionary and explorer.

faq.question: What major discoveries did David Livingstone make in Africa?
faq.answer: He discovered Lake Ngami, the Zambezi River, and Victoria Falls, and successfully crossed the Kalahari Desert and the African continent.

faq.question: How did David Livingstone die, and what happened to his body?
faq.answer: He died on May 1, 1873, on the shores of Lake Bangweulu. His heart was buried in Zambia, and his embalmed body was given a state funeral at Westminster Abbey in London.

faq.question: What social activities did David Livingstone engage in while in Africa?
faq.answer: He intervened in the slave trade by the Portuguese in the Zambezi River basin, freeing hundreds of slaves.

timeline.event: Birth of Alfred Bernhard Nobel.
Nobel's father moved the family to St. Petersburg, Russia.
Went to Paris, France to continue his studies.
Went to the United States to study for a year at the age of 18.
Filed his first patent for a gas meter at the British Patent Office.
Nobel's father left the factory to his second son, Ludvig Nobel.
Received his first Swedish patent for 'a method of preparing gunpowder'.
Invented the detonator.
An explosion at the Heleneborg factory in Stockholm killed five people, including his younger brother Emil.
Designed the blasting cap, an improvement on the detonator.
Invented dynamite and received patents in the US and UK.
Demonstrated dynamite for the first time at a quarry in Redhill, Surrey, England.
Invented gelignite.
Received a patent for gelignite.
Elected a member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences.
Patented ballistite.
Alfred Nobel's brother Ludvig died, leading some newspapers to mistakenly publish Alfred Nobel's obituary.
Received an honorary doctorate from Uppsala University.
Finally signed his will at the Swedish-Norwegian Club in Paris, establishing the Nobel Prize.
Died at his villa in Sanremo, Italy.
The Nobel Prize system was established.
The Sveriges Riksbank donated to the Nobel Foundation to establish the 'Sveriges Riksbank Prize in Economic Sciences in Memory of Alfred Nobel'.
Alfred Nobel's great-grandnephew Peter Nobel requested that the Economics Prize be distinguished from the original Nobel Prizes.
The Nobel Foundation holds investment assets of approximately 6 billion Swedish kronor.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Dynamite
keyAchievements.description: He invented dynamite, a solid explosive, and received a patent for it in 1867.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Gelignite
keyAchievements.description: In 1875, he invented gelignite, which was more stable and powerful than dynamite, and patented it in 1876.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Ballistite
keyAchievements.description: In 1887, he patented ballistite, a type of smokeless powder.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Nobel Prize
keyAchievements.description: He established the Nobel Prize with his fortune to annually award those who 'conferred the greatest benefit to humankind'.

keyAchievements.title: Held 355 Patents
keyAchievements.description: During his lifetime, he made numerous important contributions to science, registering a total of 355 patents internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Growth of Bofors
keyAchievements.description: He transformed Bofors, originally an iron and steel company, into a major manufacturer of cannons and armaments.

keyAchievements.title: Design of Detonators and Blasting Caps
keyAchievements.description: He invented the detonator in 1863 and designed the blasting cap, an improvement on the detonator, in 1865.

faq.question: What inventions is Alfred Nobel famous for?
faq.answer: He invented dynamite, a solid explosive, as well as gelignite and ballistite.

faq.question: How did the Nobel Prize begin?
faq.answer: Upon his death, Nobel donated most of his wealth to establish a fund to run the Nobel Prize, which annually awards those who have 'conferred the greatest benefit to humankind'.

faq.question: How many patents did Nobel hold during his lifetime?
faq.answer: Nobel registered a total of 355 patents internationally during his life.

faq.question: When did Alfred Nobel die?
faq.answer: Nobel died on December 10, 1896, at his villa in Sanremo, Italy.

faq.question: What things are named after Alfred Nobel?
faq.answer: The synthetic element nobelium was named after him, and there is a Nobel Prize Museum in Stockholm dedicated to his life and the Nobel Prize.

timeline.event: Born in Paris as Charles Pierre de Frédy, Baron de Coubertin.
Announced a plan to revive the Olympic Games and declared it at the congress of the Union des Sociétés Françaises de Sports Athlétiques. Officiated the French Rugby Union championship.
Organized the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and became its Secretary General.
Held the first modern Olympic Games in Athens, Greece, and assumed the position of IOC President.
The Olympics were held in Paris but were overshadowed by the World's Fair.
The Olympics were held in St. Louis but were overshadowed by the World's Fair.
Created the modern pentathlon for the Summer Olympics.
Stepped down as IOC President after the Summer Olympics held in Lausanne.
Was a nominee for the Nobel Peace Prize but did not win.
Died of a stroke in Geneva on September 2.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the Modern Olympics
keyAchievements.description: He founded the modern Olympic Games with the idea of contributing to world peace by reviving the ancient Olympic Games.

keyAchievements.title: Organization and Presidency of the International Olympic Committee (IOC)
keyAchievements.description: In 1894, he organized the IOC and became its Secretary General, later serving as its President and laying the foundation for the modern Olympics.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Olympic Charter and Protocol
keyAchievements.description: He developed the Olympic Charter and protocols, including the procedures for the opening and closing ceremonies.

keyAchievements.title: Design of the Olympic Rings Symbol
keyAchievements.description: He designed the Olympic rings, the symbol of the Olympics.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Modern Pentathlon
keyAchievements.description: He created the modern pentathlon for the 1912 Summer Olympics.

keyAchievements.title: Organization of the Universal Pedagogical Union
keyAchievements.description: He organized the Universal Pedagogical Union and was appointed as its honorary president for life.

faq.question: Who was Pierre de Coubertin?
faq.answer: He was a French educator and the founder of the modern Olympic Games.

faq.question: What was the main motive behind founding the modern Olympics?
faq.answer: While pursuing educational reform, he realized the importance of sports, and the excavation of the Olympia ruins by German archaeologists inspired him to revive the ancient Olympics.

faq.question: What was his role in the International Olympic Committee (IOC)?
faq.answer: He organized the IOC in 1894, became its first Secretary General, and subsequently served as its President, leading the Olympic movement.

faq.question: What is the significance of the Pierre de Coubertin medal?
faq.answer: It is an award given to athletes who demonstrate true sportsmanship at the Olympics, considered one of the highest honors an Olympic athlete can receive.

faq.question: What did he aim to achieve through the Olympics?
faq.answer: He sought to contribute to world peace by bringing together the youth of different countries through the Olympic Games.

timeline.event: Born in Store Frøen.
Made his first voyage to the waters off Greenland.
Received a doctorate from the University of Christiania for his thesis 'The Structure and Combination of the Histological Elements of the Central Nervous System'.
Crossed the Greenland ice cap on skis from east to west along with Otto Sverdrup and others.
Attempted to cross the Arctic Ocean aboard the Fram, an exploration ship he designed and built.
Reached a latitude of 86° 14' N with Hjalmar Johansen, recording the northernmost point reached by humans at the time.
After wintering in Franz Josef Land, met a British expedition led by Frederick George Jackson and returned.
Appointed and served as the Norwegian Ambassador to the United Kingdom.
Received the Nobel Peace Prize for his activities as the High Commissioner of the League of Nations.
Co-founded the anti-socialist political organization Fedrelandslaget (Fatherland League).
Ended his life in Lysaker, near Oslo.

keyAchievements.title: Crossing the Greenland Ice Cap
keyAchievements.description: In 1888, he crossed the Greenland ice cap on skis from east to west with Otto Sverdrup and others.

keyAchievements.title: Arctic Exploration and Reaching the Farthest North
keyAchievements.description: He attempted to cross the Arctic Ocean on the Fram, which he designed and built, and in 1895 reached 86° 14' N, the northernmost latitude reached by man at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to the Neuron Doctrine
keyAchievements.description: He became one of the founders of the neuron doctrine by arguing that the neural network consists of individual communicating cells.

keyAchievements.title: Oceanographic Research
keyAchievements.description: Based on his experiences on the Fram, he studied the nature and causes of ocean currents and served as a professor of oceanography at the University of Oslo.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Norwegian Independence and Diplomacy
keyAchievements.description: He played a major role in persuading Prince Carl of Denmark to accept the Norwegian throne, and worked as the Norwegian Ambassador to the UK to secure British support for Norway's territorial integrity.

keyAchievements.title: Refugee Relief Work and the Nansen Passport
keyAchievements.description: As the League of Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, he engaged in humanitarian work such as repatriating prisoners of war, undertaking refugee relief, and issuing the Nansen passport for refugees.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Peace Prize
keyAchievements.description: He was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1922 for his humanitarian work with the League of Nations.

faq.question: Who was Fridtjof Nansen?
faq.answer: Fridtjof Nansen was a Norwegian explorer, scientist, and diplomat who received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1922 for his activities as the League of Nations High Commissioner.

faq.question: What are Fridtjof Nansen's main exploration achievements?
faq.answer: He crossed the Greenland ice cap on skis in 1888 and attempted to cross the Arctic Ocean on the Fram in 1893, reaching the then-northernmost point of 86° 14' N in 1895.

faq.question: What were Fridtjof Nansen's academic contributions?
faq.answer: He was one of the founders of the neuron doctrine, stating that the neural network is composed of individual cells. He also studied the properties and origins of ocean currents and served as a professor of zoology and oceanography at the University of Oslo.

faq.question: Why did Fridtjof Nansen receive the Nobel Peace Prize?
faq.answer: He received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1922 for contributing to humanitarian work as the League of Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, including repatriating prisoners of war, undertaking refugee relief, and issuing Nansen passports.

faq.question: What is the Nansen passport?
faq.answer: The Nansen passport was an internationally recognized travel document issued to refugees by Fridtjof Nansen during his time as the League of Nations High Commissioner for Refugees.

US Patent 388,850 'Camera' issued (September)

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Eastman Kodak
keyAchievements.description: He founded the company known as Eastman Kodak.

keyAchievements.title: Launch of Roll Film and Popularization of Photography
keyAchievements.description: He launched roll film, contributing to bringing photography into the mainstream.

keyAchievements.title: Laying the Foundation for Motion Picture Film
keyAchievements.description: Roll film became the basis for the invention of motion picture film in 1888.

keyAchievements.title: George Eastman Museum Designated as a National Historic Landmark
keyAchievements.description: The George Eastman Museum was designated as a National Historic Landmark in the United States.

keyAchievements.title: Two Stars on the Hollywood Walk of Fame
keyAchievements.description: He is the only person to be represented by two stars on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.

faq.question: Which company is George Eastman associated with?
faq.answer: George Eastman founded the company called Eastman Kodak.

faq.question: What are George Eastman's major achievements?
faq.answer: He contributed to popularizing photography by launching roll film, which also served as the basis for the invention of motion picture film in 1888.

faq.question: What is the significance of the George Eastman Museum?
faq.answer: The George Eastman Museum is designated as a National Historic Landmark in the United States.

timeline.event: Birth of Elisha Graves Otis (August 3)
Moved to New York at the age of 19, leaving his home.
Invented an elevator equipped with a safety device.
Founded Otis Elevator in New York.
Publicly demonstrated the elevator with a safety device at the New York World's Fair.
Otis Elevator installed the first passenger elevator in New York.
Received a patent related to elevator safety but died shortly after (April 8).

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Modern Elevator
keyAchievements.description: He invented the elevator with a safety device, laying the foundation for modern elevators.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Otis Elevator
keyAchievements.description: He founded the Otis Elevator Company in New York in 1853.

keyAchievements.title: Public Demonstration of the Safety Elevator
keyAchievements.description: At the 1854 New York World's Fair, he publicly demonstrated the operation of his safety device by cutting the hoisting rope of an ascending elevator.

keyAchievements.title: Installation of the First Passenger Elevator
keyAchievements.description: Through Otis Elevator, the first passenger elevator was installed in New York in 1857.

faq.question: What is Elisha Otis famous for?
faq.answer: He is the inventor of the modern elevator and the founder of the Otis Elevator Company.

faq.question: When did Elisha Otis invent the safety elevator?
faq.answer: He invented the elevator equipped with a safety device in 1851.

faq.question: When was the Otis Elevator Company founded?
faq.answer: The Otis Elevator Company was founded in New York in 1853.

faq.question: How did Elisha Otis demonstrate the safety elevator?
faq.answer: At the 1854 New York World's Fair, he demonstrated the operation of the safety device by having the hoisting rope cut while he stood on an ascending elevator.

timeline.event: Dispatched to China with Yun Sa-ung, Choi Cheon-gu, and others to learn the designs of astronomical instruments.

Freed from his status as a government slave in recognition of his contributions to manufacturing astronomical instruments, and appointed as an official of the 5th junior rank at the Royal Bureau of Attire.
Promoted to the 5th senior rank and ordered to manufacture the Gaengjeomjigi, the first water clock.
Granted the military rank of Hogun (4th senior rank).
Completed the Jagyeokru, an automatic water clock powered by water.
Participated in the casting of the Gabinja, a movable metal type, under the general direction of Yi Cheon.
Completed the Ongnu, an astronomical clock, and installed it in the Heumgyeonggak pavilion.

keyAchievements.title: Jagyeokru
keyAchievements.description: He completed the Jagyeokru, a hydro-powered automatic water clock that strikes the time.

keyAchievements.title: Ongnu
keyAchievements.description: He manufactured the Ongnu, an astronomical clock that displays the movement of the sun and moon as models, allowing observation of time, seasons, and celestial movements.

keyAchievements.title: Astronomical Instruments
keyAchievements.description: He produced various astronomical observation instruments such as the Daeganui, Soganui, Hyeonju-ilgu, Cheonpyeong-ilgu, Jeongnam-ilgu, Angbu-ilgu, Ilseongjeongsiui, and Gyupyo.

keyAchievements.title: Gabinja
keyAchievements.description: He contributed to the advancement of printing technology by participating in the casting of the Gabinja, a movable metal type made of copper.

faq.question: What is Jang Yeong-sil's birth year?
faq.answer: Jang Yeong-sil's birth year is not clearly recorded, but it is estimated that he was born between 1389 and 1390, judging from records indicating he was about 34 years old when he was freed from slavery in 1423.

faq.question: What was Jang Yeong-sil's social status?
faq.answer: Jang Yeong-sil was originally a government slave of Dongnae-hyeon, but his outstanding technical skills were recognized, and he was promoted by King Taejong and King Sejong.

faq.question: Why did Jang Yeong-sil step down from his official position?
faq.answer: Due to an incident where King Sejong's palanquin broke, he was accused of disrespect to the King, faced dismissal from office, and subsequently disappeared from history.

faq.question: What were Jang Yeong-sil's family relations?
faq.answer: His father was an immigrant from the Yuan dynasty, originally from Suzhou/Hangzhou, and his mother was a gisaeng from Dongnae-hyeon, Joseon. He had one younger sister, and his younger female cousin is said to have married the astronomer Kim Dam.

timeline.event: Born in Myeongdong-chon, North Gando
Entered Myeongdong Elementary School and published the literary magazine 'New Myeongdong'
Graduated from Myeongdong Elementary School and transferred to Hwaryong County First Elementary School
Transferred to Soongsil Middle School in Pyongyang and published the poem 'Fantasy'
Transferred to Gwangmyeong Middle School after the closure of Soongsil Middle School

Entered Yonhi Technical College
Published a poem in 'Sonyeon' (Boy) magazine and received his first manuscript fee
Changed his name to 'Hiranuma' under the Japanese name-changing policy
Graduated from the Department of Literature at Yonhi Technical College
Entered the Department of English Literature at Rikkyo University in Tokyo
Transferred to the Department of English Literature at Doshisha University in Kyoto
Arrested by the Japanese police on charges of violating the Public Security Preservation Law
Sentenced to 2 years in prison by the Kyoto District Court and imprisoned in Fukuoka Prison
Died in prison at Fukuoka Prison
His posthumous works were first introduced in the Kyunghyang Shinmun and a memorial service was held
A preliminary edition of his posthumous poetry collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem' was provisionally published
His posthumous poetry collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem' was officially published
The 'Yun Dong-ju Monument' engraved with his posthumous work 'Prologue' was erected at Yonsei University
The Yun Dong-ju Literature Award was established and the first recipient was selected
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Independent Medal
The 'Prologue' monument was erected at Yongjeong Middle School
The 'Prologue' monument was erected at Doshisha University in Japan
Selected as a Korean artist who brightened the 20th century by the Korean Association of Art Critics

keyAchievements.title: Activities as an Independence Activist, Poet, and Writer
keyAchievements.description: He was active as a Korean independence activist, poet, and writer during the Japanese colonial period.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of the Literary Magazine 'New Myeongdong'
keyAchievements.description: During his time at Myeongdong Elementary School, he published the literary magazine 'New Myeongdong' to begin his literary activities.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Debut
keyAchievements.description: While attending Yonhi Technical College, he officially debuted in the literary world by publishing a poem in the magazine 'Sonyeon'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Posthumous Poetry Collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem'
keyAchievements.description: After his death, his posthumous poetry collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem', containing most of his works, was published and caused a great sensation.

keyAchievements.title: Recognized as a Conscientious Intellectual
keyAchievements.description: He was recognized as a conscientious intellectual in the late Japanese colonial period, and his poetry contains criticism of Japanese imperialism and self-reflection.

keyAchievements.title: Evaluated as a Nationalist Resistance Poet
keyAchievements.description: He is evaluated as a nationalist resistance poet and a poet with a strong will and gentle lyricism.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as the Most Beloved Poet by Koreans
keyAchievements.description: In 1991, he was selected as the poet most loved by Koreans.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Award of the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Independent Medal
keyAchievements.description: In 1990, the South Korean government posthumously awarded him the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Independent Medal to honor his contributions.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Yun Dong-ju Literature Award
keyAchievements.description: To inherit his poetic spirit, the Yun Dong-ju Literature Award was established by the Korean Writers Association in 1985.

faq.question: When and where was Yun Dong-ju born?
faq.answer: Yun Dong-ju was born on December 30, 1917, in Myeongdong-chon, North Gando.

faq.question: What schools did Yun Dong-ju graduate from?
faq.answer: He graduated from Myeongdong Elementary School and Jilin Gwangmyeong Middle School, and attended Yonhi Technical College.

faq.question: What is Yun Dong-ju's representative poetry collection?
faq.answer: Most of his works are contained in the posthumous poetry collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem' published after his death.

faq.question: Why was Yun Dong-ju arrested in Japan?
faq.answer: On July 14, 1943, he was arrested by the Japanese police as an ideological offender under the Public Security Preservation Law and imprisoned in Fukuoka Prison.

faq.question: When did Yun Dong-ju die?
faq.answer: Yun Dong-ju died in prison at Fukuoka Prison on February 16, 1945.

faq.question: Is it true that Yun Dong-ju changed his name to a Japanese style?
faq.answer: In late 1941, he changed his name to 'Hiranuma', and the prevailing view is that this was an unavoidable choice for studying abroad in Japan.

faq.question: How is Yun Dong-ju evaluated?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as a nationalist resistance poet and a poet with strong will and gentle lyricism, and his poetry contains the agony and self-reflection of a colonial intellectual.

timeline.event: Born in Colonus, an administrative district of Athens, as the son of a wealthy knight.
Selected as a lead choir boy to sing at a banquet celebrating the Battle of Salamis.
At the age of 29, he gained fame by defeating his senior Aeschylus in the tragic drama competition at the City Dionysia.
Elected as one of the 10 commanders of the Delian League.
Entered political life, held several key positions, and served as a naval admiral.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: One of the Three Great Tragic Poets of Greece
keyAchievements.description: Along with Aeschylus and Euripides, he is considered one of the three great tragic poets of ancient Greece.

keyAchievements.title: Victories in Tragic Drama Competitions
keyAchievements.description: He won 1st place 24 times in the tragic drama competitions at the City Dionysia, earning the title of the perfecter of tragic art.

keyAchievements.title: Innovation in Tragic Art
keyAchievements.description: He abolished the trilogy format, increased the number of chorus members, introduced a third actor, and adopted props and background painting, contributing to the development of drama.

keyAchievements.title: Political and Military Activities
keyAchievements.description: He participated in political life before and after the Peloponnesian War, serving as a commander of the Delian League and a naval admiral.

keyAchievements.title: Extant Works
keyAchievements.description: Seven tragic plays survive: 'Women of Trachis', 'Ajax', 'Antigone', 'Oedipus Rex', 'Electra', 'Philoctetes', and 'Oedipus at Colonus'.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Hero Title
keyAchievements.description: After his death, he received the hero title of 'Dexion' from the citizens of Athens.

faq.question: When was Sophocles born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Sophocles was born in 497 BC and died in 406 BC.

faq.question: How is Sophocles evaluated?
faq.answer: As a tragic poet of ancient Athens, Greece, he is considered one of the three great Greek tragic poets alongside Aeschylus and Euripides.

faq.question: What contributions did Sophocles make to tragic art?
faq.answer: Known as the perfecter of tragic art, he contributed to the development of drama by abolishing the trilogy format, increasing the chorus members, and introducing a third actor.

faq.question: What are the representative extant works of Sophocles?
faq.answer: There are 7 extant works: 'Women of Trachis', 'Ajax', 'Antigone', 'Oedipus Rex', 'Electra', 'Philoctetes', and 'Oedipus at Colonus'.

faq.question: Did Sophocles also engage in political activities?
faq.answer: He participated in political life before and after the Peloponnesian War, serving as a commander of the Delian League and a naval admiral.

sophocles.missingDataNote: 16살 되던 해에 발생한 사건의 정확한 연도는 원문에 명시되어 있지 않습니다.

timeline.event: Birth of Euclid
Active in Alexandria during the reign of Ptolemy I Soter
Death of Euclid

keyAchievements.title: 'Elements'
keyAchievements.description: Consisting of 13 books covering geometry and number theory, it is one of the most influential works in the history of mathematics due to its logical progression deriving results from a set of axioms.

keyAchievements.title: 'Data'
keyAchievements.description: A treatise dealing with geometry.

keyAchievements.title: 'Phaenomena'
keyAchievements.description: A treatise dealing with spherical geometry.

keyAchievements.title: 'Optics'
keyAchievements.description: The oldest surviving Greek treatise on optics.

keyAchievements.title: 'On Divisions of Figures'
keyAchievements.description: Deals with the problem of dividing a given figure in a given ratio; it survives only partially in an Arabic translation.

keyAchievements.title: 'Catoptrics'
keyAchievements.description: Deals with geometric problems using mirrors.

faq.question: Is there an anecdote about Euclid's disciple asking about the practicality of mathematics?
faq.answer: There is a famous anecdote that when a disciple asked about the practicality of mathematics during Euclid's lecture, Euclid gave him three coins and told him to be kicked out of the lecture hall.

euclid.missingDataNote: 에우클레이데스의 삶에 관해 알려진 바가 많지 않으며, 그에 대한 언급은 그가 살았던 세기 후에 쓰여진 것이 대부분이다. 《거울 광학》은 후대의 작품으로 추측되며, 여러 저서가 손실되어 짧은 인용만 전해진다.

timeline.event: Born as Mdungazwe, son of King Mzila of the Gaza Empire.
After his father's death, defeated his brothers in a war of succession, ascended the throne, and took the name Gungunhana.
Moved the capital from the north to Manjacaze in the south to be closer to the center of Portuguese influence.
A Portuguese army led by Joaquim Mouzinho de Albuquerque declared war, and Gungunhana was captured in the sacred village of Chaimite in December.
After being paraded in Lisbon, he was exiled to the Azores, a Portuguese archipelago in the Atlantic.
Died in exile on Terceira Island in the Azores.
His remains were repatriated to Mozambique and reburied with honors as a national hero.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of the Gaza Empire
keyAchievements.description: Following a succession crisis, successfully united the empire and established authority over rival factions.

keyAchievements.title: Skillful Diplomacy
keyAchievements.description: Adeptly attempted to play the colonial interests of Britain and Portugal against each other to preserve his empire's independence.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Resistance
keyAchievements.description: Although ultimately unsuccessful, his resolute resistance to Portuguese colonization made him a national hero of Mozambique and a symbol of the anti-colonial struggle.

faq.question: Why is he called the 'Lion of Gaza'?
faq.answer: The name reflects his fierce reputation as a warrior and his resolute resistance against the Portuguese, symbolizing the strength and pride of his Gaza Empire.

faq.question: Did Gungunhana have any allies?
faq.answer: He attempted to form an alliance with the British South Africa Company led by Cecil Rhodes to counter the Portuguese. However, these diplomatic efforts ultimately failed to provide the military support he needed.

faq.question: What happened to his body after his death?
faq.answer: He was buried in the Azores. In 1985, his remains were repatriated to Mozambique and reburied with honors as a national hero, solidifying his legacy.

timeline.event: Born as the daughter of Usman dan Fodio, the founder of the Sokoto Caliphate.
Lived through the Fulani War which led to the establishment of the Sokoto Caliphate.
Established the 'jaji' (or 'Yan Taru) network of itinerant female teachers.
Her brother Muhammad Bello became the second Caliph, and she served as one of his most trusted advisors.
Died in Sokoto, leaving a legacy of over 60 written works and an innovative educational system.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Jaji ('Yan Taru) Network
keyAchievements.description: She built a system of itinerant female teachers to educate women in rural areas across the Caliphate.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Poet and Scholar
keyAchievements.description: She authored over 60 works in Arabic, Fula, and Hausa, recording history, law, and theology.

keyAchievements.title: Advisor to the Caliph
keyAchievements.description: She served as a trusted advisor to her brother, Caliph Muhammad Bello, influencing state policies on education and social welfare.

faq.question: What does 'Nana' in her name mean?
faq.answer: 'Nana' is an honorific title similar to princess or lady, and is not her real name. Her real name was Asma'u bint Usman dan Fodio.

faq.question: What languages did Nana Asma'u speak?
faq.answer: She was a polyglot, fluent in at least four languages: Arabic, Fula (Fulfulde), Hausa, and Tamasheq (Tuareg).

faq.question: Is her legacy still relevant today?
faq.answer: Yes, her work is a vital historical precedent for Muslim feminism and women's education in Africa, and her educational movement continues to inspire several modern initiatives.

timeline.event: Born in Egypt, though her exact origins remain unclear.
Married Pharaoh Amenhotep IV, who later changed his name to Akhenaten.
Akhenaten began his religious revolution. The royal court moved to the new capital Akhetaten (modern Amarna).
Nefertiti vanished from the surviving historical record. Her fate is unknown.
After Akhenaten's death, the old gods were gradually restored under the rule of Tutankhamun.
The famous painted limestone bust of Nefertiti was discovered in Amarna by German archaeologist Ludwig Borchardt.

keyAchievements.title: Religious Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Co-led a radical shift from traditional Egyptian polytheism to the monotheistic worship of the sun god Aten.

keyAchievements.title: Co-ruler of Egypt
keyAchievements.description: Wielded immense power and was often depicted in poses signifying pharaonic authority, suggesting near-equal status with her husband Akhenaten.

keyAchievements.title: Icon of Amarna Art
keyAchievements.description: Became the central subject of a new naturalistic art style that portrayed the royal family with unprecedented intimacy and realism.

faq.question: What does your name mean?
faq.answer: My name, Nefertiti, means 'The beautiful one has come' in ancient Egyptian.

faq.question: Why did you and Akhenaten worship only one god?
faq.answer: We believed that Aten, the sun disk, was the universal creator and sustainer of all life, not just in Egypt, but across the world. We sought to replace the complex, and in our view, corrupt old gods with this single, pure source of light and truth.

faq.question: What happened to you?
faq.answer: My fate after my husband's 12th regnal year is a mystery lost to the sands of time. Some believe I died, others think I fell out of favor, and some even speculate that I subsequently ruled as pharaoh. The records are silent, but my legacy endures forever.

timeline.event: Traditional date when Dido is said to have founded Carthage according to ancient records.
Traditional date of Dido's death according to the historian Timaeus.
The Roman poet Virgil wrote the epic 'Aeneid', which contains the most famous and influential account of Dido's life and her tragic love affair with Aeneas.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Carthage
keyAchievements.description: She led her people from Tyre and, through wisdom and leadership, established one of the most powerful and enduring cities of the ancient world.

keyAchievements.title: The Oxhide Negotiation
keyAchievements.description: Her legendary wit in cutting an oxhide into thin strips to enclose a large tract of land became a symbol of ingenuity and the foundation of her new kingdom.

keyAchievements.title: Archetype of the Tragic Heroine
keyAchievements.description: Her story in Virgil's 'Aeneid' immortalized her as the archetype of a powerful woman consumed by tragic love and betrayal, influencing Western art and literature for centuries.

faq.question: Was Dido a real person?
faq.answer: Her historical existence is debated. While some ancient Greek and Roman historians recorded her as a historical figure, the most famous account of her life in Virgil's 'Aeneid' is a literary creation blending myth and historical elements.

faq.question: Why did Dido take her own life?
faq.answer: In Virgil's 'Aeneid', she commits suicide out of heartbreak after Aeneas abandons her to fulfill his destiny in Italy. In older legends, it is said she took her own life to avoid a forced marriage to a local king and remain faithful to her dead husband.

faq.question: What is the significance of Dido's curse?
